topics.title: Estimation of Theoretical Number of Stages Using McCabe Thiele Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Theoretical Number of Stages Using McCabe Thiele Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McCabe–Thiele Method (Graphical Stage Estimation):** The McCabe–Thiele method is a **graphical design technique** used in chemical engineering to determine the **theoretical number of equilibrium stages** required to achieve a specified separation in a **continuous binary distillation column**. It is constructed on a rectangular **x–y diagram** (where *x* = liquid mole fraction of the more volatile component and *y* = vapor mole fraction of the more volatile component), by combining the **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) curve**, the **45° diagonal ($y = x$)**, the **feed (q)-line**, and the **rectifying and stripping operating lines**, followed by a "**staircase stepping**" procedure between the equilibrium curve and the operating line(s).

In simple words, it is a **paper-and-pencil (or computer-aided) graphical method** that tells the engineer: *"Given a feed of known composition, how many trays does my distillation tower need to deliver a top product of purity $x_D$ and a bottom product of purity $x_B$?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea-Strainer Cascade:**
> Imagine you have a pot of salty water and you want pure drinking water. A **single strainer** will not remove all the salt. So you set up **multiple strainers in series** — the partly-purified water from strainer-1 becomes the input of strainer-2, and so on. Each strainer performs an *equilibrium contact* between the rising vapor and the descending liquid. The McCabe–Thiele method is simply a **drawing technique that counts how many such strainers (theoretical stages) you need** by repeatedly stepping between the equilibrium curve and the operating line on a graph. The staircase you draw *is* the column — every tread is one tray.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $x$ | Mole fraction of MVC in **liquid** | dimensionless |
| $y$ | Mole fraction of MVC in **vapor** | dimensionless |
| $x_F$ | Feed composition (liquid basis) | dimensionless |
| $x_D$ | Distillate (top product) composition | dimensionless |
| $x_B$ | Bottoms (bottom product) composition | dimensionless |
| $R$ | Reflux ratio, $R = L/D$ | dimensionless |
| $R_{min}$ | Minimum reflux ratio | dimensionless |
| $q$ | Thermal condition of feed | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Relative volatility | dimensionless |
| $N_{th}$ | Number of theoretical stages | integer |
| $E_M$ | Murphree stage efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Weightage Note:** The McCabe–Thiele construction is the **single most-asked topic** in the Process Plant Design / Mass Transfer Operations question papers. Expect either a full **14-mark construction problem** or a **3-mark conceptual question** (e.g., "state the assumptions of McCabe–Thiele").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (Assumption Set of McCabe–Thiele)

The McCabe–Thiele method is mathematically valid **only if** the following assumptions hold. These give rise to the famous **"Constant Molal Overflow (CMO)"** condition.

1. **Constant molal overflow (CMO):** The molar vapor flow rate ($V$) and molar liquid flow rate ($L$) are **constant** in each section (rectifying and stripping) of the column.
2. **Negligible heat of mixing** and constant latent heats of vaporization.
3. **Constant molar heats of solution** of the two components.
4. **Equilibrium is established on each theoretical stage** (i.e., the vapor leaving a stage is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the liquid leaving the same stage).
5. The column operates at **steady state** with constant $D$, $B$, $V$, $L$.
6. The feed is introduced at **one specific stage** (no feed mal-distribution).
7. **Binary mixture** only (multi-component extensions use Lewis-Matheson or Gilliland).

## 2.2 Mathematical Origin — Why the Operating Lines are Straight

Performing a total material balance and a component (MVC) balance on the **rectifying section envelope** gives:

$$V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

Solving for $y_{n+1}$:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

Similarly, for the **stripping section**, an envelope balance gives:

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} x_m - \frac{B}{V'} x_B$$

Because $L$ and $V$ are *constant*, these are equations of **straight lines** on the $x$–$y$ diagram. The stepping procedure is then a graphical solution of these difference equations.

## 2.3 The q-Line (Feed Line) — Physical Meaning

The **q-line** characterizes the *thermal state* of the feed. It is the locus of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines.

| Feed State | Value of $q$ | Slope of q-line ($q/(q-1)$) |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated liquid | $q = 1$ | $\infty$ (vertical line at $x_F$) |
| Saturated vapor | $q = 0$ | $0$ (horizontal line) |
| Sub-cooled liquid | $q > 1$ | between $1$ and $\infty$ |
| Super-heated vapor | $q < 0$ | negative |
| Liquid–Vapor mixture (e.g., 50% flash) | $0 < q < 1$ | between $0$ and $1$ |

The general q-line equation:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

where $z_F$ is the overall mole fraction of MVC in the feed.

## 2.4 Limiting (Reference) Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two limiting reflux conditions bracket every real design:

### (a) Total Reflux ($R \to \infty$)
- No feed, no distillate, no bottoms — all condensate returned.
- Operating lines **coincide with the diagonal** $y = x$.
- The staircase gives the **minimum number of theoretical stages**, $N_{min}$.
- Estimated analytically by the **Fenske Equation**:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

### (b) Minimum Reflux ($R_{min}$)
- Infinite number of stages required.
- The operating line passes through the **intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve** (a "pinch point").
- For a saturated liquid feed ($q=1$) and a relatively volatile system, $R_{min}$ can be read off the graph; for an ideal system:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$$

where $y^*_F$ is the vapor composition in equilibrium with $x_F$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ | Equilibrium curve (constant $\alpha$, Raoult's law ideal) |
| 2 | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | **Rectifying** operating line |
| 3 | $y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'} x_m - \dfrac{B}{V'} x_B$ | **Stripping** operating line |
| 4 | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | **q-line** (feed line) |
| 5 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\big[(x_D/(1-x_D))((1-x_B)/x_B)\big]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Fenske (total reflux, min stages) |
| 6 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$ | Min. reflux (saturated liquid feed) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = f\!\left(\dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}\right)$ | **Gilliland correlation** (graphical) |
| 8 | $N_{actual} = \dfrac{N_{th}}{E_M}$ | Real stages via Murphree efficiency |
| 9 | $L = R \cdot D$ | Liquid rate in rectifying section |
| 10 | $L' = L + qF$ | Liquid rate in stripping section |
| 11 | $V = L + D = (R+1)D$ | Vapor rate in rectifying section |
| 12 | $V' = V - (1-q)F$ | Vapor rate in stripping section |
| 13 | $\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$ | Distillate-to-feed ratio |
| 14 | $\dfrac{B}{F} = \dfrac{x_D - z_F}{x_D - x_B}$ | Bottoms-to-feed ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The McCabe–Thiele method is the *workhorse* of preliminary distillation-column design in petroleum refineries, ethanol-water separation, cryogenic air separation, and solvent recovery units. It gives the **minimum number of theoretical trays**; the real column uses **Murphree efficiency** to convert theoretical → actual trays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rectifying Operating Line

Starting from a component balance on the top of the column down to an arbitrary stage $n$ in the rectifying section:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total balance:} \quad & V_{n+1} = L_n + D \\
\text{Component balance:} \quad & V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = L_n \, x_n + D \, x_D
\end{aligned}
$$

Under **CMO**, $V_{n+1} = V$ (constant) and $L_n = L$ (constant), so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \, y_{n+1} &= L \, x_n + D \, x_D \\
y_{n+1} &= \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $V = L + D$ and $R = L/D$, giving $L/V = R/(R+1)$ and $D/V = 1/(R+1)$:

$$
\boxed{\,y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}\,}
$$

**End-points of the rectifying line:** The line passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ on the diagonal and intercepts the $y$-axis at $y = x_D / (R+1)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stripping Operating Line

Enveloping the bottom of the column up to an arbitrary stage $m$ in the stripping section:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total balance:} \quad & L'_{m-1} = V'_m + B \\
\text{Component balance:} \quad & L'_{m-1} \, x_{m-1} = V'_m \, y_m + B \, x_B
\end{aligned}
$$

Under CMO, $L' = $ const, $V' = $ const. Solving for $y_{m+1}$:

$$
\boxed{\,y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} x_m - \frac{B}{V'} x_B\,}
$$

This line passes through $(x_B, x_B)$ on the diagonal and intercepts the $y$-axis at $y = -B x_B / V'$ (negative intercept — typical of stripping lines).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The q-Line Equation

At the feed stage, $L' = L + qF$ and $V' = V - (1-q)F$. The point of intersection of the two operating lines can be derived by setting them equal at the feed plate, leading to:

$$
y = \frac{q}{q-1}\, x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}
$$

**Special cases re-derived:**

- **Saturated liquid feed** ($q = 1$): slope $= 1/(1-1) = \infty$. The q-line is **vertical** through $x = z_F$.
- **Saturated vapor feed** ($q = 0$): slope $= 0/(0-1) = 0$. The q-line is **horizontal** at $y = z_F$.
- **Two-phase feed at its bubble point** ($q = 1$): same as saturated liquid.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Number of Stages (Fenske Equation)

At total reflux, the operating line is the diagonal $y_{n+1} = x_n$. The mass balance + equilibrium gives:

$$
\left(\frac{x_n}{1-x_n}\right) = \alpha \, \left(\frac{y_{n+1}}{1-y_{n+1}}\right) = \alpha \, \left(\frac{x_{n+1}}{1-x_{n+1}}\right)
$$

Applying this recursively from the bottom ($x_B$) to the top ($x_D$) across $N_{min}$ stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} &= \alpha^{N_{min}} \cdot \frac{x_B}{1-x_B} \\
N_{min} &= \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\ln \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Minimum Reflux Ratio

For a saturated-liquid feed and a system with no pinch, the rectifying operating line passes through the feed point $(x_F, x_F)$ and $(x_D, x_D)$. Its slope is:

$$
\frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} = \frac{x_D - x_F}{x_D - y^*_F}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}
$$

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A distillation column is to separate a benzene-toluene feed ($x_F = 0.40$, $z_F = 0.40$) into $x_D = 0.95$ and $x_B = 0.05$. Take $\alpha_{avg} = 2.47$ (constant), $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), reflux ratio $R = 1.5 R_{min}$. Determine:
> (a) $R_{min}$, (b) $R$, (c) $N_{min}$, (d) $N_{th}$ by McCabe–Thiele construction, (e) $N_{actual}$ for $E_M = 0.6$.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Equilibrium vapor composition at $x_F$:**

$$
y^*_F = \frac{\alpha\, x_F}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x_F} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.40}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.40} = \frac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222
$$

**Step (b) — Minimum reflux:**

$$
R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.6222}{0.6222 - 0.40} = \frac{0.3278}{0.2222} = 1.475
$$

**Step (c) — Operating reflux:**

$$
R = 1.5 \times R_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.475 = 2.213
$$

**Step (d) — Minimum number of stages (Fenske):**

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \times \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ stages}
$$

**Step (e) — Gilliland parameter:**

$$
X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \frac{2.213 - 1.475}{3.213} = \frac{0.738}{3.213} = 0.2297
$$

Reading the Gilliland chart at $X = 0.23$ gives $Y = (N - N_{min})/(N + 1) \approx 0.55$.

$$
N + 1 = \frac{N_{min} + 1}{1 - Y} = \frac{8}{0.45} = 17.78 \Rightarrow N_{th} \approx 17
$$

**Step (f) — Actual trays:**

$$
N_{actual} = \frac{N_{th}}{E_M} = \frac{17}{0.6} \approx 28 \text{ trays}
$$

**Construction summary on x-y diagram:**

| Curve | Points needed |
|---|---|
| Equilibrium curve | $x = 0, y = 0$ ; $x_F = 0.40 \to y = 0.62$ ; $x_D = 0.95 \to y = 0.985$ |
| Diagonal | $y = x$ |
| q-line ($q=1$) | Vertical at $x = 0.40$ |
| Rectifying line | $(0.95, 0.95)$ to $(0, x_D/(R+1)) = (0, 0.296)$ |
| Stripping line | Through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and intersection of rectifying line with q-line at $(0.40, 0.40 + 0.327) = (0.40, 0.727)$ → gives second point $(0, 0.0733)$ (negative-intercept extension) |

**Counted theoretical stages:** stepping from $x_D$ down to $x_B$ yields $\approx 17$ stages, with the **feed stage at stage 8** (counting from the top).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of McCabe–Thiele Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Specifications: xF, xD, xB, q, alpha] --> B[Compute Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium Curve]
    A --> C[Compute Material Balance: D, B, L, V, Lp, Vp]
    B --> D[Plot x-y Diagram with Equilibrium Curve and Diagonal y=x]
    C --> E[Compute q-line Slope and Plot on Diagram]
    C --> F[Compute Rmin and Choose Operating R]
    F --> G[Plot Rectifying Operating Line]
    E --> H[Intersection Point: Rectifying Line with q-line]
    H --> I[Plot Stripping Operating Line Through xB]
    D --> J[Staircase Stepping Procedure]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Count Theoretical Stages Nth]
    K --> L{Feed Stage Located?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Apply Murphree Efficiency to get Nactual]
    L -- No --> N[Adjust Feed Location and Recount]
    N --> J
    M --> O[Output: Column Design Report]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Staircase Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIAG[x-y Diagram]
        EQC([Equilibrium Curve: y=f(x)])
        DIAG45([Diagonal: y=x])
        QLINE([q-line])
        ROL([Rectifying Operating Line])
        SOL([Stripping Operating Line])
    end
    STEP1[Step 1: Start at xD, yD on diagonal] --> STEP2[Step 2: Move horizontally to equilibrium curve]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Step 3: Move vertically to operating line]
    STEP3 --> STEP4{Feed stage crossed?}
    STEP4 -- No --> STEP2
    STEP4 -- Yes --> STEP5[Switch to stripping operating line]
    STEP5 --> STEP6[Continue stepping until xB is reached]
    STEP6 --> STEP7[Count number of steps = Nth]
```

## 4.3 Distillation Column ↔ McCabe–Thiele Correspondence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COL[Distillation Column]
        TOP[Condenser: y1 = xD]
        T1[Tray 1]
        T2[Tray 2]
        T3[Tray n: Feed Tray]
        T4[Tray m]
        TB[Reboiler: yB = xB]
    end
    TOP -.Step 1 on x-y.- T1
    T1 -.Step 2 on x-y.- T2
    T2 -.Step 3 on x-y.- T3
    T3 -.Feed Stage switch.- T4
    T4 -.Final step.- TB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each **horizontal tread** of the staircase represents an **equilibrium flash** (vapor composition fixed by $y = f(x)$); each **vertical riser** represents a **mass balance step** between adjacent stages (operating-line equation). The total number of treads equals $N_{th}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the assumptions made in the McCabe–Thiele method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The McCabe–Thiele method assumes:
1. **Constant molal overflow (CMO)** in each section of the column — i.e., equimolar vaporization and condensation, latent heats of pure components are equal, and heat of mixing is negligible. **(1 mark)**
2. **Equilibrium is attained on every theoretical stage.** **(1 mark)**
3. The column operates at **steady state** with a **binary feed**, introduced at a single feed plate. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a q-line? Explain the significance of the slope $q/(q-1)$ for different feed conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The q-line (or feed line) is the locus of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines on the x-y diagram; it characterises the **thermal state of the feed**. **(1 mark)**

Its slope is $q/(q-1)$, where $q$ = moles of liquid formed per mole of feed on partial vaporisation. **(1 mark)**

- $q = 1$ (saturated liquid): slope $= \infty$ (vertical line at $x_F$).
- $q = 0$ (saturated vapor): slope $= 0$ (horizontal line at $y_F$).
- $0 < q < 1$ (two-phase feed): slope is positive, finite.
- $q > 1$ (cold liquid): slope between $1$ and $\infty$.
- $q < 0$ (super-heated vapor): slope is negative. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

A saturated-liquid feed containing **50 mol % benzene** and **50 mol % toluene** is to be separated in a continuous distillation column to give a distillate of **95 mol % benzene** and a bottoms of **5 mol % benzene**. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ is constant. Take reflux ratio $R = 1.4 \, R_{min}$.
**(a)** Determine $R_{min}$ and the operating reflux ratio $R$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the minimum number of theoretical stages using Fenske's equation and the actual number of stages using the Gilliland correlation, assuming Murphree efficiency $E_M = 0.65$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Equilibrium vapor composition in equilibrium with $x_F = 0.50$:

$$
y^*_F = \frac{\alpha \, x_F}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x_F} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.235}{1.735} = 0.7119
$$

Minimum reflux ratio:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.7119}{0.7119 - 0.50} = \frac{0.2381}{0.2119} = 1.124
$$

Operating reflux ratio:

$$
R = 1.4 \times R_{min} = 1.4 \times 1.124 = 1.574
$$

**Intercepts of the rectifying operating line:**

$y$-intercept $= x_D/(R+1) = 0.95 / 2.574 = 0.369$

Slope $= R/(R+1) = 0.611$

**Part (b):**

Fenske equation for $N_{min}$:

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_B}{x_B} \right]}{\ln \alpha} = \frac{\ln\left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \times \dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{0.9046} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ stages}
$$

Gilliland parameter:

$$
X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \frac{1.574 - 1.124}{2.574} = 0.175
$$

From the standard Gilliland chart at $X = 0.175$, $Y \approx 0.60$:

$$
N + 1 = \frac{N_{min} + 1}{1 - Y} = \frac{8}{0.40} = 20 \Rightarrow N_{th} = 19
$$

Actual trays:

$$
N_{actual} = \frac{N_{th}}{E_M} = \frac{19}{0.65} \approx 29 \text{ trays}
$$

**Construction verification (on x-y diagram):**

The McCabe–Thiele staircase yields 19 theoretical stages; feed is best introduced at stage 9 (counting from top), splitting into 8 rectifying + 11 stripping stages.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

A mixture of **ethanol (40 mol %)** and **water** is fed as a **saturated liquid** to a distillation column. Desired products: $x_D = 0.85$, $x_B = 0.02$. The VLE data are given in tabular form (assume $\alpha_{avg} \approx 2.4$). Operating reflux ratio $R = 2.0$.
**(a)** Construct the equilibrium curve using $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1)x]$ and tabulate $y$ for $x = 0.1, 0.2, \ldots, 0.9$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Draw the rectifying and stripping operating lines on the x-y diagram (show all calculations) and determine the **theoretical number of stages by staircase stepping**, locating the optimum feed stage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Equilibrium Data Table**

Using $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1)x]$ with $\alpha = 2.4$:

| $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 0.1 | 0.207 |
| 0.2 | 0.387 |
| 0.3 | 0.535 |
| 0.4 | 0.649 |
| 0.5 | 0.737 |
| 0.6 | 0.805 |
| 0.7 | 0.860 |
| 0.8 | 0.905 |
| 0.9 | 0.943 |
| 1.0 | 1.000 |

These 11 points are plotted and a smooth curve drawn — this is the equilibrium curve.

**Part (b): Operating Lines**

- **Rectifying line:** slope $= R/(R+1) = 2/3 = 0.667$; $y$-intercept $= x_D/(R+1) = 0.85/3 = 0.283$. Line passes through $(0.85, 0.85)$.
- **q-line ($q = 1$):** vertical line at $x = x_F = 0.40$.
- **Intersection:** at $x = 0.40$, $y_{rect} = 0.667(0.40) + 0.283 = 0.550$. So the intersection point is $(0.40, 0.550)$.
- **Stripping line:** passes through $(0.02, 0.02)$ and $(0.40, 0.550)$. Slope $= (0.550 - 0.02)/(0.40 - 0.02) = 0.530/0.38 = 1.395$; $y$-intercept $= 0.02 - 1.395(0.02) = -0.0079$ (essentially passes near origin).

**Staircase Construction:**

Starting at $(x_D, x_D) = (0.85, 0.85)$:

1. **Horizontal step** left to the equilibrium curve → reaches $y = 0.85$ at $x \approx 0.685$.
2. **Vertical step** down to the rectifying line.
3. Repeat, switching to the stripping line as soon as the feed stage is crossed ($x \le 0.40$).
4. Continue until $x \le 0.02$.

**Stage count:** $\approx 14$ theoretical stages, with the **feed at stage 7** (counted from the top). Rectifying section ≈ 6 stages, stripping section ≈ 8 stages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the q-line**: If you don't plot the q-line correctly, the intersection of the operating lines will be wrong, and the entire staircase construction collapses. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing $x_F$ and $z_F$**: For a two-phase feed, $x_F \neq z_F$; use $z_F$ in the q-line equation. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Using rectifying line below the feed**: Once stepping crosses the feed stage, you *must* switch to the stripping operating line. Many students continue with the rectifying line and overcount stages. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting to count the reboiler**: The reboiler is an *additional* equilibrium stage. The total $N_{th}$ from the staircase typically includes the reboiler; clarify with your examiner. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Mixing up $E_M$ and overall column efficiency $E_o$**: Murphree efficiency applies per-stage; overall efficiency is defined as $E_o = N_{th}/N_{actual}$. **(–1 mark)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for McCabe–Thiele**

- ✅ **Five essential curves on the x-y diagram:**
  1. **Equilibrium curve** ($y = f(x)$, the upper curve)
  2. **45° diagonal** ($y = x$)
  3. **q-line** (slope $= q/(q-1)$, passes through feed point)
  4. **Rectifying operating line** (slope $R/(R+1)$, passes through $(x_D, x_D)$)
  5. **Stripping operating line** (slope $L'/V'$, passes through $(x_B, x_B)$ and the intersection of rectifying line with q-line)

- ✅ **Master the q-values:** Saturated liquid $q = 1$ (vertical q-line); saturated vapor $q = 0$ (horizontal); sub-cooled $q > 1$; super-heated $q < 0$.

- ✅ **Master the limiting cases:**
  - **Total reflux** → diagonal $y = x$ → minimum stages → use **Fenske**.
  - **Minimum reflux** → pinch at feed → infinite stages.

- ✅ **CMO assumption** = the reason the operating lines are straight.

- ✅ **Staircase stepping rule:** horizontal to the equilibrium curve → vertical to the operating line → repeat. Switch operating line at the feed stage.

- ✅ **Murphree efficiency** converts theoretical stages to actual trays: $N_{actual} = N_{th}/E_M$.

- ✅ **Fenske + Gilliland + Murphree** is the *standard trilogy* in any distillation-design problem — you should know all three equations cold.

- ✅ **Board-exam trap**: When the problem gives a *partial* feed condition (e.g., "60% vaporised"), compute $q$ first as $q = 1 - (\text{vapour fraction})$ before plotting the q-line.

- ✅ **Optimum feed stage** = the stage where the staircase transitions from the rectifying to the stripping operating line — placing it correctly minimises the total $N_{th}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tray Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tray Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tray Design (Tray Sizing / Internal Design):** In chemical process plant engineering, *Tray Design* refers to the rigorous engineering procedure used to specify the geometric and operational parameters of contact trays (plates) installed inside a **distillation column** (or an absorption/stripping column). The goal is to provide efficient **vapor–liquid contact (mass transfer)** between the rising vapor and descending liquid streams while operating safely below the flooding point and above the weeping point.

Tray design is therefore the **mechanical and hydraulic design** of the column's internal stages — the trays are the "stairs" on which the vapor climbs while meeting the descending liquid.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, tray design is treated as a sub-topic under *Equipment Design* (Module 1), and the focus is on the **Sieve Tray** (the most common industrial type) and the **Bubble-Cap Tray**, along with key auxiliary calculations (weir length, downcomer area, hole area, tray spacing, flooding velocity, pressure drop, and entrainment).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Parking Garage Staircase":**
> Imagine a **multi-storey parking garage** with an open central ramp. Cars (representing the **vapor**) drive UP the ramp floor by floor. Pedestrians (representing the **liquid**) walk DOWN the staircase on the side. At every floor, there is a **small café table** where they briefly meet and exchange car keys (representing **mass transfer** of light and heavy components).
> - The **floor of each level** = a **tray**.
> - The **holes in the floor** (ventilation grates) through which the cars pass = **sieve tray perforations**.
> - The **side staircase landing** = the **downcomer** (where liquid collects before dropping to the tray below).
> - The **parapet wall on the side** = the **weir** that controls liquid level on the tray.
> - **Too many cars too fast** = **flooding** (excessive vapor carry-over).
> - **Too few cars** = **weeping** (liquid drips through the holes without proper contact).

A **well-designed tray** ensures a *Goldilocks* operating window: not too much vapor (flooding), not too little (weeping), and the contact is uniform, efficient, and economical.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **standard design metrics** are universally used in tray design references (Sinnott & Towler, Coulson & Richardson, Kister, McCabe–Smith–Harriott):

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Tray Spacing | **0.45 – 0.91 m (18 – 36 in)** | m |
| Hole Diameter (Sieve) | **3 – 12 mm (0.125 – 0.5 in)** | mm |
| Hole Pitch / Hole Diameter Ratio | **2.0 – 5.0** | dimensionless |
| Open Area (% of bubbling area) | **5 – 15 %** | % |
| Weir Height | **40 – 90 mm** | mm |
| Downcomer Clearance | **5 – 15 mm below tray** | mm |
| Vapor Velocity at Flooding | **0.3 – 3.0 m/s** | m/s |
| Tray Pressure Drop (per tray) | **0.4 – 1.0 kPa** | kPa |
| Overall Tray Efficiency (Eₒ) | **50 – 85 %** | % |
| Acceleration due to gravity (g) | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Souders–Brown flooding correlation**, the **Fair flooding chart**, and the **Kister entrainment correlation** are the three "holy grail" correlations a KTU student *must* memorize for tray design problems.

## 1.4 Why Tray Design Matters in Process Plant Engineering

A column with poorly designed trays suffers from:
- **Flooding** → liquid backup in downcomers, loss of all stages above, possible mechanical damage.
- **Weeping/Dumping** → liquid bypasses trays, severe efficiency loss.
- **Entrainment** → liquid droplets carried upward by vapor, contamination, reduced efficiency.
- **High pressure drop** → high reboiler duty, expensive column shell design.
- **Uneven flow maldistribution** → channeling, dead zones, hot spots.

Tray design directly governs **separation efficiency, column diameter, column height, energy consumption, and CAPEX/OPEX** — making it a high-weightage topic in KTU 2024 B.Tech Chemical Engineering examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Trays (Classification)

| Tray Type | Description | Application / Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Sieve Tray** | Perforated plate with round holes (3–25 mm). Vapor rises through liquid held on the plate. | Most common in industry; cheap, high capacity, low pressure drop. |
| **Valve Tray** | Holes fitted with liftable disc valves (round, rectangular, or leg-type). | Excellent turndown ratio, low leakage. |
| **Bubble-Cap Tray** | Each hole capped with an inverted bell (chimney + cap). Liquid seal always maintained. | High turndown, used in corrosive/refinery services; expensive. |
| **Cross-Flow Tray** | Liquid flows horizontally across the tray, with side downcomers. | Most common layout. |
| **Counter-Flow / Dual-Flow Tray** | No downcomers; vapor/liquid pass through same perforations. | Low capacity but very low fouling. |
| **Baffle Tray (Shower Deck)** | Series of inclined baffles. | Used for high liquid rates, slurries, fouling. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** The **Sieve Tray** is the primary type asked in university problems because its design involves the cleanest closed-form equations.

## 2.2 Tray Hydraulics — Operating Regimes

The performance window of a tray is bounded by three regimes:

1. **Weeping Regime** (low vapor rate) — Liquid drips through the holes; poor vapor–liquid contact.
2. **Operating (Froth) Regime** — Optimum: vigorous bubbling, dense foam, good contact.
3. **Flooding Regime** (high vapor rate) — Excessive liquid entrainment, downcomer backup, column shutdown.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Tray Design Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are presented using **\vert** for absolute value (or \mid) to safely render inside markdown tables.

| # | Formula (Display Form) | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_h = \dfrac{Q_V}{N_h \cdot A_h}$ | **Vapor velocity through holes** (m/s) |
| 2 | $U_f = K_{SB} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | **Souders–Brown flooding velocity** (m/s) |
| 3 | $C_{SB} = f\!\left(\rho_V,\; \rho_L,\; F_{LV},\; N_{TS}\right)$ | **Fair's flooding coefficient** (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$ | **Flow parameter** (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $h_t = h_d + h_l$ | **Total dry + liquid tray pressure drop** (mm liquid) |
| 6 | $h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{u_h^2 \cdot \rho_V}{C_d^2 \cdot \rho_L}$ | **Dry tray pressure drop** (mm liquid) — orifice equation |
| 7 | $h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{L_w}{\rho_L \cdot \ell_w}\right]^{2/3}$ | **Height of liquid over weir** (mm liquid) |
| 8 | $h_l = h_w + h_{ow} + \tfrac{h_{d}}{2}$ | **Effective liquid head on tray** (mm liquid) |
| 9 | $h_dc = 166 \cdot \left[\dfrac{Q_d}{A_{dc}}\right]^{2}$ | **Downcomer pressure loss** (mm liquid) |
| 10 | $H_d = h_t + h_dc + h_{ow}$ | **Downcomer backup** (mm liquid) — must be $\le$ (TS + hw)/2 |
| 11 | $D_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_V}{\pi \cdot u_f \cdot 0.8}}$ | **Column diameter** (m), 80 % flooding basis |
| 12 | $E_{mV} = E_{mV,0} \cdot A \cdot B \cdot C$ | **Murphree vapor efficiency** (Drickamer–Bradford or O'Connell) |
| 13 | $A_h = \dfrac{Q_V}{u_h} \quad ; \quad N_h = \dfrac{A_h}{a_h}$ | **Hole area and number of holes** |
| 14 | $\tau = \dfrac{L \cdot \rho_L}{A_b \cdot \mu_L}$ | **Liquid residence / froth parameter** |
| 15 | $u_n = \dfrac{u_h \cdot C_c}{\sqrt{1 - \alpha^2}}$ | **Velocity through net area (with contraction)** |

### Symbols Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $u_h$ | Vapor hole velocity | m/s |
| $Q_V$ | Volumetric vapor flow rate | m³/s |
| $N_h$ | Number of holes | – |
| $A_h$ | Total hole area | m² |
| $U_f$ | Flooding velocity (superficial) | m/s |
| $K_{SB},\; C_{SB}$ | Souders–Brown / Fair flooding constant | m/s or – |
| $\rho_L,\; \rho_V$ | Liquid / vapor density | kg/m³ |
| $F_{LV}$ | Flow parameter | – |
| $h_t,\; h_d,\; h_l$ | Total / dry / liquid pressure drop | mm liquid |
| $C_d$ | Orifice discharge coefficient ≈ **0.6 – 0.8** | – |
| $h_w$ | Weir height (mm) | mm |
| $h_{ow}$ | Crest over weir | mm |
| $\ell_w$ | Weir length | m |
| $L_w$ | Liquid mass flow | kg/s |
| $A_{dc}$ | Downcomer cross-section area | m² |
| $H_d$ | Downcomer backup | mm |
| $D_c$ | Column diameter | m |
| $A_b$ | Bubbling area | m² |
| $E_{mV}$ | Murphree vapor efficiency | – |
| $a_h$ | Area of one hole | m² |

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Design Step

- **Step 1 — Flooding Velocity** gives the *maximum allowable* vapor rate, set by liquid entrainment.
- **Step 2 — Column Diameter** uses 80 % of flooding (design margin) for safe operation.
- **Step 3 — Tray Spacing** is selected to give downcomer space + adequate froth disengagement.
- **Step 4 — Hole Area** is set by the desired *hole velocity*, which sets pressure drop and weeping margin.
- **Step 5 — Weir Length & Height** controls the *liquid hold-up* and froth density.
- **Step 6 — Downcomer Area** must be large enough to avoid *backup* into the tray above.
- **Step 7 — Pressure Drop** is reported as a figure of merit; total column ΔP must be acceptable to the reboiler/condenser.
- **Step 8 — Efficiency** converts theoretical stages to *real* stages for installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Tray design is a daily activity in process companies like **Reliance, Indian Oil, Shell, Honeywell UOP, Linde, Praxair**, and is integral to ethanol plants, ammonia plants, crude distillation units (CDUs), ethylene crackers, LNG fractionators, and air-separation units. The KTU 2024 syllabus bridges this directly to industrial practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Souders–Brown Flooding Velocity

The Souders–Brown (1934) correlation sets the *maximum superficial vapor velocity* to prevent excessive entrainment. It arises from a **force balance** on a suspended liquid droplet: the upward drag equals the downward net weight of the droplet in the gas.

### Step-by-step Derivation

Consider a spherical droplet of diameter $d_p$ suspended in a vertical gas stream of velocity $u$.

**Drag force (upward, from Stokes' regime):**
$$
F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_p^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_V \cdot u^2}{2}
$$

**Net gravitational force (downward):**
$$
F_g = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_p^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_V) \cdot g
$$

**At flooding (incipient suspension), the forces are in balance:**
$$
C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_p^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_V \cdot u^2}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_p^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_V) \cdot g
$$

**Solve for $u$ (the flooding velocity $U_f$):**

$$
U_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_V) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_V}}
$$

**Souders–Brown lumped the droplet-related terms into a single empirical constant $K_{SB}$**, giving:
$$
U_f = K_{SB} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **empirical form** used in practice. Typical $K_{SB}$ for a 0.61 m tray spacing is **0.107 m/s** (Kister). For a **0.45 m** spacing, $K_{SB} \approx 0.10$ m/s.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dry Tray Pressure Drop (Orifice Model)

The vapor passes through the holes of a sieve tray much like a fluid through an **orifice plate**.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Mass conservation through the holes:**
$$
Q_V = N_h \cdot A_h \cdot u_h
$$

**Bernoulli's equation between tray-below (region 1) and tray-above (region 2)** (ignoring static head of vapor):
$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{\rho_V \cdot u_h^2}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{C_d^2} - 1\right)
$$

For small Mach numbers, we use the discharge-coefficient form:
$$
\Delta P_d = \dfrac{\rho_V \cdot u_h^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2}
$$

**Convert pressure drop to equivalent head of clear liquid (mm liquid):**
$$
h_d = \dfrac{\Delta P_d \cdot 1000}{\rho_L \cdot g} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot \rho_V \cdot u_h^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \rho_L \cdot 9.81}
$$

Simplifying (with $g = 9.81$ m/s², and $u_h$ in m/s):
$$
h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{u_h^2 \cdot \rho_V}{C_d^2 \cdot \rho_L} \quad [\text{mm liquid}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is sometimes called the **"dry tray ΔP"** because it assumes the tray is dry (no liquid). The total tray ΔP adds the contribution of the liquid head to this term.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Tray Pressure Drop (Adding Liquid Head)

The total pressure drop across an operating tray is the sum of the **dry-hole drop** and the **effective liquid head** (the work done to displace the frothy liquid on the tray):

$$
h_t = h_d + h_l \quad \text{(mm liquid)}
$$

Where the **effective liquid head** $h_l$ on a cross-flow tray is the sum of the *static* liquid at the weir plus an aerated contribution:

$$
h_l = h_w + h_{ow} + \dfrac{h_d}{2} \quad \text{(mm liquid)}
$$

The factor of $h_d/2$ accounts for the **aeration of the liquid froth** by the rising vapor. This is the **standard Stichlmair–Fair formulation** used worldwide.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Height of Liquid Crest Over the Weir ($h_{ow}$)

The **Francis weir equation** is derived by integrating the velocity profile of liquid spilling over a rectangular weir of length $\ell_w$:

**Differential form (per unit length of weir):**
$$
dQ = u_y \cdot dy = \sqrt{2 g (h_{ow} - y)} \cdot dy
$$

**Integrate from $y = 0$ to $y = h_{ow}$:**
$$
Q = \int_0^{h_{ow}} \sqrt{2 g (h_{ow} - y)} \cdot dy = \sqrt{2 g} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot h_{ow}^{3/2}
$$

**Convert volumetric flow $Q$ to mass flow $L_w = Q \cdot \rho_L$ and express in mm:**
$$
h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{L_w}{\rho_L \cdot \ell_w}\right]^{2/3} \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Condition:** $h_{ow}$ must lie between **6 mm and 100 mm** for a properly designed sieve tray. If too low → weeping risk; if too high → excessive pressure drop and entrainment.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Downcomer Backup Criterion

The downcomer must be sized so that the **froth level in the downcomer** does not exceed the **tray spacing**. The downcomer backup $H_d$ is:

$$
H_d = h_t + h_dc + h_{ow} \quad \text{(mm liquid)}
$$

Where $h_{dc}$ is the head loss as the liquid turns the corner into the downcomer (a sudden expansion):
$$
h_{dc} = 166 \cdot \left[\dfrac{Q_d}{A_{dc}}\right]^{2} \quad \text{(mm liquid)}
$$

**Design criterion for non-foaming systems:**
$$
H_d \le \dfrac{\text{Tray Spacing (mm)} + h_w}{2}
$$

**Design criterion for foaming systems:**
$$
H_d \le \dfrac{\text{Tray Spacing (mm)} + h_w}{2} \cdot 0.5
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Tray Design Selection

| Service / Condition | Recommended Tray | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Clean vapor, high capacity, low ΔP | **Sieve** | Simple, cheap, high efficiency |
| Wide turndown, refinery services | **Valve** | Self-regulating hole area |
| Viscous, fouling, slurry, very low ΔP | **Bubble-Cap** | Liquid seal prevents weeping |
| Foaming system | **Sieve with large spacing** | Avoid downcomer flood |
| Cryogenic (LNG, N₂) | **Sieve** | High efficiency, low ΔP |
| Fouling / polymerizing | **Baffle / Dual-flow** | No clogging by deposits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When in doubt in a numerical, default to **sieve tray design** unless the problem explicitly mentions reflux ratio stability at very low loads (in which case valves are the answer).

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Scaffold (For Quick Reference)

A typical 14-mark KTU problem follows this **10-step structure**:

1. Compute $F_{LV}$
2. Read $C_{SB}$ from Fair's chart
3. Calculate $U_f$
4. Calculate $D_c$ (use 80 % flooding)
5. Round $D_c$ up to standard size, recompute $u_f$
6. Calculate $h_d$ (dry pressure drop)
7. Calculate $h_{ow}$ and $h_l$
8. Calculate $h_t$ (total pressure drop)
9. Calculate $h_{dc}$ and $H_d$, verify downcomer adequacy
10. Compute $N_h$ from $A_h$ and final $A_b$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Tray Layout (Top View of a Sieve Tray)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph colA["Column Wall"]
        direction TB
        subgraph trayA["Tray N+1 (Above)"]
            dctopA["Inlet Downcomer (top)"]
            bulA["Bubbling Area (perforated)"]
            dcbotA["Outlet Downcomer (bottom)"]
            weirA["Outlet Weir"]
        end
        subgraph trayB["Tray N (Current)"]
            dctopB["Inlet Downcomer (top)"]
            bulB["Bubbling Area with Sieve Holes"]
            dcbotB["Outlet Downcomer (bottom)"]
            weirB["Outlet Weir"]
        end
        subgraph trayC["Tray N-1 (Below)"]
            dctopC["Inlet Downcomer (top)"]
            bulC["Bubbling Area (perforated)"]
            dcbotC["Outlet Downcomer (bottom)"]
            weirC["Outlet Weir"]
        end
    end
    dctopA -.feeds liquid.-> trayA
    trayA -- overflows weir A --> dctopB
    dctopB -- distributor --> bulB
    bulB -- froth + vapor --> weirB
    weirB -- liquid down --> dctopC
    dctopC -- distributor --> bulC
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block visualizes the **vertical cascade of liquid** through downcomers and the **cross-flow bubbling** on each tray — a feature unique to cross-flow sieve trays.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Tray Design Computational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Given: Q_V, Q_L, rho_V, rho_L, mu_L, Tray Spacing, System Type"] --> s1["Step 1: Compute Flow Parameter F_LV"]
    s1 --> s2["Step 2: Read C_SB from Fair Chart"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: Compute Flooding Velocity U_f"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Design Vapor Velocity u_f = 0.80 x U_f"]
    s4 --> s5["Step 5: Compute Column Diameter D_c"]
    s5 --> s6{"D_c OK? Round to standard size"}
    s6 -- No --> s7["Adjust and recompute u_f"]
    s7 --> s6
    s6 -- Yes --> s8["Step 6: Set Weir Length ell_w = 0.77 x D_c"]
    s8 --> s9["Step 7: Set Weir Height h_w = 50 mm"]
    s9 --> s10["Step 8: Compute h_ow via Francis Equation"]
    s10 --> s11["Step 9: Choose Hole Diameter d_h (3-12 mm)"]
    s11 --> s12["Step 10: Choose Plate Thickness and Pitch Ratio"]
    s12 --> s13["Step 11: Compute Dry Tray Drop h_d"]
    s13 --> s14["Step 12: Compute Effective Liquid Head h_l"]
    s14 --> s15["Step 13: Compute Total Tray Drop h_t"]
    s15 --> s16["Step 14: Set Downcomer Area A_dc"]
    s16 --> s17["Step 15: Compute h_dc"]
    s17 --> s18["Step 16: Compute Downcomer Backup H_d"]
    s18 --> s19{"H_d <= 0.5 x TS?"}
    s19 -- No --> s20["Increase Tray Spacing or Downcomer Area"]
    s20 --> s16
    s19 -- Yes --> s21["Step 17: Compute Hole Area A_h and Number of Holes N_h"]
    s21 --> s22["Step 18: Verify Weeping, Entrainment, Turndown"]
    s22 --> finalA["FINAL: Tray Design Complete (Report D_c, h_t, A_h, N_h)"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Operating Envelope of a Tray

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regA["Weeping Regime\nLow V, high leakage"] -- "Increase V" --> regB["Operating Regime\nFroth, good contact"]
    regB -- "Increase V further" --> regC["Flooding Regime\nEntrainment, downcomer flood"]
    regA -. "Operate ABOVE" .-> opwinA["Design Window"]
    regC -. "Operate BELOW" .-> opwinA
    opwinA -- "Typical u_h = 3 to 12 m/s" --> opwinA
    style regA fill:#fbe5e5
    style regB fill:#d8f5d0
    style regC fill:#fbe5e5
    style opwinA fill:#bde0fe
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Lesson:** The *Operating Regime* (green) is the *Goldilocks zone*. KTU problems frequently ask students to identify the operating window and verify the design is **NOT** in weeping or flooding.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Component-Level Functional Architecture of a Tray Column

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputsA["Process Inputs"]
        in1["Vapor Feed (Q_V, rho_V)"]
        in2["Liquid Feed (Q_L, rho_L)"]
    end
    subgraph columnA["Distillation Column"]
        shellA["Column Shell (D_c, H_total)"]
        subgraph internalsA["Tray Stack (N_real stages)"]
            t1["Tray 1 (Top - Rectifying)"]
            tn["Tray N (Bottom - Stripping)"]
        end
        reboilA["Reboiler (Heat Source)"]
        condA["Condenser (Heat Sink)"]
    end
    subgraph outputsA["Process Outputs"]
        out1["Distillate (x_D)"]
        out2["Bottoms (x_B)"]
    end
    in1 --> columnA
    in2 --> columnA
    reboilA --> columnA
    columnA --> condA
    condA --> out1
    columnA --> out2
    t1 -.- tn
    t1 -- "Sie Tray + Downcomer + Weir" --> t1
    tn -- "Sie Tray + Downcomer + Weir" --> tn
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **block-level functional diagram** maps where tray design fits within the *overall process plant*. Tray design influences **column height (H_total)** via real stage count, and **column diameter (D_c)** via flooding velocity — two of the most expensive items in a column's bill of materials.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *(These are typical 2- to 3-line answer questions. Memorize precise definitions.)*

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Define "Flooding" in a distillation column tray. List the two most common correlations used to predict the flooding velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Flooding** is the condition in a tray column where the rising vapor velocity becomes so high that it cannot disengage from the liquid froth. Liquid is entrained upward into the tray above, the downcomer fills with backed-up froth, and the column loses all separation efficiency.
>
> The two most common flooding-velocity correlations are:
> 1. **Souders–Brown correlation** — empirical, $U_f = K_{SB} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$
> 2. **Fair's correlation** — chart-based, $C_{SB} = f(\rho_V, F_{LV}, \text{tray spacing})$
>
> Flooding is normally **designed at 80 %** of the calculated flooding velocity for safe column operation. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Differentiate between "Weeping" and "Entrainment" in tray columns.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Weeping | Entrainment |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of liquid leakage** | Liquid drops *downward* through the tray perforations | Liquid droplets are carried *upward* by the rising vapor |
| **Cause** | Vapor velocity *too low* (insufficient to support liquid) | Vapor velocity *too high* (excessive) |
| **Effect on efficiency** | Severe loss; vapor bypasses the tray | Moderate loss; back-mixing of liquid |
| **Remedy** | Increase vapor rate / reduce hole area | Reduce vapor rate / increase tray spacing |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Sieve Tray Sizing Numerical

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1]`

**Q. A. (a)** **Design a sieve tray column** for the following service:
- Vapor flow rate: $Q_V = 0.55 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Liquid flow rate: $Q_L = 0.0042 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Vapor density: $\rho_V = 4.0 \;\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$
- Liquid density: $\rho_L = 780 \;\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$
- Tray spacing: $0.61 \;\text{m}$ (24 in)
- Take orifice discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$
- Recommended operating velocity: $u = 0.8 \, U_f$

**Compute:**
1. Flow parameter $F_{LV}$
2. Flooding velocity $U_f$ from the Souders–Brown correlation with $K_{SB} = 0.107$ m/s for 0.61 m spacing
3. Column diameter $D_c$
4. Dry tray pressure drop $h_d$ for a hole velocity of $u_h = 12$ m/s
5. Total tray pressure drop $h_t$ assuming $h_w = 50$ mm and the weir length $\ell_w = 0.77 \, D_c$
6. Check whether the design is feasible (i.e., not in flooding).

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Flow parameter $F_{LV}$**

$$
F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V}\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}
$$

Mass flow rates: $V = Q_V \cdot \rho_V = 0.55 \cdot 4.0 = 2.2$ kg/s, $L = Q_L \cdot \rho_L = 0.0042 \cdot 780 = 3.276$ kg/s.

$$
F_{LV} = \dfrac{3.276}{2.2}\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{4.0}{780}} = 1.489 \cdot 0.0716 = 0.107
$$

**Step 2: Flooding velocity $U_f$ (Souders–Brown)**

$$
U_f = K_{SB} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}} = 0.107 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{780 - 4.0}{4.0}}
$$

$$
U_f = 0.107 \cdot \sqrt{194} = 0.107 \cdot 13.928 = 1.490 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Operating velocity and column diameter**

$$
u_{\text{op}} = 0.8 \cdot U_f = 0.8 \cdot 1.490 = 1.192 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
D_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_V}{\pi \cdot u_{\text{op}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 0.55}{\pi \cdot 1.192}}
$$

$$
D_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.2}{3.745}} = \sqrt{0.5875} = 0.766 \;\text{m} \;\approx\; 0.77 \;\text{m}
$$

**Round up to standard size: $D_c = 0.9$ m** (recompute $u_{\text{op}} = 4 \cdot 0.55 / (\pi \cdot 0.9^2) = 0.865$ m/s; flooding fraction = $0.865/1.490 = 58\%$, OK).

**Step 4: Dry tray pressure drop** (with $u_h = 12$ m/s)

$$
h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{u_h^2 \cdot \rho_V}{C_d^2 \cdot \rho_L} = 51 \cdot \dfrac{12^2 \cdot 4.0}{(0.65)^2 \cdot 780}
$$

$$
h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{576}{329.55} = 51 \cdot 1.748 = 89.1 \;\text{mm liquid}
$$

**Step 5: Height of liquid over weir $h_{ow}$**

$\ell_w = 0.77 \cdot 0.9 = 0.693$ m

$$
h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{L_w}{\rho_L \cdot \ell_w}\right]^{2/3} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{3.276}{780 \cdot 0.693}\right]^{2/3}
$$

$$
h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{3.276}{540.5}\right]^{2/3} = 750 \cdot (0.00606)^{2/3} = 750 \cdot 0.0333 = 25.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6: Effective liquid head and total tray pressure drop**

$$
h_l = h_w + h_{ow} + \dfrac{h_d}{2} = 50 + 25.0 + \dfrac{89.1}{2} = 50 + 25 + 44.55 = 119.6 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
h_t = h_d + h_l = 89.1 + 119.6 = 208.7 \;\text{mm liquid} \;\approx\; 2.05 \;\text{kPa}
$$

(Converting: $208.7 \text{ mm liq} \times 9.81 \times 780 / 1000 = 1.598$ kPa; using $1 \text{ mm liq} \approx 9.81 \text{ Pa}$, so $\approx 2.05$ kPa.)

**Step 7: Feasibility check**
- Flooding fraction = 58 % → well below 100 % → **NOT** in flooding ✔
- $h_{ow} = 25$ mm (between 6 and 100 mm) ✔
- $h_t$ in acceptable range (50–150 mm typical) — slightly high but acceptable for high-pressure service.

**Conclusion:** The sieve tray design with $D_c = 0.9$ m, $h_w = 50$ mm, $u_h = 12$ m/s is feasible.

**(7 marks — full marks awarded if all six sub-steps are explicit, units are correctly used, and the final feasibility verdict is stated.)**

---

**Q. A. (b)** **Explain the constructional features of a sieve tray with a neat sketch (describe the diagram in words since this is text). What is the function of the downcomer in a cross-flow tray?**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Constructional Features of a Sieve Tray:**

A sieve tray is a **perforated circular plate** clamped between the flanges of successive column sections. Its main features are:

1. **Tray deck / plate** — 3–6 mm thick carbon-steel (or stainless for corrosive service) plate.
2. **Perforations (holes)** — 3–12 mm diameter, arranged in an equilateral-triangular pitch. Pitch-to-diameter ratio $p/d_h$ is typically **2.5–4**. Hole area is 5–15 % of the bubbling area.
3. **Outlet weir** — a vertical strip welded at the downstream edge, height **40–90 mm**, controlling the liquid level on the tray.
4. **Inlet (calming) zone** — a blank area (no holes) about 50–100 mm wide, where liquid enters from the downcomer.
5. **Downcomer (recessed or sloped)** — a channel that conducts liquid from one tray to the next. May be **straight**, **sloped**, or **recessed** to maximize active area.
6. **Downcomer clearance** — gap of 5–15 mm below the tray, to seal vapor flow.
7. **Manways, bolts, gasket seats** — for assembly and maintenance.

**Function of the Downcomer:**

The downcomer is a **liquid-only channel** that:
- **Transports liquid** from tray $N+1$ to tray $N$ (the tray below) by gravity.
- **Prevents vapor bypass** between trays (its lip is sealed by the liquid in it).
- **Provides residence time (~3–5 s)** for the liquid, which aids in disengaging any entrained vapor bubbles.
- **Maintains a clear liquid seal** at its top, which defines the liquid level on the tray above (via the weir).

**Sketch description (text-only):**

> Imagine a circle (the column). Cut it into two parts. The right side is a **rectangular downcomer**, sealed at the top and open at the bottom. The left side is the **bubbling area**, dotted with small circles (sieve holes). Across the bubbling area, near the downcomer, runs a **horizontal weir** (a thin vertical wall). Liquid enters from the downcomer, flows left-to-right across the tray, spills over the weir, and falls into the downcomer of the tray below.

**(7 marks — 3 for listing 6 features, 2 for function of downcomer, 2 for sketch description.)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Q. B. (a)** **Compare the working of Bubble-Cap, Sieve, and Valve trays. State clearly the advantages and disadvantages of each. Which tray is most commonly used in modern process plants and why?**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bubble-Cap | Sieve | Valve |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Most complex: each hole has chimney + cap | Simplest: just drilled holes | Holes fitted with liftable disc valves |
| **Liquid seal** | Always maintained (best turndown) | None (weeps at low V) | Partial |
| **Pressure drop** | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| **Capacity** | Lowest | Highest | High |
| **Turndown ratio** | Best (~5:1) | Poor (~2:1) | Very good (~4:1) |
| **Fouling resistance** | Poor (caps trap solids) | Best (no caps) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult | Easy | Moderate |

**Working (in one line each):**
- **Bubble-Cap:** Vapor rises through chimney, is deflected downward by the cap rim, and bubbles through the liquid. Liquid seal is always maintained → can operate at very low vapor rates.
- **Sieve:** Vapor rises through holes at high velocity, holds up the liquid as a froth. At low vapor rates, the liquid weeps through the holes.
- **Valve:** Vapor lifts a metal disc (valve) sitting on each hole, increasing the open area as vapor rate increases. Self-regulating → wide turndown.

**Which is most common?** The **Sieve Tray** is the most commonly used tray in modern process plants because it offers the **best combination of low cost, low pressure drop, high capacity, and ease of maintenance**. Modern process design favors economy and energy efficiency, and the sieve tray excels in both, provided turndown requirements are modest (>50 % of design rate). For wider turndown, **valve trays** are preferred.

**(7 marks — 3 for comparison table, 2 for working, 2 for justification.)**

---

**Q. B. (b)** **A sieve-tray column is to be designed for a benzene–toluene separation at atmospheric pressure. The column has 30 actual trays. Estimate (i) the overall tray efficiency using the Drickamer–Bradford correlation, given that the relative volatility at average column conditions is $\alpha_{avg} = 2.4$, the average liquid viscosity is $\mu_L = 0.35$ cP, and the molar feed composition is $x_F = 0.5$ with $x_D = 0.95$ and $x_B = 0.05$. Also (ii) estimate the number of theoretical stages.**

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Drickamer–Bradford correlation**

$$
E_o = 0.17 - 0.616 \cdot \log_{10}(\mu_L)
$$

where $\mu_L$ is in cP.

$$
E_o = 0.17 - 0.616 \cdot \log_{10}(0.35) = 0.17 - 0.616 \cdot (-0.4559)
$$

$$
E_o = 0.17 + 0.2808 = 0.4508 \;\approx\; 45.1\%
$$

**Step 2: Number of theoretical stages**

$$
N_{theoretical} = N_{actual} \cdot E_o = 30 \cdot 0.4508 = 13.52 \;\approx\; 14 \text{ theoretical stages}
$$

**Step 3: Verification using Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland (briefly)**

For benzene–toluene at atmospheric pressure, with $\alpha = 2.4$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$:

**Fenske minimum number of stages** ($N_m$):
$$
N_m = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\log \alpha}
$$

$$
N_m = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\cdot\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\log 2.4} = \dfrac{\log(361)}{\log 2.4} = \dfrac{2.558}{0.3802} = 6.73
$$

The minimum number of stages is ~7; actual is 30, so the reflux ratio must be significantly above minimum.

**Conclusion:** The estimated overall tray efficiency is **~45 %**, giving **~14 theoretical stages** for the column.

**(7 marks — 3 for Drickamer–Bradford calculation, 2 for theoretical stage count, 2 for the Fenske verification and conclusion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Always convert mm to m and vice versa. Most students lose 1–2 marks by mixing units in $h_d$ and $h_{ow}$ calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\rho_L$ in the denominator of $h_d$:** The dry tray pressure drop, when expressed in *mm of liquid*, MUST be divided by $\rho_L \cdot g$. Students often forget the $\rho_L$ factor and get answers 100× too high.
> 3. **Not rounding $D_c$ up to standard pipe size:** Industrial columns come in standard sizes (0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0, 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8, 2.0 m …). Always round UP, not to the nearest.
> 4. **Skipping the feasibility check:** KTU answers MUST end with a sentence like *"Design is feasible because flooding fraction < 0.85."* Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missing.
> 5. **Forgetting the $h_d/2$ aeration term in $h_l$:** This is the most often missed term — it accounts for ~25 % of the total tray ΔP.
> 6. **Wrong $K_{SB}$ value:** $K_{SB}$ depends on tray spacing. For TS = 0.45 m use 0.10 m/s; for 0.61 m use 0.107 m/s; for 0.91 m use 0.115 m/s.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook!)**

- **Tray design** = sizing the column internals for vapor–liquid mass transfer.
- **Three operating regimes:** Weeping → Operating (froth) → Flooding.
- **Sieve tray** = most common in industry; cheapest, lowest ΔP, highest capacity, but narrow turndown.
- **Valve tray** = best turndown; used in refinery columns.
- **Bubble-cap tray** = best liquid seal, highest turndown, used in old / corrosive / vacuum services.
- **Souders–Brown equation:** $U_f = K_{SB}\sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ — memorize $K_{SB}$ for 0.45, 0.61, 0.91 m spacing.
- **Fair's correlation:** chart-based, $C_{SB} = f(\rho_V, F_{LV}, \text{TS})$; more accurate than Souders–Brown.
- **Flow parameter:** $F_{LV} = (L/V)\sqrt{\rho_V/\rho_L}$ — used in Fair's chart.
- **Column diameter:** designed at **80 % of flooding velocity** for safety margin.
- **Dry tray drop:** $h_d = 51 \cdot u_h^2 \cdot \rho_V / (C_d^2 \cdot \rho_L)$ in mm liquid.
- **Francis weir crest:** $h_{ow} = 750 \cdot [L_w/(\rho_L \ell_w)]^{2/3}$ in mm.
- **Effective liquid head:** $h_l = h_w + h_{ow} + h_d/2$ — DO NOT forget the $h_d/2$.
- **Total tray drop:** $h_t = h_d + h_l$ — must be in **50–150 mm liquid** range (typical).
- **Downcomer backup criterion:** $H_d = h_t + h_{dc} + h_{ow} \le 0.5 \cdot (\text{TS} + h_w)$ for foaming systems.
- **Hole area:** $A_h = Q_V/u_h$, $N_h = A_h/a_h$.
- **Murphree efficiency (Drickamer–Bradford):** $E_o = 0.17 - 0.616 \log_{10}(\mu_L)$ — only needs liquid viscosity!
- **Diameter round-up rule:** always round to the next STANDARD size; never round down.
- **Minimum weir crest:** $h_{ow} \ge 6$ mm (else weeping); maximum $\le 100$ mm (else flooding).
- **Physical constants to remember:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $1$ atm = $101.325$ kPa.
- **Open area of sieve tray:** 5–15 % of bubbling area; $p/d_h = 2.5$–$4.0$.
- **Turndown ratio:** Sieve ≈ 2:1; Valve ≈ 4:1; Bubble-Cap ≈ 5:1.
- **For KTU numericals:** Always end with a "feasibility verdict" line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tray Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tray Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — TRAY EFFICIENCY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **distillation column**, the actual trays (plates) are never able to achieve the equilibrium separation that ideal stage theory predicts. **Tray Efficiency** is a quantitative correction factor that reconciles the **ideal (theoretical) stages** calculated from the McCabe–Thiele method with the **actual (real) trays** that must be physically installed in a real column. It is a dimensionless number, always between 0 and 1 (or expressed as a percentage 0–100 %), that expresses *how close* a real tray comes to performing like an ideal equilibrium stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *Tray efficiency is the ratio of the change in composition achieved on an actual operating tray to the change in composition that would be obtained if the tray behaved as an ideal equilibrium stage.*

Mathematically, the universal efficiency statement is:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{\text{Actual change in composition across tray}}{\text{Change predicted by thermodynamic equilibrium}}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bumpy Highway" 🚗

Imagine a car (the rising vapor) climbing a hill (the distillation column). In a **perfect world**, the car would reach the summit in one smooth drive — that is *theoretical* equilibrium. But the real road has potholes, speed breakers, and steep patches (real-world contact limitations, weeping, entrainment, finite residence time). The **efficiency** of the road is the percentage of the ideal climb that the car actually achieves per attempt. Similarly, on a tray the vapor and liquid rush past each other for only 2–5 seconds — *not enough time* for true equilibrium to be reached — so the tray's real performance falls short of the ideal. Tray efficiency is that shortfall quantified.

### 1.3 Classification of Tray Efficiencies (Three Hierarchical Levels)

| # | Efficiency Type | Symbol | Scope | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Murphree **Point** Efficiency | $E_{og}$ or $E_{ol}$ | A *single point* on a tray (focal point) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Murphree **Tray** Efficiency | $E_{Mv}$ or $E_{Ml}$ | The *entire tray* (averaged over its active area) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Overall Column** Efficiency | $E_{o}$ | The *whole column* end-to-end | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy: **Point** $\longrightarrow$ **Tray** $\longrightarrow$ **Column**. Each higher level is derived from the one below it, with extra real-world corrections (entrainment, weeping, bypassing) added along the way.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Reference Values

- **Minimum acceptable tray efficiency in industry:** **60–75 %**
- **Typical commercial column efficiency:** **70–85 %**
- **Maximum possible efficiency:** **100 %** (theoretical limit, never reached)
- **Standard tray type assumed in KTU derivations:** **Bubble-cap, Sieve, or Valve** (each has its own empirical correlation)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Tray Efficiency Exists — The Real-World Deviation

An **ideal tray** assumes:
1. Perfect vapor–liquid contact (infinite contact time).
2. Vapor and liquid leaving the tray are in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.
3. No liquid weeping, no vapor entrainment, no bypassing, no maldistribution.

A **real tray** violates all four because:
- Liquid has a residence time of only ~2–5 s on the tray.
- Vapor jets up as discrete bubbles, contacting only a fraction of the liquid.
- Some liquid drips back down (weeping) and some liquid is carried up by vapor (entrainment).
- Liquid may channel or short-circuit across the tray.

These losses are bundled into a single numerical correction — the **efficiency**.

## 2.2 The Three Levels of Efficiency — Full Mathematical Treatment

### 2.2.1 Murphree Vapor-Phase Point Efficiency ($E_{og}$)

Defined for a single point on a tray. The vapor entering at composition $y_{n+1}$ and leaving at $y_{n}$ is compared to the equilibrium value $y^{*}$ corresponding to the local liquid $x_{n}$.

$$
E_{og} \;=\; \frac{y_{n} \;-\; y_{n+1}}{y^{*}(x_{n}) \;-\; y_{n+1}}
$$

where:
- $y_{n+1}$ = vapor composition entering the tray from below
- $y_{n}$ = vapor composition leaving the tray (actual)
- $y^{*}(x_{n})$ = vapor composition in equilibrium with the local exiting liquid $x_{n}$

> [!NOTE]
> The subscript "$og$" stands for **o**verall gas (vapor) phase, point efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Murphree Liquid-Phase Point Efficiency ($E_{ol}$)

The analogous definition based on the liquid phase. Since the KTU syllabus (and AIChE) emphasizes vapor phase, $E_{ol}$ is less commonly used.

$$
E_{ol} \;=\; \frac{x_{n-1} \;-\; x_{n}}{x_{n-1} \;-\; x^{*}(y_{n})}
$$

**Interconversion relationship** (two-film theory derivation):

$$
E_{og} \;=\; \frac{E_{ol}}{E_{ol} \;+\; \dfrac{mV}{L}\,(1 - E_{ol})}
$$

where:
- $m$ = slope of the equilibrium curve (dy/dx) at the operating point
- $V$ = molar vapor flow rate
- $L$ = molar liquid flow rate
- $\dfrac{mV}{L}$ is the **stripping factor** (or its inverse $S = L/mV$ is the **absorption factor**).

### 2.2.3 Murphree Vapor-Phase Tray Efficiency ($E_{Mv}$)

The *tray* efficiency is obtained by integrating the *point* efficiency over the entire active bubbling area of the tray. It assumes the **liquid on a tray is perfectly mixed** (a single uniform composition $x_{n}$ across the tray). Then:

$$
E_{Mv} \;=\; \frac{\bar{y}_{n} \;-\; y_{n+1}}{y^{*}(x_{n}) \;-\; y_{n+1}}
$$

Under the assumption of **completely mixed liquid** on the tray:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{Mv} \;=\; E_{og}\,}
$$

(i.e., tray efficiency equals point efficiency when liquid mixing is complete.)

Under the assumption of **no liquid mixing** (plug flow of liquid across the tray), the integration yields the **Lewis case 1** relationship:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{Mv} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\bigl[\exp\bigl(\lambda E_{og}\bigr) \;-\; 1\bigr]\,}
$$

where:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{mV}{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is a **KTU favourite**. The general result is: $E_{Mv} > E_{og}$ whenever the liquid is *not* perfectly mixed, because plug flow lets the liquid "see" varying vapor compositions and achieve a richer average driving force.

### 2.2.4 Effect of Liquid Mixing on Tray Efficiency

| Liquid Mixing State | Relationship |
|---|---|
| Perfectly mixed | $E_{Mv} = E_{og}$ |
| Plug flow (no mixing) | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[\exp(\lambda E_{og}) - 1\bigr]$ |
| Partial mixing (real) | $E_{Mv}$ lies between the two limits above, corrected by a **mixing factor** from the AIChE Bubble-Tray Design Manual. |

### 2.2.5 Overall Column Efficiency ($E_{o}$)

The simplest and most widely used in industry:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{o} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{theoretical}}}{N_{\text{actual}}}\,}
$$

For preliminary design (KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions), the empirical correlations used are:

**A) Drickamer–Bradford Correlation (1943)** — for hydrocarbon distillation:

$$
E_{o} \;=\; 0.17 \;-\; 0.616\,\log_{10}(\mu_{av})
$$

where $\mu_{av}$ is the **average viscosity** of the feed (in cP or mPa·s), evaluated as the molar average of the top and bottom product liquid viscosities at their respective average column temperatures.

**B) O'Connell Correlation (1946)** — more general, includes relative volatility:

$$
E_{o} \;=\; 0.49\,(\alpha \,\mu_{av})^{-0.245}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **relative volatility** of the key components at the average column temperature and $\mu_{av}$ is the average liquid viscosity in cP.

**C) AIChE Method** — rigorous, uses point efficiency and a graphical correlation based on dimensionless groups ($\text{Pe}_{\text{liq}}$, $\text{Pe}_{\text{vap}}$, $N_{sc}$, etc.). This is the most accurate but is the most complex.

> [!NOTE]
> O'Connell's correlation is the **most cited in KTU university exam answers** for the *overall* efficiency of distillation columns.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Tray Efficiency

| Factor | Effect on $E_{o}$ | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid viscosity $\uparrow$ | $E_{o} \downarrow$ | High $\mu$ reduces mass-transfer coefficient and increases foaming |
| Relative volatility $\alpha \uparrow$ | $E_{o} \downarrow$ | Greater separation = higher driving force but easier weeping |
| Vapor velocity $u_{v} \uparrow$ | $E_{o} \uparrow$ (up to flooding) | More turbulence, better contact |
| Surface tension | Affects foaming | Low $\sigma$ → stable foam → lower $E_{o}$ |
| Tray spacing $\uparrow$ | $E_{o} \uparrow$ (slightly) | Reduces entrainment |
| Residence time $\uparrow$ | $E_{o} \uparrow$ | More time for mass transfer |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Condition / Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{og} = \dfrac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y^{*} - y_{n+1}}$ | Murphree point efficiency (vapor) | $0 \leq E_{og} \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $E_{Mv} = E_{og}$ | Mixed liquid on tray | Perfectly mixed liquid |
| 3 | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[\exp(\lambda E_{og}) - 1\bigr]$ | Lewis case 1 | Plug flow, $\lambda = mV/L$ |
| 4 | $E_{ol} = \dfrac{x_{n-1} - x_{n}}{x_{n-1} - x^{*}}$ | Murphree point efficiency (liquid) | $0 \leq E_{ol} \leq 1$ |
| 5 | $E_{o} = \dfrac{N_{\text{th}}}{N_{\text{act}}}$ | Overall column efficiency | $0 \leq E_{o} \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $E_{o} = 0.17 - 0.616 \log_{10}(\mu_{av})$ | Drickamer–Bradford | $\mu_{av}$ in cP, hydrocarbon systems |
| 7 | $E_{o} = 0.49 (\alpha \mu_{av})^{-0.245}$ | O'Connell | $\mu_{av}$ in cP |
| 8 | $\lambda = mV/L$ | Stripping factor | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The **Drickamer–Bradford** correlation uses $\log_{10}$ (base 10), while the older O'Connell equation uses $\mu_{av}$ in cP. Always state units in your answer to avoid losing a mark.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tray efficiency is the **bridge between thermodynamics and mechanical design**. Without it, a process engineer would have no way of knowing how many physical trays to fabricate. A column designed for 15 theoretical stages at 100 % efficiency would need 25 actual trays at 60 % efficiency — a difference of 10 physical trays that costs lakhs of rupees in materials, welding, and column height. Tray efficiency also influences the **diameter** of the column (entrainment correlations use $E_{Mv}$). The design of **refinery crude distillation units, cryogenic air separation, ethanol-water columns, and aromatics extraction plants** all rely critically on the accurate prediction of tray efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Murphree Vapor-Phase Point Efficiency

### Statement
The vapor entering tray $n$ from below has composition $y_{n+1}$. After contacting the liquid (composition $x_{n}$) on the tray, the vapor leaves at composition $y_{n}$. The **maximum possible** exit vapor composition (if equilibrium were achieved) is $y^{*}(x_{n})$. The ratio of actual to maximum enrichment is the Murphree vapor point efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the actual enrichment gained by the vapor across tray $n$:**

$$
\Delta y_{\text{actual}} \;=\; y_{n} \;-\; y_{n+1}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the maximum possible enrichment (if the tray were ideal, i.e., the exiting vapor were in equilibrium with the exiting liquid):**

$$
\Delta y_{\text{max}} \;=\; y^{*}(x_{n}) \;-\; y_{n+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of actual to maximum enrichment:**

$$
E_{og} \;=\; \frac{\Delta y_{\text{actual}}}{\Delta y_{\text{max}}} \;=\; \frac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y^{*}(x_{n}) - y_{n+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary check:**

If the tray is ideal: $y_{n} = y^{*}(x_{n})$, so $E_{og} = 1$ (100 %).

If no mass transfer occurs: $y_{n} = y_{n+1}$, so $E_{og} = 0$ (0 %).

This confirms the **physical bounds** of the efficiency.

> **Conversion logic:** The derivation is essentially a *definition*, but the student must explicitly state the "ideal case" and the "no-transfer case" to satisfy the KTU valuation key, which awards 1 mark for the boundary check.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between $E_{Mv}$ and $E_{og}$ Under Plug Flow (Lewis Case 1)

### Statement
For a tray with plug flow of liquid (no axial mixing) and uniform vapor flow, the Murphree **tray** efficiency exceeds the Murphree **point** efficiency. We derive the relationship.

### Setup

Consider a differential element of the tray at horizontal position $z$, of length $dz$. The vapor rises through this element with local superficial velocity and contacts liquid with local composition $x(z)$. Mass balance on the vapor:

$$
V \, dy \;=\; K_{y} \, a \, \bigl(y^{*} - y\bigr) \, dz
$$

where $K_{y}\,a$ is the overall vapor-side mass-transfer coefficient (mol/m·s).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the point efficiency locally.**

By definition, the local point efficiency relates the actual local vapor change to the equilibrium one. For a differential element, the vapor composition change is $dy$ across a contact length $dz$ where the local liquid is $x(z)$. So:

$$
E_{og} \;=\; \frac{dy}{y^{*}(x) - y_{n+1}}
$$

(Here, $y_{n+1}$ is the entering vapor — uniform across the tray.)

**Step 2 — Mass balance on vapor side across the element:**

$$
V \, dy \;=\; K_{y}\,a \,(y^{*} - y) \,dz
$$

In the dilute-diffusion limit (or for a linear equilibrium curve near the operating point), $y^{*} \approx y_{n+1} + m\,(x - x_{n+1})$, and at steady state the operating line gives the relation between $dy$ and $dx$:

$$
dy \;=\; -\frac{L}{V}\,dx
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and integrate.**

For the plug-flow assumption and linear equilibrium, integrate the differential mass-transfer equation from the inlet ($z=0$, $y = y_{n+1}$, $x = x_{n-1}$) to the outlet ($z = Z$, $y = y_{n}$, $x = x_{n}$):

$$
\int_{y_{n+1}}^{y_{n}} \frac{dy}{y^{*} - y} \;=\; \frac{K_{y}\,a\,Z}{V}
$$

The right-hand side is **NTU** (number of transfer units) and by definition equals the point efficiency expression $E_{og} \cdot \lambda$ when rearranged for a linear equilibrium curve. After algebraic manipulation (the integral evaluates to a logarithm), we obtain:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{y^{*}(x_{n}) - y_{n+1}}{y^{*}(x_{n-1}) - y_{n}}\right) \;=\; \lambda \,E_{og}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the average exit composition $y_{n}$.**

The tray efficiency is defined by the *average* exit composition $\bar{y}_{n}$ (which for plug flow equals $y_{n}$ at $z = Z$):

$$
E_{Mv} \;=\; \frac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y^{*}(x_{n}) - y_{n+1}}
$$

Using the chain of algebraic manipulation, substituting the result from Step 3, and applying $\exp(\lambda E_{og})$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{Mv} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\,\bigl[\exp(\lambda E_{og}) - 1\bigr]\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This result shows that when $\lambda E_{og}$ is small, Taylor expansion gives $E_{Mv} \approx E_{og}$ (recovering the perfectly-mixed case). When $\lambda E_{og}$ is large, $E_{Mv}$ can be much greater than $E_{og}$, which is the KTU board's classic "plug-flow bonus" question.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — O'Connell Correlation

**Problem (KTU Model):** A distillation column is to separate a benzene–toluene mixture. The feed has an average viscosity of 0.35 cP and the relative volatility $\alpha = 2.5$. The McCabe–Thiele analysis gives 12 theoretical stages. Calculate the actual number of trays using the O'Connell correlation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the O'Connell correlation:**

$$
E_{o} \;=\; 0.49\,(\alpha \, \mu_{av})^{-0.245}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
E_{o} \;=\; 0.49 \,(2.5 \times 0.35)^{-0.245}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product inside the parentheses:**

$$
2.5 \times 0.35 \;=\; 0.875
$$

**Step 4 — Raise to the power $-0.245$:**

$$
(0.875)^{-0.245} \;=\; e^{-0.245 \cdot \ln(0.875)} \;=\; e^{-0.245 \times (-0.1335)} \;=\; e^{0.0327} \;\approx\; 1.0332
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the prefactor:**

$$
E_{o} \;=\; 0.49 \times 1.0332 \;\approx\; 0.506
$$

So the overall column efficiency is approximately **0.506 or 50.6 %**.

**Step 6 — Compute the actual number of trays:**

$$
E_{o} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{th}}}{N_{\text{act}}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{act}} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{th}}}{E_{o}} \;=\; \frac{12}{0.506} \;\approx\; 23.72
$$

**Step 7 — Round up to the next integer (engineering safety):**

$$
\boxed{\,N_{\text{act}} \;=\; 24 \text{ actual trays}\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** KTU 14-mark questions always require (a) the formula, (b) substitution of given data, (c) numerical evaluation, and (d) a sensible engineering conclusion (rounding, adding a reboiler, etc.). Skipping the conclusion loses 1–2 marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Efficiency Levels

| Property | $E_{og}$ (Point) | $E_{Mv}$ (Tray) | $E_{o}$ (Column) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference scale | A point on a tray | Entire tray | Entire column |
| Liquid mixing assumption | None (point) | Mixed or plug flow | Implicit overall |
| Includes entrainment? | No | No (separate correction) | Yes (lumped) |
| Includes weeping? | No | Sometimes | Yes (lumped) |
| Most accurate method? | AIChE rigorous | Lewis cases + AIChE | Empirical (O'Connell) |
| KTU use | Theoretical / advanced | Design verification | **Industry standard** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — Hierarchy of Tray Efficiencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Thermodynamic Equilibrium Ideal Tray 100 percent Theoretical"] --> B["Real Industrial Distillation Tray"]
    B --> C["Murphree Point Efficiency Eog Single Point on Tray"]
    C --> D1["Liquid Perfectly Mixed"]
    C --> D2["Liquid Plug Flow No Mixing"]
    D1 --> E["Murphree Tray Efficiency Emv Equals Eog"]
    D2 --> F["Lewis Case 1 Emv = 1 over lambda times exp lambda Eog minus 1"]
    E --> G["Overall Column Efficiency Eo"]
    F --> G
    G --> H1["Drickamer Bradford Correlation"]
    G --> H2["O Connell Correlation"]
    G --> H3["AIChE Bubble Tray Manual Method"]
    H1 --> I["Number of Actual Trays Nact equals Nth over Eo"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J["Mechanical Column Design"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass-Transfer Picture on a Single Tray

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sub1 ["VAPOR PHASE INPUT"]
        VA["Vapor Inlet y subscript n plus 1"]
    end
    subgraph sub2 ["TRAY ACTIVE BUBBLING AREA"]
        MB["Murphree Point Efficiency Eog governs local mass transfer"]
        LV["Liquid Composition x subscript n across the tray"]
        EN["Entrainment Correction psi factor"]
        WP["Weeping Correction loss of liquid"]
    end
    subgraph sub3 ["VAPOR PHASE OUTPUT"]
        VB["Vapor Outlet y subscript n actual"]
        VEQ["Equilibrium Outlet y star of xn ideal"]
    end
    subgraph sub4 ["INTEGRATION"]
        INT["Integrate point efficiency over tray area to get Emv"]
    end
    subgraph sub5 ["OVERALL COLUMN"]
        OC["Multiply by tray count and add entrainment to get Eo"]
    end
    VA --> MB
    LV --> MB
    MB --> VB
    MB --> VEQ
    VB --> INT
```
