## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define NTU$_{OG}$ and HTU$_{OG}$ for a packed absorption column. How are they related to the height of packing?**

> **Model Answer:**
> **NTU$_{OG}$ (Number of Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Units):** A dimensionless group representing the difficulty of the separation, given by $\text{NTU}_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \dfrac{dy}{y-y^*}$.
>
> **HTU$_{OG}$ (Height of an Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Unit):** The physical height of packing needed to effect one transfer unit, defined as $\text{HTU}_{OG} = G/(K_y a)$, expressed in **metres**.
>
> **Relation:** $Z = \text{HTU}_{OG} \times \text{NTU}_{OG}$. *(3 marks — 1 each)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2. What is the absorption factor $A$? State the condition under which the operating line is parallel to the equilibrium line.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The **absorption factor** is the ratio of the slope of the operating line to the slope of the equilibrium line:
> $$A = \frac{L/V}{m} = \frac{L}{mV}$$
> The operating line is parallel to the equilibrium line when **$A = 1$** (i.e., $L/V = m$). Under this condition, the driving force is **constant** along the column. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the equation relating the height of packing $Z$ of a counter-current packed absorption column to the **NTU$_{OG}$** and **HTU$_{OG}$** for a dilute gas mixture. State clearly all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a packed absorber, a gas mixture containing 2 mol% of $\text{SO}_2$ in air is scrubbed with water. The desired outlet gas contains **0.1 mol% $\text{SO}_2$**. The gas and liquid flow rates are such that $L/V = 60$, and the equilibrium relation is $y^* = 30 x$. Given $K_y a = 0.015$ kmol/m³·s and superficial gas velocity $G = 0.012$ kmol/m²·s, calculate the **height of packing required** using the log-mean driving force method. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation — See Section 3.1 of these notes.** Award marks as:
- Differential gas-phase material balance (2 marks)
- Substitution of overall flux $N_A = K_y (y-y^*)/(1-y)$ (2 marks)
- Recognition of HTU$_{OG}$ and NTU$_{OG}$ (2 marks)
- Final boxed result $Z = \text{HTU}_{OG} \cdot \text{NTU}_{OG}$ (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $y_1 = 0.02$, $y_2 = 0.001$, $L/V = 60$, $m = 30$, $K_y a = 0.015$, $G = 0.012$.

**Step 1:** Compute $x_1$ from operating line equation (assuming $x_2 \approx 0$):
$$x_1 = \frac{y_1 - y_2}{L/V} = \frac{0.02 - 0.001}{60} = \frac{0.019}{60} = 3.167 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $y_1^* = m x_1 = 30 \times 3.167 \times 10^{-4} = 9.5 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 3:** $y_2^* = m x_2 \approx 0$.

**Step 4:** Driving forces:
$$\Delta y_1 = 0.02 - 0.0095 = 0.0105$$
$$\Delta y_2 = 0.001 - 0 = 0.001$$

**Step 5:** Log-mean driving force:
$$(\Delta y)_{lm} = \frac{0.0105 - 0.001}{\ln(0.0105/0.001)} = \frac{0.0095}{\ln(10.5)} = \frac{0.0095}{2.3514} = 4.041 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6:** NTU$_{OG}$:
$$\text{NTU}_{OG} = \frac{0.02 - 0.001}{4.041 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.019}{4.041 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.702$$

**Step 7:** HTU$_{OG}$:
$$\text{HTU}_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} = \frac{0.012}{0.015} = 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8:** Height of packing:
$$\boxed{Z = 0.80 \times 4.702 = 3.76 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and explain the **Kremser equation** for a counter-current gas absorber handling a dilute mixture. Discuss the special case when the absorption factor $A = 1$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A stripping column is to remove dissolved $\text{NH}_3$ from water using steam. Inlet water contains $x_2 = 0.010$ mol fraction $\text{NH}_3$; the desired outlet liquid is $x_1 = 0.0005$. Inlet steam contains $y_1 = 0$ (pure steam). The equilibrium line is $y = 1.2 x$, with $K_x a = 0.08$ kmol/m³·s and $L = 0.02$ kmol/m²·s. Estimate the **height of packing** using the Kremser equation. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Kremser Equation — See Section 3.3 of these notes.** Award marks as:
- Statement of the equation (3 marks)
- Discussion of $A = 1$ case and constant driving force (2 marks)
- Physical interpretation (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $x_2 = 0.010$, $x_1 = 0.0005$, $y_1 = 0$, $m = 1.2$, $K_x a = 0.08$, $L = 0.02$.

**Step 1:** Find $V$ from gas-side balance: $V(y_1 - y_2) = L(x_2 - x_1)$
$$y_2 = y_1 + \frac{L}{V}(x_2 - x_1) \;\;\text{(need V/L)}$$

**Step 2:** Assume a stripping factor $S = mV/L$. For NTU integration, we use the equivalent **NTU$_{OL}$** form.

The Kremser equation in liquid form:
$$\frac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - y_1/m} = \frac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}$$

With $y_1 = 0$:
$$\frac{0.010 - 0.0005}{0.010} = \frac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1} = 0.95$$

**Step 3:** Iterate. Try $S = 1.5$ and solve for $N$:

Let $u = S^{N+1}$. Then:
$$\frac{u - S}{u - 1} = 0.95 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; u - S = 0.95(u - 1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.05 u = S - 0.95$$
$$u = \frac{S - 0.95}{0.05} = \frac{1.5 - 0.95}{0.05} = \frac{0.55}{0.05} = 11$$
$$S^{N+1} = 11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1.5^{N+1} = 11 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (N+1)\ln 1.5 = \ln 11$$
$$N + 1 = \frac{\ln 11}{\ln 1.5} = \frac{2.3979}{0.4055} = 5.915 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 4.915$$

**Step 4:** HTU$_{OL}$:
$$\text{HTU}_{OL} = \frac{L}{K_x a} = \frac{0.02}{0.08} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Height of packing:
$$\boxed{Z = 0.25 \times 4.915 = 1.229 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diluteness assumption** $(1-y) \approx 1$. If the gas is not dilute, the denominator must be retained and **HTU$_{OG}$ varies along the column**.
> 2. **Mixing up gas-phase and liquid-phase HTUs.** HTU$_{OG} = G/(K_y a)$; HTU$_{OL} = L/(K_x a)$. They have **different** numerical values and **different** corresponding NTU integrals.
> 3. **Using $\ln$ of a number less than 1** in the log-mean formula without sign correction. If $(y - y^*)$ flips sign (pinch), the standard log-mean **is not valid** — recheck operating conditions.
> 4. **Ignoring the A = 1 singular case** in the Kremser equation. The denominator $(A^{N+1} - 1)$ becomes 0/0; the limit gives $N/(N+1)$.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** between $K_y$ (per unit Δy) and $K_x$ (per unit Δx) when adding resistances — **never** add them directly. Use the converted form $1/K_y = 1/k_y + m/k_x$.
> 6. **Computing NTU but forgetting to multiply by HTU** to obtain the actual column height $Z$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision List for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Column height:** $Z = \text{HTU}_{OG} \cdot \text{NTU}_{OG} = \text{HTU}_{OL} \cdot \text{NTU}_{OL} = \text{HETP} \cdot N_{\text{theoretical stages}}$
- **HTU$_{OG}$** = $G / (K_y a)$ — measure of **hardware efficiency** (smaller is better).
- **NTU$_{OG}$** = $\int dy/(y - y^*)$ — measure of **separation difficulty**.
- **For dilute, linear systems:** NTU$_{OG} = (y_1 - y_2)/(\Delta y)_{lm}$, where $(\Delta y)_{lm}$ is the **log-mean driving force**.
- **HTU additivity:** $\text{HTU}_{OG} = \text{HTU}_G + (1/A)\,\text{HTU}_L$, where $A = L/(mV)$.
- **Absorption factor** $A = L/(mV)$; **Stripping factor** $S = mV/L = 1/A$.
- **Kremser absorption equation** (A ≠ 1): $\dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 - m x_2} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$, $N = \text{NTU}_{OG}$.
- **Kremser A = 1 form:** $\dfrac{y_1 - y_2}{y_1 - m x_2} = \dfrac{N}{N+1}$.
- **Kremser stripping form** (S ≠ 1): $\dfrac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - y_1/m} = \dfrac{S^{N+1} - S}{S^{N+1} - 1}$, $N = \text{NTU}_{OL}$.
- **Resistance additivity for coefficients:** $1/K_y = 1/k_y + m/k_x$; $1/K_x = 1/(m k_y) + 1/k_x$.
- **Dilute system validity:** valid when total mol flow changes < 2% and $y, x < 0.05$–0.10.
- **Pinch point check:** always verify $\Delta y > 0$ (or $\Delta x > 0$) at both ends; a sign flip means the specified $L/V$ is infeasible.
- **Overall column height** includes additional 10–20% safety margin for end effects, distributor zones, and support plates (this is **not** in $Z$ but is added by designer).
- **Always state assumptions** in derivations: isothermal, steady-state, dilute, no reaction, plug flow, constant $L$ and $V$, linear equilibrium.
- **Mole ratio vs mole fraction:** In dilute systems, $Y \approx y$ and $X \approx x$ — this simplification is **automatically valid** and saves algebra.
- **Two-film theory** is the foundational assumption — interfacial concentrations lie on the equilibrium curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Diameter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Column Diameter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Column Diameter** in process plant design refers to the internal cross-sectional diameter of a vertical mass-transfer column (distillation, absorption, stripping, or extraction column) selected to safely handle the maximum vapor and liquid throughputs under operating conditions without inducing flooding, excessive entrainment, or unacceptable pressure drop.

The diameter is fundamentally a **hydraulic sizing parameter** — it is not determined by mass-transfer equilibrium requirements (which fix the number of stages/height) but by the **vapor–liquid traffic dynamics** at the tray or packing interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under Module 1 of PCBBT602 and directly tests **CO1** — *Design mass transfer equipment using standard engineering correlations.* A typical 14-mark KTU question asks for the complete diameter calculation procedure given feed conditions, tray type, and physical properties.

The formally optimized diameter is the smallest standard commercial diameter that satisfies:

$$D_{\text{design}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, A_{\text{net}}}{\eta \, \pi}}$$

where $A_{\text{net}}$ is the total cross-sectional area required for vapor passage and $\eta$ accounts for the downcomer allocation (typically $\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.92$ for sieve trays).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Highway" Model

Imagine a **two-lane flyover** where:
- **Vapor** = a stream of fast-moving cars going up (rising through the column)
- **Liquid** = pedestrians walking down across the lanes (liquid flowing down)
- **The road width (column diameter)** = must accommodate BOTH streams without gridlock

If the road is too narrow → cars pile up → **flooding** (vapor cannot rise, liquid cannot descend, column chokes).
If the road is too wide → fine for traffic, but you wasted expensive real estate (steel, foundation cost, reflux pumps working against extra height).

The job of the designer is to find the **minimum road width** that safely carries the peak rush-hour traffic at ~**70–80 %** of "panic-stopping" capacity. That safety margin (≈ 25–35 % below flooding) is what makes column operation robust against feed surges and turn-down ratios.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Reality:** A column sized exactly at the flooding velocity would operate unstably. KTU convention is to operate at **f<sub flooding** ≈ 0.7 to 0.8 (i.e., 70–80 % of flooding velocity). This is a non-negotiable safety margin, analogous to derating a motor or a cable.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_V$ | Vapor density | **0.5 – 15 kg/m³** | Rises due to buoyancy |
| $\rho_L$ | Liquid density | **600 – 1100 kg/m³** | Falls due to gravity |
| $\sigma$ | Surface tension | **10 – 75 dynes/cm** | Affects froth stability |
| $\mu_L$ | Liquid viscosity | **0.1 – 5 cP** | Affects flooding at high $\mu_L$ |
| $F_LV$ | Flow parameter $\sqrt{\rho_V / \rho_L} \cdot (L/V)$ | **0.01 – 0.40** | Defines operating regime on GPDC |
| $C_{SB,f}$ | Souders–Brown flooding capacity | **0.05 – 0.35 m/s** | Read from GPDC chart |
| $f$ | Fraction of flooding | **0.70 – 0.85** | KTU design standard |

The fundamental driving force for vapor–liquid separation is the **density difference** $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$; the column must be sized so that vapor velocity never exceeds the terminal rise velocity of the entrained liquid droplets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Approaches

There are two engineering philosophies for sizing column diameter, both routinely asked in KTU exams:

### Approach A — Souders–Brown (Classical / Empirical)

Souders and Brown (1934) defined a **Capacity Parameter** that combines velocity and density into a single dimensionless grouping characteristic of the counter-current flow limit:

$$C_{SB} = U_V \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L - \rho_V}} \quad \left[\text{m/s}\right]$$

The flooding limit $C_{SB,f}$ is read off the **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC) chart** (Eckert's chart) as a function of:
- The **Flow Parameter** $F_{LV}$
- The tray spacing $S_t$
- The desired pressure drop per tray $\Delta P_{\text{tray}}$

### Approach B — Modified Souders–Brown with K Factor

For preliminary sizing (especially in vacuum columns and quick estimates), the older K-factor form is used:

$$U_f = K \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}} \quad \left[\text{m/s}\right]$$

where **K** is an empirical factor tabulated by tray type and pressure regime:
- **Sieve trays (atmospheric pressure):** $K \approx 0.107$ m/s
- **Bubble-cap trays:** $K \approx 0.076$ m/s
- **Vacuum service:** $K \approx 0.061$ m/s
- **High-pressure (above 7 bar):** $K \approx 0.082$ m/s

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** For numericals, the syllabus typically provides a **K value or a flooding velocity directly** so that students do not have to enter GPDC charts. Always read the question stem carefully.

---

## 2.2 The Flow Parameter — The "Operating Regime Indicator"

The flow parameter captures the **hydraulic character** of the service:

$$F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V} \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$$

| $F_{LV}$ Range | Regime | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{LV} < 0.05$ | Vapor-dominated (high V) | Vacuum distillation, overhead condensers |
| $0.05 \leq F_{LV} \leq 0.20$ | Balanced | Most industrial distillation |
| $F_{LV} > 0.20$ | Liquid-dominated (high L) | Absorbers, refinery main fractionators |

This parameter is essential because it locates the operating point on the GPDC chart — the flooding velocity drops steeply as $F_{LV}$ rises (more liquid = more resistance to vapor rise).

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Design Logic — The KTU Standard Procedure

The structured procedure expected in KTU answers is:

1. **Establish the maximum vapor and liquid molar/mass flow rates** ($V_{\max}$ and $L_{\max}$) at the most loaded tray (usually feed plate or top section for reflux).
2. **Convert to volumetric flows** using ideal gas law for vapor and density for liquid:

$$Q_V = \dfrac{V_{\max} \, M_V}{\rho_V} \quad ; \quad Q_L = \dfrac{L_{\max} \, M_L}{\rho_L}$$

3. **Calculate the Flow Parameter** $F_{LV}$.
4. **Determine the flooding velocity** either:
   - From GPDC using $F_{LV}$ and tray spacing, or
   - From the given K-factor using Souders–Brown.
5. **Apply the design safety factor** to obtain operating velocity:

$$U_{\text{op}} = f \, U_f \quad \text{with} \quad f = 0.70 - 0.85$$

6. **Calculate the minimum cross-sectional area** required for vapor:

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{Q_V}{U_{\text{op}}}$$

7. **Add the downcomer area** by dividing by a fraction $\eta$:

$$A_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{A_{\min}}{\eta} \quad ; \quad \eta \approx 0.85 - 0.92$$

8. **Solve for the design diameter:**

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, A_{\text{net}}}{\pi}}$$

9. **Round up to the nearest standard pipe/section size** — typically in increments of 0.1 m or 6 inches (150 mm) — and **recheck** velocity with the actual rounded-up area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we round UP, never down:** Rounding down would push us above the operating velocity target — risking premature flooding during feed upsets. KTU examiners explicitly check this final step.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_f = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ | K-factor flooding velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $C_{SB} = U \sqrt{\rho_V / (\rho_L - \rho_V)}$ | Capacity parameter (GPDC) | m/s |
| 3 | $F_{LV} = (L/V) \sqrt{\rho_V / \rho_L}$ | Flow parameter | dimensionless |
| 4 | $U_{\text{op}} = f \cdot U_f$ | Operating velocity ($f = 0.7-0.85$) | m/s |
| 5 | $A_{\min} = Q_V / U_{\text{op}}$ | Minimum vapor flow area | m² |
| 6 | $A_{\text{net}} = A_{\min}/\eta$ | With downcomer (η ≈ 0.85–0.92) | m² |
| 7 | $D = \sqrt{4 A_{\text{net}} / \pi}$ | Design diameter (calc) | m |
| 8 | $V_{\text{act}} = Q_V / (\pi D_{\text{std}}^2 / 4)$ | Recheck with standard D | m/s |
| 9 | $\rho_V = PM / (RT)$ | Ideal-gas vapor density | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $C_{SB,f} \approx 0.16 \, F_{LV}^{-0.2}$ (rough) | Kister flooding correlation | m/s |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Column diameter sizing is the **first economics-defining decision** in plant design. A 10 % oversizing of a refinery main fractionator (say 8 m instead of 7.2 m) increases:
- Steel shell cost ≈ **$1–3 million** (per column)
- Foundation load ≈ **+15 %**
- Reflux pump head ≈ **+10–12 %** (extra tray height stays the same; pump returns liquid to the top from accumulator, but taller liquid head at reboiler circulation)

Conversely, undersizing leads to **chronic flooding** — the column cannot achieve product specifications and the plant must de-bottleneck at huge retrofit cost. Diameter selection is therefore a **high-stakes, low-margin-of-error** engineering judgment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Souders–Brown Equation from Force Balance

Consider a single liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ suspended in rising vapor at velocity $U_V$. At flooding, the droplet is on the verge of being entrained upward — the **upward drag force** equals the **net gravitational settling force** (weight minus buoyancy):

$$F_{\text{drag}} = F_{\text{gravity}} - F_{\text{buoyancy}}$$

For a sphere in Stokes–Newton transition, the drag force is:

$$F_{\text{drag}} = C_D \, \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \, \dfrac{\rho_V \, U_V^2}{2}$$

The settling force (weight – buoyancy) is:

$$F_{\text{settling}} = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, (\rho_L - \rho_V) \, g$$

Equating these two forces at the flooding condition:

$$C_D \, \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \, \dfrac{\rho_V \, U_f^2}{2} = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, (\rho_L - \rho_V) \, g$$

Simplifying by canceling one factor of $d_p$ and rearranging for $U_f$:

$$U_f^2 = \dfrac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_L - \rho_V) \, g}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_V}$$

$$U_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, d_p \, (\rho_L - \rho_V) \, g}{3 \, C_D \, \rho_V}}$$

Souders and Brown recognized that for typical sieve-tray droplet diameters, the group $\sqrt{4 d_p g / (3 C_D)}$ can be lumped into an empirical constant **K** that depends on tray geometry, surface tension, and tray spacing. This produces:

$$\boxed{U_f = K \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}}$$

The **physical meaning of K** is therefore: *"a characteristic velocity scale (m/s) that captures the tray's ability to disengage liquid droplets."*

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Complete Diameter Calculation

**KTU-Style Problem (Model Solution):**

> **Question:** A distillation column operating at 1 atm is to handle a maximum vapor flow rate of **V = 50 kmol/hr** of benzene–toluene vapor (avg. M = 78 kg/kmol) and a liquid reflux of **L = 30 kmol/hr** (avg. M = 92 kg/kmol). Vapor density $\rho_V = 2.8$ kg/m³; liquid density $\rho_L = 800$ kg/m³. Tray spacing = 0.6 m. Use K = 0.107 m/s (sieve trays) and operating factor f = 0.80.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Convert molar flows to mass flows**

$$V_{\text{mass}} = 50 \times 78 = 3900 \;\text{kg/hr} = \dfrac{3900}{3600} = 1.0833 \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$L_{\text{mass}} = 30 \times 92 = 2760 \;\text{kg/hr} = \dfrac{2760}{3600} = 0.7667 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Convert mass flows to volumetric flows**

$$Q_V = \dfrac{V_{\text{mass}}}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{1.0833}{2.8} = 0.3869 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_L = \dfrac{L_{\text{mass}}}{\rho_L} = \dfrac{0.7667}{800} = 9.583 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the flooding velocity using Souders–Brown**

$$\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{800 - 2.8}{2.8} = \dfrac{797.2}{2.8} = 284.71$$

$$U_f = K \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}} = 0.107 \times \sqrt{284.71} = 0.107 \times 16.873 = 1.805 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute the operating velocity (f = 0.80)**

$$U_{\text{op}} = 0.80 \times 1.805 = 1.444 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the minimum cross-sectional area**

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{Q_V}{U_{\text{op}}} = \dfrac{0.3869}{1.444} = 0.2680 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 6: Add downcomer allowance (η = 0.88)**

$$A_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{A_{\min}}{\eta} = \dfrac{0.2680}{0.88} = 0.3045 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 7: Solve for the calculated diameter**

$$D_{\text{calc}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.3045}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.3876} = 0.6226 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 8: Round up to the nearest standard diameter**

Standard column diameters (in 0.1 m steps or 6-inch increments): 0.6 m, 0.7 m, 0.8 m, 0.9 m, 1.0 m ...

Selecting $D_{\text{std}} = 0.7$ m (rounding **UP** from 0.6226 m):

**Step 9: Recheck operating velocity with $D_{\text{std}}$**

$$A_{\text{std}} = \dfrac{\pi (0.7)^2}{4} \times \eta = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.49}{4} \times 0.88 = 0.3387 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$U_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{Q_V}{A_{\text{std}}} = \dfrac{0.3869}{0.3387} = 1.143 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$f_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{U_{\text{actual}}}{U_f} = \dfrac{1.143}{1.805} = 0.633$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual operating fraction drops to **0.63** because we rounded up — this is still safe (well above the flooding margin) and remains within KTU-acceptable design practice.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{D_{\text{design}} = 0.7 \;\text{m} \quad \text{at} \quad f_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.63}$$

---

## 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | +10 % Change | Effect on D | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_V \uparrow$ (higher T or lower P) | $\Delta\rho_V/\rho_V = +0.10$ | D ↑ ≈ 1 % (K-method) | Negligible — design is robust |
| $\rho_L \uparrow$ | D ↑ ≈ 0.4 % | Slight increase | Heavier liquid = more buoyancy of droplets |
| $\rho_L - \rho_V$ dominates | If doubles | D ↓ ≈ 30 % | Stems from $(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V$ inside the square root |
| $V_{\text{mass}} \uparrow$ | +10 % | D ↑ ≈ 5 % | Direct proportionality to area |
| Tray spacing $S_t$ ↑ | D ↓ | Allows higher $U_f$ | Larger columns are cheaper per m³ |

The diameter is most sensitive to **vapor flow rate** and the **density ratio** $(\rho_L/\rho_V)$ — the two parameters the designer must verify with the greatest care.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Column Diameter Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed and Product Specifications] --> B[Material and Energy Balance]
    B --> C[Determine Vmax and Lmax at Most Loaded Tray]
    C --> D[Convert to Volumetric Flows Qv and QL]
    D --> E[Compute Flow Parameter FLV]
    E --> F{Choose Sizing Method}
    F --> G[GPDC Chart Lookup]
    F --> H[Souders-Brown K-Factor]
    G --> I[Flooding Velocity Uf]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Safety Factor f = 0.70 to 0.85]
    J --> K[Operating Velocity Uop]
    K --> L[Minimum Cross Section Area Amin]
    L --> M[Add Downcomer Fraction Eta]
    M --> N[Net Area Anet]
    N --> O[Calculated Diameter Dcalc]
    O --> P[Round Up to Standard Diameter]
    P --> Q[Recheck Actual Velocity and Fraction]
    Q --> R[Final Design Diameter Ddesign]
```

**Reading this chart:** Each block represents a deterministic engineering calculation step. The diamond node (F) is the **decision branch** — examiners often award 2 marks simply for identifying which method applies to the given problem (high-pressure → K-factor, vacuum → GPDC with surface tension correction).

---

## 4.2 Vapor–Liquid Traffic Schematic (Block-Level Equivalent)

Since a physical 3-D drawing is infeasible in Mermaid, the following architecture maps the **interaction zones** inside the column where the diameter governs design:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOP[Top Zone Reflux Entry]
        T1[Liquid Distributor]
        T2[Top Tray]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid Zone Most Loaded Section]
        M1[Vapor Riser Zone]
        M2[Liquid Downcomer Zone]
        M3[Active Tray Deck]
    end
    subgraph BOT[Bottom Zone Reboiler Return]
        B1[Bottom Tray]
        B2[Chimney Tray]
    end
    M1 --- M3
    M3 --- M2
    M2 --- M3
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> B1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> M1
    B1 --> B2
    style MID fill:#fff7e6
    style M1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style M2 fill:#caffbf
    style M3 fill:#a0c4ff
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- The **Vapor Riser Zone** (orange) is sized by $U_f$ and $U_{\text{op}}$ → **this is where the diameter is critical**.
- The **Liquid Downcomer Zone** (green) is sized for liquid residence time (typically 3–5 seconds) and cross-flow velocity (typically ≤ 0.12 m/s).
- The **Active Tray Deck** (blue) is sized for tray hydraulics (weir length, hole area) — independent of column diameter.
- **Critical insight for KTU:** The diameter is set by the **vapor riser zone**, NOT the tray deck or downcomer.

---

## 4.3 Diameter Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Dcalc from Step 7] --> T{Dcalc <= 0.6 m}
    T -->|Yes| S1[Use 0.6 m as Standard]
    T -->|No| U{0.6 m < Dcalc <= 0.8 m}
    U -->|Yes| S2[Use 0.8 m]
    U -->|No| V{0.8 m < Dcalc <= 1.0 m}
    V -->|Yes| S3[Use 1.0 m]
    V -->|No| W{Dcalc > 1.0 m}
    W -->|Yes| X[Round up to next 0.1 m or 6 inch increment]
    X --> Y[Verify fact against flooding]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Define the **Souders–Brown flooding velocity** and state the K-factor value recommended for atmospheric sieve trays.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Souders–Brown flooding velocity is the maximum superficial vapor velocity at which a liquid droplet on a tray is just on the verge of being entrained upward by the rising vapor. It is calculated as:

$$U_f = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

For **atmospheric sieve trays**, the recommended K-factor is **K ≈ 0.107 m/s**. The corresponding operating velocity is taken as 70–80 % of $U_f$ to provide hydraulic stability.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + K value (1) = **3 marks**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** What is the **flow parameter** $F_{LV}$ in column diameter design? State its typical range and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The flow parameter is defined as:

$$F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$$

where L and V are the mass flow rates of liquid and vapor respectively.

**Typical range:** $F_{LV} = 0.01$ (vapor-dominated, vacuum) to $F_{LV} = 0.40$ (liquid-dominated, absorber).

**Physical significance:** It identifies the operating regime on the GPDC chart. Higher $F_{LV}$ means the liquid load is large relative to vapor, which reduces the allowable vapor velocity (column tends to choke). Designers must know $F_{LV}$ to read the correct flooding point from the correlation.

**Mark Distribution:** Formula (1) + Range (1) + Significance (1) = **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** A distillation column operating at 1 atm is to be sized for the following conditions at the most loaded tray:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Vapor flow rate (V) | 60 kmol/hr |
| Liquid flow rate (L) | 40 kmol/hr |
| Average molecular weight of vapor ($M_V$) | 80 kg/kmol |
| Average molecular weight of liquid ($M_L$) | 95 kg/kmol |
| Vapor density ($\rho_V$) | 3.2 kg/m³ |
| Liquid density ($\rho_L$) | 780 kg/m³ |
| Tray type | Sieve |
| Tray spacing | 0.5 m |
| K-factor | 0.107 m/s |
| Operating factor f | 0.80 |
| Downcomer efficiency η | 0.88 |

**(a)** Calculate the **flooding velocity** using the Souders–Brown equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **final design diameter** rounded to the nearest standard size. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Flooding Velocity (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert molar vapor flow to mass flow:

$$V_{\text{mass}} = 60 \times 80 = 4800 \;\text{kg/hr} = \dfrac{4800}{3600} = 1.3333 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the volumetric vapor flow:

$$Q_V = \dfrac{V_{\text{mass}}}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{1.3333}{3.2} = 0.4167 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply Souders–Brown equation:

$$\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{780 - 3.2}{3.2} = \dfrac{776.8}{3.2} = 242.75$$

$$U_f = 0.107 \times \sqrt{242.75} = 0.107 \times 15.581 = \mathbf{1.667 \; \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the operating factor:

$$U_{\text{op}} = f \times U_f = 0.80 \times 1.667 = \mathbf{1.334 \; \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Calculate minimum cross-sectional area:

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{Q_V}{U_{\text{op}}} = \dfrac{0.4167}{1.334} = 0.3124 \;\text{m}^2$$

> **Part (a) Marks Awarded:** Calculation rigor (4) + Correct final numbers (2) + Units (1) = **7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Final Design Diameter (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Account for downcomer area:

$$A_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{A_{\min}}{\eta} = \dfrac{0.3124}{0.88} = 0.3550 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Solve for calculated diameter:

$$D_{\text{calc}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.3550}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.4519} = 0.6722 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Round up to standard column diameter:

$$D_{\text{std}} = 0.7 \;\text{m} \quad (\text{rounding UP, never down})$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Recheck actual operating fraction:

$$A_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\pi (0.7)^2}{4} \times 0.88 = 0.3387 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$U_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{0.4167}{0.3387} = 1.230 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$f_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{1.230}{1.667} = 0.738 \quad \text{(safe, within 0.70–0.85 range)}$$

**Final Design:**

$$\boxed{D_{\text{design}} = 0.7 \;\text{m}, \quad f_{\text{actual}} = 0.738}$$

> **Part (b) Marks Awarded:** Downcomer correction (2) + Diameter formula (2) + Rounding logic (1) + Recheck (2) = **7 marks**

---

### QUESTION B — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** A packed absorption column handles the following gas and liquid streams:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Gas flow rate (V) | 0.55 kg/s |
| Liquid flow rate (L) | 2.20 kg/s |
| Gas density ($\rho_V$) | 1.20 kg/m³ |
| Liquid density ($\rho_L$) | 1000 kg/m³ |
| Surface tension $\sigma$ | 30 dynes/cm |
| Tray spacing $S_t$ | 0.6 m |

From the GPDC chart, the flooding capacity parameter is found to be $C_{SB,f} = 0.15$ m/s at the calculated flow parameter.

**(a)** Calculate the **flow parameter** $F_{LV}$ and the **flooding velocity** $U_f$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **design diameter** assuming operating fraction $f = 0.80$ and downcomer efficiency $\eta = 0.90$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Flow Parameter and Flooding Velocity (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the flow parameter:

$$F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{V} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}} = \dfrac{2.20}{0.55} \sqrt{\dfrac{1.20}{1000}}$$

$$F_{LV} = 4.0 \times \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.0 \times 0.03464 = \mathbf{0.1386}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Solve for flooding velocity from capacity parameter:

$$C_{SB,f} = U_f \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L - \rho_V}}$$

$$U_f = C_{SB,f} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

$$\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{1000 - 1.2}{1.2} = 832.33$$

$$U_f = 0.15 \times \sqrt{832.33} = 0.15 \times 28.85 = \mathbf{4.327 \;\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Validate this is reasonable — for a low-density gas with large $\rho_L - \rho_V$ ratio, flooding velocities are typically 3–5 m/s. ✔

> **Part (a) Marks Awarded:** Formula setup (3) + Calculation (2) + Sanity check (2) = **7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Design Diameter (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Volumetric gas flow:

$$Q_V = \dfrac{V}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{0.55}{1.20} = 0.4583 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Operating velocity and minimum area:

$$U_{\text{op}} = 0.80 \times 4.327 = 3.462 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{0.4583}{3.462} = 0.1324 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Net area with downcomer:

$$A_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{0.1324}{0.90} = 0.1471 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculated and standard diameter:

$$D_{\text{calc}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.1471}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.1873} = 0.4328 \;\text{m}$$

$$D_{\text{std}} = 0.5 \;\text{m} \quad (\text{rounded UP})$$

> **Part (b) Marks Awarded:** Volumetric conversion (2) + Velocity and area (2) + Downcomer (2) + Rounding (1) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** — Choosing $D = 0.6$ m instead of 0.7 m when $D_{\text{calc}} = 0.6226$ m. This violates hydraulic safety. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Omitting the downcomer correction** — Calculating diameter from $A_{\min}$ directly. The KTU board expects $\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.92$ to be applied. **Loss: 2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing $C_{SB}$ and $U_f$** — Using $C_{SB}$ as the operating velocity itself. Remember: $C_{SB}$ is the *capacity parameter*, NOT a velocity. **Loss: 2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up V and L in $F_{LV}$ formula** — Putting L on top and V on bottom inverted. **Loss: 1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Not converting units** — Leaving V in kmol/hr but using ρ in kg/m³ without converting time base (÷3600) or molar flow to mass flow. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the recheck step** — Final answer must include a verification that the rounded-up diameter still satisfies the operating fraction (0.70–0.85). **Loss: 1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Column diameter is governed by **hydraulics (vapor velocity)**, NOT by mass-transfer equilibrium.
- ✅ **Souders–Brown**: $U_f = K \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ with K ≈ **0.107 m/s** for atmospheric sieve trays.
- ✅ **Operating velocity** = $f \times U_f$, with $f = 0.70$–$0.85$ (typically 0.80).
- ✅ **Flow Parameter** $F_{LV} = (L/V) \sqrt{\rho_V / \rho_L}$ tells you where you sit on the GPDC chart.
- ✅ **Capacity Parameter** $C_{SB} = U \sqrt{\rho_V / (\rho_L - \rho_V)}$ is used with GPDC for high-pressure or vacuum services.
- ✅ **Cross-section area** = $Q_V / U_{\text{op}}$, divided by downcomer efficiency $\eta$.
- ✅ **Diameter** = $\sqrt{4 A_{\text{net}} / \pi}$, then **round UP** to standard increments.
- ✅ Always **recheck** the actual fraction of flooding after rounding up — it must remain in 0.70–0.85 band.
- ✅ Units consistency: convert molar flows to mass flows, mass flows to volumetric flows, time base to seconds.
- ✅ The column is sized at its **most loaded tray**, not at average or feed conditions.
- ✅ For vacuum columns, K is smaller (~0.061) because low density makes flooding easier.
- ✅ Diameter selection is an economic compromise — small enough to minimize steel, large enough to operate 25–35 % below flooding.
- ✅ The vapor **riser zone** (not the downcomer or tray deck) is the section that defines the diameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packing Height, Liquid Distribution and Pressure Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Packing Height, Liquid Distribution and Pressure Drop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Packing Height (Z)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Packing Height is the total vertical depth of structured or random packing material installed inside an absorption, stripping, or distillation column. It is the axial length of the column over which intimate gas–liquid contact and mass transfer actually occur. Mathematically, it is the product of the number of transfer units (or theoretical stages) and the corresponding height per transfer unit (or plate).

$$Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = H_{OL} \cdot N_{OL} = N_{act} \cdot HETP$$

where every term is rigorously defined later in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Packing height is NOT a free design variable. It is the *consequence* of two independent decisions: (1) thermodynamic equilibrium requirement (number of stages/units) and (2) kinetic rate requirement (height per unit). Examiners frequently test this hierarchy.

## 1.2 Liquid Distribution

**Formal Definition:**
Liquid distribution refers to the geometric and hydrodynamic quality with which the reflux/feed liquid is spread over the top cross-section of the packing. It is quantified by the **number of drip points per unit cross-sectional area** (typically **100–200 points/m²** for random packing and **300–500 points/m²** for structured packing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (For Exam):** *Liquid distribution is the process of dividing the inlet liquid stream into a uniform, finely divided spatial pattern over the top of the packing so that the entire packing surface is adequately wetted, thereby ensuring maximum effective interfacial area for mass transfer.*

## 1.3 Pressure Drop (ΔP)

**Formal Definition:**
Pressure Drop is the loss of mechanical energy (per unit cross-section) experienced by the gas/vapour as it flows upward through a packed bed counter-current to the descending liquid. It is expressed in **mm of water per meter of packing**, **inches of water per foot**, or **Pa/m**. Critical design points are the **Loading Point** and the **Flooding Point**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building" Model

Imagine a multi-storey office building where:

| Component | Real Equipment | Building Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Packing** | Random/Structured Packing | The staircase between floors |
| **Gas/Vapour** | Rising gas stream | People walking *up* the stairs |
| **Liquid** | Falling reflux | Water leaking *down* the stairs |
| **Packing Height** | Column packed section | Total vertical length of the staircase |
| **Liquid Distribution** | Distributor tray | The shower at the top floor |
| **Pressure Drop** | Blower/pump head | The breathlessness of climbers |
| **Flooding** | Column flooding | Water gushing back up the stairs |

**The Intuition:**
- A poorly designed "shower" (bad liquid distribution) makes the staircase slippery, forcing people to crowd on one side → inefficient "transfer".
- A staircase that is too "tall" (excess packing height) makes climbers exhausted (high pressure drop).
- If too many people climb while water rushes down (flooding), the entire system jams — exactly what happens in a flooded column.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Quick-Recall:** *Good design = minimum Z that achieves the separation + uniform liquid spread + pressure drop safely below the flooding point.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Packed Column Design

A KTU 2024 question on "Packing Height" will *always* be evaluated against three logical pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Thermodynamic Requirement (How many stages?)
- Equilibrium data (x–y diagram or relative volatility α).
- Kremser equation for dilute systems, McCabe–Thiele for concentrated.
- For absorbers/strippers → **Number of Transfer Units (N_OG or N_OL)**.

### Pillar 2 — Kinetic Requirement (How tall is each stage?)
- **HETP** (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) for random/structured packing.
- **HTU** (Height of a Transfer Unit) — more fundamental than HETP.
- Manufacturer correlations (e.g., Norton, Sulzer, Koch-Glitsch).

### Pillar 3 — Hydraulic Feasibility (Will it flood?)
- Pressure drop curve.
- Flooding correlation (Eckert/Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation — GPDC).
- Liquid distribution quality check.

## 2.2 The Mass Transfer Driving Force Integral (N_OG)

For a counter-current packed absorption column, KTU expects the rigorous integral form:

$$N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}}$$

- $y_1$ = mole fraction of solute in inlet gas
- $y_2$ = mole fraction of solute in outlet gas
- $y^*$ = equilibrium mole fraction corresponding to local liquid composition $x$

**Kremser Analytical Approximation (most-tested form):**

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1 - S)\left(\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2}\right) + S \right]$$

where $S = m \frac{G}{L}$ = **stripping factor** (dimensionless).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary:** If $S = 1$, the integral becomes $\int dy/(y-y^*) = \int dy/(y-mx)$. When the operating line and equilibrium line are parallel, the Ln-approach degenerates to a *linear* average driving force form. Examiners love testing this degenerate case.

## 2.3 Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU) Correlations

$$H_{OG} = H_{G} + \frac{m G}{L} H_{L} = H_{G} + S \cdot H_{L}$$

- $H_G$ = gas-phase contribution
- $H_L$ = liquid-phase contribution
- $m$ = slope of equilibrium line

**Empirical (Cornell, Onda, Bravo-Fair) shortcut:**

$$H_{G} = \frac{0.017 \, \psi \, d_p^{1.24}}{(3600 \, G)^{0.6}} \quad \text{[English units]}$$

For KTU 2024 problems using **SI units**, the Onda correlation is the most tested:

$$H_{G} = \frac{\alpha (S c_G)^{0.5}}{C_G} \cdot \left(\frac{d_p}{0.3048}\right)^{1.24} \left(\frac{G_f}{3600}\right)^{0.6}$$

(Use manufacturer data when provided in the question.)

## 2.4 HETP — Height Equivalent to Theoretical Plate

$$HETP = \frac{Z_{packed}}{N_{actual}}$$

- For **structured packing**: $HETP \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ m
- For **25 mm Raschig rings**: $HETP \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$ m
- For **50 mm Pall rings**: $HETP \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HETP vs HTU:** HETP is *conceptual* (fictitious plate), while HTU is *fundamental* (resistance-based). KTU may ask: *"Why is HETP preferred industrially?"* — because it directly uses equilibrium-stage design data without recomputing the integral.

## 2.5 Liquid Distribution — KTU Quality Criteria

| Parameter | Random Packing | Structured Packing |
|---|---|---|
| Drip points (points/m²) | **100–200** | **300–500** |
| Wetting rate (m³/m·h) | **0.5 – 2.0** | **0.2 – 0.5** |
| Maldistribution tolerance | ± **5%** | ± **2%** |
| Drip point spacing | 75–100 mm | 30–50 mm |

**Wetting Rate Definition:**

$$\text{Wetting Rate} = \frac{L}{a_p \cdot A_c} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/m·h]}$$

where $L$ = liquid flow rate (m³/h), $a_p$ = packing surface area (m²/m³), $A_c$ = column cross-section (m²).

## 2.6 Pressure Drop — The Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)

**Eckert's GPDC** (most frequently tested in KTU 2024):

$$Y = C_p \cdot F_p^2 \cdot \mu_L^{0.2} \cdot \left(\frac{G_f^2}{\rho_G}\right) \quad \text{[dimensionless flow parameter]}$$

$$X = \frac{L_f}{G_f}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L}} \quad \text{[flow parameter ratio]}$$

**Reading the Eckert Chart:**
- Plot $(X, Y)$ on the GPDC chart.
- If point lies on a ΔP curve (e.g., 16 mm H₂O/m) → **acceptable operation**.
- If point lies on the flooding curve → **column will flood**.

**Dry Packing Pressure Drop (Ergun Equation — used for gas-only flow):**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{Z} = \frac{150 \, \mu_G \, u_s \, (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \varepsilon^3} + \frac{1.75 \, \rho_G \, u_s^2 \, (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \, \varepsilon^3}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | Packed height | m | Basic packing height |
| 2 | $Z = HETP \cdot N_{act}$ | Packed height | m | Discrete-stage method |
| 3 | $N_{OG} = \int dy/(y-y^*)$ | Transfer units | dimensionless | Rigorous design |
| 4 | $N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1-S}\ln[(1-S)\frac{y_1-mx_2}{y_2-mx_2}+S]$ | Kremser form | dimensionless | Dilute absorption |
| 5 | $H_{OG} = H_G + S \cdot H_L$ | HTU | m | Resistance-additivity |
| 6 | $HETP = Z/N_{act}$ | Plate equivalence | m | Empirical design |
| 7 | $S = m G/L$ | Stripping factor | dimensionless | Operating/Equil. line slope ratio |
| 8 | $\Delta P/Z$ via GPDC | Pressure gradient | mm H₂O/m | Hydraulic check |
| 9 | $\text{Wetting Rate} = L/(a_p A_c)$ | Liquid spread | m³/m·h | Distribution check |
| 10 | Ergun Eq. (dry bed) | Frictional loss | Pa/m | Pre-irrigation estimate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** In KTU problems, $G$ and $L$ are *molar* flow rates in the mass-transfer equations, but *mass* flow rates in the pressure-drop equation. Mixing them is the single biggest mark-losing mistake.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of the Packed Height Integral (N_OG)

### Starting Point — Differential Mass Balance on the Gas Phase

Consider a differential slice of packing of height $dZ$ at steady state. The solute transferred from gas to liquid across this slice is:

$$d(G \cdot y) = G \cdot dy$$

The mass transfer rate through the gas film (per unit interfacial area, $a$) is:

$$d(G \cdot y) = K_y \, a \, (y - y^{*}) \, dZ$$

where $K_y a$ is the **overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient × interfacial area per unit volume** [kmol/m³·s].

> **Step 1 (Equating the two expressions):**
>
> $$G \, dy = K_y \, a \, (y - y^{*}) \, dZ$$

> **Step 2 (Separating variables):**
>
> $$dZ = \frac{G}{K_y \, a} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}}$$

> **Step 3 (Defining HTU):**
>
> The first factor is defined as the **Height of an Overall Gas Transfer Unit:**
>
> $$H_{OG} \equiv \frac{G}{K_y \, a} \quad \text{[m]}$$

> **Step 4 (Defining NTU):**
>
> The integral term is the **Number of Overall Gas Transfer Units:**
>
> $$N_{OG} \equiv \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}}$$

> **Step 5 (Integrating from gas inlet to outlet):**
>
> $$\boxed{Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}}}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** every KTU 2024 answer must begin with.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem Statement:**
> A packed absorption column removes acetone from air using water at 25 °C, 1 atm. Inlet gas: $y_1 = 0.030$ (mol acetone/mol air). Outlet gas: $y_2 = 0.001$. Inlet liquid: $x_2 = 0.000$. Equilibrium: $y^* = 1.18 x$. Gas molar flow: $G = 0.020$ kmol/s. Liquid molar flow: $L = 0.085$ kmol/s. $K_y a = 0.015$ kmol/m³·s. Calculate the **packed height**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the type of system (Kremser applicability check):**
- Dilute system ✓ (mole fractions < 0.03)
- $G$ and $L$ are constant ✓
- Equilibrium is linear ($y^* = mx$) ✓
- **⇒ Kremser equation is valid.**

**Step 2 — Compute the Stripping Factor $S$:**

$$S = m \frac{G}{L} = 1.18 \times \frac{0.020}{0.085}$$

$$S = 1.18 \times 0.2353 = 0.2776$$

Since $S < 1$, absorption is *gas-phase controlling* — a typical KTU scenario.

**Step 3 — Compute $N_{OG}$ using Kremser:**

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln\left[(1 - S) \cdot \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + S\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 - 0.2776} \ln\left[(1 - 0.2776) \cdot \frac{0.030 - 1.18 \times 0}{0.001 - 0} + 0.2776\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.7224} \ln\left[0.7224 \times \frac{0.030}{0.001} + 0.2776\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.7224} \ln\left[0.7224 \times 30 + 0.2776\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.7224} \ln\left[21.672 + 0.2776\right] = \frac{1}{0.7224} \ln(21.950)$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.7224} \times 3.089 = 4.276 \text{ transfer units}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $H_{OG}$:**

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} = \frac{0.020}{0.015} = 1.333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Packed Height:**

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 1.333 \times 4.276$$

$$\boxed{Z = 5.70 \text{ m}}$$

> **Step 6 — Sanity check (KTU examiner perspective):**
> $H_{OG}$ of 1.33 m is realistic for a chemical absorption system. The number of transfer units (4.28) corresponds to roughly 4.28 actual equilibrium stages — well within the typical industrial range of 3–8 stages for solvent scrubbing.

## 3.3 Pressure Drop — Worked Problem Using Eckert GPDC

**Given:** A 0.75 m diameter packed column operates with:
- $G_f = 1.2$ kg/m²·s (gas mass flux)
- $L_f = 2.0$ kg/m²·s (liquid mass flux)
- $\rho_G = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu_L = 1.0$ cP
- 25 mm ceramic Raschig rings ($F_p = 179$ ft⁻¹)

**Step 1 — Flow Parameter Ratio (X):**

$$X = \frac{L_f}{G_f} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L}} = \frac{2.0}{1.2} \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1000}}$$

$$= 1.667 \times 0.03464 = 0.0577$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Parameter (Y):**

$$Y = \frac{G_f^2 \, C_p \, F_p^2 \, \mu_L^{0.2}}{\rho_G \, \rho_L}$$

(Note: $C_p$ is a packing-specific constant; for 25 mm Raschig rings $C_p \approx 1.246 \times 10^{-3}$ in SI-compatible form.)

Assuming the question provides: $Y_{flood}$ for $X = 0.0577$ is **0.022** (read from Eckert chart), and operating Y is **0.012**.

**Step 3 — Locate operating point:**
- Operating point lies **below** the flooding curve.
- It lies on the **ΔP = 20 mm H₂O/m** curve (from chart interpolation).
- Hence column is **safe**, operating at **~55% of flooding velocity**.

**Step 4 — Total Pressure Drop for 5.7 m of packing:**

$$\Delta P_{total} = 20 \, \text{mm H}_2\text{O/m} \times 5.7 \, \text{m} = 114 \, \text{mm H}_2\text{O}$$

$$= 114 \times 9.81 = 1118 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.12 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
- Blower must deliver at least **1.5 kPa** (margin for distributors, supports, demister).
- A 2.0 kPa industrial blower is suitable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Margin Rule (KTU convention):** Operate at 50–70% of flooding. The chosen Y = 0.012 vs Y_flood = 0.022 corresponds to a flooding fraction of 0.012/0.022 = 0.545, i.e., ~55%. This is **acceptable**.

## 3.4 Liquid Distribution — Wetting Rate Check

Using the same column (diameter 0.75 m, $a_p = 200$ m²/m³ for 25 mm Raschig):

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.75)^2 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^2$$

If $L = 0.085$ kmol/s × 18 kg/kmol = 1.53 kg/s = 5508 kg/h, volumetric (assuming water):

$$L_{vol} = 5.508 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\text{Wetting Rate} = \frac{L_{vol}}{a_p A_c} = \frac{5.508}{200 \times 0.4418} = 0.0623 \text{ m}^3/\text{m·h}$$

**Result: 0.0623 m³/m·h is BELOW the minimum wetting rate (0.5 m³/m·h) for water on ceramic Raschig rings.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The packing will be under-wetted! HETP will INCREASE dramatically, mass transfer efficiency will DROP by 30–50%. **Design change required:** Either reduce the column diameter or split the column into two smaller-diameter columns in parallel. KTU examiners will deduct marks if this conclusion is missing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Packed Column — Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GasIn[Gas Inlet y1] --> BotShell[Bottom Shell Section]
    BotShell --> PackingBed[Random or Structured Packing Z meters]
    PackingBed --> DistributorTray[Liquid Distributor]
    DistributorTray --> TopShell[Top Shell Section]
    TopShell --> GasOut[Clean Gas Outlet y2]
    LiqIn[Lean Liquid x2] --> TopShell
    TopShell --> PackingBed
    PackingBed --> BotShell
    BotShell --> LiqOut[Rich Liquid x1]
    
    PDCtrl[Pressure Drop Sensor] -.-> PackingBed
    TempCtrl[Temperature Sensor] -.-> PackingBed
    
    style GasIn fill:#FFD580
    style GasOut fill:#A0E0A0
    style PackingBed fill:#9EC6E6
    style DistributorTray fill:#FFB6C1
    style LiqIn fill:#C8A2C8
    style LiqOut fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Liquid Distribution Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InletPipe[Feed Inlet] --> Header[Distribution Header]
    Header --> SprayNozzle1[Nozzle A1]
    Header --> SprayNozzle2[Nozzle A2]
    Header --> SprayNozzle3[Nozzle A3]
    Header --> SprayNozzle4[Nozzle A4]
    Header --> SprayNozzle5[Nozzle A5]
    Header --> SprayNozzle6[Nozzle A6]
    Header --> SprayNozzle7[Nozzle A7]
    Header --> SprayNozzle8[Nozzle A8]
    Header --> SprayNozzle9[Nozzle A9]
    
    SprayNozzle1 --> PackingTop[Top of Packing Bed]
    SprayNozzle2 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle3 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle4 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle5 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle6 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle7 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle8 --> PackingTop
    SprayNozzle9 --> PackingTop
    
    PackingTop --> CoverageCheck{Uniform Wetting?}
    CoverageCheck -- Yes --> Optimal[Optimal Operation]
    CoverageCheck -- No --> Channeling[Channeling Detected]
    
    style InletPipe fill:#FFD580
    style PackingTop fill:#9EC6E6
    style Optimal fill:#A0E0A0
    style Channeling fill:#FF6B6B
```

## 4.3 Pressure Drop — Operating Regimes Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Gas Velocity Increases] --> Dry[Dry Packing Region]
    Dry --> PreLoad[Pre-Loading Region Linear dp vs v]
    PreLoad --> LoadPt[Loading Point Onset of liquid holdup]
    LoadPt --> Load[Loading Region dp rises sharply]
    Load --> FloodPt[Flooding Point Incipient column flood]
    FloodPt --> Flood[Flooding Region Liquid carried upward]
    
    Dry -.- DP1[dp less than 12 mm H2O per m]
    PreLoad -.- DP2[dp between 12 and 20 mm H2O per m]
    Load -.- DP3[dp between 20 and 40 mm H2O per m]
    Flood -.- DP4[dp greater than 40 mm H2O per m DANGEROUS]
    
    style Start fill:#FFD580
    style Flood fill:#FF6B6B
    style LoadPt fill:#FFA500
    style FloodPt fill:#FF4500
    style Dry fill:#A0E0A0
    style PreLoad fill:#9EC6E6
    style Load fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.4 Packing Height Design Procedure (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Define Separation y1 y2 x1 x2] --> B[Determine N_OG via Kremser or Graphical Integral]
    B --> C[Select Packing Type and Size]
    C --> D[Estimate H_OG from Manufacturer or Onda]
    D --> E[Calculate Z equals H_OG times N_OG]
    E --> F[Compute Wetting Rate Check]
    F --> G{Sufficient Wetting?}
    G -- No --> H[Redesign Diameter or Packing]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Check Pressure Drop via GPDC]
    I --> J{Safe vs Flooding?}
    J -- No --> K[Reduce Gas Rate or Use Larger Packing]
    K --> C
    J -- Yes --> L[Finalize Z with 10 percent Safety Margin]
    
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style L fill:#A0E0A0
    style H fill:#FFB6C1
    style K fill:#FFB6C1
```
