<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define HETP. State its typical range for (a) 25 mm Raschig rings and (b) structured packing.**

**Model Answer:**
HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) is the height of packing that gives the same change in gas composition as one ideal (theoretical) equilibrium stage.

$$HETP = \frac{Z_{packed}}{N_{actual}}$$

Typical values:
- (a) **25 mm Raschig rings:** 0.4 – 0.5 m
- (b) **Structured packing (e.g., Mellapak 250Y):** 0.3 – 0.5 m

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by "flooding" in a packed column? Mention two design precautions to avoid it.**

**Model Answer:**
**Flooding** is the condition at which the upward gas velocity becomes high enough to suspend the descending liquid, causing liquid accumulation at the top of the packing and a sharp rise in pressure drop.

**Two precautions:**
1. Operate at **50–70% of the flooding velocity** (designed using the Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation / Eckert chart).
2. Use **larger packing size** (e.g., 50 mm Pall rings instead of 25 mm) at high gas rates, as it provides higher flood capacity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the fundamental equation for calculating the height of packing in an absorption column starting from a differential mass balance. Define every term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gas mixture containing 4 mol% SO₂ in air is scrubbed with water in a packed tower. Inlet gas rate: 0.05 kmol/s; liquid rate: 2.5 kmol/s. The equilibrium relation is $y^* = 30 x$. Required outlet gas composition: $y_2 = 0.0005$. Inlet liquid is solute-free ($x_2 = 0$). If $K_y a = 0.012$ kmol/m³·s, calculate the **packed height**. Comment on whether the design is hydraulically safe at 60% of flooding.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Consider a differential packing slice of height $dZ$ at steady state.

**Step 2:** Solute transferred from gas:
$$d(G \cdot y) = G \, dy$$

**Step 3:** Same transfer expressed via driving force:
$$G \, dy = K_y \, a \, (y - y^*) \, dZ$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables:
$$dZ = \frac{G}{K_y a} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

**Step 5:** Define $H_{OG} = G/(K_y a)$ and $N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$.

**Step 6:** Integrate:

$$\boxed{Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}}$$

**Definitions (each 0.5 mark):** $G$ = molar gas flow rate, $K_y$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient, $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume, $y^*$ = equilibrium gas composition, $H_{OG}$ = height of a transfer unit, $N_{OG}$ = number of transfer units, $Z$ = total packed height.

### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Stripping factor:
$$S = m \frac{G}{L} = 30 \times \frac{0.05}{2.5} = 30 \times 0.02 = 0.60$$

**Step 2:** Kremser equation:
$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.60} \ln\left[(1 - 0.60)\frac{0.04 - 30 \times 0}{0.0005 - 0} + 0.60\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.40} \ln\left[0.40 \times \frac{0.04}{0.0005} + 0.60\right]$$

$$= 2.5 \times \ln[0.40 \times 80 + 0.60] = 2.5 \times \ln(32.6)$$

$$N_{OG} = 2.5 \times 3.485 = 8.71 \text{ transfer units}$$

**Step 3:** HTU:
$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} = \frac{0.05}{0.012} = 4.167 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Packed height:
$$Z = 4.167 \times 8.71 = 36.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** With 10% safety margin:
$$Z_{design} \approx 40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Comment on hydraulic safety:
At 60% of flooding, the column operates in the safe region. Using GPDC, the pressure drop would lie in the range 20–30 mm H₂O/m. With $Z = 40$ m, the total pressure drop would be **800–1200 mm H₂O**, which is acceptable for a well-designed industrial blower.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert $m = 30$ to the *correct* basis. If $m > 1$, the system is **gas-phase controlling**; this is the expected case in KTU problems. Also, the answer should explicitly state the *units* of $Z$ (m).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Eckert Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC). How is it used to check the hydraulic design of a packed column? Sketch the chart axes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A packed column 1.0 m diameter is to be designed for CO₂ absorption. Gas flow: 0.40 kmol/s; liquid flow: 0.45 kmol/s. Using 50 mm metal Pall rings ($a_p = 105$ m²/m³, $F_p = 41$ ft⁻¹), determine:
- (i) Wetting rate in m³/m·h (take liquid density 1000 kg/m³, MW = 18).
- (ii) Number of liquid drip points needed (assume target 200 points/m²).
- (iii) Comment on whether the packing is adequately wetted for chemical absorption.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — GPDC Explanation [7 Marks]

The **Eckert Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** is an empirical design chart that relates two dimensionless parameters to predict the operating pressure drop of a packed column and identify the flooding point.

**Axes of the GPDC Chart:**

$$\text{Abscissa (X)} = \frac{L}{G}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L}} \quad \text{(flow parameter ratio)}$$

$$\text{Ordinate (Y)} = \frac{G_f^2 \, C_p \, F_p^2 \, \mu_L^{0.2}}{\rho_G \rho_L} \quad \text{(capacity parameter)}$$

**Curves on the chart:**
- Flooding curve (upper boundary).
- ΔP lines at 4, 8, 12, 20, 40 mm H₂O per m of packing.

**Procedure:**
1. Compute X and Y for the operating condition.
2. Plot the point on the chart.
3. If the point lies **on or below** the flooding curve → safe.
4. Read the ΔP line on which the point falls → this is the operating pressure drop per meter.

**Utility:** Used to (i) select packing size, (ii) determine safe gas velocity, (iii) estimate blower head, (iv) detect incipient flooding.

### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**(i) Wetting Rate:**

Liquid mass flow: $L_m = 0.45 \times 18 = 8.1$ kg/s = **29,160 kg/h**

Volumetric: $L_v = 29.16$ m³/h

Cross-section: $A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.0)^2 = 0.785$ m²

Wetting rate = $L_v / (a_p \cdot A_c) = 29.16 / (105 \times 0.785)$

$$\text{Wetting rate} = 29.16 / 82.43 = 0.354 \text{ m}^3/\text{m·h}$$

**(ii) Number of Drip Points:**

Target = 200 points/m² × 0.785 m² = **157 drip points** (round up to 160).

**(iii) Adequacy of Wetting:**

The minimum wetting rate for **chemical absorption** is typically **0.5 m³/m·h** (rule of thumb for aqueous systems on metal Pall rings). Our calculated value of **0.354 m³/m·h is below 0.5** → **Inadequate wetting**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Action:** Either (1) reduce column diameter, (2) pre-saturate the gas to reduce $G$ requirement, or (3) use a more open packing with higher $a_p$ per unit volume. KTU 2024 examiners **will deduct 2 marks** if no design recommendation is provided.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (Compile from Past Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Mixing molar vs mass flow rates** in the pressure-drop vs mass-transfer equations.
> 2. **Forgetting the 10% safety margin** on packing height.
> 3. **Skipping the wetting rate check** — a complete design must verify that the packing is wetted.
> 4. **Confusing HETP and HTU** — write their definitions explicitly in the answer.
> 5. **Using the wrong equilibrium relation** — always read carefully whether $y^* = mx$ or $y = mx^*$ is given.
> 6. **Neglecting units** — every numeric answer must be in **m** (height), **m³/m·h** (wetting rate), and **mm H₂O/m** (pressure drop).
> 7. **Not stating the design regime** — the answer must specify whether the column operates in the pre-loading, loading, or flooding regime.

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Packing height is the **product of HTU and NTU** (kinetic × thermodynamic) OR **HETP and number of stages**.
- ✅ **HETP** = experimentally measured plate-equivalent height. Preferred for industrial design.
- ✅ **HTU** = fundamental mass-transfer resistance-based height. Used for rigorous design.
- ✅ The **Kremser equation** is the analytical shortcut for $N_{OG}$ in dilute systems with linear equilibrium.
- ✅ The **stripping factor $S = mG/L$** determines whether the gas or liquid film dominates resistance.
- ✅ **Liquid distribution** is checked via the **wetting rate** ($L / (a_p A_c)$) and **drip point density** (points/m²).
- ✅ Minimum wetting rate for **chemical absorption** ≈ **0.5 m³/m·h**; for **physical absorption** ≈ **0.2 m³/m·h**.
- ✅ **Pressure drop** is predicted using the **Eckert GPDC**; design at **50–70% of flooding** is the standard rule.
- ✅ Operating regimes: **dry → pre-loading → loading → flooding**. Stay in the **pre-loading** zone.
- ✅ The **Ergun equation** gives dry-packing pressure drop (used as a check).
- ✅ **Final design height** = calculated $Z$ + **10% safety margin**.
- ✅ For structured packing, **HETP < 0.5 m**; for 25 mm Raschig, **HETP ≈ 0.4–0.5 m**; for 50 mm Pall, **HETP ≈ 0.6–0.8 m**.
- ✅ **Units discipline** is non-negotiable in KTU valuation — height in m, ΔP in mm H₂O/m, $G$ & $L$ in kmol/s for mass transfer, kg/m²·s for hydraulics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Fermenters: Design Considerations for Maintaining Sterility of Process Streams and Process Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Fermenters: Design Considerations for Maintaining Sterility of Process Streams and Process Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sterility in Bioprocess Engineering** is defined as the *absolute absence of viable, contaminating microorganisms* (bacteria, fungi, spores, viruses, mycoplasma) in the process stream, equipment interior surfaces, headspace gases, and any material entering a fermenter. For a fermentation plant, sterility must be maintained throughout the entire upstream, the bioreactor core, and selected downstream sampling operations.

In the context of **KTU PCBBT602 – Process Plant Design (2024 Scheme)**, the design considerations for maintaining sterility of process streams and process equipment constitute the **front-line defence against contamination**, which directly governs product titre, batch consistency, regulatory compliance (cGMP / US-FDA / WHO), and operator safety.

A **Fermenter (Bioreactor)** is a closed, aseptic vessel where a controlled biological transformation is carried out using a mono- or mixed-culture of microorganisms, plant/animal cells, or enzymes. Maintaining sterility means **preventing entry of any foreign biological agent** from the moment the vessel is sealed until it is opened for harvest.

The sterility envelope includes:

1. **Liquid streams** → Nutrient feed (medium), inoculum, antifoam, acid/base, pH adjusters, inducer solutions.
2. **Gas streams** → Compressed air (or O₂), N₂, CO₂ for sparging and headspace blanketing.
3. **Solid streams** → Powdered nutrients, immobilised cell carriers.
4. **Equipment interior** → Vessel walls, agitator shaft, baffles, sparger, probes, internals, transfer lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The question paper typically tests the *integration* of mechanical, chemical, and microbiological design choices. Memorising a single filter or valve is not enough — students must justify *why* a particular configuration is chosen for a given service.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **surgical operating theatre (OT)**.

| OT Component | Bioreactor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sterile gloves & mask | Aseptic sampling valves, steam barriers |
| HEPA-filtered laminar airflow | Inlet air absolute filter (0.2 µm) |
| Autoclaved surgical tools | SIP (Steam-In-Place) sterilised internals |
| Antiseptic skin wash | CIP (Clean-In-Place) chemical rinse |
| One-way traffic for staff | Steam-locked (steam-barrier) valves on transfer lines |
| Restricted entry doors | Sterile barriers between fermentation & non-sterile zones |

Just as a single lapse (an unsterile glove, an open door) can ruin a surgery, **a single contaminated valve or probe** can wipe out an entire fermentation batch worth several lakhs of rupees. The design of a sterile plant, therefore, is like designing a **fortress** — every wall, every doorway, every window must be carefully engineered.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Sterility Design

- **Log Reduction Value (LRV)** = log₁₀(N₀/N) — measures sterilisation efficacy.
- **Filter Pore Size** = **0.2 µm** (absolute) or **0.1 µm** for mycoplasma.
- **Sterilisation Temperature** = **121 °C** at **15 psig (≈ 1.03 bar g)** for ≥ **15–30 min**.
- **Air-to-Medium Ratio** for sparging = **0.1 – 2.0 vvm** (volume of air per volume of medium per minute).
- **Steam Quality** = dryness fraction ≥ **0.95** (saturated, dry).
- **Bioburden Limit** = typically **< 10 CFU / 100 mL** in pre-sterile feed.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical constants to remember (in bold):**
> - $T_{sterilisation} = 121.5\ ^\circ C = 394.65\ K$
> - $P_{saturation} = 1.034\ bar\ (gauge) = 101.325\ kPa\ (absolute)\ at\ sea\ level$
> - **Decimal Reduction Time $D_{121}$** for *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* spores (biological indicator) ≈ **1.5 min**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Sterility Design

The design of sterile process equipment follows a **four-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Tier 1 — Primary Containment (Inherent Design):** Smooth, electropolished, crevice-free interior surfaces; sanitary fittings (Tri-Clamp, DIN 11864, Naamp). Material: **SS 316 L** (low carbon, ≤ 0.03 % C) — resists pitting corrosion in chloride-bearing media.
2. **Tier 2 — Active Sterilisation (Process Control):** SIP via saturated steam; CIP via NaOH (1–2 %) / HNO₃ (0.5–1 %) / hot water rinse.
3. **Tier 3 — Process Stream Treatment:** Filtration (0.2 µm membrane for gases; 0.1–0.45 µm for heat-sensitive liquids); UV irradiation; pasteurisation.
4. **Tier 4 — Barrier & Back-up Devices:** Steam-barrier (block) valves, sterile barriers between piping zones, pressure-relief devices fitted with 0.2 µm vent filters.

---

## 2.2 Sterilisation of Process Streams

### 2.2.1 Liquid Media Sterilisation

**A. Batch Sterilisation (in-situ)**

The medium is heated *inside* the fermenter from ambient to **121 °C**, held for **t → ∞** defined by:

$$\boxed{N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}}$$

where:
- $N$ = final viable spore count (CFU)
- $N_0$ = initial viable spore count (CFU)
- $t$ = exposure time (min)
- $D$ = Decimal Reduction Time (min) — time at a given $T$ to achieve a **10-fold reduction**.

**B. Continuous Sterilisation (using a Heat Exchanger / Flash Cooler)**

For large-scale plants, continuous sterilisation is preferred because:
- Higher product quality (shorter residence time → less nutrient destruction).
- Better steam economy.
- Easier scale-up.

Typical residence time in the holding coil:

$$\tau_{holding} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{Q}$$

where $V_{coil}$ = internal volume of holding coil (m³), $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s).

The **Del Factor (∇)** quantifies the integrated lethal effect:

$$\boxed{\nabla = \ln\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \int_0^{\tau} 10^{(T(t)-T_{ref})/z}\, dt}$$

For an ideal holding tube (isothermal):

$$\nabla = \tau \cdot 10^{(T - 121)/z}$$

where $z$ = temperature change required for a **10-fold change in $D$** (for bacterial spores, $z \approx 10\ ^\circ C$).

---

### 2.2.2 Gas Stream Sterilisation — Air Filtration

Compressed air fed to a fermenter must be free of oil, water, and microorganisms.

**Multi-stage filtration train:**

| Stage | Equipment | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inlet air pre-filter (coalescer) | Removes bulk moisture & particulates |
| 2 | Refrigerated air dryer | Lowers dew point to ≈ 2–10 °C |
| 3 | Desiccant (silica gel / molecular sieve) dryer | Dew point < –40 °C |
| 4 | Activated carbon tower | Removes oil vapour & compressor residue |
| 5 | Sterile-grade cartridge filter | **Absolute 0.2 µm**, hydrophobic PTFE / PVDF |
| 6 | Off-gas vent filter | 0.2 µm, prevents back-contamination |

**Filter Sizing Equation:**

$$\boxed{Q_{filter} = \dfrac{Q_{air,actual}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\ \% < 100\ \%}$$

The filter is selected so that its **rated flow at line pressure** is ≥ **2 × design flow** (i.e., operating at **≤ 50 % of rated capacity**), giving margin against filter clogging.

**Why Hydrophobic PTFE?**
- Repels water droplets → prevents "filter wetting" that would allow *Brevundimonas diminuta* to penetrate.
- Operates dry → sterilised *in-situ* by passing steam at 121 °C for ≥ 30 min monthly.

---

### 2.2.3 Sterilisation of Equipment — Steam-In-Place (SIP)

The fermenter vessel and all connected piping are sterilised with **saturated pure steam**.

**Design Requirements for Effective SIP:**

- All piping must be **self-draining** (slope ≥ 1:100).
- **No "dead legs"** — branch length ≤ **2.5 × branch diameter** (ASME BPE 2009 standard).
- All valves must be **steam-blocked** or **double-block-and-bleed** to prevent back-flow.
- Condensate must be continuously removed via **steam traps** (inverted bucket / float type).
- Air must be completely displaced → vent valves at all high points, opened until steam flows steadily (i.e., air displaced).

**Steam Penetration Verification:**

$$\boxed{F_{121} = \int 10^{(T(t) - 121)/z}\, dt \geq 15\ min}$$

$F_{121} \geq 15\ min$ is the **pharmacopeial minimum** (USP, EP) for moist-heat sterilisation of equipment.

---

## 2.3 Sterility of Process Equipment

### 2.3.1 Vessel Design Features

| Feature | Sterility Function |
|---|---|
| **Electropolished SS 316 L** | Reduces Ra ≤ 0.4 µm → prevents biofilm adhesion |
| **Round internal corners (≥ 6 mm radius)** | Eliminates crevices where spores survive CIP/SIP |
| **Sloped bottom (≥ 1:50)** | Allows complete drainage; prevents condensate pooling |
| **Top-driven or bottom-driven magnetic coupling** | Eliminates the shaft seal — the #1 contamination route in agitated fermenters |
| **Sanitary diaphragm pressure gauge** | Dead-leg-free reading |

### 2.3.2 Mechanical Seal vs Magnetic Coupling

**Mechanical Seal (single):**
- Risk → lip seal wear → ingress of contaminants.
- Used only with **condensate flush / barrier fluid** at lower pressure than fermenter.

**Magnetic Coupling (preferred):**
- Inner rotor (impeller-side) magnetised; outer rotor (motor-side) drives via magnetic flux through a thin SS hermetic can.
- **Zero dynamic penetration** of the vessel wall → **inherently sterile**.
- Cost ≈ 1.5–2 × mechanical seal, but eliminates recurring contamination risk.

### 2.3.3 Aseptic Sampling & Inoculation Ports

- **Sterile septum port:** silicone/Teflon-faced, needle-puncturable, autoclaved separately.
- **Steam-locked sampling valve:** two diaphragm valves separated by a steam block, sterilised continuously.
- **Sterile connect/disconnect devices (e.g., CPC AseptiQuik):** allow dry connection of lines without laminar flow cabinet.

### 2.3.4 Foam Control & Aseptic Addition

- Mechanical foam breaker (centrifugal) preferred over chemical antifoam for sensitive cultures.
- Antifoam reservoir fitted with **steam sterilised lines and sterile addition pump**.
- All nutrient feeds enter through **steam-barrier block valves**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises **every key formula** required for solving KTU questions on this topic. **Absolute value notation uses `\vert` to avoid markdown parsing issues.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$ | Survivor curve for batch sterilisation | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\nabla = \ln(N_0/N)$ | Del Factor (lethality) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\nabla = \tau \cdot 10^{(T-121)/z}$ | Del Factor (isothermal hold) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D_T = D_{121} \cdot 10^{(121-T)/z}$ | D-value temperature dependence | min |
| 5 | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}$ | Standardised sterilisation time (pharmacopeial) | min |
| 6 | $\tau_{hold} = V_{coil}/Q$ | Holding-coil residence time | s |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{filter} = K \cdot \mu \cdot Q / A$ | Filter pressure drop (Darcy) | bar |
| 8 | $Q_{safe} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow (steam block) | m³/s |
| 9 | $\theta = \dfrac{L_{branch}}{d_{branch}} \le 2.5$ | Dead-leg limit (ASME BPE) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{Re,agitator} = \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 11 | $N_{p} = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$ | Power number (impeller) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $k_L a = K \cdot (P/V)^{0.33}\cdot v_s^{0.67}$ | Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient | s⁻¹ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical antibiotics** (penicillin, streptomycin): contamination can drop yield by **> 50 %** within hours.
- **Recombinant protein** (Insulin, mAbs): contamination → entire batch rejected → loss of ₹ 50 lakh – ₹ 5 crore per batch.
- **Vaccine production** (live attenuated): a single contaminant can cross-contaminate the entire suite → facility shutdown.
- **Industrial ethanol:** wild *Acetobacter* converts ethanol → acetic acid, ruining fermenter.
- **Cell culture (mammalian):** extremely sensitive — mycoplasma contamination invisible under microscope but destroys productivity.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** Design every sterile barrier as if a single *Bacillus subtilis* spore (size ~ 1 µm, hardiest common contaminant) is determined to enter. If your design defeats it, all weaker microbes will be defeated too.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Survivor Curve Equation

We begin with first-order death kinetics of microbial spores under thermal stress.

**Step 1 — Define specific death rate.**

The rate of disappearance of viable spores at any instant is proportional to the number present:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

where $k$ is the specific death rate constant (min⁻¹) at temperature $T$.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $N = N_0$) to $t = t$ (where $N = N$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Convert to base 10.**

Since most engineers work in **base-10 logarithms**, we substitute $k = \ln(10)/D = 2.3026/D$, where $D$ is the decimal reduction time:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\ln(10)}{D} \cdot t$$

Multiplying both sides by $1/\ln(10)$:

$$\log\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{D}$$

Taking the antilog:

$$\boxed{N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

- For $t = D$ → $N = 0.1 \cdot N_0$ (90 % kill).
- For $t = 6 D$ → $N = 10^{-6} \cdot N_0$ (99.9999 % kill, i.e., **sterility assurance level $10^{-6}$** for terminally sterilised injectables).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Style)

> **Problem:** A 50 000 L fermenter contains a nutrient medium with an initial bioburden of $N_0 = 5 \times 10^{5}$ CFU/mL of thermophilic spores. The medium is to be sterilised at $T = 121\ ^\circ C$ where $D_{121} = 2.0$ min and $z = 10\ ^\circ C$. Determine:
> (a) The holding time required at 121 °C to achieve a final contamination probability of $10^{-3}$ in the entire batch.
> (b) The additional holding time required if sterilisation temperature is dropped to 115 °C for energy saving. Use $D_{121} = 2.0$ min.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate total initial spore count in the batch.**

The fermenter volume $V = 50\,000\ L = 5 \times 10^{7}\ mL$.

$$N_{0,total} = 5 \times 10^{5}\ CFU/mL \times 5 \times 10^{7}\ mL = 2.5 \times 10^{13}\ CFU$$

**Step 2 — Translate the contamination probability to final $N_{total}$.**

For a contamination probability of $10^{-3}$:

$$N_{final,total} = 2.5 \times 10^{13} \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{10}\ CFU$$

**Step 3 — Apply survivor equation (Part a, $T = 121\ ^\circ C$).**

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$

$$2.5 \times 10^{10} = 2.5 \times 10^{13} \cdot 10^{-t/2}$$

Divide both sides:

$$10^{-t/2} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{10}}{2.5 \times 10^{13}} = 10^{-3}$$

Take $\log_{10}$:

$$-\dfrac{t}{2} = -3$$

$$\boxed{t = 6\ min\ at\ 121\ ^\circ C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute D-value at $T = 115\ ^\circ C$ (Part b).**

Using $z$-value relation:

$$D_{115} = D_{121} \cdot 10^{(121-115)/z}$$

$$D_{115} = 2.0 \cdot 10^{6/10} = 2.0 \cdot 10^{0.6}$$

$$D_{115} = 2.0 \times 3.981 = 7.96\ min$$

**Step 5 — Compute new holding time at 115 °C.**

$$t' = D_{115} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

$$t' = 7.96 \times 3 = \boxed{23.9\ min\ at\ 115\ ^\circ C}$$

**Step 6 — Energy impact (qualitative engineering interpretation).**

Dropping 6 °C in temperature **quadruples the holding time** (because $z = 10\ ^\circ C$ for typical spores). Since steam cost dominates utility OPEX, 121 °C is almost always chosen unless the medium is heat-degradable (e.g., vitamin-fortified media).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Sterility Method

| Service | Recommended Sterility Method | Reason | Common Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid medium (heat-stable) | **Batch in-situ SIP** | Simple, validated | Cold spots due to poor steam distribution |
| Liquid medium (heat-labile) | **0.2 µm membrane filtration** | No thermal degradation | Filter integrity breach, wetting |
| Compressed air | **0.2 µm hydrophobic cartridge** | Dry, sterilisable in-situ | Oil aerosol fouling, condensate wetting |
| Nutrient feed (intermittent) | **Steam-block valve + 0.2 µm** | Allows addition during run | Steam trap failure → no barrier |
| Vessel interior | **SIP at 121 °C, ≥ 30 min** | Proven pharmacopeial method | Dead-leg pockets, air pocket |
| Sampling line | **Steam-locked diaphragm** | Continuous sterility | Valve seat wear |
| Probe (pH, DO) | **Autoclaved removable probe in housing** | Periodic replacement | Gasket leak |
| Off-gas | **0.2 µm vent filter + condensate trap** | Prevents back-contamination | Filter wetting from foam |
| Steam supply | **Drip trap + steam filter** | Removes rust & condensate | Wet steam → wet vessel → incomplete kill |

---

## 3.4 Validation Approach (as required for KTU design reports)

1. **Heat Distribution Study** — place ≥ 12 thermocouples; record $T(t)$; verify all points reach ≥ 121 °C.
2. **Heat Penetration Study** — coldest point must reach $F_{0} \geq 15$ min.
3. **Biological Challenge Test** — inoculate vessel with $10^{6}$ *G. stearothermophilus* spores (biological indicator); confirm 100 % kill.
4. **Filter Integrity Test** — perform **bubble point** test: $P_{BP} \ge 3.0\ bar$ for 0.2 µm PTFE.
5. **Media Fill (Process Simulation)** — run full SOP with sterile media; incubate 14 days at 20–25 °C then 7 days at 30–35 °C; zero growth required for sterile products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sterile Fermenter Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Compressed Air Inlet] --> B[Pre-filter Coalescer]
    B --> C[Refrigerated Dryer]
    C --> D[Desiccant Dryer]
    D --> E[Activated Carbon Tower]
    E --> F[Absolute 0.2 micron Filter]
    F --> G[Steam Sterilisation Block Valve]
    G --> H[Fermenter Vessel SIP]
    H --> I[Agitator Magnetic Coupling]
    H --> J[Sample Port Steam Block]
    H --> K[Probe Housing pH DO]
    H --> L[Vent Filter 0.2 micron]
    H --> M[Sterile Drain Valve]
    F -.sterile air.-> H
    J -.aseptic sampling.-> N[Sample Collector]
    L -.off-gas to environment.-> O[Safe Exhaust]
    P[Liquid Nutrient Feed Tank] --> Q[Steam Jacketed Line]
    Q --> R[Steam Block Valve]
    R --> S[0.2 micron Liquid Filter]
    S --> H
    T[Steam Supply Header] --> U[Steam Trap]
    U --> H
```

## 4.2 Sterile Piping & Steam-Blocked Valve Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fermenter
        V01[Fermenter Vessel]
    end
    subgraph FeedLine
        FL01[Feed Tank] -->|sterile barrier| SV01[Steam Block Valve 1]
        SV01 -->|condensate drain| ST01[Steam Trap 1]
        SV01 -->|sterile path| SV02[Steam Block Valve 2]
        SV02 --> V01
        SV01 -.steam block.-> ST01
    end
    subgraph SampleLine
        V01 --> SV03[Steam Block Valve 3]
        SV03 -->|trap| ST02[Steam Trap 2]
        SV03 --> SV04[Steam Block Valve 4]
        SV04 --> SC01[Sample Collection Bottle]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sterility Hierarchy Pyramid (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Operator & Environment Boundary]
    L1[L0 --> HVAC with HEPA Terminal]
    L2[L1 --> Room Pressure Cascade 15 Pa]
    L3[L2 --> Equipment Enclosure SIP]
    L4[L3 --> Process Stream Filtration 0.2 micron]
    L5[L4 --> Final Fermenter Sterility]
    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Filtration vs Heat

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Stream] --> Q1{Is the stream heat-sensitive?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the stream a liquid?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> F1[Use 0.2 micron Membrane Filter]
    Q2 -- No --> F2[Use 0.2 micron Hydrophobic Cartridge for Gas]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is continuous production desired?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> H1[Use Continuous Plate Heat Exchanger]
    Q3 -- No --> H2[Use Batch In-situ SIP in Fermenter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> Mermaid renders natively inside Markdown for instant comprehension. The diagrams above accurately capture the **functional interconnection** of all sterility barriers — the exact logic KTU examiners expect students to draw in design-question answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**Define the term "Steam-In-Place (SIP)". List any four critical design requirements to ensure effective SIP of a fermenter vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Steam-In-Place (SIP) is a method of sterilising the *interior* of a process vessel, all connected piping, valves, and instruments *in their installed position*, using **saturated pure steam at 121 °C (1.03 bar g)** for a defined contact time (≥ 30 min), without disassembling the equipment.

**Four Critical Design Requirements:**

1. **Self-draining geometry** — all pipe runs must slope continuously (≥ 1:100) toward condensate removal points; no sagging or pockets.
2. **No dead legs** — branch length $L$ must satisfy $L/d_{branch} \le 2.5$ (ASME BPE).
3. **Complete air displacement** — vent valves at every high point, kept open until steam discharges in a steady, dry plume.
4. **Continuous condensate removal** — every low point fitted with a steam trap (inverted bucket or float-and-thermostatic) sized for ≥ 3 × normal condensate load.
5. *(Bonus, if asked)* **Steam quality** — saturated steam with **dryness fraction ≥ 0.95** and superheat ≤ 5 °C.
6. *(Bonus)* **Validated by $F_0 \ge 15\ min$** at the *coldest monitored point* in the vessel.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why are 0.2 µm hydrophobic PTFE cartridge filters preferred for sterilising compressed air supplied to a fermenter? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **0.2 µm pore size** is the validated absolute rating that retains the smallest known bacterium (*Brevundimonas diminuta*) with an LRV > 7 (i.e., 10⁷ reduction).
2. **Hydrophobic PTFE membrane** repels water droplets → prevents "filter wetting" which would otherwise allow bacteria-laden liquid to *pass through* (aqueous penetration failure mode).
3. **Dry operation** — the filter can be sterilised *in-situ* by passing pure steam at 121 °C without damage, allowing repeated reuse.
4. **High flow rate / low ΔP** — PTFE offers high permeability, reducing compressor duty.
5. **Compatible with SIP cycles** — resists thermal cycling and aggressive cleaning chemicals (NaOH, HNO₃, isopropanol).
6. **Integrity testable** — supports *in-situ* bubble-point test (≥ 3.0 bar for 0.2 µm PTFE in 60 % IPA).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

> **"Describe in detail the design considerations for maintaining sterility of process streams entering a fermenter. Your answer must cover (a) liquid medium sterilisation and (b) compressed air filtration, with relevant equations and diagrams."**

#### Part (a) — Liquid Medium Sterilisation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Design intent:** Destroy all viable microorganisms including heat-resistant spores in the nutrient medium before fermentation begins. (1 mark)

2. **Choice between batch and continuous sterilisation:**
   - **Batch (in-situ):** Simple, low capex, slower, may cause nutrient degradation (Maillard browning of sugars, vitamin loss). (1 mark)
   - **Continuous (using plate heat exchanger + holding coil):** Faster, gentler on nutrients, energy-efficient at scale. Preferred when $V_{batch} > 20\ m^3$. (1 mark)

3. **Survivor curve equation:**
   
   $$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$
   
   Explanation of $D$-value and $z$-value (1 mark), derivation of $F_0$ (1 mark).

4. **Design parameters:** Hold at $T = 121\ ^\circ C$ for $\ge 30$ min; ensure $F_0 \ge 15\ min$ at coldest probe; continuously remove condensate; maintain positive vessel pressure ($\ge 0.3\ bar\ g$) after sterilisation to prevent ingress. (1 mark)

5. **Validation:** Heat distribution (≥ 12 thermocouples, all $\ge 121\ ^\circ C$), biological challenge with *B. stearothermophilus* spores, media fill. (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — Compressed Air Sterilisation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Design intent:** Remove oil, water, particulates and microorganisms from intake air before it enters the fermenter. (1 mark)

2. **Multi-stage filtration train:** (block diagram — refer SECTION 4 diagram)
   - Stage 1: Coalescing pre-filter (5 µm) → bulk water removal.
   - Stage 2: Refrigerated dryer → dew point 2–10 °C.
   - Stage 3: Desiccant dryer → dew point $< -40\ ^\circ C$.
   - Stage 4: Activated carbon → oil vapour removal.
   - Stage 5: Sterile filter (0.2 µm PTFE) → absolute microbial retention. (2 marks)

3. **Filter selection criteria:** Operate at ≤ 50 % of rated flow; **ΔP ≤ 0.5 bar** at design flow; install upstream pressure gauge and downstream pressure gauge for plugging detection. (1 mark)

4. **Integrity testing:** Bubble point test (BP ≥ 50 psig for 0.2 µm), forward-flow test, water intrusion test for hydrophobic filters. (1 mark)

5. **Operational sterility safeguards:**
   - Steam-block valve downstream of filter.
   - Fermenter vented through 0.2 µm filter.
   - Pressure relief device vent line protected by 0.2 µm filter. (1 mark)

6. **Diagrammatic representation:** Train of 5 stages shown as a flow diagram. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **"Explain the various design features incorporated in a modern fermenter vessel to ensure maintenance of sterility throughout the equipment. Discuss the role of mechanical seals, magnetic couplings, sanitary valves, and sampling ports with appropriate justification."**

#### Part (a) — Vessel Internals & Materials (7 Marks)

1. **Material of construction:** **SS 316 L** (low-carbon austenitic stainless steel; C ≤ 0.03 %). Resistant to chloride-induced pitting; passes USP Class VI biocompatibility. (1 mark)
2. **Surface finish:** Internal surfaces **electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.4 µm**. Eliminates micro-crevices where spores can lodge and resist CIP/SIP. (1 mark)
3. **Geometry:**
   - Hemispherical or torispherical top/bottom for pressure rating.
   - Internal corners radius ≥ 6 mm (no sharp edges).
   - Bottom slope ≥ 1:50 (complete drainage). (1 mark)
4. **Baffles & internals:** Four flat baffles, width = $D/10$, removable for CIP. (0.5 mark)
5. **Jacket:** Dimple or half-pipe jacket for heating (SIP) and cooling. Welded, not gasketed. (0.5 mark)
6. **Instrumentation ports:** Standard sanitary Tri-Clamp connections for pH, DO, temperature, foam probes — all individually steam-sterilisable. (1 mark)
7. **Sight glass / light port:** Sanitary, fitted with wiper, sterilised in-place. (0.5 mark)
8. **Pressure relief:** Sanitary rupture disc + 0.2 µm vent filter on discharge. (0.5 mark)

#### Part (b) — Shaft Seals, Magnetic Couplings & Aseptic Valves (7 Marks)

1. **Mechanical seal (conventional):**
   - Single mechanical seal with **condensate flush** at pressure < fermenter head pressure.
   - Risk → seal lip wear, fugitive organism ingress.
   - Now considered obsolete for high-value cGMP fermenters. (1 mark)
2. **Double mechanical seal:** Barrier fluid (glycol/water) pressurised between two seals; barrier fluid monitored for sterility. Higher cost but provides > 99.9 % containment. (1 mark)
3. **Magnetic coupling (preferred):**
   - Inner and outer rotor separated by a thin **SS 316 L hermetic can** (thickness ≤ 1.5 mm).
   - No dynamic penetration of vessel wall.
   - Zero leakage path → **inherently sterile**.
   - Energy loss: 5–15 % over mechanical seal.
   - Used in all modern cGMP fermenters (Sartorius, Applikon, Eppendorf). (2 marks)
4. **Aseptic / sanitary valves:**
   - **Diaphragm valves** (only moving part is a flexible PTFE/EPDM diaphragm; no stem packing) — preferred for sterile duty. (0.5 mark)
   - **Steam-block (steam-locked) valves** — two diaphragm valves with continuous steam bleed between them; prevent back-flow from non-sterile side. (1 mark)
   - **Double-block-and-bleed** — used at fermenter–manifold interface; bleeds to atmosphere monitored for steam. (0.5 mark)
5. **Aseptic sampling ports:**
   - **Steam-locked sample valve** → continuous steam barrier between fermenter and sample line.
   - **Septum port** for needle sampling; septum replaced every batch.
   - **Sterile connect/disconnect** (CPC AseptiQuik) for sampling without laminar flow hood. (0.5 mark)
6. **Validation:** Each valve position bubble-point tested pre-SIP. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Writing "filter" without specifying 0.2 µm absolute + hydrophobic PTFE material.** Examiners deduct up to 1 mark if the rating and material are missing.
> 2. **Confusing SIP with autoclave.** SIP is *in-place* sterilisation of the assembled fermenter; autoclaving is for small removable parts.
> 3. **Ignoring the dead-leg rule.** Many students draw piping with long branches → no credit.
> 4. **Forgetting the off-gas vent filter** in sketches. Examiners explicitly check for a back-contamination guard at the vent.
> 5. **Writing equations without defining variables** ($D$, $z$, $F_0$). Each undefined variable costs half a mark.
> 6. **Forgetting steam traps** on condensate lines in SIP diagrams.
> 7. **Wrong units** — many students use °F or bar a instead of °C and bar g.
> 8. **Mechanical vs magnetic coupling** — calling them "the same" → 0/2 marks for the comparison question.
> 9. **Not justifying the need for electropolishing** (Ra ≤ 0.4 µm) — examiners want biofilm-prevention reasoning.
> 10. **Mixing up LRV, SAL and Del factor** — they are related but not interchangeable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Sterility Design**

- [ ] **Sterility = absolute absence of viable microorganisms** in vessel, streams, and headspace.
- [ ] **Four-tier hierarchy** → material design → active SIP/CIP → filtration → barrier valves.
- [ ] **Standard sterilisation condition:** 121 °C, 1.03 bar g, ≥ 30 min ($F_0 \ge 15$).
- [ ] **Survivor curve:** $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$ — first-order kinetics.
- [ ] **Del Factor (∇)** = $\ln(N_0/N)$ = $\tau \cdot 10^{(T-121)/z}$ for isothermal hold.
- [ ] **$z$-value** = 10 °C for typical bacterial spores.
- [ ] **D-value at 121 °C** for *G. stearothermophilus* ≈ 1.5 min; for *B. subtilis* ≈ 0.5–1.0 min.
- [ ] **Compressed air train:** Coalescer → Refrigerated dryer → Desiccant → Carbon → **0.2 µm PTFE** → Steam block → Fermenter.
- [ ] **Filter integrity:** Bubble-point ≥ 50 psig for 0.2 µm PTFE in 60 % IPA.
- [ ] **Vessel material:** SS 316 L, Ra ≤ 0.4 µm, electropolished, no dead legs.
- [ ] **Dead-leg rule:** $L/d \le 2.5$ (ASME BPE).
- [ ] **Self-draining:** Slope ≥ 1:100 toward steam traps.
- [ ] **Magnetic coupling > mechanical seal** for cGMP sterility.
- [ ] **Steam-block (steam-locked) valve** = two diaphragm valves with continuous steam between them.
- [ ] **Off-gas vent** must be protected by 0.2 µm hydrophobic filter.
- [ ] **Validation triad:** Heat distribution + Heat penetration ($F_0 \ge 15$) + Biological challenge.
- [ ] **Biological indicator:** *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* ATCC 7953 spores ($10^6$ count).
- [ ] **Steam quality:** dryness ≥ 0.95, superheat ≤ 5 °C.
- [ ] **CIP chemicals:** NaOH 1–2 % @ 80 °C, then HNO₃ 0.5–1 %, then hot DI rinse.
- [ ] **Probe design:** retractable housing for pH/DO — autoclaved replacement offline, hot-swappable.
- [ ] **Pressure cascade:** fermenter always ≥ 0.3 bar g positive to prevent air in-leakage.
- [ ] **SOP item:** Never open a sterile boundary (flange, probe port) without prior steam flush for ≥ 5 min.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the sterile air train:** **"C-R-D-C-S"** — Coalescer, Refrigerated dryer, Desiccant, Carbon, Sterile filter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Number of Pipes Used for Hairpin Structure: Thermal Design and Momentum Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Estimation of Number of Pipes Used for Hairpin Structure: Thermal Design and Momentum Transfer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Hairpin Heat Exchanger** (also called a **double-pipe hairpin exchanger** or **fin-fan hairpin**) is a heat transfer apparatus consisting of two concentric pipes bent into a U-shape, where the process (hot) fluid flows through the **inner tube** and the service (cold) fluid flows through the **annular space** between the two concentric pipes. The U-bend returns the flow, and multiple hairpins are stacked in parallel and/or series to achieve the required heat transfer area while inherently maintaining a **true counter-current flow** configuration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Process Plant Design*, the **Estimation of the Number of Pipes** for a hairpin structure is a coupled **Thermal Design (heat-transfer-driven sizing)** and **Momentum Transfer (pressure-drop-constrained sizing)** problem. The number of pipes is *not* chosen arbitrarily; it is the convergence point of two simultaneous engineering constraints — the **required heat transfer area** and the **allowable pressure drop budget**. Mathematically, the design target is to find the integer count $N_p$ of inner pipes (or equivalently $N_{hp} = N_p / 2$ hairpin assemblies) such that: $$N_p = \frac{A_{\text{required}}}{a_s \cdot L}$$ where $A_{\text{required}}$ comes from the **LMTD equation**, and the resulting velocity $v = \dot{m} / (N_p \cdot \rho \cdot A_c)$ must keep the **tube-side pressure drop** $\Delta P_t$ and **annulus-side pressure drop** $\Delta P_a$ **below the maximum permissible** $\Delta P_{\max}$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Yoga Mat Roll":** > Imagine you have two long, flat yoga mats. You sandwich a hot iron rod between them and roll the assembly into a tight spiral. The hot rod travels through the inner spiral, the cold fluid flows through the outer spiral in the *opposite* direction. The hairpin exchanger works exactly like this — two concentric pipes wound into U-bends. The **iron rod's heat** must pass through the inner-pipe wall into the annulus cold stream. To get enough heat transfer area without building a kilometer-long pipe, engineers stack **many U-bends (hairpins) in parallel**. The question *"How many pipes do we need?"* is essentially: *"How many U-bends must we stack to exchange the desired heat, while keeping the pumping cost (pressure drop) affordable?"* ## 1.3 Why the Hairpin Geometry Matters - It guarantees **true counter-current flow**, so the **LMTD correction factor $F_T = 1$** (no shell-side bypass streams to degrade temperature driving force, unlike TEMA shell-and-tube). - Each U-bend adds about **60°–120°** of additional frictional loss per bend, so the number of bends directly increases pumping cost. - The number of parallel pipes sets the **flow velocity** for a fixed total mass flow rate — too few pipes ⇒ high velocity ⇒ excessive $\Delta P$; too many pipes ⇒ low velocity ⇒ laminar flow ⇒ low $h$ and oversized area. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Inner pipe outer diameter $D_o$ | **0.75 – 2.0** | inch | | Inner pipe wall thickness | **BWG 16 to BWG 10** | — | | Annulus equivalent diameter $D_e$ | **0.5 – 1.5** | inch | | Effective tube length per pipe $L$ | **10 – 40** | ft (3 – 12 m) | | Allowable tube-side $\Delta P$ | **5 – 10** | psi (35 – 70 kPa) | | Allowable annulus-side $\Delta P$ | **5 – 10** | psi (35 – 70 kPa) | | Operating overall $U$ | **50 – 500** | Btu/(hr·ft²·°F) | | Return-bend loss factor $K_b$ | **1.5 – 2.0** | — | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Engineering Levers (Thermal vs. Momentum) The number of pipes in a hairpin structure sits at the **intersection of two engineering constraints**: **Lever 1 — Thermal Design (Heat Transfer Demand):** The first law of thermodynamics demands a certain heat duty $Q$. The second law requires a temperature driving force $\Delta T_{lm}$. Together they demand a heat transfer area $A$. The area is supplied by the **wetted surface** of the inner pipe: $$A = N_p \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L$$ The bigger the $N_p$ (more parallel pipes), the more area is available — but each pipe now carries less flow, lowering the heat transfer coefficient $h_i$. **Lever 2 — Momentum Transfer (Pumping Power Budget):** Every pipe, every U-bend, every sudden contraction and enlargement eats pressure. The process engineer is given a **maximum allowable $\Delta P$** by the plant's pumping system. If the calculated $\Delta P > \Delta P_{\max}$, the design must be revised — usually by **increasing $N_p$** (lower velocity) or **shortening $L$** (fewer pipes per hairpin). ## 2.2 Logical Steps to Estimate Number of Pipes 1. **Specify the problem**: hot stream mass flow $\dot{m}_h$, cold stream mass flow $\dot{m}_c$, inlet/outlet temperatures $T_{h,in}, T_{h,out}, T_{c,in}, T_{c,out}$, allowable pressure drops. 2. **Calculate heat duty** $Q$ from energy balance. 3. **Compute LMTD** $\Delta T_{lm}$ (counter-current for hairpin, $F_T = 1$). 4. **Estimate overall heat transfer coefficient** $U_{\text{est}}$ from service heuristic tables. 5. **Compute required area** $A_{\text{req}} = Q / (U_{\text{est}} \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$. 6. **Assume a trial value for the number of pipes** $N_p^{(0)}$ (or pipe length $L$). 7. **Calculate per-pipe mass flow** $\dot{m}_{pipe} = \dot{m}_{\text{total}} / N_p$. 8. **Calculate per-pipe velocity** $v = \dot{m}_{pipe} / (\rho \cdot A_c)$. 9. **Calculate Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ (use $D_i$ for tube, $D_e = D_{shell} - D_o$ for annulus). 10. **Calculate $h_i$ and $h_o$** using Sieder–Tate, Dittus–Boelter, or equivalent correlations. 11. **Recompute $U$** from $1/U = 1/h_i + (D_o \ln(D_o/D_i))/k_w + 1/h_o$. 12. **Recompute $A_{\text{req}}$** and hence a new $N_p$. 13. **Compute pressure drops** on both tube side and annulus side including return bends. 14. **Compare with allowable $\Delta P$**; iterate until convergence. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use the table below as the master reference for solving any hairpin pipe-estimation problem. **Bookmark this table — KTU board questions routinely require 4–6 of these equations in a single 14-mark problem.** | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units (Engineering) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat duty, mass flow, specific heat, temperature | Btu/hr, lb/hr, Btu/(lb·°F), °F | | 2 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD for counter-current hairpin ($F_T = 1$) | °F | | 3 | $A_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Required heat transfer area | ft² | | 4 | $N_p = \dfrac{A_{\text{req}}}{\pi D_o L}$ | Number of inner pipes | dimensionless | | 5 | $N_{hp} = N_p / 2$ | Number of hairpin (U-bend) assemblies | dimensionless | | 6 | $v = \dfrac{\dot{m}/N_p}{\rho \cdot (\pi D_i^2 / 4)}$ | Tube-side velocity per pipe | ft/s | | 7 | $Re_D = \dfrac{D_i v \rho}{\mu}$ | Tube-side Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 8 | $D_e = \dfrac{D_s^2 - D_o^2}{D_s + D_o} = D_s - D_o$ | Annulus equivalent diameter (concentric) | ft or in | | 9 | $Re_e = \dfrac{D_e v_a \rho_a}{\mu_a}$ | Annulus-side Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 10 | $Nu_D = 0.023 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ (turbulent, $Re > 10{,}000$) | Dittus–Boelter (heating fluid) | dimensionless | | 11 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | Btu/(hr·ft²·°F) | | 12 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall resistance (clean condition) | hr·ft²·°F/Btu | | 13 | $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c}$ | Friction loss (Fanning friction factor) | lbf/ft² | | 14 | $\Delta P_{b} = K_b \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c}$ | Return-bend loss per bend ($K_b \approx 1.5$) | lbf/ft² | | 15 | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_f + N_b \Delta P_b$ | Total tube-side pressure drop | lbf/ft² | | 16 | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ (laminar, $Re < 2100$) | Fanning friction factor (laminar) | dimensionless | | 17 | $f = 0.046 \, Re^{-0.2}$ (turbulent, smooth pipe) | Blasius approximation (turbulent) | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall Alert:** When writing the LMTD formula in your exam answer, always verify that $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ correspond to the **same two stream ends** (counter-current pairing). Hairpin exchangers operate in **true counter-current**, so $F_T = 1$. Students frequently incorrectly apply the $F_T$ correction factor from the TEMA tables, which is irrelevant here. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Refinery crude pre-heat trains**: hairpins are used for viscous duty with high $L/D$ ratios. - **Cryogenic LNG vaporizers**: hairpin geometry handles the very high $U$ values for gas–liquid boiling. - **Pharmaceutical batch reactors**: where small area and clean-in-place (CIP) requirements favour double-pipe units. - **Pilot plants**: because hairpins can be expanded one hairpin at a time without re-tagging the entire exchanger. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Required Number of Pipes from First Principles ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Energy Balance For a hairpin exchanger with hot fluid on the tube side and cold fluid in the annulus (no phase change, no heat loss): $$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \, \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c$$ The mean temperature driving force for counter-current flow: $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$. ### 3.1.2 Rate Equation (Newton's Law of Cooling) $$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$ Solving for area: $$A_{\text{req}} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Geometric Constraint Each straight pipe contributes a wetted outer surface area of $\pi D_o L$ on the heat-transfer side. With $N_p$ pipes: $$A_{\text{available}} = N_p \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L$$ Setting $A_{\text{available}} = A_{\text{req}}$: $$N_p = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L}$$ This is the **fundamental sizing equation** for the inner pipe count. ## 3.2 Derivation of Tube-Side Momentum Balance For a single straight pipe of length $L$, mass flow $\dot{m}_{pipe}$, density $\rho$, and viscosity $\mu$, the Reynolds number is: $$Re_D = \frac{4 \dot{m}_{pipe}}{\pi D_i \mu} = \frac{\rho v D_i}{\mu}$$ The **Darcy–Weisbach** equation in engineering units: $$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L}{D_i} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c}$$ where $g_c = 32.174 \, \text{lbm·ft/(lbf·s²)}$ in English engineering units. Each return bend adds a minor loss: $$\Delta P_b = K_b \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c}, \quad K_b \approx 1.5 \text{ (standard hairpin 180° return)}$$ For a hairpin with $N_{hp}$ assemblies in series (each contributing one 180° bend), the total tube-side drop is: $$\Delta P_{t,\text{total}} = N_{hp} \left[ \frac{4 f (2L)}{D_i} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c} + K_b \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c} \right]$$ Note that the factor of $2L$ accounts for the **two straight legs** of each U-bend. ## 3.3 Derivation of Annulus-Side (Shell-Side) Momentum Balance For concentric annular flow: $$D_e = D_s - D_o \quad \text{(equivalent diameter for concentric annulus)}$$ The cross-sectional flow area: $$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_s^2 - D_o^2)$$ The velocity in the annulus: $$v_a = \frac{\dot{m}_c / N_{hp}}{A_a \cdot \rho_c}$$ The friction factor follows the same Blasius-type correlation, with $D_e$ substituted for $D$: $$Re_e = \frac{D_e v_a \rho_c}{\mu_c}, \quad f_a = 0.046 \, Re_e^{-0.2}$$ The total annulus-side pressure drop: $$\Delta P_{a,\text{total}} = N_{hp} \left[ \frac{4 f_a (2L)}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho_c v_a^2}{2 g_c} + K_{b,a} \cdot \frac{\rho_c v_a^2}{2 g_c} \right]$$ with $K_{b,a} \approx 1.5$ for the outer return bend. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU University Exam — May 2024 pattern):** > A hairpin heat exchanger cools $30{,}000 \, \text{lb/hr}$ of an oil from $250^\circ\text{F}$ to $150^\circ\text{F}$ using water entering at $90^\circ\text{F}$ and exiting at $120^\circ\text{F}$. Oil $C_p = 0.5 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, water $C_p = 1.0 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, oil density $\rho_o = 55 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, water density $\rho_w = 62.4 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, oil viscosity $\mu_o = 4.5 \, \text{lb/(ft·hr)}$, water viscosity $\mu_w = 1.8 \, \text{lb/(ft·hr)}$. Pipe inner diameter $D_i = 1.049 \, \text{in}$, outer diameter $D_o = 1.315 \, \text{in}$, shell ID $D_s = 2.067 \, \text{in}$, effective pipe length $L = 20 \, \text{ft}$, $k_w = 26 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft·°F)}$ (carbon steel), allowable $\Delta P_{\max} = 10 \, \text{psi}$ on each side, $g_c = 32.174 \, \text{lbm·ft/(lbf·s²)}$. Estimate the number of pipes. ### 3.4.1 Step 1 — Heat Duty $$Q = 30{,}000 \times 0.5 \times (250 - 150) = 1.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Btu/hr}$$ ### 3.4.2 Step 2 — Required Water Flow $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{Q}{C_{p,w} \Delta T_w} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times (120 - 90)} = 50{,}000 \, \text{lb/hr}$$ ### 3.4.3 Step 3 — LMTD $$\Delta T_1 = 250 - 120 = 130^\circ\text{F}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 150 - 90 = 60^\circ\text{F}$$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{130 - 60}{\ln(130/60)} = \frac{70}{\ln(2.1667)} = \frac{70}{0.7732} = 90.53^\circ\text{F}$$ ### 3.4.4 Step 4 — Assume Overall $U$ and Required Area Assume (initial guess, hot oil / cooling water service): $$U_{\text{est}} = 90 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$$ $$A_{\text{req}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{90 \times 90.53} = 184.0 \, \text{ft}^2$$ ### 3.4.5 Step 5 — Number of Pipes Per-pipe surface (using $D_o = 1.315 \, \text{in} = 0.1096 \, \text{ft}$, $L = 20 \, \text{ft}$): $$a_s = \pi \times 0.1096 \times 20 = 6.886 \, \text{ft}^2/\text{pipe}$$ $$N_p = \frac{184.0}{6.886} = 26.7 \Rightarrow \text{round up to } N_p = 28$$ This gives $N_{hp} = 14$ hairpin assemblies. ### 3.4.6 Step 6 — Recompute Per-Pipe Oil Velocity $$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.049)^2 = 0.864 \, \text{in}^2 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{ft}^2$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{30{,}000}{28} = 1071.4 \, \text{lb/hr} = 0.2976 \, \text{lb/s}$$ $$v_t = \frac{0.2976}{55 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.9017 \, \text{ft/s}$$ ### 3.4.7 Step 7 — Tube-Side Reynolds Number Convert viscosity: $\mu_o = 4.5 \, \text{lb/(ft·hr)} = 4.5/3600 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{lb/(ft·s)}$ $$Re_t = \frac{55 \times 0.9017 \times (1.049/12)}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{55 \times 0.9017 \times 0.0874}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$Re_t \approx 3466$$ This is in the **transition region** — design conservatively as turbulent using Sieder–Tate. For pedagogical clarity, we proceed with Blasius (acceptable for $Re \sim 3500$): $$f = 0.046 \times (3466)^{-0.2} = 0.046 \times 0.1965 = 0.00904$$ ### 3.4.8 Step 8 — Tube-Side Pressure Drop $$\frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c} = \frac{55 \times (0.9017)^2}{2 \times 32.174} = \frac{44.71}{64.348} = 0.695 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ Friction loss (14 hairpins × 2 straight legs × 20 ft = 560 ft total): $$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 \times 0.00904 \times 560}{0.0874} \times 0.695 = 231.5 \times 0.695 = 161.0 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ Bend loss ($N_{hp} = 14$ bends): $$\Delta P_b = 14 \times 1.5 \times 0.695 = 14.6 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ $$\Delta P_{t,\text{total}} = 161.0 + 14.6 = 175.6 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2 = \frac{175.6}{144} = 1.22 \, \text{psi}$$ This is **well below** the allowable $10 \, \text{psi}$ — the design is momentum-transfer safe. ### 3.4.9 Step 9 — Recalculate $h_i$ and $h_o$ for New $U$ For oil (tube side, Sieder–Tate correlation, viscosity correction ignored for first iteration): $$Pr_o = \frac{C_p \mu}{k} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.078} \approx 8.0$$ $$Nu_t = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4} = 0.023 \times (3466)^{0.8} \times (8.0)^{0.4}$$ $$Nu_t = 0.023 \times 624.0 \times 2.297 = 32.97$$ $$h_i = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D_i} = \frac{32.97 \times 0.078}{0.0874} = 29.4 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$$ For water in annulus — similar calculation yields $h_o \approx 750 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$ (typically very high for water). ### 3.4.10 Step 10 — New $U$ $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{29.4} + \frac{0.1096 \ln(0.1096/0.0874)}{26} + \frac{1}{750}$$ $$\frac{1}{U} = 0.0340 + \frac{0.1096 \times 0.2273}{26} + 0.00133$$ $$\frac{1}{U} = 0.0340 + 0.000958 + 0.00133 = 0.0363 \, \text{hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F/Btu}$$ $$U_{\text{new}} = 27.5 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$$ Since $U_{\text{new}}$ is **much lower** than the assumed $90$, the area requirement is now larger: $$A_{\text{req,new}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{27.5 \times 90.53} = 602 \, \text{ft}^2$$ ### 3.4.11 Step 11 — Iterate to Convergence $$N_p^{\text{new}} = \frac{602}{6.886} = 87.4 \Rightarrow N_p = 88, \, N_{hp} = 44$$ Re-running Step 6 with $N_p = 88$: $$\dot{m}_{\text{pipe}} = 30{,}000 / 88 = 340.9 \, \text{lb/hr}, \quad v_t = 0.287 \, \text{ft/s}, \quad Re_t \approx 1103$$ Now flow is **laminar**! This drastically lowers $h_i$. A second iteration is needed (use laminar correlation $Nu = 1.86 (Re \, Pr \, D/L)^{0.33}$). The design converges when $\Delta P$ constraints are satisfied simultaneously with the area constraint — typically after 3–5 iterations in practice. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convergence Strategy for KTU Board Exam:** > Show **two iterations minimum** in your answer script. State the convergence criterion explicitly: > $$\left\vert \frac{N_p^{(k+1)} - N_p^{(k)}}{N_p^{(k+1)}} \right\vert \times 100\% \le 2\%$$ > Most KTU evaluators give full marks only when convergence is demonstrated, not just an assumed value. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Correlation to Use? | Flow Regime | Tube Side | Annulus Side | Friction Factor $f$ | Heat Transfer Correlation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Laminar ($Re < 2100$) | $Nu = 1.86 (Re \, Pr \, D/L)^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Same, with $D_e$ | $f = 16/Re$ | Sieder–Tate | | Transition ($2100 < Re < 10{,}000$) | Use interpolation or turbulent correlation conservatively | Same | Blasius | Dittus–Boelter | | Turbulent ($Re > 10{,}000$) | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Same, with $D_e$ | Blasius / Colebrook | Dittus–Boelter | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Hairpin Pipe Estimation The following block diagram maps the **data flow** of the design calculation. Each block represents an engineering calculation step, and the arrows indicate how data cascades through the iteration. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Process Inputs:

Return to Step 6"] --> B6 ``` ## 4.2 Counter-Current Flow Schematic (Textual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR H_in["HOT INLET
T_h_in"] --> H_leg["Straight Leg 1
Length L
Flow direction: A to B"] H_leg --> H_bend["U-BEND 180 degrees
K_b loss approx 1.5"] H_bend --> H_leg2["Straight Leg 2
Length L

T_h_out"] C_in["COLD INLET
T_c_in"] --> C_leg["Annulus Leg 1

Flow direction: B to A"] C_leg2 --> C_out["COLD OUTLET
T_c_out"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this schematic:** Hot fluid travels **down** leg 1 (path A→B) inside the inner pipe, makes a 180° turn, and travels **up** leg 2 (path B→C). The cold fluid in the annulus travels **up** leg 2 (path B→C), turns at the U-bend, and travels **down** leg 1 (path A→B). At every axial location, the two fluids flow in **opposite directions** — this is true counter-current flow, so $F_T = 1$. ## 4.3 Annulus Geometry Cross-Section (Concentric Annulus) ``` +-----------------------------+ | OUTER SHELL | | ID = D_s = 2.067 in | | | | +---------------------+ | | | INNER PIPE | | | | OD = D_o | | | | ID = D_i | | | | | | | | Process fluid | | | | (TUBE SIDE) | | | | | | | +---------------------+ | | | | Annulus Service Fluid | | D_e = D_s - D_o | | | +-----------------------------+ ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **Q:** Why is the LMTD correction factor $F_T = 1$ for a hairpin heat exchanger? **Model Answer:** A hairpin heat exchanger operates in **true counter-current flow**. The hot fluid moves through the inner pipe while the cold fluid moves through the annulus in the **opposite axial direction** at every point along the exchanger length. There are no shell-side bypass streams (unlike TEMA shell-and-tube exchangers with multiple tube passes and shell passes). Because the geometry enforces strict counter-current flow end-to-end, the temperature driving force is never reduced by mixing or bypassing, so the LMTD correction factor $F_T$ takes its maximum value of unity. This is one of the principal design advantages of hairpin exchangers. ### Question A.2 **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Q:** Define the equivalent diameter $D_e$ for a concentric annular flow passage and state its physical significance. **Model Answer:** For a concentric annulus with outer pipe inner diameter $D_s$ (shell ID) and inner pipe outer diameter $D_o$, the **equivalent (hydraulic) diameter** is defined as: $$D_e = \frac{4 \times \text{cross-sectional flow area}}{\text{wetted perimeter}} = \frac{4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_s^2 - D_o^2)}{\pi (D_s + D_o)} = \frac{D_s^2 - D_o^2}{D_s + D_o} = D_s - D_o$$ Physically, $D_e$ is the diameter of a circular pipe that would carry the same flow with the same frictional resistance as the annulus. It is used to substitute for $D$ in all standard pipe-flow correlations (Reynolds number, Fanning friction factor, Nusselt number) when calculating pressure drop and heat transfer on the shell-side (annulus) of a hairpin exchanger. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design Problem **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`** A process plant requires a hairpin heat exchanger to condense and cool $25{,}000 \, \text{lb/hr}$ of an organic vapour from $300^\circ\text{F}$ to $120^\circ\text{F}$ using cooling water that enters at $80^\circ\text{F}$ and leaves at $110^\circ\text{F}$. Relevant data: - Vapour $C_p = 0.55 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, $\rho = 0.045 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, $\mu = 0.018 \, \text{cP} = 5.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{lb/(ft·s)}$ - Water $C_p = 1.0 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, $\rho = 62.4 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \, \text{cP} = 6.72 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{lb/(ft·s)}$ - Pipe: $D_i = 0.870 \, \text{in}$, $D_o = 1.050 \, \text{in}$, shell ID $D_s = 1.900 \, \text{in}$, $L = 15 \, \text{ft}$ - Tube-side: vapour; Annulus-side: water - Allowable $\Delta P$ on each side: **$5 \, \text{psi}$** - Wall conductivity $k_w = 26 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft·°F)}$ - Return-bend loss factor $K_b = 1.5$ **Tasks:** **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the **number of hairpin pipes** required based on the heat transfer area, using an assumed overall $U = 75 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$. Show the complete LMTD and area calculation. **(b)** [7 marks] With the number of pipes from part (a), compute the **tube-side pressure drop** and the **annulus-side pressure drop**, and verify whether the design satisfies the allowable pressure-drop constraint. State your final design recommendation. --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Heat Duty, LMTD, Area, Number of Pipes **Step 1 — Heat duty:** $$Q = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T = 25{,}000 \times 0.55 \times (300 - 120) = 2.475 \times 10^6 \, \text{Btu/hr}$$ **Step 2 — Required water mass flow:** $$\dot{m}_w = \frac{Q}{C_{p,w} \Delta T_w} = \frac{2.475 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times (110 - 80)} = 82{,}500 \, \text{lb/hr}$$ **Step 3 — LMTD (counter-current):** $$\Delta T_1 = 300 - 110 = 190^\circ\text{F}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 120 - 80 = 40^\circ\text{F}$$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{190 - 40}{\ln(190/40)} = \frac{150}{\ln(4.75)} = \frac{150}{1.5581} = 96.27^\circ\text{F}$$ **Step 4 — Required area:** $$A_{\text{req}} = \frac{2.475 \times 10^6}{75 \times 96.27} = 342.7 \, \text{ft}^2$$ **Step 5 — Per-pipe surface:** $$D_o = 1.050 \, \text{in} = 0.0875 \, \text{ft}, \quad L = 15 \, \text{ft}$$ $$a_s = \pi \times 0.0875 \times 15 = 4.124 \, \text{ft}^2/\text{pipe}$$ **Step 6 — Number of pipes:** $$N_p = \frac{342.7}{4.124} = 83.1 \Rightarrow N_p = 84 \text{ pipes} \quad (N_{hp} = 42 \text{ hairpins})$$ #### Part (b) — Pressure Drops & Design Verification **Step 7 — Tube-side velocity per pipe:** $$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.870/12)^2 = 5.158 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{ft}^2$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{25{,}000}{84} = 297.6 \, \text{lb/hr} = 0.0827 \, \text{lb/s}$$ $$v_t = \frac{0.0827}{0.045 \times 5.158 \times 10^{-3}} = 356.2 \, \text{ft/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Velocity check:** $v_t = 356 \, \text{ft/s}$ is **unrealistically high** for a vapour in a hairpin. This signals either (i) the assumed $U$ was too optimistic, or (ii) we should re-examine the flow regime. KTU evaluators will deduct marks if you proceed without flagging this anomaly. **Step 8 — Tube-side Reynolds number:** Convert viscosity to consistent units: $\mu = 5.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{lb/(ft·s)}$ $$Re_t = \frac{0.045 \times 356.2 \times 0.0725}{5.4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.162}{5.4 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 21{,}520$$ Turbulent flow — Blasius applicable: $$f_t = 0.046 \times (21{,}520)^{-0.2} = 0.046 \times 0.0894 = 0.00411$$ **Step 9 — Tube-side $\Delta P$:** $$\frac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c} = \frac{0.045 \times (356.2)^2}{2 \times 32.174} = \frac{5710.5}{64.348} = 88.74 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ Total straight length per pipe = $2L = 30 \, \text{ft}$ per hairpin. Total length across $N_{hp} = 42$ hairpins = $42 \times 30 = 1260 \, \text{ft}$. $$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 \times 0.00411 \times 1260}{0.0725} \times 88.74 = 285.7 \times 88.74 = 25{,}348 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ $$\Delta P_b = 42 \times 1.5 \times 88.74 = 5590 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$$ $$\Delta P_{t,\text{total}} = 25{,}348 + 5590 = 30{,}938 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2 = \frac{30{,}938}{144} = 214.8 \, \text{psi}$$ **This grossly exceeds the 5 psi limit.** Design must be revised. **Step 10 — Annulus-side $\Delta P$ (water):** Per-hairpin water flow: $\dot{m}_w / N_{hp} = 82{,}500 / 42 = 1964.3 \, \text{lb/hr}$ $D_e = D_s - D_o = (1.900 - 1.050)/12 = 0.0708 \, \text{ft}$ $A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}((1.900)^2 - (1.050)^2)/144 = \frac{\pi}{4}(3.610 - 1.1025)/144 = 0.01367 \, \text{ft}^2$ $v_a = (1964.3 / 3600) / (62.4 \times 0.01367) = 6.41 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{ft/s}$ $Re_a = \frac{62.4 \times 0.0641 \times 0.0708}{6.72 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 421$ — laminar $f_a = 16 / 421 = 0.0380$ $\frac{\rho v^2}{2g_c} = \frac{62.4 \times (0.0641)^2}{64.348} = 3.98 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$ $\Delta P_{a,\text{total}} = 42 \times [4 \times 0.0380 \times 30 / 0.0708 + 1.5] \times 3.98 \times 10^{-3}$ $\Delta P_{a,\text{total}} = 42 \times [64.4 + 1.5] \times 3.98 \times 10^{-3} = 42 \times 65.9 \times 3.98 \times 10^{-3} = 11.02 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2 = 0.077 \, \text{psi}$ Annulus-side drop is **negligible**. **Step 11 — Design Recommendation:** The tube-side pressure drop is unacceptably high. The vapour mass flow per pipe is too large. **Recommended action:** 1. **Reduce per-pipe flow** by increasing the number of parallel pipes. To bring $\Delta P_t$ below $5 \, \text{psi} = 720 \, \text{lbf/ft}^2$, the velocity must drop by a factor of $\sqrt{214.8 / 5} \approx 6.55$. Since $\Delta P \propto v^2 \propto (1/N_p)^2$, the new $N_p$ should be approximately $84 \times 6.55 = 550$ pipes. 2. With $N_p = 550$, the new area available is $550 \times 4.124 = 2268 \, \text{ft}^2$, far exceeding the $342.7 \, \text{ft}^2$ requirement — the exchanger would be thermally oversized. 3. **Better solution:** Either (a) **reduce $L$** to $5 \, \text{ft}$ and use $N_p \approx 84$ to satisfy momentum constraint, or (b) **switch to a different exchanger type** (e.g., shell-and-tube) since the high vapour volumetric flow makes hairpins uneconomical for this duty. **Final answer:** $N_p = 84$ **fails the momentum constraint.** The recommended design is $L = 5 \, \text{ft}$ with $N_p = 84$ (recheck) or a re-evaluation of exchanger type. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long-Answer Problem **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Alternative Module Choice)]`** A hairpin exchanger heats $20{,}000 \, \text{lb/hr}$ of a process liquid from $100^\circ\text{F}$ to $180^\circ\text{F}$ using an oil that enters at $300^\circ\text{F}$ and exits at $200^\circ\text{F}$. Pipe data: $D_i = 1.380 \, \text{in}$, $D_o = 1.660 \, \text{in}$, shell ID $D_s = 2.900 \, \text{in}$, $L = 25 \, \text{ft}$. Process liquid (tube side): $\rho = 65 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, $\mu = 1.5 \, \text{cP}$, $C_p = 0.45 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, $k = 0.10 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft·°F)}$. Oil (annulus): $\rho = 53 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$, $\mu = 12 \, \text{cP}$, $C_p = 0.52 \, \text{Btu/(lb·°F)}$, $k = 0.080 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft·°F)}$. Wall $k_w = 26 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft·°F)}$. Allowable $\Delta P = 8 \, \text{psi}$ each side. **(a)** [7 marks] Estimate the number of pipes required using $U_{\text{est}} = 70 \, \text{Btu/(hr·ft}^2\cdot^\circ\text{F)}$. **(b)** [7 marks] Recalculate $h_i$, $h_o$, and $U$, then iterate to a converged $N_p$. Check momentum constraints. > *(Model solution for Question B is structurally identical to Question A's solution method — follow the same 11-step framework using the new numerical values. Students are encouraged to write out the full solution as self-practice.)* ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the iteration step.** Writing only one trial value of $N_p$ without iterating leads to **–3 marks** in the 14-mark problem. > 2. **Using Fanning vs. Darcy confusion.** KTU exam key uses **Fanning friction factor** ($f$) with the $4fL/D$ form, **not** Darcy ($f_D$ with $f_D L/D$). Mixing these costs **–2 marks**. > 3. **Neglecting return-bend losses.** A frequent omission. Each U-bend contributes $K_b \approx 1.5$ velocity heads. Ignoring this under-predicts $\Delta P$ by 10–20%. > 4. **Forgetting the viscosity correction** $\left(\mu/\mu_w\right)^{0.14}$ in the Sieder–Tate correlation for liquids. The wall viscosity differs significantly from bulk for viscous oils. > 5. **Reporting a non-integer $N_p$.** Always round **up** to the next even integer (so that $N_{hp}$ is an integer). A non-physical $N_p = 83.1$ must be reported as $N_p = 84$. > 6. **Using $D_i$ instead of $D_o$ for the heat transfer area calculation.** The outer diameter is the wetted heat-transfer surface, not the bore. > 7. **Forgetting to state convergence criterion.** Always show the percentage change in $N_p$ between iterations. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hairpin Pipe Estimation** - **Hairpin geometry** = two concentric pipes bent in U-shape ⇒ **true counter-current** ⇒ $F_T = 1$. - **Fundamental sizing equation:** $N_p = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot \pi D_o L)$. - **Per-pipe area** uses $D_o$ (outer), not $D_i$. - **Equivalent diameter** for annulus: $D_e = D_s - D_o$. - **Reynolds number** uses $D_i$ (tube) or $D_e$ (annulus). - **Pressure drop** uses **Fanning** $f$ with the $4fL/D$ form in English engineering units. - **Return-bend loss** $= K_b \cdot \rho v^2 / (2 g_c)$ per bend with $K_b \approx 1.5$. - **Iteration is mandatory** — show at least 2 iterations with convergence check $\le 2\%$. - **Round up** $N_p$ to the next **even integer** so $N_{hp}$ is whole. - **Allowable $\Delta P$** is the deciding constraint — if violated, **shorten $L$** or **increase $N_p$** or **change exchanger type**. - **Dittus–Boelter** ($Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$) for turbulent flow; **Sieder–Tate** with $\left(\mu/\mu_w\right)^{0.14}$ for laminar liquid flow. - **Overall resistance** series: $1/U = 1/h_i + (D_o \ln(D_o/D_i))/k_w + 1/h_o$. - **Annulus-side pressure drop** is typically much smaller than tube-side (water has low viscosity and high density). - **Vapour services** are often momentum-transfer limited; **liquid services** are often area-limited.

topics.title: Estimation of Number of Tubes and Shells for Different Shell and Tube Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Number of Tubes and Shells for Different Shell and Tube Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is the most widely used process equipment for transferring heat between two fluid streams across an industrial plant. The estimation of the number of tubes ($N_t$) and the number of shells ($N_s$) constitutes the **mechanical sizing phase** of STHE design, which translates the calculated heat duty ($Q$) into a physically realizable geometry of standard tubes arranged inside pressure vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:**
> The **number of tubes** is the total count of heat transfer elements required to provide sufficient surface area ($A$) to satisfy the energy balance at a chosen overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$). The **number of shells** represents the count of separate pressure vessels required when the corrected log-mean temperature difference ($\Delta T_{lm} \times F_T$) falls below the available driving force in a single-shell unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Drinking Straw Bundle in a Pipe"

Imagine trying to warm up a swimming pool using a single garden hose. It would take days. Now imagine replacing that single hose with a bundle of **1000 thin drinking straws**, all packed inside a larger pipe (the shell). Cold pool water flows through the straws while hot water flows around them in the outer pipe. Heat crosses the thin straw walls — instantly the pool warms up.

- **Each straw** = one tube.
- **The bundle count** = Number of tubes ($N_t$).
- **The outer pipe** = Shell.
- **Need more straws than one pipe can hold?** = Add another identical pipe → another shell ($N_s$).

This analogy perfectly captures the engineer's challenge: *How many straws (tubes) do I need? And can I fit them all in one big pipe, or must I split them across multiple pipes (shells)?*

## 1.3 Classification Basis — TEMA Standards

The **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** classifies STHE into three-letter designations. Only the front letter (shell type) and rear letter (head type) are critical for tube/shell estimation:

> [!NOTE]
> **TEMA Shell Types used in KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - **E-Shell**: One pass on shell side; the most common academic choice. Simplest LMTD calculation.
> - **F-Shell**: Two-pass shell with longitudinal baffle; provides higher heat transfer coefficient.
> - **G-Shell**: Split-flow, used for low-pressure drop duties.
>
> **TEMA Head Types:**
> - **A-Head (Channel & Cover)**: Removable bundle — typical in process plants.
> - **B-Head (Bonnet)**: Fixed tubesheet, cheaper but bundle not removable.
> - **C-Head (Channel Integral)**: Used only for clean, non-fouling fluids.

For KTU Module 2 numericals, the dominant configuration is the **AES (A-Head, E-Shell, S-Tubesheet with fixed tubesheet)** or **AEM (A-Head, E-Shell, M-Tubesheet with floating head)** types.

## 1.4 Key Physical Inputs (Bold Constants & Metrics)

The estimation algorithm requires these engineering inputs:

- **Outside tube diameter ($d_o$)** in **mm** — typically **19.05 mm (¾")** or **25.4 mm (1")**.
- **Tube length ($L$)** in **m** — standard values are **2.44 m (8 ft)**, **3.66 m (12 ft)**, **4.88 m (16 ft)**, **6.10 m (20 ft)**.
- **Tube pitch ($p_t$)** in **mm** — minimum value is **1.25 × $d_o$**.
- **Heat duty ($Q$)** in **W** or **kW**.
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$)** in **W/m²·K** — values range from **250 to 1500 W/m²·K** for liquid-liquid services.
- **LMTD correction factor ($F_T$)** — dimensionless, **must lie between 0.75 and 1.0** for acceptable design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Workflow — Six Logical Steps

The estimation of tubes and shells proceeds in a strict sequential logic. Skipping any step leads to non-convergent design.

**Step 1 — Establish Heat Duty from Energy Balance**
The fundamental duty equation derives from the steady-state enthalpy balance between the hot stream losing heat and the cold stream gaining heat. Both sides must yield the identical $Q$ (sanity check during valuation).

**Step 2 — Select Tube Geometry ($d_o$ and $L$)**
TEMA and KTU Module 2 syllabus recommends selecting the **smallest practical $d_o$** (improves turbulence and $U$) and the **longest practical $L$** (reduces shell diameter and cost-per-area). Standard combinations:
- $d_o = 19.05$ mm, $L = 4.88$ m
- $d_o = 25.4$ mm, $L = 6.10$ m

**Step 3 — Assume Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)**
Since $U$ depends on the final tube-side and shell-side velocities (which depend on $N_t$ and $D_s$), this is an **iterative assumption**. KTU problems always pre-supply $U$ for the first iteration.

**Step 4 — Compute Log-Mean Temperature Difference ($\Delta T_{lm}$)**
The $\Delta T_{lm}$ is the true driving force assuming pure counter-current flow.

**Step 5 — Apply LMTD Correction Factor ($F_T$)**
