For multi-pass shell-and-tube units (1-shell, 2-pass; 2-shell, 4-pass etc.), the true mean temperature difference is $\Delta T_{lm} \times F_T$. The factor $F_T$ is read from TEMA charts using the dimensionless parameters $P$ and $R$.

**Step 6 — Calculate Total Surface Area, then Tube Count**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T}$$

$$N_t = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE 2024**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Heat Duty | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph} \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_c C_{pc} \Delta T_c$ | W | Energy balance check |
| 2 | Required Area | $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T)$ | m² | Corrected LMTD form |
| 3 | Number of Tubes | $N_t = A / (\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L)$ | — | Round up to integer |
| 4 | LMTD (Counter-current) | $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | K | End temperature differences |
| 5 | LMTD Correction Params | $P = (T_{2o} - T_{2i}) / (T_{1i} - T_{2i})$, $R = (T_{1i} - T_{1o}) / (T_{2o} - T_{2i})$ | — | Read $F_T$ from TEMA |
| 6 | Shell-Side Heat Balance | $\Delta T_{h,s} = Q / (\dot{m}_s \cdot C_{ps})$ | K | Verify stream outlet |
| 7 | Bundle Diameter | $D_b = d_o \cdot (N_t / K_1)^{1/n_1}$ | mm | $K_1, n_1$ from layout table |
| 8 | Shell Diameter | $D_s = D_b + (C_1 + C_2)$ | mm | Clearance terms |
| 9 | Number of Shells | $N_s = [F_T^{\text{desired}} / F_T^{\text{single}}]^{1/(\text{pass ratio})}$ | — | Iteration on $F_T$ |
| 10 | Tubes per Pass | $N_{tp} = N_t / N_p$ | — | $N_p$ = number of passes |

**Tube Layout Constants Table** (used in Bundle Diameter formula):

| Layout Pattern | $K_1$ | $n_1$ | Pitch $p_t$ |
|----------------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Triangular (30°) | 0.249 | 2.207 | $1.25 \, d_o$ |
| Square (90°) | 0.175 | 2.285 | $1.25 \, d_o$ |
| Rotated Square (45°) | 0.215 | 2.207 | $1.25 \, d_o$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The estimation of tubes and shells is the **gateway** to all downstream mechanical design activities: baffle spacing selection, nozzle sizing, pressure drop calculations, support bracket placement, and cost estimation. In oil refineries, a single **crude preheat train** may use **6 to 8 shells in series** with hundreds to thousands of tubes each. Underestimating $N_t$ leads to fouling-prone undersized units; overestimating $N_t$ inflates capital cost. Hence the accuracy of this estimation step directly governs the plant's **capital expenditure (CAPEX)** and **operating expenditure (OPEX)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The $F_T$ factor is not a "fudge factor" — it is a thermodynamic correction for non-ideal flow geometry. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student will reject a configuration where $F_T < 0.75$ (impractical) and re-design with more shells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number-of-Tubes Equation

We start from the **fundamental rate equation of heat transfer** (Newton's Law of Cooling applied to a tubular surface):

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

where $\Delta T_m$ is the true mean temperature difference. For a multi-pass shell, this becomes:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T$$

Solving for the required total heat transfer area:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T}$$

For $N_t$ tubes of outside diameter $d_o$ and length $L$, the total outside surface area available is:

$$A = N_t \cdot (\pi \cdot d_o \cdot L)$$

Equating the two expressions for $A$:

$$N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T}$$

Rearranging for the design variable $N_t$:

$$\boxed{N_t = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T}}$$

**Engineering Logic of Each Term:**
- $Q$ in numerator: more heat to transfer → more tubes needed (linear dependence).
- $U$ in denominator: cleaner fluids (higher $U$) → fewer tubes needed (inverse dependence).
- $d_o \cdot L$ in denominator: longer or larger tubes → fewer tubes needed (geometric economy).
- $F_T$ in denominator: poor correction factor (e.g., 0.6) → significantly more tubes required (factor of 1.67× penalty).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bundle Diameter (Shell Diameter) Equation

For a **triangular pitch** layout (most common in process plants), the bundle diameter $D_b$ is derived from geometric packing theory. The empirical TEMA correlation is:

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1}$$

**Derivation Logic:**

The number of tubes $N_t$ that fit inside a circular bundle of diameter $D_b$ scales with the cross-sectional area. Hence $N_t \propto D_b^2$. Inverting:

$$D_b^2 \propto N_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_b = (\text{constant}) \cdot N_t^{1/2}$$

Including the pitch ratio $p_t / d_o$, the empirical fit yields:

$$D_b = d_o \cdot \left(\frac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1}$$

where $K_1$ and $n_1$ are layout-specific constants tabulated earlier. The shell diameter adds a clearance term:

$$D_s = D_b + C_1 + C_2$$

where:
- $C_1$ = bundle-to-shell diametral clearance based on TEMA Tables (function of $D_b$ and shell-side fluid).
- $C_2$ = fixed pass-partition clearance for split-ring or pull-through floating heads.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Number of Shells Correction

When the calculated $F_T$ from the single-shell configuration falls below the practical threshold of **0.75**, the engineer increases the number of shells in series. The relation between $F_T$ and $N_s$ is:

$$F_T(N_s) = \frac{\left[\frac{(1 - R \cdot P)}{(1 - P)}\right] \cdot \frac{\ln M_1}{\ln M_2}}{\ln \left[\frac{1 + P - R}{1 - P}\right]}$$

where $M_1$ and $M_2$ are correction factors for the multi-shell case. A simplified working formula used at the KTU undergraduate level is:

$$N_s = \frac{\ln(1 - E_1) \cdot \text{denom}}{\ln(1 - E_{\text{new}})}$$

**Step-by-Step Iteration Algorithm:**

1. Calculate $F_T$ for one shell using TEMA chart with given $P, R$.
2. If $F_T \geq 0.75$: Design is acceptable → **$N_s = 1$**.
3. If $F_T < 0.75$: Calculate new effective $F_T$ for **$N_s = 2$ shells in series** (4 tube-passes).
4. Repeat until $F_T \geq 0.75$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Given (KTU Typical Problem):**
- Hot stream: oil cooling from $T_{1i} = 120°C$ to $T_{1o} = 60°C$; flow rate $\dot{m}_h = 5$ kg/s; $C_{ph} = 2000$ J/kg·K.
- Cold stream: water heating from $T_{2i} = 30°C$ to $T_{2o} = ?$
- $U = 500$ W/m²·K; $d_o = 25.4$ mm; $L = 4.88$ m; 1-2 STHE (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes).

**Solution Path:**

**Step 1 — Heat Duty:**
$\Delta T_h = 120 - 60 = 60°C$

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{ph} \cdot \Delta T_h = 5 \times 2000 \times 60 = 600{,}000 \text{ W} = 600 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Cold Stream Outlet (assumed reach):**
Assume water reaches $T_{2o} = 50°C$ (typical KTU trial).

**Step 3 — LMTD Calculation:**
End temperature differences:
$\Delta T_1 = T_{1i} - T_{2o} = 120 - 50 = 70°C$
$\Delta T_2 = T_{1o} - T_{2i} = 60 - 30 = 30°C$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{70 - 30}{\ln(70/30)} = \frac{40}{\ln(2.333)} = \frac{40}{0.8473} = 47.21°C$$

**Step 4 — TEMA Parameters for 1-2 STHE:**
$P = (50 - 30)/(120 - 30) = 20/90 = 0.2222$
$R = (120 - 60)/(50 - 30) = 60/20 = 3.0$

Reading TEMA chart for $P = 0.222$, $R = 3.0$, 1-shell-2-pass: $F_T \approx 0.88$.

**Step 5 — Required Area:**
$A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T) = 600{,}000 / (500 \times 47.21 \times 0.88)$

$$A = \frac{600{,}000}{20{,}773.6} = 28.88 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Number of Tubes:**
$A_{\text{per tube}} = \pi \times 0.0254 \times 4.88 = 0.3894$ m²/tube

$$N_t = \frac{28.88}{0.3894} = 74.16 \approx 76 \text{ tubes (rounded to even for 2-pass)}$$

**Step 7 — Bundle Diameter (Triangular pitch, $K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 2.207$):**
$$D_b = 25.4 \cdot \left(\frac{76}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207} = 25.4 \cdot (305.22)^{0.4530} = 25.4 \cdot 17.69 = 449.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Shell Diameter:**
For $D_b \approx 450$ mm, TEMA clearance $C_1 \approx 50$ mm, $C_2 \approx 0$ (split-ring assumed):

$$D_s = 449.4 + 50 + 0 = 499.4 \text{ mm} \approx 500 \text{ mm (standard)}$$

**Step 9 — Number of Shells:**
Since $F_T = 0.88 \geq 0.75$, the design is acceptable in a **single shell** ($N_s = 1$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Tube & Shell Estimation Workflow

The following Mermaid block maps the entire estimation algorithm as a **block-level functional architecture**. This is used in place of a physical drawing because the estimation procedure is fundamentally a sequential decision flow rather than a static geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Heat Duty Q from Energy Balance] --> B[Select Tube Geometry: d_o and L]
    B --> C[Assume Initial U from Service Table]
    C --> D[Compute Stream Outlet Temperatures]
    D --> E[Calculate LMTD using End Differences]
    E --> F[Determine P and R Parameters]
    F --> G[Read F_T from TEMA Chart for Selected Configuration]
    G --> H{F_T >= 0.75 ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Acceptable: Proceed to Single Shell Design]
    H -- No --> J[Increase Number of Shells N_s]
    J --> K[Recompute F_T for N_s shells in series]
    K --> L{New F_T >= 0.75 ?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Accept: N_s shells confirmed]
    L -- No --> J
    I --> N[Compute Required Area A = Q divided by U times DeltaTlm times F_T]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Number of Tubes N_t = A divided by pi times d_o times L]
    O --> P[Select Tube Layout Pattern: Triangular or Square]
    P --> Q[Compute Bundle Diameter D_b using K1 and n1 Constants]
    Q --> R[Add TEMA Clearances C1 and C2 to obtain D_s]
    R --> S[Round D_s to nearest Standard Shell Diameter]
    S --> T[Output: N_t, N_s, D_s, Layout Pattern]
    T --> U[End: Pass Sizing Data to Detailed Mechanical Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Physical Layout of Tube Bundle in Shell

This Mermaid schematic represents the spatial arrangement of tubes inside the shell, viewed from the front (tube-sheet face). It clarifies how tubes-per-pass, total tube count, and bundle diameter relate geometrically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Shell_Assembly[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger Cross Section]
        direction TB
        TS["Tubesheet - Left Face"]
        TE["Tubesheet - Right Face"]
        subgraph Inside_Shell[Inside Shell Volume]
            T1["Tube Row 1 - Hot Inlet Manifold"]
            T2["Tube Row 2 - Center Bundle"]
            T3["Tube Row 3 - Outlet Pass Partition"]
            BP["Baffles Cut 25 to 45 percent Diameter"]
            SH["Shell Side Flow Direction"]
        end
        TS --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> TE
        BP -.guides shell flow.-> SH
    end
    NozH["Hot Nozzle Inlet"] --> Shell_Assembly
    NozC["Cold Nozzle Outlet"] --> Shell_Assembly
```

## 4.3 Comparison Table — Layout Patterns (Visual Reference)

| Layout | Visual Sketch (ASCII) | Bundle Density | Cleaning Access |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Triangular (30°)** | `△ △ △` (offset rows) | **Highest** | Poor (hard to clean between tubes) |
| **Square (90°)** | `□ □ □` (aligned rows) | Lower | **Best** (mechanical cleaning possible) |
| **Rotated Square (45°)** | `◇ ◇ ◇` (rotated aligned rows) | Intermediate | Better than triangular |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the shell-side fluid is a **fouling liquid** (e.g., crude oil, cooling tower water), examiners expect the answer to use the **square pitch** layout. When the shell-side fluid is a **gas or clean vapor**, the **triangular pitch** is preferred.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define the term "Number of Shells" in a shell and tube heat exchanger and explain when multiple shells are required in series.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The number of shells ($N_s$) is the count of separate pressure vessel shells connected in series to handle a process heat duty when a single shell fails to provide an acceptable LMTD correction factor. Multiple shells are required when the calculated $F_T$ for a single-shell configuration falls below the practical limit of **0.75** (meaning the effective temperature driving force would be too low for feasible heat transfer). For each additional shell in series, the correction factor improves because the flow approaches true counter-current behavior. KTU and TEMA standards recommend $F_T \geq 0.75$; otherwise, the design is iterated by adding shells until this threshold is satisfied. Each new shell also multiplies the number of tube passes (e.g., 2 shells in series = 4 tube passes).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between triangular pitch and square pitch tube layouts in a STHE.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Triangular Pitch (30°) | Square Pitch (90°) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Tube density | **Highest** per unit bundle area | Lower by ~10–15% |
| Cleaning access | Poor (interstitial gap is narrow) | **Good** (straight lane between tubes) |
| Shell-side $h$ | Higher turbulence → higher $h$ | Lower turbulence |
| Typical service | Clean fluids, gases, vapors | Fouling liquids, slurries |
| Tube pitch | $p_t = 1.25 \, d_o$ minimum | $p_t = 1.25 \, d_o$ minimum |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $F_T$ correction** — loses **3 to 4 marks** instantly because the calculated $A$ becomes physically inconsistent.
> 2. **Confusing end-difference assignment** — KTU examiners specifically check $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$. Mixing them up gives wrong LMTD even if the formula is written correctly.
> 3. **Not rounding tube count to the next even number** when specifying tube passes — an odd tube count cannot be split equally between two passes.
> 4. **Using $d_i$ instead of $d_o$** in the area equation — the rate equation is based on outside area because $U$ is referenced to outside surface.
> 5. **Stating $N_s$ without checking $F_T \geq 0.75$** — examiners deduct marks for an "acceptable" design that fails the validity criterion.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question - 2024 Scheme Style]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for estimating the **number of tubes** in a shell and tube heat exchanger from basic heat transfer principles. List all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1-2 STHE (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) is to be designed to cool **15 kg/s** of an oil from **150°C to 80°C** using cooling water available at **30°C** and allowed to heat up to **50°C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **400 W/m²·K**. The exchanger uses tubes of **25.4 mm OD** and **4.88 m length** arranged on a **triangular pitch**. Determine:
- (i) The heat duty $Q$
- (ii) The LMTD
- (iii) The required number of tubes
- (iv) The bundle and shell diameters **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

The rate of heat transfer across the tube surface of an STHE is given by:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

where $\Delta T_m$ is the true mean temperature difference accounting for flow geometry. For a 1-2 STHE, this becomes:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T$$

The total outside heat transfer area provided by $N_t$ tubes of outside diameter $d_o$ and length $L$ is:

$$A = N_t \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation (no transient heat storage).
2. Constant $U$ throughout the exchanger (no spatial variation).
3. Negligible heat losses to surroundings (well-insulated shell).
4. Constant specific heats for both fluids over the temperature range.
5. No phase change on either side.
6. LMTD method is valid (no significant axial conduction in tubes).

Equating both area expressions and solving for $N_t$:

$$\boxed{N_t = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(i) Heat Duty:**
$\Delta T_h = 150 - 80 = 70°C$. Assuming $C_{ph} = 2200$ J/kg·K (typical for oil):

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{ph} \cdot \Delta T_h = 15 \times 2200 \times 70 = 2{,}310{,}000 \text{ W} = 2310 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) LMTD:**
End differences: $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 50 = 100°C$, $\Delta T_2 = 80 - 30 = 50°C$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} = \frac{50}{\ln(2)} = \frac{50}{0.6931} = 72.13°C$$

**TEMA parameters:**
$P = (50 - 30)/(150 - 30) = 20/120 = 0.1667$
$R = (150 - 80)/(50 - 30) = 70/20 = 3.5$

Reading TEMA chart for 1-2 STHE: $F_T \approx 0.92$ (acceptable, $> 0.75$). So $N_s = 1$.

**(iii) Number of Tubes:**
Required area:
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T} = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{400 \times 72.13 \times 0.92} = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{26{,}544} = 87.02 \text{ m}^2$$

Per-tube area:
$$A_{\text{tube}} = \pi \times 0.0254 \times 4.88 = 0.3894 \text{ m}^2$$

Tube count:
$$N_t = \frac{87.02}{0.3894} = 223.5 \approx 224 \text{ tubes (round to even for 2-pass)}$$

**(iv) Bundle and Shell Diameters (Triangular pitch):**
Using $K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 2.207$:

$$D_b = 25.4 \cdot \left(\frac{224}{0.249}\right)^{1/2.207} = 25.4 \cdot (899.6)^{0.4530} = 25.4 \cdot 26.93 = 683.9 \text{ mm}$$

TEMA clearance for $D_b \approx 684$ mm with pull-through floating head: $C_1 = 84$ mm, $C_2 = 0$ mm.

$$D_s = 683.9 + 84 + 0 = 767.9 \text{ mm} \approx 800 \text{ mm (nearest standard)}$$

**Final Design Output:**
$N_t = 224$ tubes, $N_s = 1$ shell, $D_s = 800$ mm, Triangular pitch 30°, 2 tube passes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **different tube layout patterns** (triangular, square, and rotated square) used in STHE bundles. Mention how each layout affects bundle diameter estimation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **2-4 STHE** (2 shells in series, 4 tube passes) handles a process duty of **$Q = 1.2$ MW**. The fluid pair gives $\Delta T_{lm} = 50°C$ and an overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 600$ W/m²·K. The TEMA correction factor for **single shell** at the same $P, R$ is **0.65** (unacceptable). Estimate the number of tubes required using **25.4 mm OD, 4.88 m long** tubes, the bundle diameter (square pitch), and state whether the proposed 2-shell design is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Tube Layouts (7 Marks)

The three standard TEMA tube layout patterns are:

**1. Triangular Pitch (30° Layout):**
Tubes in successive rows are offset by half a pitch. Provides the highest tube density. Formula constants: $K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 2.207$. Used for clean fluids. Highest shell-side heat transfer coefficient.

**2. Square Pitch (90° Layout):**
Tubes aligned in straight rows and columns along the bundle axes. Provides lanes between tubes for mechanical cleaning. Formula constants: $K_1 = 0.175$, $n_1 = 2.285$. Used for fouling fluids.

**3. Rotated Square Pitch (45° Layout):**
Tubes aligned as in square pitch but rotated 45° relative to shell axis. Intermediate density between triangular and square. Formula constants: $K_1 = 0.215$, $n_1 = 2.207$.

**Effect on Bundle Diameter:**
Smaller $K_1$ → larger $D_b$ for the same $N_t$. Hence square pitch requires larger shells than triangular pitch for identical tube count, increasing shell cost.

---

#### Part (b) — Multi-Shell Correction (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Required Area if $F_T$ were acceptable:**
For 2-shell design, $F_T$ improves. For 2-4 STHE, the correction factor at the same $P, R$ typically rises to $\approx 0.85$. Take $F_T = 0.85$.

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{600 \times 50 \times 0.85} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{25{,}500} = 47.06 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Number of Tubes:**
Per-tube area:
$$A_{\text{tube}} = \pi \times 0.0254 \times 4.88 = 0.3894 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_t = \frac{47.06}{0.3894} = 120.86 \approx 122 \text{ tubes (rounded even, 4-pass)}$$

**Step 3 — Bundle Diameter (Square pitch, $K_1 = 0.175$, $n_1 = 2.285$):**
$$D_b = 25.4 \cdot \left(\frac{122}{0.175}\right)^{1/2.285} = 25.4 \cdot (697.14)^{0.4376} = 25.4 \cdot 22.93 = 582.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Shell Diameter:**
With TEMA fixed tubesheet (B-head), $C_1 \approx 50$ mm, $C_2 \approx 0$ mm:
$$D_s = 582.5 + 50 + 0 = 632.5 \text{ mm} \approx 650 \text{ mm (standard)}$$

**Step 5 — Acceptability Check:**
$F_T(\text{new}) = 0.85 \geq 0.75$ ✓ → **The 2-shell design is acceptable**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024**

- ☐ **Master Equation:** $N_t = Q / (U \cdot \pi \cdot d_o \cdot L \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F_T)$
- ☐ **Always use $d_o$ (outside diameter)** in the area equation — never $d_i$.
- ☐ **$F_T$ threshold = 0.75** for acceptable single-shell design.
- ☐ **Triangular pitch** gives highest tube density ($K_1 = 0.249$, $n_1 = 2.207$).
- ☐ **Square pitch** gives best cleaning access ($K_1 = 0.175$, $n_1 = 2.285$).
- ☐ **Standard tube sizes**: 19.05 mm (¾"), 25.4 mm (1") OD.
- ☐ **Standard tube lengths**: 2.44 m, 3.66 m, 4.88 m, 6.10 m.
- ☐ **TEMA parameters**: $P = (T_{2o} - T_{2i})/(T_{1i} - T_{2i})$, $R = (T_{1i} - T_{1o})/(T_{2o} - T_{2i})$.
- ☐ **Bundle diameter**: $D_b = d_o \cdot (N_t/K_1)^{1/n_1}$.
- ☐ **Shell diameter**: $D_s = D_b + C_1 + C_2$ → round up to nearest standard.
- ☐ **Energy balance check**: $Q_{hot\ side} = Q_{cold\ side}$ (mandatory sanity check).
- ☐ **Tube passes**: Round $N_t$ to next even number for 2-pass, multiple of 4 for 4-pass.
- ☐ **$N_s$ interpretation**: If single-shell $F_T < 0.75$, iteratively add shells until $F_T \geq 0.75$.
- ☐ **Units discipline**: Convert $d_o$ from mm to m **before** computing area; convert $Q$ from kW to W if $U$ is in W/m²·K.
- ☐ **Clearance values**: Read from TEMA tables based on shell-side fluid service and bundle-to-shell ratio.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Drawing of Various Types of Evaporators Employed in Bioprocess Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design and Drawing of Various Types of Evaporators Employed in Bioprocess Operation ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Evaporator (KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **evaporator** is a unit operation equipment that employs heat transfer through a metal surface (calandria) to vaporize a volatile solvent (predominantly water) from a non-volatile solute, thereby concentrating a heat-sensitive bioproduct solution under controlled pressure and temperature conditions. In bioprocess operations, evaporators are specifically engineered to handle **bio-active, thermo-labile products** such as: - Enzymes (amylase, protease, pectinase) - Antibiotic broths (penicillin clarification concentrate) - Amino acid and vitamin concentrates - Fermented fruit juices and dairy derivatives - Yeast extract and single-cell protein (SCP) slurries > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** > The course outcome (CO2 / CO3) requires students to demonstrate the ability to draw P&ID-style schematics of evaporator configurations and justify equipment selection based on **residence time, viscosity, fouling tendency, and thermal degradation kinetics** of bioproducts. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine boiling water in a tea kettle versus boiling milk in a double boiler (bain-marie) versus boiling sugar syrup in a vacuum pan. - The **kettle** is open, hot, and uncontrolled → resembles an *open pan evaporator* (good for non-thermal products only). - The **double boiler** uses a barrier (steam jacket) to keep the product gentler → analogous to a *jacketed/calandria evaporator* (used for moderately viscous biological broths). - The **vacuum pan** operates at reduced pressure (lower temperature) → analogous to a *vacuum climbing-film or falling-film evaporator* (the standard for bioproducts because enzymes denature above ~50–60 °C). The bioproduct is the "milk"; it must not scorch, denature, or caramelize. Therefore, the engineer selects evaporator geometry and operating pressure such that the **wall temperature is just sufficient to drive evaporation** while **bulk liquid temperature remains low**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (BOLD):** > - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: **2257 kJ/kg** > - Latent heat at 50 °C: **2382 kJ/kg** > - Typical bioproduct target bulk temperature: **30–55 °C** > - Standard steam pressure for calandria: **0.3 MPa (gauge)** giving saturation temperature **≈ 143 °C** > - Standard vacuum in last effect of MEE: **0.05 bar (absolute)** > - **Steam Economy** (the KPI of evaporators): typically **0.8–0.9 for single-effect**, **2.5–3.5 for triple-effect MEE**, **up to 15–20 for MVR with 5-effect** ### 1.3 Classification Matrix (Overview) Evaporators are classified along three engineering axes: | Axis | Class A (Driving Force) | Class B (Flow Geometry) | Class C (Energy Integration) | |------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Natural Circulation | Rising Film / Climbing Film | Single Effect | | 2 | Forced Circulation | Falling Film | Multiple Effect (MEE) | | 3 | Mechanical Recompression | Agitated Thin Film (ATFE) | Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR) | | 4 | Centrifugal | Wiped Film | Thermal Vapor Recompression (TVR) | --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three Coupled Phenomena Every evaporator, regardless of geometry, simultaneously performs three rate processes: 1. **Heat Transfer** — from condensing steam (shell side) through the metal wall to the boiling liquid (tube side). 2. **Mass Transfer** — vapor bubbles nucleate at the wall, grow, detach, and leave the liquid surface. 3. **Momentum Transfer** — natural or forced circulation replenishes the wall with fresh sub-cooled liquor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the design must consider all three:** > In bioprocess evaporators, the liquor becomes increasingly viscous (e.g., enzyme concentrates can reach 200–500 cP). As viscosity rises, **Prandtl number increases**, the **thermal boundary layer thickens**, and the overall heat transfer coefficient (U) collapses. The designer must therefore switch from a *natural circulation* evaporator (e.g., rising film) to a *mechanical scraped/forced* evaporator (ATFE) as the concentration progresses. ### 2.2 Governing Heat Transfer Equation The rate of heat transferred in the evaporator is governed by: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where: - $Q$ = heat duty (W or kJ/h) - $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $A$ = effective heat transfer area (m²) - $\Delta T$ = log-mean temperature difference (K) between condensing steam and boiling liquor **Typical U values for bioprocess evaporators:** | Evaporator Type | U (W/m²·K) | Viscosity Range (cP) | |------------------|-------------|----------------------| | Rising Film (natural) | 1500–2500 | 1–100 | | Falling Film | 1200–3000 | 1–300 | | Forced Circulation | 2000–5000 | 50–2000 | | Agitated Thin Film (ATFE) | 800–2000 | 1000–100,000 | | Wiped Film | 1000–2500 | 500–50,000 | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat duty | W | | 2 | $Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat from steam | W | | 3 | $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_w$ | Heat to vaporize water | W | | 4 | $E = m_w / m_s$ | Steam Economy | — | | 5 | $F = L + V$ | Mass balance (total) | kg/h | | 6 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Mass balance (solute) | kg/h | | 7 | $V = F - L = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ | Water evaporated | kg/h | | 8 | $\Delta T_{total} = T_s - T_{v,last}$ | Available temperature drop | K | | 9 | $\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{\sum_i \left(\frac{1}{U_i A_i}\right) / \frac{1}{U_i A_i}}$ | Boiling-point elevation corrected allocation (for equal areas) | K | | 10 | $\sum \Delta T_i = (T_s - T_v) - \sum BPE - \sum (\text{hydrostatic head loss})$ | Effective driving force in MEE | K | | 11 | $\Delta P_{effect} = \Delta T_i \cdot \frac{\lambda}{T_{sat}^2 / (P \cdot v_{fg})}$ | Pressure drop (Clapeyron approximation) | Pa | | 12 | $t_{res} = V_{tube} / u_{bulk}$ | Residence time (Falling Film) | s | | 13 | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot u \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds no. for film | — | | 14 | $Nu = h \cdot d / k = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (for turbulent film) | — | | 15 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_c / T_h$ | Thermodynamic ceiling for MVR | — | | 16 | $\text{COP}_{MVR} = Q_{evap} / W_{comp}$ | Compressor performance | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** > Students frequently forget to subtract the **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** and **hydrostatic head loss** from $\Delta T_{total}$ when designing Multiple Effect Evaporators. This results in *over-rated* areas and actual steam consumption exceeding design. Always decompose: > $$\Delta T_{eff} = (T_{steam} - T_{v,last}) - \sum BPE - \sum \text{Raoult correction} - \sum \Delta T_{loss}$$ ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Bioprocess Industries | Bioproduct | Recommended Evaporator | Justification | |------------|--------------------------|----------------| | Fruit juice concentrate (orange, apple) | Multi-effect **Falling Film** (3–5 effects) | Low residence, low fouling, high economy | | Enzyme concentrates (pectinase, lactase) | **MVR + Falling Film** | Lowest temperature (~45 °C), preserves activity | | Antibiotic broth concentrate | **Forced Circulation** with external heat exchanger | Handles suspended solids, prevents fouling | | Whey protein concentrate | **Agitated Thin Film Evaporator (ATFE)** | Handles high viscosity at end-of-evaporation | | Ethanol fermentation broth | **Rising Film** distillation-coupled | Moderate viscosity, high volatility | | Yeast extract / SCP | **Falling Film + ATFE in series** | Two-stage: bulk water first, viscous finish last | --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Mass and Energy Balance for a Single-Effect Evaporator **Given:** - Feed flow rate $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h - Feed concentration $x_F = 0.10$ (mass fraction) - Product concentration $x_L = 0.50$ (mass fraction) - Steam at $T_s = 120$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2205$ kJ/kg - Feed enters at $T_F = 30$ °C, specific heat $c_p = 4.0$ kJ/(kg·K) - Boiling point of liquor $T_b = 105$ °C (BPE = 5 °C) - Vapor leaves at $T_v = 100$ °C, $\lambda_v = 2257$ kJ/kg **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance:** $$F = L + V$$ The input stream equals output liquor plus vapor. **Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance (solute is non-volatile):** $$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$ Substitute $L = F - V$: $$F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$$ **Step 3 — Solve for V (water evaporated):** $$V = F \cdot \frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$ Substituting numerical values: $$V = 10{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right) = 10{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.80 = 8{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$ This means **8,000 kg/h of water must be removed**. **Step 4 — Compute Outlet Liquor Flow L:** $$L = F - V = 10{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 5 — Energy Balance (steady state, no heat loss):** $$\underbrace{m_s \lambda_s}_{\text{steam condenses}} + \underbrace{F c_p T_F}_{\text{feed enthalpy}} = \underbrace{V \lambda_v}_{\text{vapor enthalpy}} + \underbrace{L c_p T_b}_{\text{liquor enthalpy}}$$ Rearranging for steam mass: $$m_s = \frac{V \lambda_v + L c_p T_b - F c_p T_F}{\lambda_s}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute numerical values:** Convert temperatures to absolute convenience: we keep in °C since $c_p$ is given per °C. $$m_s = \frac{(8000)(2257) + (2000)(4.0)(105) - (10000)(4.0)(30)}{2205}$$ Compute numerator piece by piece: - $(8000)(2257) = 18{,}056{,}000$ kJ/h - $(2000)(4.0)(105) = 840{,}000$ kJ/h - $(10000)(4.0)(30) = 1{,}200{,}000$ kJ/h $$m_s = \frac{18{,}056{,}000 + 840{,}000 - 1{,}200{,}000}{2205} = \frac{17{,}696{,}000}{2205}$$ $$m_s \approx 8{,}025.9 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 7 — Compute Steam Economy:** $$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{8000}{8025.9} \approx 0.997$$ This is consistent with a **single-effect evaporator** (economy < 1) — confirming why multiple-effect systems are used in bioprocess plants. **Step 8 — Compute Heat Transfer Area:** $$\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 120 - 105 = 15 \text{ °C (or K)}$$ Assume $U = 2000$ W/m²·K = $7200$ kJ/(m²·h·K) $$Q = m_s \lambda_s = (8025.9)(2205) = 17{,}697{,}109.5 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$A = \frac{Q}{U \Delta T} = \frac{17{,}697{,}109.5}{(7200)(15)} = \frac{17{,}697{,}109.5}{108{,}000}$$ $$A \approx 163.9 \text{ m}^2$$ **Interpretation:** A 164 m² calandria is required for this duty — typical commercial size for a fruit-juice concentration line. ### 3.2 Derivation: Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE) — Triple Effect For a **triple-effect evaporator** with backward feed and equal area assumption: **Step 1 — Effective Total Temperature Drop:** $$\Delta T_{total} = T_{s,\text{eff 1}} - T_{v,\text{eff 3}}$$ Let $T_s = 143$ °C (saturated steam at 0.4 MPa gauge), and last-effect vacuum of 0.1 bar giving $T_{v,3} = 45.8$ °C. Assume BPE per effect: 2 °C, hydrostatic head loss: 1 °C, boiling point drop in piping: 1 °C each effect. Total losses per effect: $2 + 1 + 1 = 4$ °C; for 3 effects: 12 °C. $$\Delta T_{eff} = 143 - 45.8 - 12 = 85.2 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2 — Equal Area Distribution:** For equal heat transfer area and approximately equal U across effects: $$\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{eff}}{n} = \frac{85.2}{3} = 28.4 \text{ °C per effect}$$ **Step 3 — Vapor Temperature in Each Effect:** - $T_{v,1} = T_s - \Delta T_1 = 143 - 28.4 = 114.6$ °C - $T_{v,2} = T_{v,1} - \Delta T_2 = 114.6 - 28.4 = 86.2$ °C - $T_{v,3} = T_{v,2} - \Delta T_3 = 86.2 - 28.4 = 57.8$ °C (Compare with target 45.8 °C — small mismatch indicates BPE must be slightly refined; iterative design is normal.) **Step 4 — Steam Economy (3-effect):** Theoretical maximum economy for $n$ effects: $$E_{n} \leq n \cdot (0.80 \text{ to } 0.95)$$ For triple effect: $$E_3 \approx 2.4 \text{ to } 2.85$$ This means **2.4–2.85 kg of water evaporated per kg of live steam** — a major operating cost saving for bioprocess plants running 24×7. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree (Textual) For a given bioproduct, the design selection follows this logic (visible in the Mermaid flowchart of Section 4): 1. If the product is **highly heat-sensitive** (enzyme activity lost > 5 % per 10 min at 60 °C) → **MVR + Falling Film**. 2. If the product is **viscous at low concentration** (e.g., pectin broth) → **Falling Film** (gravity film avoids pumping losses). 3. If the product has **suspended solids** (fermentation broth) → **Forced Circulation** with external heat exchanger to avoid fouling. 4. If the product becomes **highly viscous at end-concentration** (60–80 % solids) → **ATFE / Wiped Film** as a *finisher* downstream. 5. If the plant runs **large capacity (> 50 TPD water removal)** → **Multiple Effect** with 3–6 effects for steam economy. 6. If the plant runs **small capacity (< 10 TPD)** → **Single-effect or MVR** (capital cost dominates). ### 3.4 Analytical Calculation: ATFE for High-Viscosity Enzyme Concentrate **Given:** Final product viscosity $\mu = 5$ Pa·s at $x_L = 0.65$, rotor speed $N = 300$ rpm, rotor diameter $D = 0.4$ m, gap $\delta = 0.005$ m. **Step 1 — Shear Rate in the Gap:** $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{\delta} = \frac{\pi (0.4)(300/60)}{0.005} = \frac{6.283}{0.005} = 1256 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Reynolds Number for Film (using gap as characteristic length):** $$Re_{\delta} = \frac{\rho \dot{\gamma} \delta^2}{\mu}$$ Assuming $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³: $$Re_{\delta} = \frac{(1200)(1256)(0.005)^2}{5} = \frac{1200 \cdot 1256 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{5} = \frac{37.68}{5} = 7.54$$ This indicates **highly laminar flow** (typical for ATFE); use laminar film correlations. **Step 3 — Heat Transfer Coefficient (laminar film with agitation):** $$Nu_{\delta} \approx 0.018 \cdot Re_{\delta} \cdot Pr^{0.5}$$ With $Pr = c_p \mu / k$, take $c_p = 3500$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.5$ W/(m·K): $$Pr = \frac{(3500)(5)}{0.5} = 35{,}000$$ $$Nu_{\delta} = 0.018 \cdot 7.54 \cdot \sqrt{35{,}000} = 0.018 \cdot 7.54 \cdot 187.1 \approx 25.4$$ $$h = \frac{Nu_{\delta} \cdot k}{\delta} = \frac{25.4 \cdot 0.5}{0.005} = 2540 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$ This high $h$ (despite the laminar flow) is precisely why ATFE is used — the rotating blades continuously renew the film at the wall. --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Effect Evaporator (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank F, xF, TF] --> B[Feed Pump P1] B --> C[Pre-heater E0 using flash vapor] C --> D[Calandria / Heat Exchanger E1] D -- Steam In --> D D -- Condensate Out --> D D --> E[Vapor-Liquid Separator S1] E --> F[Condenser C1 with barometric leg] F --> G[Vacuum Pump VP1] E --> H[Product Outlet L, xL] E --> I[Vent to Vapor Space] ``` **Reading this schematic:** - The feed is pumped from the storage tank (A) via a centrifugal pump (B). - A pre-heater (C) recovers sensible heat from the flash vapor of the previous batch. - The calandria (D) is the heart — steam condenses on the shell side, transferring latent heat to the boiling liquor on the tube side. - The vapor-liquid separator (E) prevents entrainment of product droplets into the vapor stream. - The condenser (F) and vacuum pump (G) maintain sub-atmospheric pressure for low-temperature operation. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Triple-Effect Forward-Feed MEE ```mermaid flowchart LR F0[Feed F] --> P1[Pump 1] --> E1[Effect 1 Calandria] E1 -- Steam from Boiler --> E1 E1 --> S1[Separator 1] S1 -->|Vapor to E2 shell| E1 S1 -->|Liquid to P2| E2[Effect 2 Calandria] E2 --> S2[Separator 2] S2 -->|Vapor to E3 shell| E2 S2 -->|Liquid to P3| E3[Effect 3 Calandria] E3 --> S3[Separator 3] S3 -->|Vapor to Condenser| EC[Final Condenser] EC --> VP[Vacuum Pump] S3 --> LO[Concentrated Product L] ``` **Key Engineering Detail (KTU Board Expectation):** In **forward feed** (feed flows 1 → 2 → 3 in the same direction as vapor/heat), the most viscous concentrate leaves in **Effect 3** (the coldest). This is desirable for bioproducts because lower temperature + lower viscosity reduces fouling — but the temperature driving force in effect 3 is small, requiring larger area. In **backward feed** (feed enters Effect 3 and exits Effect 1), the concentrate leaves at the highest temperature, which is *undesirable* for thermolabile bioproducts unless an ATFE polisher is used downstream. ### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Flow — Evaporator Selection for Bioprocess ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Biostream to concentrate]) --> Q1{Is product
heat sensitive?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is stream
clean or fouling?} Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Multiple Effect Evaporator] Q2 -- Clean --> MVR[MVR + Falling Film] Q2 -- Fouling --> FC[Forced Circulation] Q3 --> Q4{Final viscosity
> 1000 cP?} Q4 -- Yes --> ATFE[Add ATFE as finisher] Q4 -- No --> MEE3[Triple Effect Falling Film] MVR --> Rec1[Recompress vapor
to use as steam] FC --> Rec1 MEE3 --> Rec2[Multi-effect
steam economy] ATFE --> Rec3[Mechanical scraping
renews film] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic of an Agitated Thin Film Evaporator (ATFE) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ATFE direction TB A1[Feed Inlet near top] --> R1[Rotor with hinged blades] R1 --> W1[Heated Jacket Steam In] R1 --> W2[Condensate Out] R1 --> D1[Concentrate Outlet at bottom] R1 --> V1[Vapor Outlet to condenser] end R1 -- scrapes film --> FILM[Thin film on inner wall] FILM -- Heat transfer --> W1 V1 --> COND[External condenser + vacuum] ``` **Reading the ATFE schematic:** - The rotor (R1) carries hinged blades that centrifugally press against the heated wall. - Feed is distributed as a thin film (~0.5–2 mm) on the inside wall. - The residence time is **5–30 seconds only** — ideal for thermolabile enzymes and antibiotics. - Vapor exits at the top, concentrate at the bottom. ### 4.5 Salient Features Matrix — Why Each Type Suits a Bioprocess | Evaporator Type | Residence Time | Heat-Sensitive? | Viscous Service? | Typical Bio Application | |------------------|----------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------------| | **Open Pan** | Hours | ❌ No | ❌ No | Salt, sugar only — *NOT used in bioprocess* | | **Jacketed Pan** | 30–60 min | ❌ Limited | ⚠ Limited | Vitamin broth preconcentration | | **Rising Film** | 30–90 s | ✅ Yes | ⚠ < 100 cP | Fruit juice, alcohol broth | | **Falling Film** | 20–60 s | ✅ Yes | ✅ < 300 cP | Enzyme, juice concentrate | | **Forced Circulation** | 1–5 min | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ < 2000 cP | Antibiotic broth, fermentation liquor | | **ATFE / Wiped Film** | 5–30 s | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Up to 100,000 cP | Final enzyme concentrate, amino acids | | **Multiple Effect** | Cumulative 1–10 min | ✅ Yes (vacuum) | ✅ Yes | All large-scale bioprocess (>10 TPD) | | **MVR** | < 30 s | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Yes | Premium enzyme/antibiotic plant | | **Centrifugal (Conical)** | < 1 s | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Yes | Heat-labile pharmaceuticals, vaccine excipients | --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]** **Q1.** Define the term **"Steam Economy"** of an evaporator. What is the typical steam economy of a single-effect and a triple-effect evaporator? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Steam Economy** $E$ is defined as the mass of water evaporated per unit mass of live steam consumed: > $$E = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$ > For a **single-effect** evaporator, $E \approx 0.8$–$0.95$ (theoretically < 1). For a **triple-effect** evaporator (Multiple Effect), $E \approx 2.4$–$2.85$. Higher steam economy directly reduces operating cost; bioprocess plants thus favor MEE or MVR configurations. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark numerical range with justification.)* --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]** **Q2.** Why are **falling film evaporators** preferred over **rising film evaporators** for concentrating thermolabile bioproducts such as enzyme solutions? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Falling film evaporators offer: > 1. **Lower residence time** (20–60 s) versus rising film (30–90 s) — reduces thermal degradation of enzymes. > 2. **Gravity-driven flow** — no flooding limit; can operate at very low pressure drop, enabling deeper vacuum and lower boiling temperature. > 3. **Better handling of viscous fluids** — film is mechanically stable up to ~300 cP versus ~100 cP for rising film. > 4. **No hydrostatic head at tube bottom** — entire tube operates near saturation, eliminating the suppressed boiling point at lower section that exists in rising film. > *(1 mark per valid reason, max 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below are TWO alternative questions, exactly matching the KTU 2024 scheme internal-choice structure. --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the working principle of a **Forced Circulation Evaporator**. Discuss its suitability for concentrating **antibiotic fermentation broth**. **(7 marks)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Working Principle (3 marks):** > A forced circulation evaporator uses a **recirculation pump** to push the liquor through the heat exchanger at high velocity (typically 2–6 m/s), even when the liquor is viscous or contains suspended solids. The heated liquor then flashes in a **vapor-liquid separator** (often at lower pressure than the heat exchanger). The pump prevents the boiling inside the tubes (which would cause scaling and tube vibration), ensuring **non-boiling heat transfer** with high U. > > **Schematic:** *(draw a vertical shell with: feed inlet → pump → tube-side heat exchanger (steam on shell) → flash separator → vapor to condenser → concentrated liquor recycle or discharge).* > > **Suitability for Antibiotic Broth (4 marks):** > 1. Fermentation broth contains mycelial solids, residual sugars, and antifoam — prone to **fouling**. Forced circulation's high velocity (≥ 3 m/s) provides **scouring action** that keeps tubes clean. > 2. Antibiotics (penicillin, cephalosporin) are **heat-labile** — the flash separator lets the liquor briefly reach low pressure, reducing bulk temperature to ~35–45 °C. > 3. The pump provides **independent control** of velocity and ΔT, allowing operation at optimum $Re$ for the broth's evolving viscosity. > 4. Continuous discharge of concentrate prevents **thermal hold-up** of the most-viscous fraction. **(b)** A single-effect evaporator is used to concentrate **10,000 kg/h of a fruit juice** from 12 % solids to 50 % solids. The feed enters at 30 °C, the steam temperature is 120 °C (latent heat 2205 kJ/kg), the boiling point of the concentrated juice is 105 °C, the specific heat is 4.0 kJ/(kg·K), and the latent heat of vapor is 2257 kJ/kg. Calculate **(i)** water evaporated per hour, **(ii)** steam required per hour, **(iii)** steam economy, and **(iv)** the heat transfer area if U = 2000 W/m²·K. **(7 marks)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h; $x_F = 0.12$; $x_L = 0.50$; $T_F = 30$ °C; $T_s = 120$ °C; $\lambda_s = 2205$ kJ/kg; $T_b = 105$ °C; $c_p = 4.0$ kJ/(kg·K); $\lambda_v = 2257$ kJ/kg; $U = 2000$ W/m²·K. > > **(i) Water evaporated (2 marks):** > $$V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.50}\right) = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.76 = 7{,}600 \text{ kg/h}$$ > > **(ii) Steam required (2 marks):** > $$L = F - V = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/h}$$ > $$m_s = \frac{V \lambda_v + L c_p T_b - F c_p T_F}{\lambda_s}$$ > $$m_s = \frac{(7600)(2257) + (2400)(4.0)(105) - (10000)(4.0)(30)}{2205}$$ > $$m_s = \frac{17{,}153{,}200 + 1{,}008{,}000 - 1{,}200{,}000}{2205} = \frac{16{,}961{,}200}{2205} \approx 7{,}692.2 \text{ kg/h}$$ > > **(iii) Steam economy (1 mark):** > $$E = \frac{V}{m_s} = \frac{7600}{7692.2} \approx 0.988$$ > > **(iv) Heat transfer area (2 marks):** > $$\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 120 - 105 = 15 \text{ °C (K)}$$ > Convert U: $2000$ W/m²·K $= 2000 \cdot 3.6 = 7200$ kJ/(m²·h·K) > $$A = \frac{V \lambda_v}{U \Delta T} = \frac{17{,}153{,}200}{7200 \cdot 15} = \frac{17{,}153{,}200}{108{,}000} \approx 158.8 \text{ m}^2$$ --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **(a)** Compare **Mechanical Vapor Recompression (MVR)** with **Thermal Vapor Recompression (TVR)** as applied to bioproduct concentration. **(7 marks)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > | Parameter | MVR | TVR | > |-----------|-----|-----| > | Driver | Mechanical blower/centrifugal compressor | Steam-jet ejector (no moving parts) | > | Energy input | Electricity (~0.05–0.10 kWh/kg water) | High-pressure live steam | > | Steam Economy | Up to 15–20 (with multiple effects) | 1.5–2.5 typical | > | Capital cost | High (compressor + driver) | Moderate | > | Operating cost | Very low (electricity cheaper than steam) | Depends on steam availability | > | Reliability | Requires compressor maintenance, surge control | Robust, no rotating parts | > | Best suited bioprocess | Heat-sensitive, premium products (enzymes, APIs) | Bulk products where steam is cheap (citrate, ethanol) | > | COP / η | 8–25 (vs Carnot ceiling ~7 at T = 50 °C) | 1.2–1.8 | > | **Conclusion** | Best for batch/continuous pharma-grade bioprocess | Best for large-tonnage low-margin fermentation products | > > *(4 marks for tabulated comparison; 3 marks for engineering judgment and selection rule.)* **(b)** A **Triple-Effect Falling Film Evaporator** is to concentrate an enzyme broth. The total effective temperature drop available across the three effects is 75 °C. Assuming equal heat transfer areas and equal overall coefficients, determine **(i)** the temperature drop in each effect, **(ii)** the steam economy range expected, and **(iii)** sketch the flow diagram identifying the location of the most-viscous concentrate. **(7 marks)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Solution (7 marks):** > > **(i) Temperature drop per effect (2 marks):** > $$\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{eff}}{n} = \frac{75}{3} = 25 \text{ °C per effect}$$ > > **(ii) Steam economy range (2 marks):** > Theoretical maximum economy $E_n \leq n$ for $n$ effects. With practical losses (BPE, temperature losses in piping): > $$E_3 \approx 2.4 \text{ to } 2.85$$ > > **(iii) Flow diagram and concentrate location (3 marks):** > For **forward feed** (feed flows from Effect 1 → Effect 2 → Effect 3 in same direction as vapor): > - Effect 1: 12 % solids → 18 % solids (hot, low viscosity) > - Effect 2: 18 % solids → 28 % solids > - Effect 3: 28 % solids → 50 % solids (cold, highest viscosity) > > The **most-viscous concentrate leaves Effect 3** (the coldest and last). For thermolabile enzymes this is the safest location because **bulk temperature is lowest there**. *(A clean labelled block diagram of forward-feed MEE is required for full marks.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Board):** > > 1. **Forgetting BPE correction.** Always subtract $\sum BPE$ from $\Delta T_{total}$. A 2–5 °C BPE per effect is typical for sugar/protein solutions. Students who omit it **lose 2–3 marks per problem**. > 2. **Unit conversion errors in $U$.** $U$ is commonly given in W/(m²·K), but $Q$ is computed in kJ/h. Convert: $1$ W/m²·K = $3.6$ kJ/(m²·h·K). Forgetting this causes the area to be **off by 3.6×**. > 3. **Confusing rising film with falling film.** Rising film has a hydrostatic head (boiling suppressed at bottom); falling film does not. This affects $\Delta T_{eff}$ and hence area. > 4. **Drawing missing labels.** KTU expects **every stream labelled** (composition, flow rate, temperature). A diagram without stream labels typically gets only **60 % of the marks allotted for the diagram**. > 5. **Not specifying vacuum or pressure.** Always indicate the operating pressure of each effect — for MVR and falling-film bioprocess units, **absolute pressure in last effect should be 0.05–0.15 bar**. > 6. **Omitting residence time.** KTU 2024 specifically tests residence-time awareness. State whether each design is short-residence (ATFE, falling film) or long-residence (forced circulation, open pan). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > > ✅ An evaporator performs **heat transfer + mass transfer + momentum transfer** simultaneously. > > ✅ The three governing equations are: $Q = U A \Delta T$, $E = V / m_s$, $V = F (1 - x_F/x_L)$. > > ✅ **Steam economy** is the principal economic KPI: ~0.9 (single), ~2.5 (triple), ~15 (MVR). > > ✅ **Falling film** is the workhorse for heat-sensitive bioproducts; **ATFE** is the finisher for viscous concentrates. > > ✅ **BPE (Boiling Point Elevation)** MUST be subtracted from total $\Delta T$ when designing MEE. > > ✅ **Forward feed** = concentrate exits in the coldest effect (preferred for thermolabile bioproducts). > > ✅ **Backward feed** = concentrate exits in the hottest effect (NOT preferred for enzymes; used when viscosity rises sharply and we want maximum $\Delta T$ at the viscous end). > > ✅ **MVR** is the most energy-efficient modern technology for bio-enzyme plants — compressor duty = $\approx 8$–$25$ kg water/kWh. > > ✅ For **antibiotic broth with suspended solids** → **Forced Circulation** is mandatory. > > ✅ Typical $U$ values (W/m²·K): rising film 1500–2500; falling film 1200–3000; forced circulation 2000–5000; ATFE 800–2000. > > ✅ Residence times (sec): falling film 20–60; rising film 30–90; ATFE 5–30; forced circulation 60–300. > > ✅ **Vacuum operation** lowers boiling point by ~10–30 °C, preserving enzyme activity. > > ✅ **Clausius-Clapeyron** relation governs vapor pressure vs temperature; design must respect the saturation curve. > > ✅ For KTU board diagrams: always label **streams with composition, flow, temperature, and pressure**. ---

topics.title: Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator: Estimation of Heat Transfer Area, Diameter of Calandria, Diameter and Height of Vapour Release Chamber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator: Estimation of Heat Transfer Area, Diameter of Calandria, Diameter and Height of Vapour Release Chamber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator (Calandria Evaporator / Short-Tube Evaporator):**
> A **natural circulation evaporator** in which the heating medium (typically steam) is confined within a horizontal bundle of vertical tubes (the *calandria* or *steam chest*) immersed in the boiling liquid. The tube length is short (1–2 m), the liquid level is maintained above the top tube sheet, and a large central *downtake* ensures recirculation of the unvaporized liquor. Vapour disengagement occurs in the upper **vapour release chamber** (also called the vapour head or separator).

The standard vertical tube evaporator is the most widely used evaporator in the **sugar, salt, caustic soda, paper, and food processing industries** because of its low cost, mechanical simplicity, robust operation, and ability to handle viscous and fouling liquors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kettle with Internal Steam Pipes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:**
> Imagine a large pressure cooker (the shell). Inside it, instead of a heating coil at the bottom, you install a horizontal drum pierced with hundreds of vertical drinking-straws (the *tubes*). You pump steam into this drum; the steam condenses inside the straws and heats the surrounding soup. The soup boils, bubbles rise into the lid region, and the vapour leaves through an outlet pipe at the top. The unevaporated soup falls down through a wide central chimney (the *downtake*) and re-enters the calandria — completing a natural convective loop driven purely by the density difference between the boiling two-phase mixture inside the tubes and the cold liquid in the downtake.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Tube outside diameter, $d_o$ | **25 – 75 mm** (commonly 38–50 mm) |
| Tube length, $L$ | **1.0 – 2.0 m** |
| Tube pitch ratio, $p/d_o$ | **1.25 – 1.5** (triangular pitch) |
| Steam-side pressure | **0.5 – 4.0 bar (gauge)** |
| Overall heat-transfer coefficient, $U$ | **1500 – 3500 W/(m²·K)** |
| Vapour velocity in release chamber, $v_v$ | **1 – 3 m/s** |
| Liquid residence time in chamber | **30 – 75 s** |
| Steam economy | **0.8 – 1.2 kg water/kg steam** |

## 1.4 NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Alignment:** This topic directly addresses **CO2 – *Design mechanical design of process equipment used in chemical process industries*** under the Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyse (L4)**. The student is expected to *apply* standard correlations and *analyse* the geometry of process plant components.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Three Sequential Sizing Problems

The complete sizing of a Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator proceeds in **three sequential stages**, each feeding data into the next:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 → Heat Transfer Area (A):** Determined from the *energy balance* (duty required) divided by the *available driving force* $(U \cdot \Delta T)$.
>
> **Stage 2 → Calandria Diameter $(D_c)$:** Determined from the *number of tubes* required to provide area A, arranged on a standard pitch.
>
> **Stage 3 → Vapour Release Chamber Diameter $(D_v)$ and Height $(H_v)$:** Determined from *vapour–liquid separation* requirements — permissible vapour velocity and liquid residence time.

## 2.2 Stage 1 — Heat Transfer Area (A)

The fundamental heat-transfer design equation (valid for any evaporator):

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_m}
$$

where:
- $A$ = heating surface area, **m²**
- $Q$ = total heat duty, **W** (or J/s)
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient, **W/(m²·K)**
- $\Delta T_m$ = mean temperature driving force, **K**

For a vertical-tube evaporator with steam condensing on one side and a *boiling* liquid on the other, $\Delta T_m = T_s - T_b$ (arithmetic mean is acceptable because the boiling point is essentially constant — the BPR correction is added to $T_b$, not to the LMTD):

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_s - T_b^{\,\text{true}} \;=\; T_s - \bigl(T_b^{\,\text{sat}} + \text{BPR} + \text{static head effect}\bigr)
$$

**Sources of $Q$:**

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s \qquad \text{(from steam side — preferred)}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_f \, C_{p,f}\, \bigl(T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}\bigr) \qquad \text{(from feed side — check)}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_v \, \lambda_v \qquad \text{(from vapour side — used in multiple-effect design)}
$$

## 2.3 Stage 2 — Calandria Diameter $(D_c)$

The number of tubes required to provide area $A$:

$$
N_t \;=\; \dfrac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L}
$$

The calandria diameter depends on the **tube layout geometry**:

**Equilateral Triangular Pitch (most common):**

$$
D_c \;=\; d_o \left(\dfrac{N_t \, \sqrt{3}\, (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}\right)^{1/2}
$$

**Square Pitch:**

$$
D_c \;=\; d_o \left(\dfrac{N_t\, (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}\right)^{1/2}
$$

Alternatively, the empirical **Hugmark / Kern correlation** (used for fixed-tube-sheets):

$$
D_c \;=\; d_o \, \left(\dfrac{N_t}{K_1}\right)^{1/n_1}
$$

For triangular pitch with $p/d_o = 1.25$, $K_1 = 0.319$, $n_1 = 2.142$.
For square pitch with $p/d_o = 1.25$, $K_1 = 0.215$, $n_1 = 2.207$.

The **downtake area** is sized equal to the total cross-sectional flow area of all tubes:

$$
A_{\text{downtake}} \;=\; N_t \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d_i^{\,2}
$$

Downtake diameter:

$$
D_{\text{downtake}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4\, A_{\text{downtake}}}{\pi}} \;=\; d_i \sqrt{N_t}
$$

## 2.4 Stage 3 — Vapour Release Chamber

### 2.4.1 Diameter $(D_v)$

Sized to limit the **riser vapour velocity** so that entrained droplets fall back:

$$
D_v \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, \dot{V}_v}{\pi \, v_v^{\text{allow}}}}
$$

where $v_v^{\text{allow}}$ is the **maximum permissible superficial vapour velocity**, typically chosen by the **Souders–Brown correlation**:

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; K_s \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}
$$

$K_s$ is the Souders–Brown coefficient. For evaporators with **0.5 m disengagement height**, $K_s \approx 0.045 \text{ m/s}$. For a more conservative design (3 m disengagement), $K_s$ can be raised to $0.076 \text{ m/s}$.

The vapour volumetric flow rate:

$$
\dot{V}_v \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\rho_v}
$$

### 2.4.2 Height $(H_v)$

$$
H_v \;=\; H_s + H_d + H_{ls}
$$

| Component | Description | Typical |
|---|---|---|
| $H_s$ | Liquid level above top tube sheet | **0.3 – 0.6 m** |
| $H_d$ | Disengagement height (vapour–liquid separation) | **0.6 – 1.5 m** |
| $H_{ls}$ | Liquid surge / residence volume (above $H_s$) | **0.3 – 0.5 m** |

Total $H_v \approx 1.2 - 2.5$ m in most industrial designs.

The liquid surge region provides a residence time $\tau$:

$$
\tau \;=\; \dfrac{V_L}{\dot{L}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_v^{\,2}\, H_{ls}}{\dot{L}}
$$

A typical target is $\tau = 30 - 75$ s to allow adequate foam collapse.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transfer area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \, \Delta T}$ | m² |
| 2 | Duty from steam | $Q = \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s$ | W |
| 3 | Duty from vapour | $Q = \dot{m}_v \, \lambda_v$ | W |
| 4 | Number of tubes | $N_t = \dfrac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L}$ | — |
| 5 | Calandria diameter (triangular) | $D_c = d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{N_t \sqrt{3}(p/d_o)^2}{\pi}}$ | m |
| 6 | Calandria diameter (square) | $D_c = d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{N_t (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}}$ | m |
| 7 | Downtake diameter | $D_{\text{downtake}} = d_i \sqrt{N_t}$ | m |
| 8 | Vapour volumetric flow | $\dot{V}_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\rho_v}$ | m³/s |
| 9 | Souders–Brown velocity | $v_v^{\text{allow}} = K_s \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Release chamber diameter | $D_v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{V}_v}{\pi \, v_v^{\text{allow}}}}$ | m |
| 11 | Release chamber height | $H_v = H_s + H_d + H_{ls}$ | m |
| 12 | Liquid residence time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_L}{\dot{L}}$ | s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The sizing relationships developed above are direct inputs to the **mechanical design** of the calandria shell, the **tube-sheet ligament stress analysis**, the **vapour-head saddle/support design**, and the **overall plant layout**. They are also the basis for **rating** existing evaporators — given $A$, $D_c$, and $D_v$, one can back-calculate the throughput the unit can handle at a given $\Delta T$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU Board Examination Standard)

> A single-effect **standard vertical tube evaporator** is to concentrate **20,000 kg/h** of a dilute caustic solution from **10 wt% to 30 wt% solids**. Saturated steam at **3.0 bar (g)** is available; the solution boils at **105 °C** (includes BPR). The overall heat-transfer coefficient is **U = 2000 W/(m²·K)**. Tubes of **50 mm OD, 1.6 m long** are arranged on a **triangular pitch with $p/d_o = 1.5$**. The vapour release chamber operates at **1.013 bar**, with $K_s = 0.045 \text{ m/s}$ for Souders–Brown. The vapour latent heat is **2250 kJ/kg** at the boiling temperature, and vapour density is **0.70 kg/m³**; liquid density is **1100 kg/m³**. Steam latent heat at 3 bar (g) is **2137 kJ/kg**.
>
> **Determine:**
> (a) Heat transfer area, number of tubes, and calandria diameter.
> (b) Diameter and height of the vapour release chamber.

---

## 3.2 Part (a) — Heat Transfer Area, $N_t$, $D_c$

### Step 1 — Mass balance to find water evaporated

Feed: $F = 20{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$, $x_F = 0.10$ → solids $= 2000 \text{ kg/h}$.
Product: $L = \dfrac{2000}{0.30} = 6666.67 \text{ kg/h}$.
Vapour: $V = F - L = 20{,}000 - 6666.67 = 13{,}333.33 \text{ kg/h} = 3.704 \text{ kg/s}$.

### Step 2 — Steam-side heat duty (preferred, since $\lambda_s$ is given)

Saturation temperature of steam at 3 bar (g) ≈ 3 bar (g) + 1.013 bar ≈ **4.013 bar (abs)** ≈ **143 °C**.

$$
\dot{m}_s \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_v \, \lambda_v}{\lambda_s} \;=\; \dfrac{3.704 \times 2250}{2137} \;=\; 3.900 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s \;=\; 3.900 \times 2137 \times 10^3 \;=\; 8.334 \times 10^6 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3 — Temperature driving force

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_s - T_b \;=\; 143 - 105 \;=\; 38 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 4 — Heat transfer area

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{U \, \Delta T} \;=\; \dfrac{8.334 \times 10^6}{2000 \times 38} \;=\; 109.66 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Number of tubes

$$
N_t \;=\; \dfrac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L} \;=\; \dfrac{109.66}{\pi \times 0.050 \times 1.6} \;=\; 436.2 \;\approx\; \mathbf{437 \text{ tubes}}
$$

### Step 6 — Calandria diameter (triangular pitch)

$$
D_c \;=\; d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{N_t \, \sqrt{3} \, (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D_c \;=\; 0.050 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{437 \times 1.7321 \times (1.5)^2}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D_c \;=\; 0.050 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{437 \times 1.7321 \times 2.25}{3.1416}}
$$

$$
D_c \;=\; 0.050 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1703.26}{3.1416}} \;=\; 0.050 \times \sqrt{542.13} \;=\; 0.050 \times 23.284
$$

$$
\boxed{D_c \;=\; 1.164 \text{ m}}
$$

Round up to nearest standard shell ID — typically **1.20 m** or **1.25 m**.

### Step 7 — Downtake diameter (assuming $d_i \approx 0.044 \text{ m}$ for 50 mm NB Schedule 40 tube)

$$
D_{\text{downtake}} \;=\; d_i \, \sqrt{N_t} \;=\; 0.044 \times \sqrt{437} \;=\; 0.044 \times 20.905 \;=\; 0.920 \text{ m}
$$

---

## 3.3 Part (b) — Diameter and Height of Vapour Release Chamber

### Step 8 — Vapour volumetric flow rate

$$
\dot{V}_v \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\rho_v} \;=\; \dfrac{3.704}{0.70} \;=\; 5.291 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 9 — Souders–Brown allowable velocity

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; K_s \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}
$$

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; 0.045 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1100 - 0.70}{0.70}} \;=\; 0.045 \times \sqrt{1570.43}
$$

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; 0.045 \times 39.629 \;=\; \mathbf{1.783 \text{ m/s}}
$$

### Step 10 — Chamber diameter

$$
D_v \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, \dot{V}_v}{\pi \, v_v^{\text{allow}}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 5.291}{\pi \times 1.783}}
$$

$$
D_v \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{21.164}{5.601}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.779}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_v \;=\; 1.944 \text{ m} \;\approx\; \mathbf{2.0 \text{ m}}}
$$

### Step 11 — Chamber height (component-wise)

Choose:
- $H_s$ (liquid level above top tube sheet) = **0.5 m**
- $H_d$ (disengagement height, sufficient for $v_v < 1.8 \text{ m/s}$) = **1.2 m**
- $H_{ls}$ (liquid surge for residence time $\tau = 60 \text{ s}$) — to be calculated

Check residence time with assumed $H_{ls} = 0.5$ m:

$$
V_L \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D_v^{\,2} \, H_{ls} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (2.0)^2 \times 0.5 \;=\; 1.571 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\dot{L} \;=\; \dfrac{F}{\rho_L} \;=\; \dfrac{20{,}000 / 3600}{1100} \;=\; 5.051 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\tau \;=\; \dfrac{1.571}{5.051 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 311 \text{ s}
$$

Since 311 s ≫ 75 s, we can reduce $H_{ls}$ to maintain $\tau \approx 60$ s:

$$
H_{ls} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau \, \dot{L}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_v^{\,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 5.051 \times 10^{-3}}{3.142} \;=\; 0.0965 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 0.10 \text{ m}
$$

Use $H_{ls} = 0.15$ m for design margin.

### Step 12 — Total height

$$
H_v \;=\; H_s + H_d + H_{ls} \;=\; 0.5 + 1.2 + 0.15 \;=\; \mathbf{1.85 \text{ m}}
$$

Round up to **2.0 m**.

---

## 3.4 Final Design Summary

| Parameter | Calculated | Final Design |
|---|:-:|:-:|
| Heat transfer area, $A$ | 109.66 m² | **110 m²** |
| Number of tubes, $N_t$ | 436.2 | **437** |
| Calandria diameter, $D_c$ | 1.164 m | **1.25 m (standard)** |
| Downtake diameter, $D_{\text{downtake}}$ | 0.920 m | **0.95 m** |
| Vapour chamber diameter, $D_v$ | 1.944 m | **2.00 m** |
| Vapour chamber height, $H_v$ | 1.85 m | **2.00 m** |

    S2 --> S3[Energy Balance - Compute Q from steam]

    S4 --> S5[Compute A from Q over U delta T]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Define a Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A standard vertical tube evaporator (calandria evaporator) is a natural-circulation evaporator in which steam condenses inside a horizontal bundle of short vertical tubes immersed in the boiling liquid. **Main components:** (1) Calandria shell with horizontal tube sheets, (2) vertical heating tubes (1–2 m long), (3) central downtake for natural circulation, (4) vapour release chamber (vapour head), (5) steam inlet and condensate outlet, (6) feed inlet and concentrate outlet, (7) vapour outlet. *(3 marks)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **What is the Souders–Brown correlation? Why is it used in sizing vapour release chambers?**

**Model Answer:**
The Souders–Brown correlation gives the maximum permissible superficial vapour velocity that prevents entrainment of liquid droplets in a vapour space: $v_v^{\text{allow}} = K_s \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_v)/\rho_v}$. **It is used** in sizing the vapour release chamber diameter because above this velocity, liquid droplets are carried over with the vapour, reducing separation efficiency and causing product loss. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Derive the expression for **calandria diameter** of a standard vertical tube evaporator for (i) triangular and (ii) square pitch tube layouts. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-effect evaporator is to concentrate **15,000 kg/h** of a solution from **8 wt% to 25 wt%**. Steam is supplied at **2.5 bar (g)**. The solution boiling point is **110 °C** including BPR. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is **1800 W/(m²·K)**. Latent heat of steam = **2160 kJ/kg**, latent heat of vapour = **2300 kJ/kg**. Tubes are **38 mm OD, 1.5 m long**, on triangular pitch with $p/d_o = 1.5$. Calculate the **heat transfer area, number of tubes, and calandria diameter**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation

**(i) Triangular pitch:** Consider a single tube of OD $d_o$. The unit cell occupied per tube on an equilateral-triangular pitch $p$ is an area $A_{\text{cell}} = (\sqrt{3}/2) \, p^2$. The number of tubes in a calandria of diameter $D_c$ is the calandria cross-sectional area divided by $A_{\text{cell}}$ (after subtracting tube-sheet area outside tubes):

$$
N_t \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D_c^{\,2}/4}{(\sqrt{3}/2)\, p^2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D_c^{\,2}}{2\sqrt{3}\, p^2}
$$

Solving for $D_c$:

$$
D_c^{\,2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, p^2 \, N_t}{\pi} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_c \;=\; p \sqrt{\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, N_t}{\pi}} \;=\; d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, N_t\, (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}}
$$

**(ii) Square pitch:** The unit cell area per tube is $A_{\text{cell}} = p^2$. Thus:

$$
N_t \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D_c^{\,2}/4}{p^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_c \;=\; d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{N_t\,(p/d_o)^2}{\pi}}
$$

*(7 marks for both derivations, with assumptions and diagrams.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical

**Step 1 — Mass balance:**
Solids in feed $= 0.08 \times 15{,}000 = 1200 \text{ kg/h}$. Product $L = 1200/0.25 = 4800 \text{ kg/h}$. Vapour $V = 15{,}000 - 4800 = 10{,}200 \text{ kg/h} = 2.833 \text{ kg/s}$.

**Step 2 — Steam consumption and heat duty:**
Steam temperature at 2.5 bar (g) ≈ 2.5 + 1.013 ≈ 3.513 bar (abs) ≈ **139 °C**.

$$
\dot{m}_s \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_v \, \lambda_v}{\lambda_s} \;=\; \dfrac{2.833 \times 2300}{2160} \;=\; 3.017 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s \;=\; 3.017 \times 2160 \times 10^3 \;=\; 6.516 \times 10^6 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Driving force:**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_s - T_b \;=\; 139 - 110 \;=\; 29 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer area:**

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{U \, \Delta T} \;=\; \dfrac{6.516 \times 10^6}{1800 \times 29} \;=\; \mathbf{124.83 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of tubes:**

$$
N_t \;=\; \dfrac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L} \;=\; \dfrac{124.83}{\pi \times 0.038 \times 1.5} \;=\; \dfrac{124.83}{0.17907} \;=\; \mathbf{697 \text{ tubes}}
$$

**Step 6 — Calandria diameter (triangular pitch, $p/d_o = 1.5$):**

$$
D_c \;=\; d_o \sqrt{\dfrac{N_t \, \sqrt{3} \, (p/d_o)^2}{\pi}} \;=\; 0.038 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{697 \times 1.7321 \times 2.25}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D_c \;=\; 0.038 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2715.79}{3.1416}} \;=\; 0.038 \times \sqrt{864.41} \;=\; 0.038 \times 29.40
$$

$$
\boxed{D_c \;=\; 1.117 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 1.20 \text{ m (standard)}}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 mass balance + 2 duty + 2 area/tubes + 1 calandria diameter.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the function of the **vapour release chamber** in a standard vertical tube evaporator. Derive the equation used to compute its **diameter** and **height**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vapour release chamber handles **2.5 kg/s** of water vapour at **100 °C and 1.013 bar**. Liquid density is **1000 kg/m³**, vapour density is **0.60 kg/m³**, $K_s = 0.045 \text{ m/s}$, and residence time is to be **60 s**. Liquid mass flow rate is **8.0 kg/s**. Calculate the **chamber diameter and total height**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Theory

**Function of vapour release chamber:**
1. Provides a low-velocity zone for **vapour–liquid disengagement** — separating vapour bubbles from entrained liquid.
2. Acts as a **liquid reservoir** above the calandria to ensure adequate static head over the top tube sheet for stable natural circulation.
3. Provides **residence time** for foamy liquors to allow foam collapse before the concentrate leaves.

**Diameter derivation:** Mass balance on vapour gives $\dot{m}_v = \rho_v \, \dot{V}_v = \rho_v \, (\pi/4) D_v^{\,2} \, v_v^{\text{allow}}$. Using Souders–Brown:

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; K_s \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_v}{\rho_v}}
$$

Solving:

$$
D_v \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, \dot{V}_v}{\pi \, v_v^{\text{allow}}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, \dot{m}_v}{\pi \, \rho_v \, v_v^{\text{allow}}}}
$$

**Height derivation:**

$$
H_v \;=\; H_s + H_d + H_{ls}
$$

where $H_s = 0.3 - 0.6 \text{ m}$ (static head over tubes), $H_d = 0.6 - 1.5 \text{ m}$ (disengagement height, function of Souders–Brown), and $H_{ls} = \tau \, \dot{L}/(\pi D_v^{\,2}/4)$ with $\dot{L} = \dot{m}_L / \rho_L$. Typical total $H_v = 1.2 - 2.5 \text{ m}$.

*(7 marks: 2 function + 2 D_v derivation + 2 H_v derivation + 1 numerical constants.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical

**Step 1 — Vapour volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{V}_v \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\rho_v} \;=\; \dfrac{2.5}{0.60} \;=\; 4.167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Souders–Brown velocity:**

$$
v_v^{\text{allow}} \;=\; 0.045 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1000 - 0.60}{0.60}} \;=\; 0.045 \times \sqrt{1665.67} \;=\; 0.045 \times 40.812 \;=\; 1.837 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Chamber diameter:**

$$
D_v \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 4.167}{\pi \times 1.837}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{16.667}{5.769}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.889} \;=\; \mathbf{1.70 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Liquid surge height for $\tau = 60$ s:**

$$
\dot{L} \;=\; \dfrac{8.0}{1000} \;=\; 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
H_{ls} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau \, \dot{L}}{(\pi/4) D_v^{\,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.7854 \times (1.70)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.480}{2.270} \;=\; 0.211 \text{ m}
$$

Take $H_{ls} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ for design margin.

**Step 5 — Total height:** Choose $H_s = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $H_d = 1.0 \text{ m}$.

$$
H_v \;=\; 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.25 \;=\; \mathbf{1.75 \text{ m}}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 V_v + 1 v_v_allow + 2 D_v + 2 H_ls + 1 H_v total.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (mark-losing traps):**
> 1. **Forgetting BPR/static head correction** in $\Delta T$. Always use $T_b^{\text{true}}$, not the atmospheric BPT.
> 2. **Using vapour latent heat** in $Q = \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s$ but **steam mass flow** must be computed first via $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_v \lambda_v / \lambda_s$. Mixing these is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Square vs. triangular pitch confusion** — examiners test whether you remembered the $\sqrt{3}$ factor.
> 4. **Downtake area** must equal the **total tube cross-sectional area**, not the same as the calandria diameter.
> 5. **Souders–Brown $K_s$ value** — must be stated explicitly with units; the chosen $K_s$ reflects the disengagement height assumed.
> 6. **No unit consistency check** — every quantity must be in SI before plugging into the formula; partial credit is lost on dimensional errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standard Vertical Tube Evaporator Sizing**
>
> ✅ Three-stage design: **(1) Area from duty, (2) Calandria from tube layout, (3) Vapour chamber from separation.**
>
> ✅ Master equation for area: $A = Q / (U \, \Delta T)$ where $\Delta T = T_{\text{steam}} - T_b^{\text{true}}$.
>
> ✅ Tubes: short (1–2 m), $d_o = 25-75 \text{ mm}$, pitch ratio $p/d_o = 1.25-1.5$.
>
> ✅ Number of tubes: $N_t = A / (\pi d_o L)$.
>
> ✅ Calandria diameter (triangular pitch): $D_c = d_o \sqrt{N_t \sqrt{3}(p/d_o)^2 / \pi}$.
>
> ✅ Calandria diameter (square pitch): $D_c = d_o \sqrt{N_t (p/d_o)^2 / \pi}$.
>
> ✅ Downtake diameter: $D_{\text{downtake}} = d_i \sqrt{N_t}$ (to match total tube flow area).
>
> ✅ Vapour release chamber diameter via Souders–Brown: $D_v = \sqrt{4 \dot{V}_v / (\pi v_v^{\text{allow}})}$.
>
> ✅ Souders–Brown velocity: $v_v^{\text{allow}} = K_s \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_v)/\rho_v}$, with $K_s = 0.045$ typical.
>
> ✅ Chamber height: $H_v = H_s + H_d + H_{ls}$, where $H_{ls}$ is fixed by residence time $\tau = V_L / \dot{L}$.
>
> ✅ Always round up to the nearest **standard pipe/shell size** (1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 2.0 m).
>
> ✅ Include **BPR + static head + hydrostatic head correction** in the effective boiling temperature.
>
> ✅ Always perform **mass balance first**, then energy balance, then geometric sizing — in that order.
>
> ✅ Final mechanical design must additionally check **tube-sheet ligament stress, shell thickness (UG-27 of ASME Sec VIII), saddle/support spacing, and nozzle reinforcement**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Design of Tall Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Design of Tall Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Tall Pressure Vessel** is a cylindrical pressure vessel in which the ratio of its tangent-to-tangent length (L) to the outside diameter (D₀) is large enough that **secondary stresses produced by external environmental loads** — primarily wind, seismic, and eccentric process loads — become **comparable in magnitude to the primary membrane stresses** generated by internal/external pressure. Per KTU 2024 syllabus (PCBBT602, Module 2), a vessel is generally classified as *tall* when:

$$\frac{L}{D_o} \;\; \geq \;\; 5 \quad \text{(and typically } L \geq 6 \text{ m, or } L \geq 10 D_o \text{ for slender columns)}$$

In such cases, the vessel must be designed as a **cantilever beam fixed at the base**, with the skirt (or lug) support treated as a structural column, and the shell must satisfy both **ASME Section VIII Div. 1 allowable stresses** *and* the additional **combined stress check** of:

$$\sigma_L \;\; \leq \;\; S \cdot E \quad \text{(circumferential joint efficiency)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Tall vessel design is **not** a simple extension of short-vessel design. It is governed by the **interaction of pressure-induced membrane stresses** with **wind/seismic-induced bending stresses**, and the resulting design is dominated by the **stability and overturning moment** of the vessel column rather than by the internal pressure alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bamboo Pole vs. Soda Can" Intuition

Imagine two cylinders made from the **same wall thickness and material**:

* **Soda can (Short Vessel, L/D ≈ 1.5):** Standing on a table, it happily holds carbonated liquid. The internal pressure tries to pop the ends off, and the shell pushes outward like a balloon — that is **hoop (circumferential) stress** and **longitudinal stress** working against an *almost* negligible bending influence. You can push it gently and it barely flexes.

* **Bamboo pole (Tall Vessel, L/D ≈ 20):** The same thin cylinder, now standing 30 m tall and holding the same pressure, behaves nothing like a soda can. A gust of wind **bends** it sideways. The base tries to **tip over** (overturning moment), the windward side gets **extra tension**, the leeward side gets **extra compression**, and any small imperfection can cause **buckling**.
