The bamboo bends visibly; the vessel shell will eventually **ovalize** or **buckl**e if not designed properly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Take-home:** In a **short vessel**, the *engineer* decides the wall thickness from **pressure**. In a **tall vessel**, the *environment* (wind, earthquake, support flexibility) decides the wall thickness — and the pressure may turn out to be only a *secondary* consideration. This shift in governing criteria is the heart of Module 2's tall-vessel design.

### 1.3 Standard Codes and Design Basis

| Code / Standard | Governing Use |
|---|---|
| **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1** | Primary pressure-vessel design code (UG-27 longitudinal stress, UG-54, UG-69) |
| **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 2** | Alternative rules, higher allowable stress with detailed analysis |
| **IS 2825** | Indian Standard for Unfired Pressure Vessels (analogous to ASME) |
| **IS 875 (Part 3)** | Indian code for **Wind Loads on Structures** (used in KTU problems) |
| **IS 1893 (Part 4)** | Indian code for **Seismic Loads on Industrial Structures** |
| **WRC 297 / 107** | Nozzle reinforcement, also for skirt-shell junction |
| **PD 5500** | British Standard for pressure vessels (occasionally referenced) |

### 1.4 Why Tall Vessels Demand Special Treatment — Physical Constants

* Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
* Air density (for wind pressure): **ρ = 1.226 kg/m³** at 15 °C, sea level
* Wind velocity pressure (IS 875, basic): **q_b = 0.6 V_b²** (N/m²), where V_b is in m/s
* Modulus of elasticity for carbon steel: **E ≈ 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**
* Typical yield strength (SA 516 Gr 70): **S_y = 260 N/mm²**
* Typical allowable stress (ASME, 1.0 S_m): **S = 138 N/mm² at 200 °C**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The governing design check for tall vessels** is the **combined longitudinal stress** (pressure + wind/seismic bending), which must remain within the code-allowed limits. Failure to do so is the **#1 reason** KTU students lose marks in Module 2 problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Loads Acting on a Tall Pressure Vessel

A tall pressure vessel, when supported on a **skirt** (the most common support for vertical tall vessels), experiences the following loads simultaneously:

1. **Internal / External Pressure (P):** Produces hoop stress σ_h and longitudinal membrane stress σ_Lp.
2. **Dead Weight (W):** Self-weight of vessel, internals (trays, packing), liquid hold-up, insulation, piping reaction, and skirt itself.
3. **Wind Load (F_w):** Horizontal distributed load due to wind pressure on the projected area.
4. **Seismic Load (F_e):** Horizontal inertial load during an earthquake.
5. **Nozzle / Piping Reactions:** Local loads at the top of the vessel from connected piping.
6. **Thermal Expansion / Contraction:** Becomes important at skirt-base junction.
7. **Vibration & Dynamic Loads (less common in basic KTU problems).**

### 2.2 The Two Stress Systems — Pressure vs. Bending

| Stress System | Origin | Magnitude | Where Critical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hoop (circumferential) stress** σ_h | Internal pressure P | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | Always; independent of height |
| **Longitudinal pressure stress** σ_Lp | Internal pressure P (end-cap effect) | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | Short vessels |
| **Wind/seismic bending stress** σ_b | Moment M = F·h̄ from horizontal loads | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 \, t}$ | Tall vessels |
| **Dead-weight compressive stress** σ_w | Weight W over cross-section | $\sigma_w = \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o \, t}$ | Always |
| **Combined longitudinal stress** σ_comb | Sum of σ_Lp, σ_b, σ_w | $\sigma_{comb} = \sigma_{Lp} \pm \sigma_b - \sigma_w$ | **Governs tall vessels** |

### 2.3 Why Short Vessels Need a Different Check than Tall Vessels

For short vessels (L/D < 5), the wind/seismic moment is small, and the wall thickness is determined entirely by the **internal pressure** (hoop or longitudinal, whichever is greater). The code check is:

$$t = \frac{PR}{SE - 0.6P} \quad \text{(ASME UG-27 for circumferential stress)}$$

For tall vessels, the wall thickness is determined by **iteratively solving** the combined-stress equation, because the bending term itself contains the thickness `t` in the denominator (through the section modulus Z = πD²t/4). The procedure is **convergent iteration** starting from a pressure-based thickness.

### 2.4 Wind Load Calculation (IS 875 Part 3 — Standard KTU Method)

The horizontal wind force on a tall vessel at any elevation is:

$$F_w = C_f \cdot q_z \cdot A \cdot C_d$$

where:
* **C_f** = force coefficient (≈ 0.7 for cylindrical surfaces, 1.2 for flat surfaces)
* **q_z** = design wind pressure at height z (N/m²)
* **A** = projected (frontal) area exposed to wind (m²)
* **C_d** = dynamic augmentation factor (typically 1.0 for static analysis, up to 1.5–2.0 for tall slender structures)

The **design wind pressure** at height z is:

$$q_z = 0.6 \cdot V_b^2 \cdot \left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{1/6} \quad \text{(Power Law, IS 875)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **first KTU-level estimate**, the wind pressure is often taken as a **uniform value** over the entire height, computed from a reference velocity at the **top of the vessel** (V_top). This is the "equivalent uniform pressure" approach, used in most textbook problems.

### 2.5 Overturning Moment (M_OT)

The overturning moment is the resultant moment of the **horizontal wind forces** about the base of the vessel (or skirt-to-shell junction):

$$M_{OT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{w,i} \cdot h_i$$

where **h_i** is the height of the centroid of each wind-loaded strip above the base. For a uniform equivalent pressure acting on a vertical column of total height L:

$$M_{OT} = \frac{F_{w,\text{total}} \cdot L}{2} \quad \text{(rectangular pressure distribution)}$$

If the wind pressure **increases with height** (power law), the centroid is **above** L/2 — typically at **2L/3** for linear increase, and **3L/4** for typical power-law distribution.

### 2.6 Bending Stress from Wind/Seismic Moment

The **wind/seismic moment** produces a **bending stress** in the shell. At any elevation z above the base, the **bending moment** is:

$$M(z) = \int_{z}^{L} F_w(\zeta) \, d\zeta$$

For uniform pressure on a column from the base (z=0) to top (z=L), with the **maximum moment at the base**:

$$M_{max} = \frac{F_w \cdot L}{2}$$

The **bending stress** at the base (windward fibre in tension, leeward in compression):

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{32 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^3 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{D_i}{D_o}\right)^4\right]} \;\approx\; \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 \, t} \quad \text{(thin shell)}$$

### 2.7 The Combined Stress Check (THE KTU CRITICAL EQUATION)

At the **base of the vessel (most critical section)**, the longitudinal stress is the algebraic sum:

$$\sigma_L \;=\; \frac{PD}{4t} \;+\; \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 \, t} \;-\; \frac{W}{\pi D_o \, t}$$

The **two failure modes** checked separately:

**(a) Tensile failure (windward side):**
$$\sigma_L \;=\; \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \;\;\leq\;\; S \cdot E$$

**(b) Compressive failure — buckling check (leeward side):**
$$\vert \sigma_L \vert \;=\; \left\vert \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{PD}{4t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \right\vert \;\;\leq\;\; S \cdot E$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **maximum compressive longitudinal stress** on the leeward side can be **larger** than the maximum tensile stress on the windward side, because the internal pressure *adds* to compression on that fibre. Always check **both sides**!

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = L / D_o$ | dimensionless | Tall if $\lambda \geq 5$ |
| 2 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | N/mm² | Governs short vessels |
| 3 | Longitudinal (pressure) stress | $\sigma_{Lp} = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Always present |
| 4 | Design wind pressure (IS 875) | $q_z = 0.6 \, V_b^2 \,(z/z_0)^{1/6}$ | N/m² | $V_b$ in m/s |
| 5 | Wind force on projected area | $F_w = C_f \, q \, A$ | N | $C_f \approx 0.7$ for cylinder |
| 6 | Overturning moment (uniform) | $M_{OT} = F_w \, L / 2$ | N·m | Max at base |
| 7 | Overturning moment (general) | $M_{OT} = \sum F_{w,i} h_i$ | N·m | Discrete sum |
| 8 | Bending stress in thin shell | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{4 M}{\pi D_o^2 \, t}$ | N/mm² | At base |
| 9 | Dead-weight stress | $\sigma_W = \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o \, t}$ | N/mm² | Compression |
| 10 | Combined longitudinal stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t} + \dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o t}$ | N/mm² | **Tall-vessel governing** |
| 11 | Allowable tensile stress (ASME) | $\sigma_L \leq S \cdot E$ | N/mm² | $E$ = joint efficiency |
| 12 | Minimum shell thickness (corr. + mill) | $t_{min} = t_{calc} + c_a + c_m$ | mm | $c_a$ = corrosion, $c_m$ = mill |
| 13 | Skirt thickness (ASME UG-54) | $t_{sk} = \dfrac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_{sk}^2 \, S \cdot E}$ | mm | Or empirical |
| 14 | Max compression (leeward) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} + \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o t} - \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Buckling check |
| 15 | Section modulus of shell | $Z = \pi D_o^2 t / 4$ | mm³ | Used in $\sigma_b = M/Z$ |
| 16 | Maximum wind-induced moment (with $C_d$) | $M_{max} = C_d \cdot F_w \cdot L/2$ | N·m | Dynamic factor |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tall-vessel design procedure is the **backbone of distillation column design** in petroleum refineries, petrochemical plants, and LNG terminals. Distillation columns for crude oil fractionation can exceed **60 m in height** with diameters of 4–8 m. The wall thickness required for **internal pressure** alone would be ~12 mm; however, the **wind/seismic check** often pushes it to 20–30 mm. The skirt support, typically 1.5–2.0 times the vessel diameter in height, is then designed as a **thin-walled pressure vessel** subject to the same combined stresses. This same methodology is applied to **reactors, absorbers, regenerators, and regenerator-reactor vessels** in catalytic cracking units.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bending Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell (Wind/Seismic)

**Step 1:** Consider a thin-walled cylinder of outer diameter D_o, inner diameter D_i, and wall thickness t = (D_o − D_i)/2. The vessel acts as a cantilever beam fixed at the skirt base.

**Step 2:** The moment of inertia of a hollow circular cross-section is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right) \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{64}\left[(D_o^2)^2 - (D_i^2)^2\right] \\
  &= \frac{\pi}{64}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)(D_o^2 + D_i^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a thin shell, t ≪ D_o, so D_o ≈ D_i ≈ D, and (D_o² + D_i²) ≈ 2D², (D_o² − D_i²) = (D_o − D_i)(D_o + D_i) ≈ 2D·t. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &\approx \frac{\pi}{64}(2D t)(2D^2) = \frac{\pi D^3 t}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The distance from the neutral axis to the outermost fibre is c = D_o/2 ≈ D/2. The bending stress is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (D/2)}{\pi D^3 t/8} = \frac{8 M}{2 \pi D^2 t} = \frac{4 M}{\pi D^2 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Hence the **section modulus** of a thin cylindrical shell is $Z = I/c = \pi D^2 t / 4$, and $\sigma_b = M/Z = 4M/(\pi D^2 t)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combined Longitudinal Stress in a Tall Vessel

**Step 1:** Identify all longitudinal stresses acting at the **base of the vessel (or any critical section)**:

* **Pressure-induced longitudinal (membrane):** $\sigma_{Lp} = PD/(4t)$ (tensile for internal pressure)
* **Bending-induced longitudinal:** $\sigma_b = 4M/(\pi D_o^2 t)$ — tensile on the **windward side**, compressive on the **leeward side**
* **Dead-weight longitudinal (compressive):** $\sigma_W = W/(\pi D_o t)$

**Step 2:** The combined longitudinal stress on the **windward fibre** (tension side):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{\text{wind}} &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This must satisfy: $\sigma_L^{\text{wind}} \leq S \cdot E$ (tensile allowable).

**Step 3:** The combined longitudinal stress on the **leeward fibre** (compression side):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{\text{lee}} &= -\frac{PD}{4t} - \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \quad \text{(entirely negative = compressive)} \\
\vert \sigma_L^{\text{lee}} \vert &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} + \frac{W}{\pi D_o t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This must satisfy: $\vert \sigma_L^{\text{lee}} \vert \leq S \cdot E$ (compressive allowable; buckling check also required).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** In the **leeward (compression) fibre**, the pressure-induced **longitudinal** stress is itself **compressive**? Wait — no! For a closed-end cylinder under **internal pressure**, the longitudinal stress is **tensile** (think of the cylinder wanting to "stretch" to keep the cap on). However, when we sum the bending compression and the dead-weight compression at the leeward side, the *net* stress becomes **compressive** if the moment is large enough. The pressure **adds** to the tension on the windward side, and **subtracts** from the leeward side.

Let me re-derive cleanly with sign convention (tension positive):

**Sign convention:** All stresses are taken as **algebraic** (tension positive, compression negative).

* Pressure longitudinal stress: $\sigma_{Lp} = +PD/(4t)$ (always tensile for internal pressure)
* Bending stress (windward = tension): $+4M/(\pi D_o^2 t)$
* Bending stress (leeward = compression): $-4M/(\pi D_o^2 t)$
* Dead weight: $\sigma_W = -W/(\pi D_o t)$ (always compressive)

**Windward side (max tension):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{L,\text{max}}^{+} &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \;\;\leq\;\; S \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Leeward side (max compression):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{L,\text{max}}^{-} &= \frac{PD}{4t} - \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $M_{max}$ is large, the leeward stress can become **net compressive**. The compressive check is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \sigma_{L,\text{max}}^{-} \vert &= \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \;\;\leq\;\; S \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

Both sides must be checked; **whichever is more critical determines the thickness**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Iterative Solution for Required Thickness

Because the **bending stress term contains t in its denominator**, the combined-stress equation is **transcendental in t** for a given moment and pressure. The iterative procedure is:

**Step 1:** Compute the **pressure-based thickness** first:

$$
t_p = \frac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} \quad \text{(ASME UG-27, circumferential)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **weight of vessel** W using t_p as initial guess.

**Step 3:** Compute the **wind force** F_w and the **overturning moment** M_OT at the base (using the actual vessel height and projected area).

**Step 4:** Solve the combined-stress equation for the **new thickness t_new**, treating t as the unknown:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{PD}{4 \, t_{\text{new}}} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2 \, t_{\text{new}}} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o \, t_{\text{new}}} = S \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for t_new:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{new}} &= \frac{1}{S \cdot E} \left[ \frac{PD}{4} + \frac{4 M_{max}}{\pi D_o^2} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare t_new with the previous t. If they differ by more than ~5 %, **recalculate W** with t_new and **re-solve**. Repeat until convergence (usually 2–3 iterations suffice for KTU problems).

**Step 6:** Add corrosion allowance c_a and mill tolerance c_m:

$$
t_{\text{final}} = t_{\text{new}} + c_a + c_m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark problem, the marker expects **at least 2 iterations** with a clearly shown convergence. Do not present a single-shot answer.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stress Check Locations

| Location on Vessel | Pressure σ_Lp | Bending σ_b | Dead-weight σ_W | Net σ_L | Code Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Base, windward** | +PD/(4t) | +4M/(πD²t) | −W/(πDt) | Tension, max | $\sigma_L \leq S \cdot E$ |
| **Base, leeward** | +PD/(4t) | −4M/(πD²t) | −W/(πDt) | Compressive, max | $\vert \sigma_L \vert \leq S \cdot E$ |
| **Top of shell** | +PD/(4t) | small (M→0) | −W_top/(πDt) | Tension, small | Often satisfied automatically |
| **Skirt base (concrete)** | none (skirt not pressure) | +4M/(πD_sk² t_sk) | −W_total/(πD_sk t_sk) | Mixed | UG-54 + skirt buckling |
| **Skirt-shell junction** | Step in D from shell to skirt | Concentration factor | Continuity | Fatigue concern | WRC 107 / FEA |

---

### 3.5 Work-through Example (KTU Pattern)

**Given:** Vertical pressure vessel, D_o = 2.0 m, L = 25 m, P = 2.0 MPa (internal), S = 138 N/mm², E = 1.0, c_a = 1.5 mm, c_m = 0.5 mm. Wind pressure q = 1.2 kN/m² (equivalent uniform). C_f = 0.7, C_d = 1.0. Vessel weight (estimated) = 200 kN. Liquid hold-up weight = 50 kN. Allowable tensile stress in skirt = 110 N/mm².

**Step 1: Projected area** $A = D_o \times L = 2.0 \times 25 = 50 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2: Wind force** $F_w = C_f \cdot q \cdot A = 0.7 \times 1200 \times 50 = 42{,}000 \text{ N} = 42 \text{ kN}$

**Step 3: Overturning moment at base** $M = F_w \cdot L/2 = 42{,}000 \times 12.5 = 525{,}000 \text{ N·m} = 525 \text{ kN·m}$

**Step 4: Total weight** $W = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ kN} = 250{,}000 \text{ N}$

**Step 5: Combined longitudinal stress check, using initial guess** t = 20 mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{+} &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \\
&= \frac{2.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 20} + \frac{4 \times 525{,}000 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 2000^2 \times 20} - \frac{250{,}000 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 2000 \times 20} \\
&= 50.0 + \frac{2.1 \times 10^9}{2.513 \times 10^8} - \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{1.257 \times 10^5} \\
&= 50.0 + 8.36 - 1.99 \;\;\text{(in N/mm²)} \\
&\approx 56.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: For unit clarity, $M$ in N·mm = 525 × 10⁶ N·mm, D in mm = 2000, t in mm = 20. $W$ in N = 250,000 N.

**Re-doing in consistent mm-units:**

$M = 525 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $D_o = 2000$ mm, $t = 20$ mm, $W = 250 \times 10^3$ N, $P = 2.0$ N/mm², $D = 2000$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{+} &= \frac{2.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 20} + \frac{4 \times 525 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 2000^2 \times 20} - \frac{250 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 2000 \times 20} \\
\sigma_{Lp} &= 50.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma_b &= \frac{2.1 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 4 \times 10^7} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^9}{1.2566 \times 10^8} \approx 16.71 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma_W &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.2566 \times 10^5} \approx 1.99 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma_L^{+} &= 50.0 + 16.71 - 1.99 = 64.72 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $64.72 \leq 138 \cdot 1.0 = 138$ N/mm² ✓

**Step 6: Leeward (compressive) side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{-} &= \frac{PD}{4t} - \frac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \\
&= 50.0 - 16.71 - 1.99 = 31.30 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\; (\text{still tensile, OK})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Conclusion: t = 20 mm is **acceptable** for combined stress. But the **hoop check** also must be done:
> $\sigma_h = PD/(2t) = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq 138$ N/mm² ✓

**Final thickness:** $t = 20 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 22$ mm → choose next standard plate = 22 mm or 24 mm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic of a Tall Pressure Vessel with Wind Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tall Pressure Vessel<br>Outside diameter Do = 2.0 m<br>Total height L = 25 m"] --> B["Internal Pressure P<br>Hoop stress sigma_h = PD over 2t<br>Longitudinal stress sigma_Lp = PD over 4t"]
    A --> C["Skirt Support<br>Height H_sk = 4 m<br>Diameter D_sk = 2.2 m<br>Thickness t_sk"]
    A --> D["Wind Load Distribution<br>Equivalent uniform pressure q = 1.2 kN per m2<br>Resultant F_w = Cf times q times A"]
    A --> E["Dead Weight W<br>Vessel shell plus internals plus liquid"]
    D --> F["Overturning Moment M_OT<br>at skirt base<br>M_OT = F_w times L by 2"]
    F --> G["Bending Stress sigma_b<br>in vessel shell<br>sigma_b = 4M over pi Do squared t"]
    B --> H["Combined Longitudinal Stress Check"]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I1{"Windward side<br>tension check<br>sigma_L+ less than SE"}
    H --> I2{"Leeward side<br>compression check<br>absolute value sigma_L- less than SE"}
    I1 --> J["Iterate until thickness t converges"]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K["Final Thickness<br>t_final = t_calc plus c_a plus c_m"]
    C --> L["Skirt Design Check<br>ASME UG-54<br>sigma_sk = 4M over pi D_sk squared t_sk"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Tall-Vessel Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Input Geometry and Loads<br>D_o, L, P, S, E, q, C_f, W"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute Pressure Based Thickness<br>t_p = PD over 2SE minus 1.2P"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute Wind Force and Moment<br>F_w and M_OT"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Initial Combined Stress Check<br>sigma_L = PD over 4t plus 4M over pi Do squared t minus W over pi Do t"]
    S4 --> S5{"sigma_L within allowable S times E"}
    S5 -- No --> S6["Increase t and Recalculate W"]
    S6 --> S4
    S5 -- Yes --> S7["Step 5: Check Leeward Compression Side"]
    S7 --> S8{"Leeward compression OK"}
    S8 -- No --> S6
    S8 -- Yes --> S9["Step 6: Add Corrosion and Mill Allowances<br>t_final = t_calc plus c_a plus c_m"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 7: Skirt Design and Anchorage<br>UG-54 plus base ring plus bolts"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 8: Final Documentation and Drawing"]
```

### 4.3 Stress Distribution Across the Vessel Cross-Section (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR["Cross Section at Skirt Base"] --> WND["Windward Fibre<br>sigma_L = sigma_Lp plus sigma_b minus sigma_W<br>Maximum Tensile Stress"]
    CR --> LEE["Leeward Fibre<br>sigma_L = sigma_Lp minus sigma_b minus sigma_W<br>Maximum Compressive or Reduced Tensile"]
    CR --> NEU["Neutral Axis<br>sigma_L = sigma_Lp minus sigma_W<br>Pure Membrane plus Weight Only"]
    WND --> CHK1["Check sigma_L less than or equal S times E"]
    LEE --> CHK2["Check absolute sigma_L less than or equal S times E"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the cross-section at the **most critical location (skirt base)**, the three fibres (windward, neutral, leeward) experience **three different stress states** simultaneously. The bending stress **varies linearly** from +σ_b on the windward side to −σ_b on the leeward side, while the pressure and weight stresses are **uniform** across the section. The two checks (tension on windward, compression on leeward) must **both** be performed and **both** must pass.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define a **tall pressure vessel**. Under what condition is a vessel classified as *tall*? Mention the dominant stress that governs its design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A tall pressure vessel is a vertical cylindrical pressure vessel in which the **length-to-diameter ratio (L/D_o) is large enough that the secondary stresses from wind, seismic, or other environmental loads become comparable to the primary pressure-induced membrane stresses**. A vessel is generally classified as tall when **L/D_o ≥ 5** (and L ≥ 6 m, or L ≥ 10 D_o for slender columns). The **dominant stress** governing tall-vessel design is the **combined longitudinal stress** at the base — the sum of the pressure-induced longitudinal stress (PD/4t), the wind/seismic bending stress (4M/πD²t), and the dead-weight stress (W/πDt). This combined stress must satisfy the ASME Section VIII Div. 1 allowable-stress criterion: σ_L ≤ S·E.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark L/D condition + 1 mark dominant stress — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

List **any three loads** that act on a tall pressure vessel supported on a skirt, and state which load produces the **maximum bending moment** at the base of the vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principal loads acting on a tall pressure vessel are:

1. **Internal/external pressure** (produces hoop and longitudinal membrane stresses).
2. **Dead weight** (self-weight of vessel, trays, packing, liquid, insulation, piping) — produces compressive longitudinal stress.
3. **Wind load** (horizontal distributed load) — produces **overturning moment and bending stress** in the shell.
4. **Seismic (earthquake) load** — produces lateral inertial forces and bending moment.
5. **Nozzle / piping reaction loads** — local loads at the top of the vessel.

The **wind load** typically produces the **maximum bending moment** at the base of the vessel under normal operating conditions. (In seismically active zones, the seismic moment may govern if it exceeds the wind moment.)

*(1 mark for each of three correct loads = 3 marks; 1 bonus if student correctly identifies wind as producing max bending.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full-Design Problem**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper — Module 2 Internal Choice Set A]**

A vertical cylindrical pressure vessel has the following data:

* Outside diameter: D_o = 2.4 m
* Tangent-to-tangent length: L = 30 m
* Design pressure: P = 1.5 MPa (internal, gauge)
* Design temperature: 200 °C
* Material: SA 516 Gr 70 (S = 138 N/mm², E = 1.0, joint efficiency)
* Corrosion allowance: c_a = 1.5 mm
* Mill tolerance: c_m = 0.8 mm
* Equivalent uniform wind pressure (with dynamic factor): q = 1.5 kN/m²
* Force coefficient for cylinder: C_f = 0.7
* Estimated total weight: W = 400 kN
* Skirt outside diameter: D_sk = 2.6 m

**(a)** Calculate the **wind force and overturning moment** at the base of the vessel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Determine the **required shell thickness** by combined-stress check, using two iterations, and state the **standard plate thickness** to be used. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**(a) Wind force and overturning moment (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Projected area exposed to wind** *(1 mark)*

The projected area on which the wind acts is the silhouette of the vessel as seen from the wind direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= D_o \times L \\
  &= 2.4 \times 30 = 72.0 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Wind force calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_w &= C_f \times q \times A \\
    &= 0.7 \times 1.5 \times 10^3 \, \text{N/m}^2 \times 72.0 \, \text{m}^2 \\
    &= 0.7 \times 108{,}000 \\
    &= 75{,}600 \, \text{N} \\
    &= 75.6 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Centroid of wind load and overturning moment** *(2 marks)*

For a uniform pressure distribution on a vertical column, the resultant acts at the **mid-height**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h} &= \frac{L}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **overturning moment at the base**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{OT} &= F_w \times \bar{h} = 75.6 \times 10^3 \times 15.0 \\
       &= 1.134 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·m} \\
       &= 1134 \, \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to consistent mm-units for stress calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{OT} &= 1.134 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·m} = 1.134 \times 10^9 \, \text{N·mm} \\
W &= 400 \times 10^3 \, \text{N} \\
D_o &= 2400 \, \text{mm} \\
P &= 1.5 \, \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (a):** $F_w = 75.6$ kN; $M_{OT} = 1134$ kN·m ≈ 1.134 × 10⁹ N·mm.

---

**(b) Required shell thickness (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1: Initial pressure-based thickness** *(1.5 marks)*

Use the ASME UG-27 formula for circumferential (hoop) stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_p &= \frac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} \\
    &= \frac{1.5 \times 2400}{2 \times 138 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 1.5} \\
    &= \frac{3600}{276 - 1.8} \\
    &= \frac{3600}{274.2} \\
    &= 13.13 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1: Combined stress check at t = 13.13 mm** *(2.5 marks)*

The combined longitudinal stress on the **windward (tension) side**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \\
&= \frac{1.5 \times 2400}{4 \times 13.13} + \frac{4 \times 1.134 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 2400^2 \times 13.13} - \frac{400 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 2400 \times 13.13}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term:

* $\sigma_{Lp} = \dfrac{3600}{52.52} = 68.55$ N/mm²
* $\sigma_b = \dfrac{4.536 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 5.76 \times 10^6 \times 13.13} = \dfrac{4.536 \times 10^9}{2.376 \times 10^8} = 19.09$ N/mm²
* $\sigma_W = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{9.899 \times 10^4} = 4.04$ N/mm²

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L &= 68.55 + 19.09 - 4.04 = 83.60 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Allowable: $S \cdot E = 138 \times 1.0 = 138$ N/mm².
**Check:** 83.60 < 138 ✓ — passes on the **tension** side.

**Leeward (compression) side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{-} &= \frac{PD}{4t} - \frac{4M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \\
&= 68.55 - 19.09 - 4.04 = 45.42 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\;(\text{tensile, OK})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Hence, in this problem, the **t = 13.13 mm** thickness already satisfies both checks. The vessel weight will be **slightly less** than 400 kN (since thickness is lower than estimated), but for a 14-mark answer with 2 iterations, we proceed to **Iteration 2** to demonstrate convergence.

**Iteration 2: Refine t using the combined-stress equation solved for t** *(2.5 marks)*

Rearranging the **tensile-side combined stress** to solve for t:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L = S \cdot E \quad \Rightarrow \quad t &= \frac{1}{S \cdot E}\left[ \frac{PD}{4} + \frac{4 M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

* $\dfrac{PD}{4} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 2400}{4} = 900$ N/mm
* $\dfrac{4 M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.134 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 5.76 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{4.536 \times 10^9}{1.8096 \times 10^7} = 250.66$ N/mm
* $\dfrac{W}{\pi D_o} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 2400} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{7539.8} = 53.05$ N/mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{1}{138}\left[ 900 + 250.66 - 53.05 \right] \\
  &= \frac{1097.61}{138} \\
  &= 7.95 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **direct rearrangement** yields a thickness lower than the pressure-based value, because the term $\dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2}$ **contains t implicitly** in the **flexibility of the vessel** — but in this simple stress-based model, t cancels out. Hence the **Iteration 2 thickness** is t = 7.95 mm, but the **Iteration 1** direct-check value (t = 13.13 mm) governs because the bending term was computed with t = 13.13.

The **correct procedure** is therefore:

* **Iteration 1:** Use pressure-based t = 13.13 mm; combined stress = 83.6 N/mm² < 138 N/mm² → ACCEPT.

The **hoop check** also must be confirmed: $\sigma_h = PD/(2t) = 1.5 \times 2400/(2 \times 13.13) = 137.1$ N/mm² ≤ 138 N/mm² ✓ (very close; safe).

**Final thickness including corrosion + mill allowance:** *(0.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{final} &= t_{calc} + c_a + c_m \\
          &= 13.13 + 1.5 + 0.8 \\
          &= 15.43 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Choose next standard plate thickness = 16 mm.**

**Result (b):** Calculated thickness = 13.13 mm; final design thickness = **16 mm (standard plate)**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper — Module 2 Internal Choice Set B]**

A vertical distillation column is to be designed for the following conditions:

* Outside diameter: D_o = 3.0 m
* Total height: L = 40 m
* Design pressure: P = 0.8 MPa (internal)
* Material: SA 240 Gr 304 (S = 115 N/mm² at design temperature, E = 0.85)
* Corrosion allowance: c_a = 2.0 mm
* Basic wind speed: V_b = 47 m/s (as per IS 875, zone 5, terrain category 2)
* Force coefficient: C_f = 0.7
* Total weight of vessel and internals: W = 800 kN
* Dynamic augmentation factor: C_d = 1.0

**(a)** Compute the **design wind pressure** using the IS 875 power law, the **wind force**, and the **overturning moment** at the base. Use an equivalent uniform pressure for simplicity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Determine the **required shell thickness** using the combined longitudinal stress check, and verify both **windward (tensile)** and **leeward (compressive)** sides. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**(a) Wind pressure, force, and moment (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design wind pressure at top of vessel (height z = 40 m)** *(2.5 marks)*

Reference parameters (IS 875):
* Terrain category 2: power-law exponent $k = 1/7 \approx 0.143$
* Reference height: $z_0 = 10$ m
* Basic wind speed: $V_b = 47$ m/s

The **design wind speed at height z**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_z &= V_b \times \left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{k} \\
    &= 47 \times \left(\frac{40}{10}\right)^{1/7} \\
    &= 47 \times 4^{0.1429} \\
    &= 47 \times 1.2155 \\
    &= 57.13 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **design wind pressure**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_z &= 0.6 \times V_z^2 \\
    &= 0.6 \times (57.13)^2 \\
    &= 0.6 \times 3263.8 \\
    &= 1958.3 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
    &\approx 1.96 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** For KTU problems, students may use the **basic wind pressure** $q_b = 0.6 V_b^2 = 0.6 \times 47^2 = 1325.4$ N/m² if the problem says "use basic wind pressure." In this case, we have used the height-adjusted value.

**Step 2: Projected area** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= D_o \times L = 3.0 \times 40 = 120 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Wind force** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_w &= C_f \times C_d \times q_z \times A \\
    &= 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 1958.3 \times 120 \\
    &= 164{,}500 \text{ N} \\
    &\approx 164.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Overturning moment at base** *(2 marks)*

For uniform pressure, the resultant acts at mid-height:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{OT} &= F_w \times \frac{L}{2} \\
       &= 164.5 \times 10^3 \times 20 \\
       &= 3.29 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} \\
       &= 3290 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

In mm-units: $M_{OT} = 3.29 \times 10^9$ N·mm.

**Result (a):** $q_z = 1.96$ kN/m²; $F_w = 164.5$ kN; $M_{OT} = 3290$ kN·m.

---

**(b) Required shell thickness (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert to mm-units** *(0.5 mark)*

$P = 0.8$ N/mm², $D_o = 3000$ mm, $W = 800 \times 10^3$ N, $S \cdot E = 115 \times 0.85 = 97.75$ N/mm².

**Step 2: Initial pressure-based thickness (ASME UG-27, hoop)** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_p &= \frac{PD}{2SE - 1.2P} \\
    &= \frac{0.8 \times 3000}{2 \times 97.75 - 1.2 \times 0.8} \\
    &= \frac{2400}{195.5 - 0.96} \\
    &= \frac{2400}{194.54} \\
    &= 12.34 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Combined stress check at t = 12.34 mm** *(2.5 marks)*

**Windward (tensile) side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{+} &= \frac{PD}{4t} + \frac{4M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o t} \\
&= \frac{0.8 \times 3000}{4 \times 12.34} + \frac{4 \times 3.29 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 3000^2 \times 12.34} - \frac{800 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 3000 \times 12.34}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term:

* $\sigma_{Lp} = \dfrac{2400}{49.36} = 48.62$ N/mm²
* $\sigma_b = \dfrac{1.316 \times 10^{10}}{\pi \times 9 \times 10^6 \times 12.34} = \dfrac{1.316 \times 10^{10}}{3.488 \times 10^8} = 37.73$ N/mm²
* $\sigma_W = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{\pi \times 3000 \times 12.34} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{1.163 \times 10^5} = 6.88$ N/mm²

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{+} &= 48.62 + 37.73 - 6.88 = 79.47 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $79.47 \leq 97.75$ N/mm² ✓ — passes.

**Leeward (compression) side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L^{-} &= 48.62 - 37.73 - 6.88 = 4.01 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\;(\text{tensile, just barely}) \\
\vert \sigma_L^{-} \vert &= 4.01 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq 97.75 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Important observation:** The leeward stress is **almost zero** because the bending moment is **almost balanced** by the pressure-induced longitudinal stress. The vessel is in a **near-axial-tension state** at the leeward side, which is favourable for buckling (no net compression to worry about).

**Step 4: Iteration 2 — Verify with refined t** *(2 marks)*

The **direct rearrangement** from the combined-stress equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{1}{S \cdot E}\left[\frac{PD}{4} + \frac{4M_{OT}}{\pi D_o^2} - \frac{W}{\pi D_o}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{97.75}\left[\frac{0.8 \times 3000}{4} + \frac{4 \times 3.29 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 3000^2} - \frac{800 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 3000}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{97.75}\left[600 + 465.13 - 84.88\right] \\
&= \frac{980.25}{97.75} \\
&= 10.03 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The lower value of t (10.03 mm) reflects that the **direct rearrangement** is an algebraic simplification that **does not** account for the implicit t-dependence of M (through the vessel's stiffness affecting its dynamic response). In a 14-mark answer, the **Iteration 1 direct check** is the accepted value. The **second iteration** confirms the design is on the **safe side**.

**Step 5: Final thickness with corrosion + mill allowance** *(0.5 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{final} &= t_{calc} + c_a + c_m \\
          &= 12.34 + 2.0 + 0.5 \;\;(\text{typical c_m for SS}) \\
          &= 14.84 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Choose next standard plate thickness = 16 mm.**

**Result (b):** Calculated thickness = 12.34 mm; final design thickness = **16 mm (standard plate)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (KTU 2024 Module 2, Tall Vessels):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the joint efficiency E:** Many students compute $\sigma_L$ but compare directly with S, ignoring E. For welded vessels with partial RT, E may be 0.7–0.85, and **forgetting E can over-stress the design by 15–30 %**. Always state E explicitly. (–1 to –2 marks)
>
> 2. **Only checking the windward (tensile) side:** The leeward (compressive) side is **the more critical** side in **taller** vessels where wind/seismic moment dominates. Failure to check it loses 2–3 marks easily.
>
> 3. **Wrong moment arm:** Using L (full height) instead of L/2 (centroid of uniform pressure) for the moment calculation. The correct moment is $F_w \cdot L/2$ for uniform pressure. For triangular (linearly increasing) pressure distribution, the centroid is at **2L/3**. Read the problem carefully.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing m, mm, N, kN, and N/mm² without conversion. KTU markers **strictly penalize** this. Always convert everything to **N and mm** (or N and m) before substituting.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the corrosion and mill allowances:** The final answer must be $t_{calc} + c_a + c_m$ rounded up to a standard plate size. Many students stop at $t_{calc}$ and lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping the hoop stress check:** Even though the combined longitudinal stress governs the **thickness**, the **hoop stress** check is also mandatory and must be shown. –1 mark if missing.
>
> 7. **Treating the skirt as part of the pressure boundary:** The skirt is **not** designed for internal pressure; it is a **structural support**. Mixing skirt and shell thicknesses is a common conceptual error.
>
> 8. **No iteration shown:** The combined-stress equation is transcendental in t (because t appears in the denominator). KTU examiners expect **at least one explicit iteration** showing convergence. Single-shot answers are penalized 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Mechanical Design of Tall Pressure Vessels**
>
> ✅ **Tall vessel = L/D_o ≥ 5**; governed by **combined longitudinal stress**, not hoop stress.
>
> ✅ **Three stress components** at the base: (1) Pressure-induced $\sigma_{Lp} = PD/(4t)$, (2) Bending-induced $\sigma_b = 4M/(\pi D_o^2 t)$, (3) Weight-induced $\sigma_W = W/(\pi D_o t)$.
>
> ✅ **Combined stress equation (TENSION side, windward):**
> $\sigma_L^{+} = \dfrac{PD}{4t} + \dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o t} \leq S \cdot E$
>
> ✅ **Combined stress equation (COMPRESSION side, leeward):**
> $\vert \sigma_L^{-} \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{PD}{4t} - \dfrac{4M}{\pi D_o^2 t} - \dfrac{W}{\pi D_o t} \right\vert \leq S \cdot E$
>
> ✅ **Wind force:** $F_w = C_f \cdot q \cdot A \cdot C_d$, with $C_f = 0.7$ for cylinder.
>
> ✅ **Overturning moment (uniform pressure):** $M_{OT} = F_w \cdot L/2$.
>
> ✅ **Design codes:** ASME Section VIII Div. 1 (UG-27, UG-54); IS 875 (wind); IS 1893 (seismic).
>
> ✅ **Allowable stress check:** $\sigma_L \leq S \cdot E$; $S$ is the stress value from the code at design temperature.
>
> ✅ **Required thickness includes** corrosion allowance $c_a$ and mill tolerance $c_m$: $t_{final} = t_{calc} + c_a + c_m$ → round up to next standard plate.
>
> ✅ **Skirt is a structural member**, not pressure boundary. Skirt thickness from $t_{sk} = 4M/(\pi D_{sk}^2 \cdot S \cdot E)$ (ASME UG-54) plus buckling check.
>
> ✅ **Iterative procedure:** Pressure-based t → combined stress check → revise t → re-check until convergence.
>
> ✅ **Hoop stress check is also mandatory:** $\sigma_h = PD/(2t) \leq S \cdot E$.
>
> ✅ **Unit discipline:** Always convert to **N and mm** before substituting; present all intermediate numerical steps.
>
> ✅ **Common error zones:** (a) Forgetting E, (b) checking only one side, (c) wrong moment arm, (d) missing corrosion allowance, (e) not iterating.
>
> ✅ **Real-world examples:** Distillation columns, catalytic-cracking reactors, regenerators, LNG storage tanks, ammonia converters — all of which are typically tall vessels.
>
> ✅ **Dynamic augmentation factor** $C_d$ accounts for wind gusts and resonance; for very tall, slender vessels, $C_d$ can be 1.5–2.0 per IS 875.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Thin Walled Multi-Course Pressure Vessels as per IS 2825
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Thin Walled Multi-Course Pressure Vessels as per IS 2825 # 📘 MODULE 2 — DESIGN OF THIN WALLED MULTI-COURSE PRESSURE VESSELS AS PER IS 2825 ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **multi-course (or multi-layered) cylindrical pressure vessel** is a shell constructed by welding together **two or more rolled plate courses (layers)** along circumferential girth joints, rather than being fabricated from a single thick plate. As per **IS 2825 : 1969 (Reaffirmed 2005)** — *Code for Unfired Pressure Vessels*, this construction is adopted when the required shell thickness exceeds the maximum thickness of commercially rolled plates (typically **> 32 mm** for carbon steel), or when economy, weldability, or reduced residual stress is desired. In the multi-course configuration: - The **innermost course (Course 1)** is the thinnest and carries the **full internal design pressure (P)**. - Each **successive outer course (Course 2, 3, ...)** carries only the **differential pressure** between its inner and outer surfaces. - The vessel may be designed for either **equal hoop stress** in every course (the most common IS 2825 approach) or for **equal thickness** across courses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key IS 2825 Classification (Sec 5 of the code):** > - **Class 1 Vessel**: Highest category — full radiographic examination of all welds; high joint efficiency (η ≈ 1.0). > - **Class 2 Vessel**: Spot radiography; η ≈ 0.95. > - **Class 3 Vessel**: No radiography; η ≈ 0.85 (for butt welds), lower for other types. > Joint efficiency **η** is a critical input for multi-course thickness calculation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Doll of Pressure" Imagine a set of **concentric cardboard tubes of increasing diameter**, all stacked one inside another. If you pump air into the innermost tube, only the **innermost tube feels the full pressure**, while the **outer tubes feel only the pressure transmitted through the inner one(s)**. A multi-course pressure vessel works on the **exact same principle**: - The **inner course** is like the smallest tube — it sees the **full internal pressure `P`**. - The **second course** sees only `P − P₁` (the drop in pressure across the inner course wall). - The **third course** sees only `P₂ − P₃`, and so on. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Justification for Multi-Course Construction:** > 1. **Plate availability** — rolling mills rarely produce plates thicker than 32–40 mm in carbon steel. > 2. **Weldability** — thick plates require preheating and post-weld heat treatment; thinner plates weld easily. > 3. **Residual stresses** — reduced compared to single-thick-plate construction. > 4. **Cost economy** — using two thinner plates is cheaper than one very thick plate. > 5. **Safety** — failure of one course does not cause catastrophic rupture. --- ## 1.3 Scope as per IS 2825 IS 2825 governs **cylindrical and spherical shells**, dished ends, flat ends, and openings. For multi-course vessels, **Sec 5.4** of IS 2825 specifies: - **Maximum 4 courses** permitted in cylindrical shells. - Each course must be a **complete cylindrical ring** (no partial courses). - Girth (circumferential) joints must be **butt-welded**. - Longitudinal joints of adjacent courses must be **staggered by at least 90°** to avoid coincident weld seams. - The **inner course must be the thinnest**, with subsequent courses of equal or greater thickness (typically equal thickness in the equal-stress design). # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Fundamental Stress Equations in a Thin-Walled Cylinder For any thin-walled cylindrical shell (membrane theory, valid when **t/D ≤ 1/20 or 1/10** as per IS 2825): The **internal pressure `P`** produces two principal stresses: 1. **Hoop (circumferential) stress** — acts tangentially around the cylinder: $$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$ 2. **Longitudinal (axial) stress** — acts along the axis of the cylinder: $$\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$ > [!NOTE] > Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a closed-ended thin cylinder — this is why **longitudinal joints (parallel to axis)** are more critical than girth joints, and why IS 2825 assigns higher joint efficiency to longitudinal welds. --- ## 2.2 Why Multi-Course Design? A single-plate vessel of thickness `t` requires: $$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \eta \cdot f - P}$$ where: - `P` = internal design pressure (N/mm²) - `D` = inside diameter of shell (mm) - `η` = joint efficiency (0.7 – 1.0) - `f` = maximum allowable stress of material (N/mm²) If calculated `t` exceeds **~32 mm**, IS 2825 recommends a **multi-course construction**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (IS 2825 Multi-Course Design) | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hoop stress in a single course | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 2 | Longitudinal stress in a single course | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 3 | Required thickness (single plate) | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 \eta f - P}$ | mm; IS 2825 Cl. 5.4.1 | | 4 | Inner-course thickness (full pressure) | $t_1 = \dfrac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \eta_1 f - P}$ | mm; Course 1 | | 5 | Differential pressure on Course *i* | $\Delta P_i = P_{i-1} - P_i$ | N/mm² | | 6 | Thickness of course *i* (equal-stress design) | $t_i = \dfrac{\Delta P_i \cdot D_i}{2 \eta_i f - \Delta P_i}$ | mm | | 7 | Inner diameter of course *i* | $D_i = D_{i-1} + 2 t_{i-1}$ | mm | | 8 | Outer diameter of course *i* | $D_{o,i} = D_i + 2 t_i$ | mm | | 9 | Total shell thickness | $t_{total} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | mm | | 10 | Equivalent single-course thickness for longitudinal stress | $t_{eq} = t_1 + \eta_2 t_2 + \eta_3 t_3 + \dots$ | mm (Cl. 5.4.5 of IS 2825) | | 11 | Longitudinal stress (multi-course) | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D_1}{4 \, t_{eq}}$ | N/mm² | | 12 | Minimum thickness (no corrosion) | $t_{min} = \dfrac{D}{500} + 2$ | mm (Cl. 5.4.4) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Permissible Stress `f` for Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr. 70** at design temperature ≈ **138 N/mm² @ 200 °C**, ≈ **118 N/mm² @ 300 °C**, ≈ **103 N/mm² @ 400 °C** (refer IS 2825 Table 2.1). --- ## 2.4 The Three Design Philosophies for Multi-Course Vessels | Method | Principle | Application | |---|---|---| | **(a) Equal Hoop Stress** | Each course designed for same hoop stress `σ_h = f` | Most common; recommended by IS 2825 | | **(b) Equal Thickness** | All courses of equal thickness `t = t₁` | Simplest fabrication | | **(c) Equal Pressure Drop** | Each course carries same `ΔP` | Used when courses have different materials | > [!NOTE] > The **equal hoop stress** method yields the **lightest (most economical)** design and is the **default reference in IS 2825**. We will derive all formulas under this assumption. --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Multi-Course Design In real-world chemical process plants: - **Ammonia converters** (operating at 250–400 bar) → built with 3 or 4 courses. - **Hydrocracker reactors** (high-pressure hydrogen service) → 2–3 course design. - **Ethylene polymerization reactors** (1000–3000 bar) → heavily multi-course. - **High-pressure LDPE service vessels** → classical multi-course application. The multi-course concept therefore directly underpins the design of **virtually every large high-pressure vessel** encountered in petrochemical and fertilizer industries. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thickness of the Inner Course (Course 1) The inner course experiences the **full internal pressure `P`**. By membrane theory: $$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \, t_1}$$ Allowing for joint efficiency `η₁` and maximum permissible stress `f`, we set `σ_h = η₁ · f` (or `σ_h ≤ η₁ f`): $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_1 \, f &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \, t_1} \\[6pt] t_1 &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \, \eta_1 \, f} \quad \text{(without pressure correction)} \end{aligned} $$ But IS 2825 Cl. 5.4.1 introduces the **pressure correction term** (because the membrane formula overestimates allowable P for very thick walls). The exact IS 2825 form is: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \, \eta_1 \, f - P} \end{aligned} $$ This equation is **the starting point of every multi-course thickness calculation** and is heavily tested in KTU examinations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Pressure on Outer Courses Consider a vessel with the inner course already designed. The pressure at the **outer surface of course 1** (= inner surface of course 2) is not zero — it is a value `P₁` < `P`. **Force balance on the inner course wall (hoop direction)** gives: $$ \begin{aligned} P \cdot D_1 &= P_1 \cdot D_{o,1} \\[6pt] P_1 &= P \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_{o,1}} = P \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_1 + 2 t_1} \end{aligned} $$ Hence the **net differential pressure** acting on Course 2 is: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P_2 &= P - P_1 = P - P \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_1 + 2 t_1} = P \cdot \frac{2 t_1}{D_1 + 2 t_1} \end{aligned} $$ Generalising for course *i*: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P_i &= P_{i-1} - P_i = P_{i-1} \cdot \frac{2 t_{i-1}}{D_{i-1} + 2 t_{i-1}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thickness of Course *i* (Equal Hoop Stress Design) For course *i* with internal diameter `D_i` and differential pressure `ΔP_i`: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_i \, f &= \frac{\Delta P_i \cdot D_i}{2 \, t_i} \\[6pt] t_i &= \frac{\Delta P_i \cdot D_i}{2 \, \eta_i \, f - \Delta P_i} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **general multi-course thickness formula** of IS 2825. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Thickness for Longitudinal Stress Check In multi-course construction, **only the inner course has full longitudinal efficiency**, while outer courses have **lower joint efficiency** because their girth welds are typically not radiographed to the same extent. IS 2825 Cl. 5.4.5 defines an **equivalent shell thickness** for longitudinal stress verification: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{eq} &= t_1 + \eta_2 \cdot t_2 + \eta_3 \cdot t_3 + \dots + \eta_n \cdot t_n \end{aligned} $$ The longitudinal stress is then: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{4 \, t_{eq}} \end{aligned} $$ And the design check requires: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_l &\le \eta_1 \cdot f \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This longitudinal check is **often the binding (governing) constraint** in multi-course vessels — even though hoop stress may be lower, the longitudinal stress in the inner course may exceed permissible limits if outer courses are not accounted for. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Two-Course Vessel (Step-by-Step) **Given:** - Internal diameter `D₁ = 2000 mm` - Design pressure `P = 5.0 N/mm²` (≈ 50 bar) - Allowable stress `f = 138 N/mm²` (SA-516 Gr. 70 at 200 °C) - Joint efficiencies: `η₁ = 1.0` (inner, fully radiographed), `η₂ = 0.85` (outer, spot radiography) - Two courses required. **Step 1 — Thickness of inner course (Course 1):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \, \eta_1 \, f - P} \\[4pt] &= \frac{5.0 \times 2000}{2 \times 1.0 \times 138 - 5.0} \\[4pt] &= \frac{10000}{271} \\[4pt] &= 36.90 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ Adopt `t₁ = 38 mm` (next standard plate thickness). **Step 2 — Outer diameter of Course 1 (inner diameter of Course 2):** $$ \begin{aligned} D_2 = D_{o,1} &= D_1 + 2 t_1 = 2000 + 2 \times 38 = 2076 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Differential pressure on Course 2:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P_2 &= P \cdot \frac{2 t_1}{D_1 + 2 t_1} \\[4pt] &= 5.0 \times \frac{2 \times 38}{2000 + 76} \\[4pt] &= 5.0 \times \frac{76}{2076} \\[4pt] &= 0.1830 \text{ N/mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Thickness of Course 2:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_2 &= \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot D_2}{2 \, \eta_2 \, f - \Delta P_2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.1830 \times 2076}{2 \times 0.85 \times 138 - 0.1830} \\[4pt] &= \frac{379.91}{234.42 - 0.183} \\[4pt] &= \frac{379.91}{234.24} \\[4pt] &= 1.622 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ Adopt `t₂ = 2 mm` (minimum practical, may even be governed by `t_min` check). **Step 5 — Longitudinal stress check:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{eq} &= t_1 + \eta_2 \, t_2 = 38 + 0.85 \times 2 = 39.7 \text{ mm} \\[4pt] \sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D_1}{4 \, t_{eq}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2000}{4 \times 39.7} = 62.97 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\[4pt] \sigma_l &\le \eta_1 \, f = 1.0 \times 138 = 138 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** `t₁ = 38 mm`, `t₂ = 2 mm` → **two-course vessel design is feasible**. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When Does Multi-Course Become Mandatory? | Calculated `t₁` (mm) | Course Configuration | |---|---| | < 16 | Single course (no need for multi-course) | | 16 ≤ t₁ < 32 | Single course, full radiography | | 32 ≤ t₁ < 50 | **Two courses** recommended | | 50 ≤ t₁ < 75 | **Three courses** | | ≥ 75 | **Four courses** (maximum per IS 2825) | # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multi-Course Cylindrical Shell (Telescoping Arrangement) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INNERC["INNER COURSE 1 — Internal Pressure P"] A1["Course 1
Inner Diameter D1
Thickness t1
Carries FULL pressure P
Highest joint efficiency η1 = 1.0"] end subgraph MIDCOURSE["MIDDLE COURSE 2 — Differential Pressure ΔP2"] A2["Course 2
Inner Diameter D2 = D1 + 2t1
Thickness t2
Carries ΔP2 = P x 2t1 / D2
Joint efficiency η2 = 0.85"] end subgraph OUTERC["OUTER COURSE 3 — Differential Pressure ΔP3"] A3["Course 3
Inner Diameter D3 = D2 + 2t2
Thickness t3
Carries ΔP3 only
Joint efficiency η3 = 0.80"] end subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL"] EXT["Atmospheric Pressure = 0
Outermost shell surface"] end A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> EXT ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Multi-Course Design Procedure (IS 2825) ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["START: Inputs
P, D1, f, material"]) --> S1["Step 1: Compute single-course thickness
t_single = P x D1 / 2η1f - P"] S1 --> DEC1{"t_single > 32 mm?"} DEC1 -- NO --> SINGLE["Design as Single Course
End Procedure"] DEC1 -- YES --> S2["Step 2: Decide n = number of courses
Typically 2, 3, or 4"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute t1 for full pressure P
t1 = P x D1 / 2η1f - P"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute D2 = D1 + 2t1"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute ΔP2 = P x 2t1 / D2"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute t2 = ΔP2 x D2 / 2η2f - ΔP2"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Compute D3, ΔP3, t3 likewise"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compute t_eq = t1 + η2t2 + η3t3 + ..."] S8 --> S9["Step 9: Check longitudinal stress
σl = P x D1 / 4 t_eq <= η1 f"] S9 --> DEC2{"σl OK?"} DEC2 -- NO --> ADDCOURSE["Add another outer course
Repeat from Step 7"] DEC2 -- YES --> S10["Step 10: Check minimum thickness
t_min = D/500 + 2 mm"] S10 --> S11["Step 11: Add corrosion allowance
t_final = t + c"] S11 --> END(["END: Output t1, t2, t3 ... and n"]) ``` --- ## 4.3 Stress Distribution Block Diagram (Hoop Stress Path) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STRESS["Hoop Stress Distribution Across Course Walls"] PAXL["σh in Course 1 = P D1 / 2 t1"] PAX2["σh in Course 2 = ΔP2 x D2 / 2 t2"] PAX3["σh in Course 3 = ΔP3 x D3 / 2 t3"] EQOK["All equal to ηi x f
under EQUAL STRESS design"] end PAXL --> EQOK PAX2 --> EQOK PAX3 --> EQOK ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above represent a **block-level functional architecture** of how pressure, stress, and thickness flow through each successive course — analogous to a "voltage divider" in electrical engineering. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the conditions under which a multi-course construction is preferred over a single-plate pressure vessel as per IS 2825.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A multi-course construction is preferred when: 1. The required shell thickness from the formula `t = PD / (2ηf − P)` **exceeds the maximum thickness of commercially available rolled plates** (≈ 32 mm for carbon steel). 2. **Welding economy**: thick single plates demand pre-heating and PWHT, which is expensive — splitting into thinner courses is more weld-friendly. 3. **Reduced residual stresses** during fabrication. 4. **Better availability of material** in thinner gauges. 5. **Improved safety** — vessel does not fail catastrophically if one course fails. *(Each correct point = ½ mark × 5 = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the role of joint efficiency (η) in multi-course vessel design as per IS 2825?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Joint efficiency (η) is the **ratio of strength of the welded joint to the strength of the unwelded base metal**, and it accounts for the weakening effect of welds on the shell. 1. IS 2825 specifies three vessel classes with different η: - **Class 1** (full radiography) → η = 1.0 - **Class 2** (spot radiography) → η = 0.95 - **Class 3** (no radiography) → η = 0.85 2. In multi-course design, the **inner course is assigned η₁ = 1.0** because its longitudinal welds are fully radiographed. 3. **Outer courses carry progressively lower joint efficiencies** (η₂ = 0.85, η₃ = 0.80, etc.) because their girth joints are not as critically inspected. 4. Joint efficiency directly enters the thickness formula: `t = P·D / (2ηf − P)`. Lower η ⇒ greater thickness required. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **Note:** KTU ESE Part B offers **Module-level internal choice**. Below, two alternative 14-mark questions are provided — *Answer either A or B*. --- ### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* — *Two-Course Vessel Design* A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel is to be designed for the following service: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Internal diameter | `D₁ = 1500 mm` | | Design pressure | `P = 4.0 N/mm²` (40 bar) | | Design temperature | `250 °C` | | Material | SA-516 Grade 70 | | Allowable stress `f` @ 250 °C | `130 N/mm²` | | Joint efficiency, inner course | `η₁ = 1.0` | | Joint efficiency, outer course | `η₂ = 0.85` | | Corrosion allowance | `c = 1.5 mm` | **Design the vessel as a two-course shell. Compute: (a) thicknesses of both courses, and (b) verify longitudinal stress.** #### **Part (a) — Thicknesses of Both Courses (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Single-course thickness check:** $$ t_{single} = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \eta_1 f - P} = \frac{4.0 \times 1500}{2 \times 1.0 \times 130 - 4.0} = \frac{6000}{256} = 23.44 \text{ mm} $$ Since `23.44 < 32 mm`, technically a single course would suffice — but the question mandates a two-course design. We continue. **Step 2 — Inner course thickness (Course 1):** $$ t_1 = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \eta_1 f - P} = 23.44 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{adopt } t_1 = 24 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Outer diameter of Course 1 = Inner diameter of Course 2:** $$ D_2 = D_1 + 2 t_1 = 1500 + 48 = 1548 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Differential pressure on Course 2:** $$ \Delta P_2 = P \cdot \frac{2 t_1}{D_2} = 4.0 \times \frac{2 \times 24}{1548} = 0.1240 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Thickness of Course 2:** $$ t_2 = \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot D_2}{2 \eta_2 f - \Delta P_2} = \frac{0.1240 \times 1548}{2 \times 0.85 \times 130 - 0.1240} = \frac{191.95}{220.876} = 0.869 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6 — Apply minimum thickness rule:** $$ t_{min} = \frac{D_1}{500} + 2 = \frac{1500}{500} + 2 = 5 \text{ mm} $$ Hence adopt `t₂ = 5 mm` (minimum thickness governs). Add corrosion allowance to **Course 2 only** (inner course already accounts for it via fabrication): → **`t₁ = 24 mm`**, **`t₂ = 5 mm`** *(5 marks for derivation, 2 marks for the final answer)* #### **Part (b) — Longitudinal Stress Verification (7 marks)** **Step 7 — Equivalent thickness:** $$ t_{eq} = t_1 + \eta_2 \cdot t_2 = 24 + 0.85 \times 5 = 28.25 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 8 — Longitudinal stress:** $$ \sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{4 \, t_{eq}} = \frac{4.0 \times 1500}{4 \times 28.25} = \frac{6000}{113} = 53.10 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 9 — Compare with allowable:** $$ \eta_1 \cdot f = 1.0 \times 130 = 130 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ Since `53.10 < 130` → **`σ_l` is within permissible limits** ✅ **Design is safe. Use `t₁ = 24 mm`, `t₂ = 5 mm`, both with Class-1 inner and Class-2 outer.** *(7 marks for full longitudinal check)* --- ### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]* — *Three-Course Vessel Design* A vertical multi-course cylindrical pressure vessel is to be designed for an ammonia converter with the following data: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Inside diameter | `D₁ = 1200 mm` | | Internal pressure | `P = 8.0 N/mm²` | | Allowable stress | `f = 125 N/mm²` | | Joint efficiencies | `η₁ = 1.0, η₂ = 0.95, η₃ = 0.85` | | Corrosion allowance | `1.5 mm` | **Determine: (a) thicknesses of all three courses using the equal-hoop-stress method, and (b) the longitudinal stress and the total shell thickness.** #### **Part (a) — Three-Course Thickness Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Inner course (Course 1):** $$ t_1 = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{2 \eta_1 f - P} = \frac{8 \times 1200}{2 \times 1.0 \times 125 - 8} = \frac{9600}{242} = 39.67 \text{ mm} $$ Adopt `t₁ = 40 mm`. **Step 2 — `D₂ = D₁ + 2 t₁ = 1200 + 80 = 1280 mm`** **Step 3 — Differential pressure on Course 2:** $$ \Delta P_2 = P \cdot \frac{2 t_1}{D_2} = 8 \times \frac{80}{1280} = 0.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Thickness of Course 2:** $$ t_2 = \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot D_2}{2 \eta_2 f - \Delta P_2} = \frac{0.5 \times 1280}{2 \times 0.95 \times 125 - 0.5} = \frac{640}{237} = 2.70 \text{ mm} $$ Apply minimum thickness rule: `t_min = 1200/500 + 2 = 4.4 mm` → adopt `t₂ = 5 mm` (next standard gauge). **Step 5 — `D₃ = D₂ + 2 t₂ = 1280 + 10 = 1290 mm`** **Step 6 — Differential pressure on Course 3:** $$ \Delta P_3 = \Delta P_2 \cdot \frac{2 t_2}{D_3} = 0.5 \times \frac{10}{1290} = 3.876 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Thickness of Course 3:** $$ t_3 = \frac{\Delta P_3 \cdot D_3}{2 \eta_3 f - \Delta P_3} = \frac{0.003876 \times 1290}{2 \times 0.85 \times 125 - 0.003876} = \frac{5.00}{212.50} = 0.0235 \text{ mm} $$ → governed by `t_min` → adopt `t₃ = 5 mm`. > ✅ **Final design:** `t₁ = 40 mm`, `t₂ = 5 mm`, `t₃ = 5 mm`. *(7 marks — full derivation; marks split: 3 for t₁, 2 for t₂, 2 for t₃)* #### **Part (b) — Longitudinal Stress & Total Thickness (7 marks)** **Step 8 — Equivalent shell thickness:** $$ t_{eq} = t_1 + \eta_2 t_2 + \eta_3 t_3 = 40 + 0.95 \times 5 + 0.85 \times 5 = 40 + 4.75 + 4.25 = 49.0 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 9 — Longitudinal stress:** $$ \sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D_1}{4 \, t_{eq}} = \frac{8 \times 1200}{4 \times 49} = \frac{9600}{196} = 48.98 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 10 — Allowable check:** $$ \eta_1 \cdot f = 1.0 \times 125 = 125 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 48.98 < 125 \checkmark $$ **Step 11 — Total shell thickness:** $$ t_{total} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 40 + 5 + 5 = 50 \text{ mm} $$ > ✅ **Conclusion:** Three-course vessel with `t₁ = 40 mm, t₂ = t₃ = 5 mm` is safe in both hoop and longitudinal directions. *(7 marks — 4 for longitudinal calc, 3 for total thickness and conclusion)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply `t_min = D/500 + 2 mm`** — students often compute small `t₂` and `t₃` values (< 2 mm) and stop. Always apply minimum thickness rule (IS 2825 Cl. 5.4.4). > 2. **Ignoring joint efficiency for outer courses** — using η = 1.0 for *all* courses is wrong. Outer courses must use lower η (0.95 or 0.85). > 3. **Using `D` (outer diameter) instead of `D₁` (inner diameter)** in hoop stress for inner course — this introduces large error. > 4. **Not staggering longitudinal joints by 90°** between adjacent courses — IS 2825 mandatory requirement. > 5. **Forgetting the longitudinal stress check** — hoop stress may be safe, but `σ_l` in inner course can exceed allowable if outer courses are not counted in `t_eq`. > 6. **Not adding corrosion allowance** to thickness — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 7. **Forgetting to state adopted (standard plate) thickness**, not just calculated value. --- ## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST | # | Key Takeaway | |---|---| | 1 | Multi-course construction is mandated by IS 2825 when **single-plate thickness > 32 mm**. | | 2 | The **inner course** always carries the **full internal pressure `P`** and is assigned **η = 1.0**. | | 3 | Each outer course carries only a **differential pressure `ΔP_i`**, not the full pressure. | | 4 | IS 2825 thickness formula: **`t = P·D / (2ηf − P)`** (pressure correction included). | | 5 | The **differential pressure** on outer course: `ΔP_i = P_{i-1} · (2 t_{i-1}) / D_i`. | | 6 | **Equivalent shell thickness** for longitudinal check: `t_eq = t₁ + η₂t₂ + η₃t₃ + ...` | | 7 | Longitudinal stress check: `σ_l = P·D₁ / (4·t_eq) ≤ η₁·f` — **often the binding constraint**. | | 8 | **Minimum thickness rule**: `t_min = D/500 + 2 mm` (always enforced). | | 9 | **Joint efficiency** decreases from inner (η=1.0) to outer courses (η=0.85). | | 10 | **Maximum 4 courses** permitted in any cylindrical shell per IS 2825. | | 11 | **Equal hoop stress design** is the most economical and is the IS 2825 default. | | 12 | Longitudinal joints of **adjacent courses must be staggered by ≥ 90°**. | | 13 | Always **add corrosion allowance** to the final adopted thickness. | | 14 | Multi-course vessels are **lighter, cheaper, safer** than equivalent single-thick-plate vessels. | | 15 | The design is governed by the **more critical of hoop and longitudinal stress** — usually longitudinal when outer courses have low η. |

topics.title: Estimation of Thickness of Shells Subject to Internal and External Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Thickness of Shells Subject to Internal and External Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shell thickness estimation** is the foundational mechanical design calculation performed during the incipient stage of any pressure-containing process equipment (columns, reactors, heat-exchanger shells, storage drums, separators, etc.). It is the analytical procedure by which a design engineer selects a **minimum required wall thickness** such that the metallic envelope can safely contain or resist the design pressure conditions imposed by the process fluid, while simultaneously accounting for material degradation, fabrication imperfections, and code-stipulated safety margins.

In the strict lexicon of **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII, Division 1 (UG-27 to UG-32)**, this is termed the **"required thickness"** (`t_req`) — the theoretical minimum derived from membrane-stress analysis that must subsequently be combined with **corrosion allowance** (`CA`) and **mill tolerance** (`mm`) to arrive at the **ordered/computed thickness** (`t_order`) which is rounded up to the nearest commercially available plate gauge.

Formally stated:

$$
t_{order} = \lceil t_{req} + CA + mm \rceil_{nominal\ plate\ gauge}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** The syllabus distinguishes sharply between **Internal Pressure Loading** (where the shell is in **bi-axial membrane tension**) and **External Pressure Loading** (where the shell is in **compression** and is susceptible to **elastic instability / shell buckling**). The governing design philosophies for these two regimes are fundamentally different — internal pressure design is **strength-controlled**, whereas external pressure design is **stability-controlled**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon vs. Thin Drinking Straw" Intuition

Imagine two everyday objects subjected to pressurized fluid:

- **Internal Pressure Analogy — The Balloon:** When you inflate a balloon, the rubber membrane experiences a uniform tensile pull in every direction. The rubber stretches but does not collapse. The wall thickness required is purely a function of how hard you blow (`P`), how big the balloon is (`D`), and how strong the rubber is (`S`). A bigger balloon, higher pressure, or weaker material → thicker wall. This is the **tensile (strength) regime** and is straightforward.

- **External Pressure Analogy — The Drinking Straw:** A thin-walled paper/cardboard straw resists a squeezing force from outside (your fingers) reasonably well initially. But squeeze it harder, or make the straw slightly longer, or change the wall material to be more flexible, and suddenly it **buckles** and collapses with a loud CRACK — even though the material itself never yielded! This is **buckling (stability)**, and it happens at stresses far below the material's yield strength. External pressure shells behave like this straw.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters:** A naive engineer might design an external-pressure vessel using the same tensile formula used for internal pressure. This is the **single most dangerous error** in pressure-vessel design because the resulting shell will fail catastrophically by **elastic instability** at a pressure often **70–85% lower** than predicted by tensile-strength formulas alone.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standardized parameters appear in every thickness-estimation equation:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Definition | Typical Value / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Design Pressure** | `P` (or `P_design`) | Maximum expected working pressure + safety margin (usually 10% or 1 bar, whichever is greater) | **bar** or **MPa** |
| **Inside Diameter** | `D_i` (or `D`) | Internal diameter of the vessel | **mm** or **m** |
| **Outside Diameter** | `D_o` | External diameter of the vessel (`D_o = D_i + 2t`) | **mm** or **m** |
| **Allowable Stress** | `S` | Code-permitted stress = `S_m` (specified min. stress) / safety factor | **MPa** |
| **Joint Efficiency** | `E` | Weld quality factor — accounts for radiography | 0.70 – **1.00** (dimensionless) |
| **Corrosion Allowance** | `CA` | Extra thickness provided for expected material loss over service life | **1.5 – 3.0 mm** typical |
| **Mill Tolerance** | `mm` | Minus tolerance on rolled plates (permitted by the rolling mill) | **0.0 mm** (for plate ordered to nominal thickness in some codes) |
| **Modulus of Elasticity** | `E_mod` | Young's Modulus of the shell material at design temperature | **MPa** (e.g., CS at 38 °C ≈ 1.95 × 10⁵ MPa) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Design Pressure Rule:** `P_design` must always be the **maximum** of:
> (a) the maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP) at the coincident design temperature,
> (b) the set pressure of any pressure-relieving device × 1.10 (10% accumulation),
> (c) the pressure developed under any upset or failure condition (e.g., blocked-outlet, external fire).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic for Internal Pressure — Step-by-Step Framework

When a thin cylindrical shell is subjected to an internal pressure, the force balance across a diametrical section produces two principal **membrane stresses**:

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on a longitudinal half-section (diameter cut):**
The internal pressure acting on the projected area `D × L × P` is resisted by the tensile hoop stress `σ_h` acting over two wall sections `2 × t × L`.

**Step 2 — Solve for hoop stress:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P D}{2 t}
$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal stress (cut perpendicular to axis):**

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{P D}{4 t}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the governing stress:**
Always `σ_h = 2 σ_L`, therefore the **hoop (circumferential) stress governs** the design of cylindrical shells under internal pressure.

**Step 5 — Apply the allowable-stress design rule with weld efficiency:**

$$
\sigma_h \le \frac{S E}{1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P}
$$

The `-1.2P` term is the **ASME correction** that acknowledges the small but non-zero contribution of longitudinal stress to the stress at the point where the code equation is applied (it is not a true 2:1 combination — see ASME note for `P/SE > 0.385` cases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Equivalence Note (KTU Expected):**
> - **ASME VIII Div 1 (UG-27):** `t = PD / (2SE - 1.2P)` — for `t < 0.5R` (thin shell)
> - **IS 2825 (Indian Standard, mirrors ASME):** Same expression as above
> - **Simple/Quick form for very low pressures:** `t = PR/SE` (valid when `P << SE`)

### 2.2 Operational Logic for External Pressure — Stability Framework

External pressure does not cause yielding in the classical sense. It causes **elastic shell buckling**, a geometric instability governed by the **modulus of elasticity (`E_mod`)** rather than yield strength. The design procedure is iterative and chart-based (using ASME VIII Div 1 Figure G — the geometric chart for cylindrical shells, and Figure HA-2 for spherical shells).

**Step 1 — Assume a trial thickness** `t_trial` (start with `t_req` from internal-pressure calculation as a lower bound).

**Step 2 — Calculate the ratio `L_e / D_o`** where `L_e` is the effective length (shell length between stiffening rings or heads) and `D_o = D_i + 2 t_trial`.

**Step 3 — Calculate the ratio `D_o / t_trial`.**

**Step 4 — Enter the ASME geometric chart with `L_e / D_o`** and find the **geometric factor `A`**.

**Step 5 — Calculate factor `B`:**

$$
B = \frac{2 A E_{mod}}{3 \left(\dfrac{D_o}{t}\right)}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-enter the chart with `B`** to find **factor `A_2`**, then calculate the **maximum allowable external pressure**:

$$
P_a = \frac{4 B}{3 \left(\dfrac{D_o}{t}\right)}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the design:**

$$
P_a \ge P_{design} \quad \text{(with appropriate factor of safety, typically } F.S. = 3.0 \text{ for buckling)}
$$

If `P_a < P_design`, **increase `t_trial`** or **add stiffener rings** (reduce `L_e`) and **iterate**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Loading | Governing Equation | Applicable Code Clause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cylindrical shell** | Internal | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P}$ | ASME UG-27 / IS 2825 |
| 2 | **Cylindrical shell** | Internal (low-P approximation) | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P R}{S E}$ | Quick check (P/SE ≤ 0.04) |
| 3 | **Hemispherical head** | Internal | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P D}{4 S E - 0.4 P}$ | ASME UG-32 |
| 4 | **Spherical shell** | Internal | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P D}{4 S E - 0.4 P}$ | ASME UG-27 |
| 5 | **Ellipsoidal 2:1 head** | Internal | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | ASME UG-32 |
| 6 | **Cylindrical shell** | External (buckling) | $\displaystyle P_a = \frac{4 B}{3 (D_o / t)}$ | ASME UG-28 |
| 7 | **Spherical shell** | External (buckling) | $\displaystyle P_a = \frac{2 B}{3 (D_o / t)}$ | ASME UG-28 |
| 8 | **Geometric factor B (cyl.)** | External | $\displaystyle B = \frac{2 A E_{mod}}{3 (D_o / t)}$ | ASME UG-28 |
| 9 | **Torispherical head** | Internal | $\displaystyle t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} \cdot \frac{M}{2}$ | ASME UG-32 (with factor M) |
| 10 | **Ordered thickness** | Both | $\displaystyle t_{order} = t_{req} + CA + mm$ | Best practice |
| 11 | **Hoop stress (membrane)** | Internal | $\displaystyle \sigma_h = \frac{P D}{2 t}$ | Membrane theory |
| 12 | **Longitudinal stress** | Internal | $\displaystyle \sigma_L = \frac{P D}{4 t}$ | Membrane theory |
| 13 | **Knuckle radius (torispherical)** | Internal | $\displaystyle r_k = 0.06 D + \text{depth} \cdot 0.18$ (typical) | ASME UG-32 |
| 14 | **Effective length (cyl.)** | External | $\displaystyle L_e = \min(L_{shell},\ L_{uncorrected})$ adjusted for heads | ASME UG-28 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When writing equations inside markdown tables, never use the vertical bar `|` character as it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` (LaTeX) for absolute-value brackets, as shown above for `\vert B \vert` style entries if needed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Refineries:** Crude distillation columns (often 5–8 m diameter, 30–50 m tall) operating under 1–4 barg internal pressure require careful thickness calculations. External pressure considerations dominate in **vacuum columns** (operate at -1 barg with steam ejectors).
- **Cryogenic Storage:** LNG tanks operate at near-vacuum external pressure with stiffening ring arrays.
- **Pharmaceutical Reactors:** Internal pressure design dominates, with high `E` (1.0) due to full radiography of all category-A welds.
- **Subsea Pipelines & Submersibles:** Pure external pressure with hydrostatic loading — buckling design dominates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell (Internal Pressure)

**Setting:** Consider a thin-walled cylindrical shell of inside diameter `D`, wall thickness `t`, and an arbitrary axial length `L`, subjected to an internal pressure `P`. The shell wall is assumed to be **thin** (`t < D/10`, though ASME allows up to `t ≤ 0.5 R`).

**Step 1 — Free-body cut along a diametrical plane.**
Cut the cylinder longitudinally along its full length `L` to obtain a semi-cylindrical shell. The internal pressure acts on the **projected rectangular area** of the cut:

$$
A_{projected} = D \cdot L
$$

The resultant **axial thrust** (force pushing the two halves apart) is:

$$
F_{axial} = P \cdot A_{projected} = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the resisting area.**
The membrane tensile stress `σ_h` (hoop stress) acts uniformly across the **two cut wall edges**, each having cross-sectional area:

$$
A_{wall} = 2 \cdot (t \cdot L) = 2 t L
$$

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium (`ΣF = 0`).**
The total tensile resisting force must balance the axial thrust from the pressure:

$$
\sigma_h \cdot (2 t L) = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for `σ_h`:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D \cdot L}{2 \cdot t \cdot L}
$$

$$
