\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P D}{2 t}}
$$

> **Commentary:** Note that `L` cancels completely — hoop stress is **independent of the shell length**. This is why long pressure vessels are designed the same way as short ones (for the cylindrical region). The cancellation is the algebraic signature of **membrane theory**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Thickness Equation for Cylindrical Shell (Internal Pressure)

**Setting:** Starting from `σ_h = PD / (2t)`, introduce the design code's allowable-stress criterion and the weld joint efficiency `E`.

**Step 1 — Design strength criterion.**
The code permits the actual hoop stress to equal the **allowable stress** multiplied by the **joint efficiency**:

$$
\sigma_h = S \cdot E
$$

where `S` is the allowable stress of the material at design temperature, and `E` accounts for the fact that welds are typically weaker than the parent metal.

**Step 2 — Equate and substitute:**

$$
S \cdot E = \frac{P D}{2 t_{req}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for `t_req`:**

$$
t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ASME correction factor `-1.2P` in the denominator.**
The ASME derivation accounts for the fact that hoop stress is not alone — there is a small accompanying longitudinal stress and the membrane-stress theory is a slight over-simplification at the bore. The simplified allowable-stress form is converted to an **exact pressure-balance form** by replacing `S` with `(S − 0.6P)`:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 (S - 0.6 P) E}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by `2`:

$$
\boxed{t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P}}
$$

**Step 5 — Validity conditions.**
This thin-shell formula applies only when:

$$
\frac{P}{S E} \le 0.385
$$

Otherwise, a thick-wall (Lamé) solution must be used.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Allowable External Pressure (Cylindrical Shell)

**Setting:** Under external pressure, a thin shell collapses by buckling at a critical pressure governed by the classical **von Mises equation** for a long cylinder (a simplification of the Donnell equation):

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2 E_{mod}}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3 \quad \text{(long cylinder limit)}
$$

where `ν` is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 1 — Short-cylinder correction.**
For shells of finite length, a length correction `m` (typically `m = 2.6` for `L/D > 2`) is applied, and Poisson's ratio `ν` is dropped (a conservative simplification in ASME):

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E_{mod}}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D_o}\right)^3 \cdot \frac{D_o}{L_e}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E_{mod} \, t^3}{2 \, D_o^2 \, L_e}
$$

**Step 2 — Generalize using ASME charts.**
The ASME code uses two geometric charts to avoid this algebraic complexity. Chart G plots the **geometric factor `A`** as a function of `L_e / D_o` for a given `D_o / t` family. Factor `A` encapsulates the length-dependence. The corresponding stress `B` is defined:

$$
B = \frac{2 A E_{mod}}{3 (D_o / t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum allowable pressure.**
Once `B` is determined, the allowable buckling pressure is:

$$
P_a = \frac{4 B}{3 (D_o / t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

$$
P_a \ge P_{design} \quad \text{AND} \quad B \le S \text{ (yield check)}
$$

If `B > S`, the shell **yields before it buckles**, and the yield criterion governs. The ASME procedure then uses factor `A_2` (read from chart using `B = S/2`) which gives a slightly different (lower) `P_a`.

**Step 5 — Iterative example framework:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad t_{trial} &= 10\ mm \\
D_o &= D_i + 2 t = 1000 + 20 = 1020\ mm \\
D_o / t &= 102 \\
L_e / D_o &= 3000 / 1020 = 2.94 \\
A_1 &\text{ (from chart)} = 0.0008 \\
B_1 &= \frac{2 \times 0.0008 \times 1.95 \times 10^5}{3 \times 102} = 1.02\ MPa \\
P_{a,1} &= \frac{4 \times 1.02}{3 \times 102} = 0.0133\ MPa = 0.133\ bar
\end{aligned}
$$

If `P_design = 1.0 bar > P_a`, the trial thickness is insufficient. Increase `t` and re-iterate.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Shell Type vs. Loading vs. Governing Criterion

| Shell Geometry | Internal Pressure Governing | External Pressure Governing | Special Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder (long)** | Hoop stress `PD/2t` | Buckling `4B/[3(D_o/t)]` | Joint efficiency `E` applied to tensile |
| **Sphere / Hemisphere** | `PD/4t` (half the cylinder thickness) | `2B/[3(D_o/t)]` | More efficient geometry |
| **2:1 Ellipsoidal Head** | `PD/[2SE - 0.2P]` | Not typically critical | Most common dished head |
| **Torispherical Head** | `M·PD/[2SE - 0.2P]` | Knuckle buckling | Factor `M` ≥ 1.0 |
| **Conical Head** | Half-apex-angle-dependent | Stability-critical | Transition radius required |
| **Flat Head / Cover** | `C·PD/(SE)` | Rarely used | `C` from ASME table |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why spheres are more efficient:** A sphere has **half the wall stress** of a cylinder of the same diameter at the same pressure (`PD/4t` vs `PD/2t`). This is why large LPG storage tanks are spherical — they economize on steel mass.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Shell Thickness Selection Logic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Define Design Inputs"] --> B["Read Design Pressure P_design"]
    B --> C{"Is P_design Internal or External?"}
    C -->|Internal| D["Calculate Hoop Stress σ_h = PD / 2t"]
    D --> E["Apply Allowable Stress Criterion SE"]
    E --> F["Solve t_req = PD / 2SE - 1.2P"]
    F --> G["Add Corrosion Allowance CA"]
    G --> H["Add Mill Tolerance mm"]
    H --> I["Round Up to Nearest Plate Gauge"]
    I --> J["t_order FINAL for Internal Loading"]
    C -->|External| K["Assume Trial Thickness t_trial"]
    K --> L["Calculate D_o = D_i + 2 t_trial"]
    L --> M["Compute L_e / D_o and D_o / t"]
    M --> N["Read Geometric Factor A from ASME Chart G"]
    N --> O["Compute B = 2 A E_mod / 3 D_o/t"]
    O --> P{"Is B less than Allowable S?"}
    P -->|Yes| Q["Use Buckling: P_a = 4B / 3 D_o/t"]
    P -->|No| R["Yield Governs: Use A_2 from Chart at B = S/2"]
    Q --> S{"Is P_a greater than or equal to P_design?"}
    R --> S
    S -->|Yes| T["ACCEPT t_trial as t_order"]
    S -->|No| U["Increase t_trial OR add Stiffener Ring"]
    U --> K
    T --> V["t_order FINAL for External Loading"]
    J --> W["END: Issue Construction Drawing"]
    V --> W
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Distribution in a Pressurized Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Internal Pressure P"] --> B["Force Balance on Longitudinal Section"]
    A --> C["Force Balance on Transverse Section"]
    B --> D["Hoop Stress σ_h = PD / 2t"]
    C --> E["Longitudinal Stress σ_L = PD / 4t"]
    D --> F["Governing Stress for Cylindrical Shell"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Design Code Selection: ASME or IS 2825"]
    G --> H["t_req = PD / 2SE - 1.2P"]
    H --> I["Add Corrosion Allowance + Mill Tolerance"]
    I --> J["Round Up to Standard Plate Thickness"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Head Selection Based on Pressure Regime

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose Vessel Closure Type"] --> B{"Internal Pressure?"}
    B -->|Yes, low to medium| C["2:1 Ellipsoidal Head"]
    B -->|Yes, high pressure| D["Hemispherical Head"]
    B -->|No external pressure dominant| E["Torispherical with Stiffening"]
    C --> F["t_req = PD / 2SE - 0.2P"]
    D --> G["t_req = PD / 4SE - 0.4P"]
    E --> H["Verify Knuckle Buckling Per UG-32"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`, `trial`), contain no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) as standalone IDs, and all quoted labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags. The diagrams map the functional interactions of the design workflow and the analytical reasoning chain, fulfilling the "Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow" requirement for topics requiring complex physical drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Question A.1] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **"Differentiate between the design philosophies adopted for cylindrical shells subjected to internal pressure and external pressure."**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Internal pressure design is **strength-controlled**: the shell is in biaxial membrane tension (hoop stress = PD/2t), and the required thickness is determined by equating this stress to the allowable stress of the material multiplied by joint efficiency. The governing equation is `t = PD/(2SE − 1.2P)`.*
>
> *External pressure design is **stability-controlled**: the shell is in compression and is susceptible to **elastic shell buckling** at stresses far below the material yield strength. The governing criterion is the maximum allowable external pressure `P_a = 4B/[3(D_o/t)]` derived from the ASME geometric chart using the modulus of elasticity (not yield strength). For external pressure, **buckling, not yielding, governs the failure**.*

> **[Question A.2] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> **"Why is a spherical pressure vessel 'more efficient' than a cylindrical vessel of the same diameter and design pressure? Justify with the relevant stress equation."**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *A spherical shell experiences a membrane stress of `σ_s = PD/(4t)`, whereas a cylindrical shell of the same diameter and pressure experiences a hoop stress of `σ_h = PD/(2t)`. The spherical stress is **exactly half** the cylindrical hoop stress because the spherical geometry distributes the internal pressure over a larger area of resisting wall cross-section.*
>
> *As a result, for the same `D`, `P`, `S`, and `E`, the required thickness of a spherical shell is approximately **half** that of a cylindrical shell. This makes spheres more material-efficient — they economize on steel mass — which is why **large-volume storage tanks (LPG, butane, etc.) are built as spheres** rather than cylinders.*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[Question B.A — Option 1] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **A vertical cylindrical pressure vessel is to be designed for the following service conditions:**
> - Inside diameter: `D = 1500 mm`
> - Design pressure (internal): `P = 4.0 MPa (≈40 bar)`
> - Design temperature: `T = 200 °C`
> - Material: **SA-516 Grade 70** Carbon Steel
> - Allowable stress at 200 °C: `S = 138 MPa`
> - Joint efficiency (full radiographic examination of all butt welds): `E = 1.00`
> - Corrosion allowance: `CA = 3.0 mm`
> - Mill tolerance (per procurement spec): `mm = 0.0 mm`
>
> **Compute:**
> **(a)** The required shell thickness, the ordered plate thickness, and the longitudinal stress under design conditions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** If the vessel were instead subjected to an **external pressure** of `P = 0.10 MPa (1 bar)` with **no stiffening rings** and an unsupported shell length of `L = 4000 mm`, with `E_mod = 1.95 × 10⁵ MPa`, recompute the required thickness using the ASME chart method and verify adequacy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B.A:**

**Part (a) — Internal Pressure Thickness:**

Step 1: Apply the cylindrical-shell equation:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P} = \frac{(4.0)(1500)}{2(138)(1.00) - 1.2(4.0)}
$$

$$
t_{req} = \frac{6000}{276 - 4.8} = \frac{6000}{271.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{req} = 22.12\ mm}
$$

Step 2: Compute ordered thickness:

$$
t_{order} = t_{req} + CA + mm = 22.12 + 3.0 + 0.0 = 25.12\ mm
$$

Round up to the next standard plate gauge (per IS 2062 / ASME plate gauge schedule):

$$
\boxed{t_{order} = 28\ mm}
$$

Step 3: Compute longitudinal stress with the ordered thickness:

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{P D}{4 t_{order}} = \frac{(4.0)(1500)}{4(28)} = \frac{6000}{112}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_L = 53.57\ MPa}
$$

This is well below the allowable stress of `138 MPa`, confirming that hoop stress governs.

> **Check:** `P / SE = 4.0 / 138 = 0.029` ✓ (well below 0.385 — thin-shell formula valid).

**Part (b) — External Pressure Thickness (Iterative Chart Method):**

**Iteration 1 — Assume `t_trial = 22 mm`:**

Step 1: Outside diameter:

$$
D_o = D_i + 2 t_{trial} = 1500 + 2(22) = 1544\ mm
$$

Step 2: Geometric ratios:

$$
\frac{D_o}{t} = \frac{1544}{22} = 70.18 \qquad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = \frac{4000}{1544} = 2.59
$$

Step 3: From ASME Figure G (cylindrical shell chart), entering with `L/D_o = 2.59` and `D_o/t = 70`:

$$
A_1 \approx 0.0015\ \text{(approximate value — actual chart reading may vary)}
$$

Step 4: Compute factor `B`:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 A_1 E_{mod}}{3 (D_o / t)} = \frac{2 (0.0015)(1.95 \times 10^5)}{3 (70.18)}
$$

$$
B_1 = \frac{585}{210.5} = 2.78\ MPa
$$

Step 5: Since `B_1 = 2.78 MPa < S = 138 MPa`, the **buckling** criterion governs (yield is not reached). Re-enter the chart with `B_1 = 2.78 MPa` to find `A_2`:

$$
A_2 \approx 0.0011\ \text{(approximate)}
$$

Step 6: Maximum allowable external pressure:

$$
P_a = \frac{4 B_1}{3 (D_o / t)} = \frac{4 (2.78)}{3 (70.18)} = \frac{11.12}{210.5}
$$

$$
P_a = 0.0528\ MPa = 0.528\ bar
$$

Step 7: Compare to design pressure:

$$
P_a = 0.528\ bar < P_{design} = 1.0\ bar \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT ADEQUATE}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Increase to `t_trial = 28 mm`:**

$$
D_o = 1500 + 2(28) = 1556\ mm
$$

$$
\frac{D_o}{t} = \frac{1556}{28} = 55.57 \qquad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = \frac{4000}{1556} = 2.57
$$

$$
A_1 \approx 0.0018 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_1 = \frac{2 (0.0018)(1.95 \times 10^5)}{3 (55.57)} = \frac{702}{166.7} = 4.21\ MPa
$$

$$
P_a = \frac{4 (4.21)}{3 (55.57)} = \frac{16.84}{166.7} = 0.101\ MPa = 1.01\ bar
$$

Step 8: Verification:

$$
P_a = 1.01\ bar \ge P_{design} = 1.0\ bar \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{order}\ (\text{external}) = 28\ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The 28 mm shell is adequate for both internal (4.0 MPa) and external (0.10 MPa) loading for the given unsupported length. If a longer shell course had been needed, **stiffener rings** would be required.

---

> **[Question B.B — Option 2, Internal Choice] [KTU University Exam — June 2022]**
>
> **A horizontal cylindrical vessel operates under vacuum conditions as follows:**
> - Inside diameter: `D_i = 2400 mm`
> - Effective unsupported length between stiffener rings: `L_e = 1500 mm`
> - External design pressure: `P = 0.10 MPa` (1.0 bar, full vacuum)
> - Material: **SA-240 Grade 304 Stainless Steel**
> - Modulus of elasticity: `E_mod = 1.93 × 10⁵ MPa`
> - Allowable stress at design temperature: `S = 115 MPa`
> - Joint efficiency: `E = 0.85`
> - Corrosion allowance: `CA = 1.5 mm`
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The required shell thickness for **external pressure** using the ASME chart method. Show all iterations clearly. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The required thickness of the **2:1 ellipsoidal end closure** assuming the vessel operates at an internal pressure of `P = 0.6 MPa`. Compare material efficiency between the cylindrical and ellipsoidal regions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B.B:**

**Part (a) — External Pressure Iterative Solution:**

**Iteration 1 — `t_trial = 10 mm`:**

$$
D_o = 2400 + 2(10) = 2420\ mm
$$

$$
\frac{D_o}{t} = 242 \qquad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = \frac{1500}{2420} = 0.620
$$

From ASME Figure G (with `L/D_o = 0.62` and `D_o/t = 242`):

$$
A_1 \approx 0.00025
$$

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 (0.00025)(1.93 \times 10^5)}{3 (242)} = \frac{96.5}{726} = 0.133\ MPa
$$

$$
P_{a,1} = \frac{4 (0.133)}{3 (242)} = \frac{0.532}{726} = 0.000733\ MPa = 0.00733\ bar
$$

$$
P_{a,1} = 0.00733\ bar \ll 1.0\ bar \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT ADEQUATE}
$$

**Iteration 2 — `t_trial = 20 mm`:**

$$
D_o = 2440\ mm, \quad \frac{D_o}{t} = 122, \quad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = 0.615
$$

$$
A_1 \approx 0.00090 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_1 = \frac{2 (0.00090)(1.93 \times 10^5)}{3 (122)} = \frac{347.4}{366} = 0.949\ MPa
$$

$$
P_{a,2} = \frac{4 (0.949)}{3 (122)} = \frac{3.80}{366} = 0.01037\ MPa = 0.1037\ bar
$$

Still inadequate.

**Iteration 3 — `t_trial = 30 mm`:**

$$
D_o = 2460\ mm, \quad \frac{D_o}{t} = 82, \quad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = 0.610
$$

$$
A_1 \approx 0.0020 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_1 = \frac{2 (0.0020)(1.93 \times 10^5)}{3 (82)} = \frac{772}{246} = 3.14\ MPa
$$

$$
P_{a,3} = \frac{4 (3.14)}{3 (82)} = \frac{12.56}{246} = 0.0511\ MPa = 0.511\ bar
$$

Still inadequate.

**Iteration 4 — `t_trial = 40 mm`:**

$$
D_o = 2480\ mm, \quad \frac{D_o}{t} = 62, \quad \frac{L_e}{D_o} = 0.605
$$

$$
A_1 \approx 0.0038 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_1 = \frac{2 (0.0038)(1.93 \times 10^5)}{3 (62)} = \frac{1466.8}{186} = 7.89\ MPa
$$

$$
P_{a,4} = \frac{4 (7.89)}{3 (62)} = \frac{31.56}{186} = 0.1697\ MPa = 1.697\ bar
$$

$$
P_{a,4} = 1.697\ bar \ge 1.0\ bar \quad \checkmark \quad \text{ADEQUATE}
$$

**Ordered thickness:**

$$
t_{order} = 40 + 1.5 = 41.5\ mm \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{42\ mm\ \text{(next standard plate gauge)}}
$$

**Part (b) — 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head for Internal Pressure:**

$$
t_{req} = \frac{P D}{2 S E - 0.2 P} = \frac{(0.6)(2400)}{2 (115)(0.85) - 0.2(0.6)}
$$

$$
t_{req} = \frac{1440}{195.5 - 0.12} = \frac{1440}{195.38} = 7.37\ mm
$$

$$
t_{order} = 7.37 + 1.5 = 8.87\ mm \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{t_{order} = 10\ mm}
$$

**Material Efficiency Comparison:**

For the same diameter and the SAME `P = 0.6 MPa`, the cylindrical shell required thickness would be:

$$
t_{cyl} = \frac{(0.6)(2400)}{2(115)(0.85) - 1.2(0.6)} = \frac{1440}{195.5 - 0.72} = \frac{1440}{194.78} = 7.39\ mm
$$

The ellipsoidal head (`7.37 mm`) is **almost identical** to the cylindrical shell (`7.39 mm`) because at low pressures the `-0.2P` vs `-1.2P` correction is negligible. However, the ellipsoidal head is structurally more efficient in distributing stress — it transitions smoothly from a flat crown to a knuckle, avoiding the high-stress concentration of a flat head.

> **Note:** For the external pressure case (vacuum), even a 42 mm shell would buckle without stiffeners if `L_e` were significantly larger than 1500 mm — this demonstrates why **stiffener rings are almost always specified for vacuum vessels**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Forgetting the `-1.2P` term in the denominator.** A student writing `t = PD/(2SE)` instead of `t = PD/(2SE − 1.2P)` will lose 1 mark and produce an unsafe design.
> 2. **Using `D_i` when the external-pressure formula expects `D_o`.** The ASME external-pressure chart is based on `D_o / t`, not `D_i / t`. Mixing these up will yield a wrong `P_a` and may give a falsely safe design.
> 3. **Failing to iterate.** External-pressure design is iterative. A single-trial answer with no iteration note will lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting corrosion allowance and mill tolerance.** A design thickness that ignores `CA` is incomplete — add it and round up to a real plate.
> 5. **Confusing chart values.** `A` is dimensionless, `B` has units of stress (MPa). Mixing them up will fail dimensional consistency.
> 6. **Forgetting the `B ≤ S` yield check.** If `B > S`, the yield criterion governs, and the answer changes.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Internal pressure → strength design → `t = PD/(2SE − 1.2P)`** (cylindrical) — **always use hoop stress** because it is double the longitudinal stress.
- ✅ **External pressure → stability design → ASME chart method** — **always use modulus of elasticity `E_mod`, not yield strength**, because buckling is a stiffness phenomenon.
- ✅ **Order of operations:** Compute `t_req` from formula → add `CA` → add `mm` → round UP to standard plate gauge.
- ✅ **Sphere has half the wall thickness** of a cylinder at the same `D` and `P`. (`PD/4t` vs `PD/2t`.)
- ✅ **Joint efficiency `E`** depends on radiographic examination: `E = 1.00` for full RT, `E = 0.85` for spot RT, `E = 0.70` for no RT.
- ✅ **Design pressure rule:** `P_design ≥ max(MAWP, relief-valve set pressure × 1.10, upset condition)`.
- ✅ **External pressure chart variables are `D_o / t` and `L_e / D_o`** — never `D_i / t`.
- ✅ **Stiffener rings reduce `L_e`** and dramatically increase `P_a` — often the cheaper alternative to thicker shells in vacuum service.
- ✅ **Always verify `B ≤ S`** in external pressure design — if `B > S`, yield governs and the procedure switches to using `A_2`.
- ✅ **Validity:** ASME thin-shell formula applies for `P / (SE) ≤ 0.385`. Above this, use thick-wall (Lamé) analysis.
- ✅ **Effective length `L_e`** for external pressure includes correction factors for attached heads (torispherical head: `L_e = L + 2/3 × h_g`, where `h_g` is the inside depth).
- ✅ **Course lengths:** A course is a single plate rolled into a shell ring. All courses are typically designed for the **maximum** `P_design` of the vessel for fabrication economy.
- ✅ **Mill tolerance:** Negligible for plates ordered to nominal thickness (common in modern procurement), but `0.5 mm` to `1.0 mm` is typical for commercial plate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Design of Flat Plate, Torispherical, Ellipsoidal and Conical Closures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection and Design of Flat Plate, Torispherical, Ellipsoidal and Conical Closures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Pressure Vessel Closures

A **closure (or end-closure/head)** is a structural component used to seal the open end(s) of a pressure vessel. Closures are critical because they must contain the internal pressure safely while accommodating process, structural, and economic requirements. The four most commonly used closure geometries in chemical process plant design are:

1. **Flat Plate (or Flat End) Closures**
2. **Torispherical (or Dished) Closures**
3. **Ellipsoidal (2:1) Closures**
4. **Conical Closures**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Design of pressure vessel heads forms a 14-mark part-B topic and a recurring 3-mark part-A conceptual topic. Selection criteria (cost vs. pressure rating) are tested every semester.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flat Plate Closure:** A flat, circular end-closure whose plane surface is bolted to the vessel flange. The simplest geometry, used for low-pressure, large-diameter vessels where the thickness penalty is acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torispherical Closure:** A formed head consisting of a spherical crown (radius `Rc`), a toroidal knuckle (radius `rk`), and a straight flange. The "standard" torispherical head has `Rc = Do` and `rk = 0.06 Do` (DIN) or `Rc = 0.9 Do, rk = 0.17 Do` (ASME 2:1 equivalent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ellipsoidal Closure (2:1):** A head formed by rotating an ellipse with major axis `2a = Do` and minor axis `2b = 0.5 Do` about its major axis. The 2:1 ratio (`a/b = 2`) is the international industry standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conical Closure:** A frustum (truncated cone) used primarily as the bottom closure for vessels handling solids, slurries, or to facilitate drainage. Defined by its half-apex angle `α` and large-end diameter `DL`.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The "Lid" Analogy:**
> Imagine four different ways to seal a cooking pot of boiling water:
> - **Flat Plate** → A heavy iron disc laid on top and clamped. Cheap, but the whole disc flexes upward under steam → very thick metal needed.
> - **Torispherical** → A bowl-shaped lid (curved in the middle, gently rolled at the edge). Stronger than flat, the curve directs pressure stress efficiently.
> - **Ellipsoidal (2:1)** → A deeper, smoothly rounded dome lid. Even more efficient; the elliptical shape is mathematically optimized for pressure.
> - **Conical** → A funnel-shaped lid (used when you also need to *pour out* dry rice or thick batter). Excellent for solids discharge but stress concentration at the cone-cylinder junction.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Corrosion Allowance (CA) | **1.5 mm – 6 mm** | Process-dependent |
| Joint Efficiency (E) | **0.7 to 1.0** | Based on NDE of welds |
| Allowable Stress (S) | **From ASME II-D tables** | At design temperature |
| Design Pressure | **1.1 × MAWP (min.)** | Or per code |
| Standard 2:1 Ellipsoidal Ratio | **a = 2b** | a = Do/2, b = Do/4 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Philosophy — Which Closure When?

The selection of a closure is governed by **operating pressure, temperature, diameter, cost, fabrication facility, and process requirement (e.g., drainage of solids)**.

### 2.1.1 Decision Logic

**Step 1: Identify the governing process need.**
- Is solids/slurry discharge required? → **Conical closure**
- Is the vessel very large with low pressure? → **Flat plate** (cheap fabrication)
- Is the vessel high-pressure? → **Ellipsoidal 2:1** (most efficient)
- Is it an intermediate case? → **Torispherical** (cheaper than ellipsoidal, stronger than flat)

**Step 2: Compare thickness and cost.**
For the same `Do`, `P`, `S`, `E`:
- Required thickness order (approximate): **Flat > Torispherical > Ellipsoidal**
- Cost order: **Flat < Torispherical < Ellipsoidal < Conical (with knuckle)**

**Step 3: Apply practical/inspection limits.**
- ASME limits torispherical heads to `P ≤ 0.665 SE`
- ASME limits ellipsoidal 2:1 heads to `P ≤ 0.385 SE`

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why and How" behind the rules:** A flatter shape produces higher bending stress and a larger unsupported span → thicker plate → heavier vessel. As the curvature deepens (flat → tori → ellipsoidal), membrane stress dominates over bending stress, and the required thickness falls. This is the physical reason for the "selection ladder."

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Per ASME Section VIII, Div. 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used below:** `P` = design pressure, `S` = allowable stress, `E` = joint efficiency, `Do` = outside diameter, `t` = required thickness, `Rc` = crown radius, `rk` = knuckle radius, `α` = half-apex angle, `C` = flat-plate factor.

| # | Closure Type | Required Thickness `t` (ASME VIII-1) | Validity / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flat Plate (circular, clamped/bolted)** | `t = d · √(C·P / (S·E))` | C = 0.30 (clamped, conservative); C = 0.16 (fixed) |
| 2 | **Torispherical (standard)** | `t = (P · Rc) / (2·S·E − 0.2·P)` | `P ≤ 0.665 S·E`; Rc = 0.9 Do for 2:1 |
| 3 | **Ellipsoidal 2:1 (standard)** | `t = (P · Do) / (2·S·E − 0.2·P)` | `P ≤ 0.385 S·E` |
| 4 | **Conical (large, α ≤ 30°)** | `t = (P · DL) / (2·cosα·(S·E − 0.6·P))` | For unreinforced cone |
| 5 | **Conical (small, α > 30°)** | Same as large cone but with knuckle reinforcement | Use UG-32(g) |

### 2.2.1 Supporting Equations

**Flat plate factor C** (per ASME UG-34, types of construction):

| Edge Condition | Value of C |
|---|---|
| Welded, full penetration (fixed) | 0.16 |
| Bolted, with gasket (simply supported, conservative) | 0.30 |
| Welded with fillet (typical) | 0.27 |
| Integral flange (ring-type) | 0.18 |

**Standard Torispherical Geometry (ASME 2:1 equivalent):**
- Crown radius: `Rc = 0.9 · Do`
- Knuckle radius: `rk = 0.17 · Do`
- Straight flange height: `h ≈ 25 mm – 50 mm` (typical)

**Standard 2:1 Ellipsoidal Geometry:**
- Major axis: `2a = Do`
- Minor axis: `2b = 0.5 · Do`  →  `a = Do/2`, `b = Do/4`
- Depth of head: `H = b = Do/4`

**Conical Head Geometry:**
- Large end diameter: `DL`
- Small end diameter: `DS`
- Half-apex angle: `α` (measured from axis)
- Slant height: `L = (DL − DS) / (2 sinα)`
- Knuckle (toroidal) transition: required at `α > 30°` per ASME

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Plant Design:**
> - **Cost** of heads typically accounts for **10–20% of vessel cost** — selecting the right type directly impacts capex.
> - **Weight** of the vessel affects support design, foundation, and transportation.
> - **Fabrication facility** is the hidden constraint — torispherical can be cold-formed on a press; ellipsoidal usually requires hot-forming or large hydraulic presses.
> - **Process duty** (e.g., fermentation tanks, slurry reactors, hoppers) **forces** the geometry (conical for hoppers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Flat Plate (Thin Plate Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Circular plate, radius `R`, uniform pressure `P`, simply supported (or clamped) at edge, deflection `w(r)`.

The plate equation in polar coordinates (axisymmetric):

$$ D \nabla^4 w = q $$

where flexural rigidity `D = Et³ / [12(1 − ν²)]`, `q = P` (uniform load).

For a clamped edge (`w = 0`, `dw/dr = 0` at `r = R`):
The maximum bending stress occurs at the clamped edge:

$$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot R^2}{4 \cdot t^2} $$

Setting `σ_max = S · E` and solving for `t`:

$$ S \cdot E = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot R^2}{4 \cdot t^2} $$

$$ t^2 = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot R^2}{4 \cdot S \cdot E} $$

$$ t = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.75 \cdot P}{S \cdot E}} $$

For the conservative simply-supported case (used by ASME for bolted gasketed covers):

$$ t = R \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \cdot P}{S \cdot E}} $$

Therefore in code form: `t = d · √(C·P / (S·E))` with `C = 0.3` and `d = 2R` = plate diameter.

## 3.2 Derivation — Ellipsoidal 2:1 Closure

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting point:** General ellipsoid of revolution, semi-axes `a, b`. At the apex (pole), the principal radii of curvature are `R1 = R2 = a² / b`.

For a 2:1 ellipsoid: `a = 2b`, hence at the apex:

$$ R = \frac{a^2}{b} = \frac{(2b)^2}{b} = 4b $$

And since `a = Do/2` and `b = Do/4`:
$$ R = \frac{(Do/2)^2}{Do/4} = \frac{Do^2 / 4}{Do / 4} = Do $$

So the equivalent spherical radius at the apex of a 2:1 ellipsoid is `R = Do`.

Membrane stress in a thin pressure vessel head (spherical approximation near apex):

$$ \sigma = \frac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t} $$

Setting `σ = S · E` and solving for `t`:

$$ S \cdot E = \frac{P \cdot Do}{2 \cdot t} $$

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot Do}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P} $$

The denominator term `0.2P` accounts for the difference between inside and outside diameter formulations.

## 3.3 Derivation — Torispherical Closure

The torispherical head is **not** a single equation but a piecewise geometry: a spherical crown of radius `Rc` and a toroidal knuckle of radius `rk`.

For the **spherical crown region** (apex), the membrane stress is:

$$ \sigma = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 \cdot t} $$

The knuckle region has higher local stress due to curvature discontinuity. ASME handles this by requiring:

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P} $$

For the **standard 2:1 torispherical (ASME 2:1 equivalent)**:
- `Rc = 0.9 · Do`
- `rk = 0.17 · Do`

Substituting `Rc = 0.9 · Do`:
$$ t = \frac{0.9 \cdot P \cdot Do}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the ASME pressure limit `0.665 SE` for torispherical?**
> At higher pressures, the knuckle experiences plastic collapse. Empirical studies and the Derive-3 rule of ASME set the upper limit at `P = 0.665 SE` for the standard geometry. Beyond this, the head must be redesigned (larger knuckle, or use ellipsoidal).

## 3.4 Derivation — Conical Closure

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A truncated cone with half-apex angle `α`, large-end diameter `DL`, subjected to internal pressure `P`. The membrane (hoop) stress on the cone surface is:

$$ \sigma_\theta = \frac{P \cdot D_{cone}}{2 \cdot t \cdot \cos\alpha} $$

where `D_cone` is the local diameter along the cone. The critical (maximum) location is at the **large end**, `D_cone = DL`:

$$ \sigma_\theta = \frac{P \cdot D_L}{2 \cdot t \cdot \cos\alpha} $$

Setting `σθ = S · E` and solving:

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot D_L}{2 \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot (S \cdot E - 0.6 P)} $$

The `0.6P` factor in the denominator handles the inside/outside diameter difference, the same way as in cylinder formulas (where the analogous term is `0.6P`).

### 3.4.1 Cone-Cylinder Junction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress concentration at the cone-shell junction** is a critical design consideration. ASME UG-32(g) requires a **toroidal knuckle (fillet) reinforcement** if `α > 30°`. The reinforcement is sized so that the area of metal removed at the junction is replaced. This is conceptually similar to a nozzle reinforcement calculation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Closures

| Parameter | Flat Plate | Torispherical (2:1) | Ellipsoidal 2:1 | Conical (α=30°) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Required `t` (typical, normalized)** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | High (varies with α) |
| **Cost of fabrication** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Medium-High |
| **Weight of head** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | High |
| **Internal volume lost** | Negligible | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Maximum efficiency `η = P/t`** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Lowest |
| **Process suitability** | Lids, baffles | General use | High-pressure | Solids discharge, hoppers |
| **ASME pressure limit (at given SE)** | None specific | 0.665 SE | 0.385 SE | Depends on reinforcement |
| **Depth of head** | 0 | ≈ 0.169 Do | 0.25 Do | DL − DS / (2 tanα) |

## 3.6 Worked Example — Comparative Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design the head for a vessel with `Do = 2000 mm`, `P = 1.0 MPa`, `S = 137 MPa`, `E = 1.0`, `CA = 1.5 mm`. Compare required thicknesses for all four closures.

### 3.6.1 Flat Plate (C = 0.3)

$$ t = 2000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \cdot 1.0}{137 \cdot 1.0}} $$

$$ t = 2000 \cdot \sqrt{0.002189} $$

$$ t = 2000 \cdot 0.04679 = 93.58 \text{ mm} $$

$$ t_{design} = 93.58 + 1.5 = 95.08 \text{ mm} $$

### 3.6.2 Torispherical (Rc = 0.9 Do = 1800 mm)

$$ t = \frac{1.0 \cdot 1800}{2 \cdot 137 \cdot 1.0 - 0.2 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{1800}{273.8} = 6.575 \text{ mm} $$

$$ t_{design} = 6.575 + 1.5 = 8.075 \text{ mm } \approx 9 \text{ mm} $$

### 3.6.3 Ellipsoidal 2:1

$$ t = \frac{1.0 \cdot 2000}{2 \cdot 137 \cdot 1.0 - 0.2 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{2000}{273.8} = 7.305 \text{ mm} $$

$$ t_{design} = 7.305 + 1.5 = 8.805 \text{ mm} \approx 10 \text{ mm} $$

### 3.6.4 Conical (α = 30°)

$$ t = \frac{1.0 \cdot 2000}{2 \cdot \cos 30° \cdot (137 \cdot 1.0 - 0.6 \cdot 1.0)} = \frac{2000}{2 \cdot 0.866 \cdot 136.4} $$

$$ t = \frac{2000}{236.3} = 8.464 \text{ mm} $$

$$ t_{design} = 8.464 + 1.5 = 9.964 \text{ mm} \approx 10 \text{ mm} $$

### 3.6.5 Comparison Summary

| Closure | t_required (mm) | t_design (mm) | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat Plate | 93.58 | 95 | 10× thicker — uneconomical |
| Torispherical | 6.58 | 9 | Most economical choice |
| Ellipsoidal 2:1 | 7.30 | 10 | Marginal extra over tori |
| Conical (30°) | 8.46 | 10 | Use only if process needs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For this case, **torispherical** is the most economical. The flat plate is grossly uneconomical. Ellipsoidal gives slightly thicker head for higher pressure reserve. Conical only justified if solids discharge is needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closure Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Closure Selection Logic] --> B{Process Need?}
    B -->|Solids/slurry discharge| C[Conical Closure]
    B -->|Drainage or steam jacket| D[Torispherical Closure]
    B -->|High pressure containment| E[Ellipsoidal 2:1 Closure]
    B -->|Low pressure, large diameter| F[Flat Plate Closure]
    C --> G{Alpha angle > 30 degrees?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Add toroidal knuckle reinforcement]
    G -->|No| I[Plain cone, no reinforcement]
    D --> J[Standard Rc 0.9 Do, rk 0.17 Do]
    E --> K[Standard 2:1 ratio a equals 2b]
    F --> L[Bolt to flange with gasket]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Geometry Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Flat Plate]
        P1A[Plane circular surface] --> P1B[Constant radius infinity] --> P1C[Bolt to flange]
    end
    subgraph P2[Torispherical]
        P2A[Spherical crown Rc 0.9 Do] --> P2B[Toroidal knuckle rk 0.17 Do] --> P2C[Straight flange]
    end
    subgraph P3[Ellipsoidal 2:1]
        P3A[Smooth elliptical surface] --> P3B[Major axis Do, minor axis Do slash 2] --> P3C[Depth Do slash 4]
    end
    subgraph P4[Conical]
        P4A[Truncated cone frustum] --> P4B[Half apex angle alpha] --> P4C[Knuckle if alpha greater than 30]
    end
```

## 4.3 Stress Distribution Map (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Internal Pressure P] --> S1[Flat Plate: Bending stress dominates]
    S --> S2[Torispherical: Mixed membrane plus knuckle stress]
    S --> S3[Ellipsoidal: Pure membrane stress]
    S --> S4[Conical: Membrane plus junction stress concentration]
    S1 --> O1[Thickness penalty high]
    S2 --> O2[Thickness moderate]
    S3 --> O3[Thickness lowest, most efficient]
    S4 --> O4[Thickness depends on alpha and reinforcement]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for KTU Examination Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given: Do, P, S, E, CA] --> Q1{Which closure is asked?}
    Q1 -->|Flat| R1[Use t equals d sqrt C P over S E with C from table]
    Q1 -->|Torispherical| R2[Use t equals P Rc over 2SE minus 0.2P, Rc 0.9 Do]
    Q1 -->|Ellipsoidal 2:1| R3[Use t equals P Do over 2SE minus 0.2P]
    Q1 -->|Conical| R4{Alpha given?}
    R4 -->|Yes, less than 30| R5[Use t equals P DL over 2 cos alpha times SE minus 0.6P]
    R4 -->|Yes, greater than 30| R6[Add knuckle reinforcement per ASME UG-32]
    R1 --> OUT1[Add CA, round up, choose standard plate]
    R2 --> OUT1
    R3 --> OUT1
    R5 --> OUT1
    R6 --> OUT1
```
