# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]`
**"List any three criteria for the selection of pressure vessel heads."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, complete in 3 points):**

The three primary criteria for the selection of pressure vessel heads are:

1. **Operating Pressure and Temperature:** Higher pressures and temperatures favor ellipsoidal 2:1 heads due to superior stress distribution. Low-pressure applications can use flat plates economically.

2. **Process Function:** If the vessel handles solids, slurries, or requires internal drainage, a conical closure is mandatory for proper discharge. Sloped bottoms require conical geometry.

3. **Cost and Fabrication Facility:** Flat plates are the cheapest to fabricate. Torispherical heads are economical and can be cold-formed on standard presses. Ellipsoidal 2:1 heads are most expensive (require deeper draw) but provide the most efficient design. The available fabrication facility in the workshop often dictates the practical choice.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set B]`
**"State the ASME design formula for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head and the limit on design pressure."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, in formula + limit format):**

The ASME Section VIII-1 design formula for a 2:1 ellipsoidal head is:

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P} $$

where `t` = required thickness, `P` = design pressure, `Do` = outside diameter, `S` = allowable stress, `E` = joint efficiency.

**Limit on design pressure:** The formula is valid only when `P ≤ 0.385 · S · E`. Beyond this limit, the ellipsoidal head must be redesigned with a different ratio, or an entirely different closure geometry must be used.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:**
> A pressure vessel of inside diameter 1500 mm is fitted with a standard 2:1 torispherical head. The design pressure is 2.5 MPa, design temperature is 200°C, joint efficiency is 0.85, and corrosion allowance is 3 mm. The material is SA-516 Grade 70 with allowable stress 138 MPa at 200°C. Calculate the required head thickness.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: List the data.**

$$ D_o = D_i + 2t_{assumed} $$

For an initial estimate, assume `t ≈ 10 mm`:
$$ D_o = 1500 + 2(10) = 1520 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2: For a standard 2:1 torispherical head, crown radius:**

$$ R_c = 0.9 \cdot D_o = 0.9 \cdot 1520 = 1368 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3: Apply ASME VIII-1 formula:**

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot R_c}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2 \cdot P} $$

$$ t = \frac{2.5 \cdot 1368}{2 \cdot 138 \cdot 0.85 - 0.2 \cdot 2.5} $$

**Step 4: Compute denominator:**

$$ 2 \cdot 138 \cdot 0.85 = 234.6 $$

$$ 234.6 - 0.2 \cdot 2.5 = 234.6 - 0.5 = 234.1 $$

**Step 5: Compute thickness:**

$$ t = \frac{3420}{234.1} = 14.61 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 6: Check pressure limit.**

$$ P_{limit} = 0.665 \cdot S \cdot E = 0.665 \cdot 138 \cdot 0.85 = 78.0 \text{ MPa} $$

Since `2.5 MPa < 78 MPa`, the design is within the allowable limit. ✔

**Step 7: Add corrosion allowance and round up:**

$$ t_{design} = 14.61 + 3 = 17.61 \text{ mm} \approx 18 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 8: Verify outside diameter assumption.**

$$ D_o = 1500 + 2 \cdot 18 = 1536 \text{ mm} $$

This is a small change (1520 → 1536), so the iteration converges. Final answer:

$$ \boxed{t_{design} = 18 \text{ mm}, \quad D_o = 1536 \text{ mm}} $$

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:**
> Explain why a conical head with half-apex angle greater than 30° requires reinforcement at the cone-cylinder junction. Describe the type of reinforcement used.

**Model Answer (7 marks, in structured points):**

**1. Stress Concentration at Junction (3 marks):**
At the cone-cylinder junction, the geometry changes abruptly from a cylindrical shell to a conical shell. This discontinuity produces:
- A **membrane stress discontinuity** in the hoop direction
- A **bending stress** in the meridional direction
- A **shear stress** transferred from the cone to the cylinder

These concentrated stresses, if unmitigated, can cause local yielding, fatigue cracking, and ultimate failure at the junction.

**2. Mathematical Justification (2 marks):**
The hoop stress in a cone of half-apex angle `α` is `P·DL / (2·t·cosα)`, which at `α = 0°` reduces to the cylinder hoop stress `P·D/(2t)`. As `α` increases, the denominator `cosα` decreases, increasing the hoop stress. For `α > 30°`, the stress concentration factor becomes large enough (typically `> 1.5`) to require reinforcement.

**3. Type of Reinforcement (2 marks):**
ASME Section VIII-1 UG-32(g) requires a **toroidal knuckle (fillet) transition** between the cone and the cylinder. The knuckle radius is selected so that:
- The knuckle thickness at the cone end equals or exceeds the cone thickness
- The knuckle thickness at the cylinder end equals or exceeds the cylinder thickness
- The transition is smooth, with no sharp corner

The knuckle is typically formed by hot or cold pressing, and its radius is empirically chosen (typically `rk ≥ 0.5 · t_cone` and `rk ≥ 10% of DL`).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:**
> A vertical storage tank has `Do = 3000 mm` and a conical bottom closure with half-apex angle 25°. The tank operates at 0.5 MPa internal pressure, with material allowable stress 120 MPa, joint efficiency 0.9, and corrosion allowance 2 mm. Calculate the required thickness of the cone at its large end. State whether knuckle reinforcement is required.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: List the data.**

$$ D_L = D_o = 3000 \text{ mm}, \quad \alpha = 25°, \quad P = 0.5 \text{ MPa} $$

$$ S = 120 \text{ MPa}, \quad E = 0.9, \quad CA = 2 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2: Apply the ASME conical head formula.**

$$ t = \frac{P \cdot D_L}{2 \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot (S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P)} $$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator terms.**

$$ S \cdot E = 120 \cdot 0.9 = 108 \text{ MPa} $$

$$ S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P = 108 - 0.6 \cdot 0.5 = 108 - 0.3 = 107.7 \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \cos 25° = 0.9063 $$

$$ 2 \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot (S \cdot E - 0.6P) = 2 \cdot 0.9063 \cdot 107.7 = 195.21 $$

**Step 4: Compute thickness.**

$$ t = \frac{0.5 \cdot 3000}{195.21} = \frac{1500}{195.21} = 7.684 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5: Add corrosion allowance.**

$$ t_{design} = 7.684 + 2 = 9.684 \text{ mm} \approx 10 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 6: Knuckle reinforcement check.**

Since `α = 25° < 30°`, per ASME UG-32(g), **no knuckle reinforcement is mandatory** at the cone-cylinder junction. However, a small transition fillet is still good engineering practice.

$$ \boxed{t_{cone} = 10 \text{ mm, no mandatory knuckle reinforcement}} $$

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:**
> Compare the design and selection of flat plate, torispherical, ellipsoidal, and conical closures under the following headings: (i) required thickness, (ii) fabrication cost, (iii) typical process application. Give one example for each closure.

**Model Answer — Tabular Comparison (7 marks):**

| Closure Type | Required Thickness | Fabrication Cost | Process Application | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Plate** | Highest (bending stress dominates) | Lowest (no forming needed, just a cut disc) | Low-pressure, large-diameter tanks, manways, inspection covers | Manhole cover on a water storage tank |
| **Torispherical** | Moderate (mixed membrane and knuckle stress) | Moderate (cold-formed on a press) | General-purpose medium-pressure vessels, storage tanks | Diesel storage tank at a refinery |
| **Ellipsoidal 2:1** | Lowest (membrane stress only, optimal shape) | Highest (requires deep draw, hot-forming often needed) | High-pressure reactors, distillation columns | Ammonia converter shell closure |
| **Conical** | Variable (depends on `α`) | High (especially with knuckle reinforcement) | Solids discharge, slurry, hoppers, settling tanks | Bottom cone of a sugar crystallizer |

**Key Engineering Insight (1 mark bonus point):**
The choice of closure is rarely made on **thickness alone** — it is governed by the **process function**. A high-pressure vessel might still use a conical bottom if it discharges solids (e.g., a slurry polymerization reactor). In that case, the cone is reinforced and the vessel may also have ellipsoidal top closure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in This Topic (Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use `Do` (outside diameter) instead of `Di` (inside diameter):** ASME formulas for heads use **outside diameter**. Using `Di` gives a non-conservative (too thin) result and is marked wrong.
>
> 2. **Omitting the `0.2P` (or `0.6P`) term in the denominator:** Many students write `t = PR/(2SE)` and lose **2–3 marks**. The `0.2P` term handles the inside/outside diameter difference and **must be retained**.
>
> 3. **Not adding corrosion allowance:** The required thickness `t` is the *minimum*. The design thickness is `t + CA`. Examiners specifically check this.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong value of `C` for flat plates:** `C = 0.30` is for bolted gasketed covers (conservative). `C = 0.16` is for welded fixed plates. Mixing these values is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify ASME pressure limits** (`0.665 SE` for torispherical, `0.385 SE` for ellipsoidal 2:1): If the design pressure exceeds the limit, the formula is **invalid** and the student must either redesign or switch closure type.
>
> 6. **Conical head — using `DL` for the diameter:** The formula uses the **large-end** diameter, not the small end. Some students mistakenly use `DS` and get a too-thin result.
>
> 7. **Not rounding up the design thickness to the next standard plate size** (e.g., 6.58 mm → 8 mm, not 6.58 mm). This is worth **1 mark** in design questions.
>
> 8. **Mixing up units:** Pressure in MPa, stress in MPa, dimensions in mm — the formula is consistent **only if all units are in MPa and mm** (MPa = N/mm²). Mixing bar and MPa is a common pitfall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Selection Rules
- **Flat plate** → lowest pressure, largest diameter, cheapest.
- **Torispherical (2:1 standard)** → most economical general-purpose choice.
- **Ellipsoidal 2:1** → highest pressure, most efficient.
- **Conical** → solids/slurry discharge, hoppers, drainage.

### Critical Formulas (one-line memorization)
- **Flat:** `t = d · √(0.3P / SE)` for bolted clamped.
- **Tori:** `t = (0.9 · P · Do) / (2SE − 0.2P)` for standard 2:1.
- **Ellipsoidal:** `t = (P · Do) / (2SE − 0.2P)`.
- **Conical:** `t = (P · DL) / (2 cosα · (SE − 0.6P))`.

### Critical Constants to Remember
- ASME limit for torispherical: **`P ≤ 0.665 SE`**.
- ASME limit for ellipsoidal 2:1: **`P ≤ 0.385 SE`**.
- Standard 2:1 ratio: **`a = 2b`**, where `a = Do/2`, `b = Do/4`.
- Knuckle reinforcement threshold for cones: **`α > 30°`**.

### Common Engineering Numbers
- Flat plate: ~10× thicker than ellipsoidal for the same conditions.
- Torispherical gives ~10% weight saving over flat for medium pressure.
- Ellipsoidal 2:1 gives ~20–30% weight saving over torispherical for high pressure.

### Process-Driven Selection
- Storage of liquids → **torispherical** (cheap, standard).
- Reactor with catalyst withdrawal → **conical** bottom.
- High-pressure column → **ellipsoidal** 2:1 top and bottom.
- Manway / inspection cover → **flat plate** (bolt-on).
- Slurry settling tank → **conical** with small α (15°–20°).

### Examiner's Quick-Fire Facts
- Torispherical = "dished head" = combination of sphere + torus.
- Ellipsoidal 2:1 = ASME standard because its apex radius equals `Do` (simple formula).
- Conical half-angle `α` is measured **from the axis**, not from the cone surface.
- "Design thickness" = `t_required + CA`, rounded up to next standard plate.
- "Nominal thickness" = `t_design` (the value used in drawings).
- "Corrosion allowance" is always on the **inside** (the wetted side) for carbon steel vessels.

### KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- 3-mark questions often ask: "List criteria for head selection" → answer with **at least 3** distinct criteria.
- 7-mark questions: always show the **formula first**, then plug in numbers, then add CA, then round.
- 14-mark questions: usually split into **(a) design calculation (7 marks)** + **(b) selection/justification (7 marks)**.
- Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "Assume `t ≈ 10 mm` for outside diameter estimation").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compensation of Openings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT602 - PROCESS PLANT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Compensation of Openings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Compensation of Openings** is the design methodology used in pressure vessel engineering to restore the structural strength lost when a section of the vessel shell, head, or formed section is cut out to accommodate an **opening** (such as a nozzle, manhole, handhole, or branch connection). Because material is removed, the membrane stress that the original wall could carry is disrupted and concentrated around the discontinuity. Compensation restores this lost load-carrying capacity by adding **reinforcement material** in the form of excess shell thickness, excess nozzle neck thickness, or an external/integral **reinforcement pad (pad reinforcement)**. The governing philosophy is the **"Area Replacement Rule"** — the cross-sectional metal area that resists membrane stress near an opening must be equal to or greater than the area removed by the opening, evaluated over a defined **reinforcement zone (parallel pitch zone)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **wooden plank** of uniform thickness that is carrying a load along its length. Now drill a circular hole through it. The load that was carried by the wood fibres where the hole is now must be **redirected** around the hole, which causes **stress concentration** at the hole's edge. To prevent the plank from failing at the hole, you would: 1. Either make the plank **thicker** near the hole (extra shell/nozzle thickness). 2. Or **glue a thicker patch** (reinforcement pad) over the hole to share the load. A pressure vessel behaves identically. The membrane force $F = P \cdot R$ per unit length (where $R$ is the inner radius) must flow around the opening. The material removed must be "replaced" by **strength-equivalent material** within a specific zone parallel to the vessel wall. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Compensation is **NOT** about making the opening area equal — it is about making the **load-carrying cross-sectional area**, evaluated in planes parallel to the vessel surface (longitudinal and transverse), equal to or greater than the area that would have existed without the opening. ### 1.3 Physical Standards & Metrics The widely accepted standard adopted globally and referenced in the KTU syllabus for compensation design is: - **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII, Division 1** — UG-36 to UG-43 - Key metric: **Reinforcement Area** $A$, measured in mm² (or in² in imperial units) - Design pressure: **$P$** (MPa or psi) - Allowable stress: **$S$** (MPa or psi) - Joint efficiency: **$E$** (dimensionless, $0 \le E \le 1$) - Corrosion allowance: **$C_A$** (mm or in) The principle is **equal area**, not equal thickness or equal diameter. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Compensation Is Required — The Mechanics When an opening is cut into a pressurized shell, three failure modes must be checked: 1. **Excess membrane stress** in the shell around the opening — addressed by **area replacement**. 2. **Excess shear stress** at the junction (nozzle-to-shell weld) — addressed by **weld sizing** and **weld leg overlap checks** (UG-41, UG-41.1). 3. **Excess local bending stress** at the discontinuity — addressed by **rounding the opening corner radius** $r_1$ (UG-36 c), and by **pad reinforcement** stiffening the local zone. The KTU syllabus primarily tests **membrane area replacement** (UG-36). ### 2.2 The Area Replacement Rule — Step Logic **Step 1 — Establish the reinforcement zone (parallel pitch).** - The zone extends a distance of **$d$ (nozzle inside diameter)** parallel to the vessel wall on every side of the opening axis of symmetry. - It is bounded on the outside by the **outer surface of the shell + pad** and on the inside by the **inner surface of the nozzle neck** (projected). **Step 2 — Compute the required reinforcement area $A_{req}$.** - This is the area of metal that would have been present (after corrosion) had the opening not been made. - Located within the limits set by the design rules. **Step 3 — Compute the available reinforcement area $A_{avail}$.** - Sum of contributions from: 1. Excess shell thickness beyond required. 2. Excess nozzle neck thickness beyond required. 3. Pad reinforcement area within the zone (subject to height limit). 4. Weld metal area (subject to height limit and a stress-reduction factor). 5. Material in the "drop" of the nozzle projecting inward. **Step 4 — Check the inequality $A_{avail} \ge A_{req}$.** ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Required shell thickness (cylindrical) | $t_{sh} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$ | mm | UG-27(c)(1) | | 2 | Required head thickness (hemispherical) | $t_{hd} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 S E - 0.2 P}$ | mm | UG-27(f) | | 3 | Required nozzle neck thickness | $t_{nn} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{S \cdot E - 0.6 P}$ | mm | Same form as shell, $r$ = nozzle inner radius | | 4 | Required reinforcement area | $A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh} \cdot F$ | mm² | $F$ = strength-reduction factor for branch | | 5 | Parallel pitch (zone width) | $L = 2d$ | mm | Extends $d$ on each side of opening | | 6 | Maximum pad thickness usable | $t_{p,max} = \min(\text{actual pad thickness},\, 1.5\,t_{sh})$ | mm | UG-40(b) limit | | 7 | Pad effective width each side | $b = \min(d,\, 0.5 d_{pad})$ | mm | UG-40(b) | | 8 | Strength reduction factor | $F = \min\!\left(1.0,\;\dfrac{S_n}{S_{sh}}\right)$ | – | $S_n$ = nozzle allowable, $S_{sh}$ = shell allowable | | 9 | Stress ratio for branch | $f_r = \dfrac{S_{branch}}{S_{shell}}$ | – | Used only when branch connected to shell | | 10 | Limit on pad contribution | $A_{pad}^{eff} \le A_{req}$ | mm² | UG-40(a) cap | > [!IMPORTANT] > When the nozzle material has **lower** allowable stress than the shell, the factor $F < 1.0$ **increases** $A_{req}$ — this is the "strength reduction factor" of UG-37. > When the **branch** material is weaker than the shell, the factor $f_r < 1.0$ also scales the required area. ### 2.4 Engineering Significance The compensation methodology is the single most-failed check in static-equipment design. Industrial consequences of under-compensation include: - **Ductile rupture** at nozzle junctions during hydrotest or operation. - **Fatigue cracking** in cyclic-service vessels (FCC reactors, hydrocracker reactors, ammonia converters). - **Costly retrofits** — a single under-reinforced nozzle can shut down an entire refinery column. Compensation calculations are mandatory for every nozzle ≥ **NPS 3 (DN 80)** or any nozzle exceeding the limits of UG-36(c)(3). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Required Reinforcement Area $A_{req}$ #### 3.1.1 Origin of the Formula Consider a segment of cylindrical shell of unit axial length, cut along the longitudinal seam. The membrane tensile force per unit circumferential length that the shell must carry is: $$ F = P \cdot R \quad \text{(force per unit length, N/mm)} $$ If the shell thickness required to carry this force is $t_{sh}$, then the cross-sectional metal area per unit length that resists the membrane force is: $$ A_{per\;length} = t_{sh} \quad \text{(mm² per mm length)} $$ When an opening of inside diameter $d$ is cut into the shell, the metal removed over a projected axial length $d$ is: $$ A_{removed} = d \cdot t_{sh} $$ To restore the original load path, **the same area must be replaced** within the reinforcement zone: $$ A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh} \cdot F $$ where the **factor $F$** accounts for the possibility that the nozzle or branch material is weaker than the shell. The formal ASME definition (UG-37) is: $$ F = \frac{S_n}{S_{sh}} \quad \text{for a simple radial nozzle on the shell} $$ If $S_n \ge S_{sh}$, then $F$ is capped at **$1.0$**. If $S_n < S_{sh}$, then $F < 1.0$ and $A_{req}$ increases accordingly. #### 3.1.2 Worked Derivation in Aligned Steps **Given:** - Shell inner radius $R$ - Nozzle inner radius $r$ - Design pressure $P$ - Allowable stress of shell $S_{sh}$, nozzle $S_n$ - Joint efficiency $E$ - Corrosion allowance $C_A$ **Step (i):** Compute the required shell thickness (after corrosion): $$ t_{sh} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S_{sh} \cdot E - 0.6 P} + C_A $$ This is the form $t = PR/(SE-0.6P)$ plus the corrosion allowance. **Step (ii):** Compute the required nozzle neck thickness: $$ t_{nn} = \frac{P \cdot r}{S_n \cdot E - 0.6 P} + C_A $$ **Step (iii):** Compute the inside diameter of the nozzle: $$ d = 2 r $$ **Step (iv):** Compute the strength reduction factor: $$ F = \min\!\left(1.0,\;\frac{S_n}{S_{sh}}\right) $$ **Step (v):** Compute required reinforcement area: $$ A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh} \cdot F $$ This is the master formula. The student **must remember** that $t_{sh}$ is the *required* thickness (not actual thickness) used in this calculation. #### 3.1.3 Generalisation for Branch Connections When a branch pipe is attached to a shell, an additional reduction factor $f_r$ is applied (UG-37, branches): $$ A_{req}^{branch} = d_{branch} \cdot t_{sh} \cdot F \cdot f_r $$ where $$ f_r = 1.0 \quad \text{when } S_{branch} \ge S_{shell} $$ $$ f_r = \frac{S_{branch}}{S_{shell}} \quad \text{when } S_{branch} < S_{shell} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The two factors $F$ and $f_r$ are conceptually identical but apply to **different members**: $F$ relates the *nozzle neck* to the *shell*; $f_r$ relates the *branch pipe* to the *shell*. They both ensure that weaker attached members do not under-reinforce the joint. ### 3.2 Derivation of Available Reinforcement Area $A_{avail}$ The available area is the sum of five geometric contributions inside the reinforcement zone. We denote the **limits of reinforcement** as: - Longitudinal direction: $L = 2 d$ (extends $d$ on each side of opening centerline) - Transverse direction: bounded by the outer surface of the shell (or pad) above and by the inner surface of the nozzle neck below. The five contributions are: #### Contribution (1): Excess Shell Thickness $$ A_1 = \big(t_{sh}^{actual} - t_{sh}^{req}\big) \cdot L_1 $$ where $L_1$ is the longitudinal extent of shell in the zone, capped at $L = 2d$. #### Contribution (2): Excess Nozzle Neck Thickness $$ A_2 = 2 \big(t_{nn}^{actual} - t_{nn}^{req}\big) \cdot t_{nn}^{actual} \quad \text{(when nozzle projects inward)} $$ If the nozzle projects outward, a similar formula applies to the projecting length $h$: $$ A_2 = 2 \cdot h \cdot \big(t_{nn}^{actual} - t_{nn}^{req}\big) $$ #### Contribution (3): Pad Reinforcement Area The effective pad area is computed as: $$ A_3 = b \cdot t_p $$ where - $t_p$ is the **effective pad thickness** $= \min(t_p^{actual},\;1.5\,t_{sh}^{actual})$ - $b$ is the **effective width on each side** of the opening $= \min(d,\; 0.5\, d_{pad})$ So $A_3 = t_p \cdot \min(d,\;0.5 d_{pad})$, but on **both sides** of the nozzle: $$ A_3 = 2 \cdot t_p \cdot \min\!\big(d,\;0.5\,d_{pad}\big) $$ #### Contribution (4): Weld Metal Area The weld that joins the nozzle to the shell contributes reinforcement, but is heavily penalised (only 50% or 25% is credited depending on code). The leg-of-fillet dimensions $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ give: $$ A_4 = (\ell_1)^2 + (\ell_2)^2 \quad \text{(triangular fillet area approximation)} $$ The ASME cap is that the **weld credit cannot exceed one-half of $A_{req}$** for a nozzle directly welded to the shell. #### Contribution (5): Inward Projection of Nozzle If the nozzle projects into the vessel, the inward lip acts as additional reinforcement: $$ A_5 = 2 \cdot h_i \cdot \big(t_{nn}^{actual} - t_{nn}^{req}\big) $$ where $h_i$ is the inward projection height (subject to limits). #### Total Available Area $$ A_{avail} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5 $$ ### 3.3 The Governing Inequality $$ \boxed{\;A_{avail} \;\ge\; A_{req}\;} $$ If the inequality holds, the opening is **adequately reinforced**. If it does not hold, the designer must: 1. Increase shell thickness, OR 2. Increase nozzle neck thickness, OR 3. Add (or thicken) a reinforcement pad, OR 4. Use a forged nozzle with greater integral thickness (integral reinforcement — preferred for high-pressure service). ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel has shell inner diameter $D = 1500$ mm, design pressure $P = 2.5$ MPa, shell material SA-516 Gr.70 ($S_{sh} = 138$ MPa), nozzle material SA-106 Gr.B ($S_n = 138$ MPa — same as shell), joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $C_A = 1.5$ mm. A nozzle of **inside diameter $d = 250$ mm** is welded to the shell. Determine the required reinforcement area. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Required shell thickness:** $$ t_{sh} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S_{sh} E - 0.6P} + C_A = \frac{2.5 \times 750}{138 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 2.5} + 1.5 $$ Compute the denominator first: $$ 138 - 1.5 = 136.5 $$ Compute the numerator: $$ 2.5 \times 750 = 1875 $$ Therefore: $$ t_{sh}^{req,\,bare} = \frac{1875}{136.5} = 13.736 \text{ mm} $$ Add corrosion allowance: $$ t_{sh}^{req} = 13.736 + 1.5 = 15.236 \text{ mm} $$ Round up to the next standard plate: **actual shell thickness $t_{sh}^{act} = 16$ mm**. **Step 2 — Required nozzle neck thickness:** Nozzle inner radius $r = 125$ mm: $$ t_{nn}^{bare} = \frac{P \cdot r}{S_n E - 0.6P} + C_A = \frac{2.5 \times 125}{138 - 1.5} + 1.5 $$ Compute: $$ t_{nn}^{bare} = \frac{312.5}{136.5} + 1.5 = 2.289 + 1.5 = 3.789 \text{ mm} $$ Round up: **actual nozzle neck thickness $t_{nn}^{act} = 5$ mm** (standard schedule). **Step 3 — Strength reduction factor:** Since $S_n = S_{sh}$: $$ F = \min\!\left(1.0,\;\frac{138}{138}\right) = 1.0 $$ **Step 4 — Required reinforcement area:** $$ A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh}^{req} \cdot F = 250 \times 15.236 \times 1.0 = 3809 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Compute available area (assume no pad initially):** Excess shell: $$ A_1 = (t_{sh}^{act} - t_{sh}^{req}) \cdot (2d) = (16 - 15.236) \times (2 \times 250) = 0.764 \times 500 = 382 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Excess nozzle neck (assume nozzle projects inward by $h_i = 25$ mm): $$ A_2 = 2 \times 25 \times (t_{nn}^{act} - t_{nn}^{req}) = 50 \times (5 - 3.789) = 50 \times 1.211 = 60.55 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Weld area (fillet leg $\ell_1 = \ell_2 = 8$ mm): $$ A_4 = (8)^2 + (8)^2 = 128 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Check inequality:** $$ A_{avail} = 382 + 60.55 + 128 = 570.55 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ A_{avail} = 570.55 \;\ll\; A_{req} = 3809 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{INADEQUATE} $$ **Step 7 — Add a reinforcement pad.** We need additional area: $$ A_{pad,\,min} = A_{req} - A_{avail} = 3809 - 570.55 = 3238.45 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Choose a pad of overall diameter $d_{pad} = 1.5 d = 375$ mm, i.e., effective width each side $= 0.5(d_{pad} - d) = 0.5(375 - 250) = 62.5$ mm. Effective pad thickness (limit $1.5 t_{sh}^{act} = 1.5 \times 16 = 24$ mm): $$ t_p = \frac{A_{pad,\,min}}{2 \times b} = \frac{3238.45}{2 \times 62.5} = 25.9 \text{ mm} $$ This exceeds the 24 mm limit. Therefore, increase pad diameter to $d_{pad} = 1.8 d = 450$ mm: $$ b = 0.5(450 - 250) = 100 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_p = \frac{3238.45}{2 \times 100} = 16.19 \text{ mm} $$ Use a **18 mm thick pad** of diameter **450 mm** — adequate reinforcement is achieved. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Limits and Rules (UG-36 to UG-43) | Code Reference | Rule | Numerical Limit | |----------------|------|----------------| | UG-36(b)(1) | Minimum nozzle size exempted from check | $d \le 2$ in (≈ 51 mm) — typically NPS 2 | | UG-36(c)(3) | Exemption when $d$ small relative to shell | $d \le \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{R\, t_{sh}}$ — optional short-circuit check | | UG-36(c)(2) | Corner radius at opening | $r_1 \ge 0.25\,t_{sh}$, $\ge 3\,t_{sh}$ preferred | | UG-40(a) | Pad area credit | $A_{pad} \le A_{req}$ — cannot over-credit pad | | UG-40(b) | Pad thickness limit | $t_p \le 1.5\,t_{sh}^{actual}$ | | UG-40(b) | Pad effective width | $b = \min(d,\;0.5\,d_{pad})$ | | UG-41 | Weld leg minimum | $\ell_{min} = 0.7\,t_{nn}$ for full-strength weld | | UG-43 | Reinforcement of multiple openings | Spacing $> 2 d$ — treated independently; spacing $\le 2 d$ — treated as one combined opening | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Nozzle Junction Reinforcement — Annotated Schematic (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Pressure Vessel Shell] --> B[Reinforcement Zone
Parallel Pitch 2d] B --> C1[Excess Shell Thickness
A1 contribution] B --> C2[Excess Nozzle Neck Thickness
A2 contribution] B --> C3[Pad Reinforcement
A3 contribution] B --> C4[Weld Metal Area
A4 contribution] B --> C5[Inward Projection of Nozzle
A5 contribution] C1 --> D[Total Available Area Aavail] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D D --> E{Is Aavail greater than or equal to Areq?} E -- Yes --> F[Opening ADEQUATELY Reinforced] E -- No --> G[Increase Shell Thickness
OR Increase Nozzle Thickness
OR Add Pad] G --> H[Re-iterate Compensation Check] H --> E ``` ### 4.2 Reinforcement Zone Geometry — Side View ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outer Surface of Shell] --> B[Reinforcement Pad
thickness tp] B --> C[Inner Surface of Shell
total thickness ts] C --> D[Required Thickness Line
after corrosion] D --> E[Nozzle Inside Diameter d] E --> F[Nozzle Neck
thickness tnn] F --> G[Weld Fillet 1
leg L1] G --> H[Pad Weld Fillet
leg L2] H --> I[Parallel Pitch Boundary
distance d from opening centerline] ``` ### 4.3 Compensation Decision Flow (Block Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB P0[Inputs: P, R, d, tsh, tnn, Ssh, Sn, E, CA] --> P1[Step 1: Compute Required Thicknesses
tsh req, tnn req] P1 --> P2[Step 2: Compute Strength Factor F] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute Required Area
Areq = d times tsh req times F] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Enumerate Contributions A1 through A5] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Total Available Area Aavail] P5 --> P6{Comparison
Aavail vs Areq} P6 -- Aavail greater than or equal to Areq --> P7[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE
Document in Data Sheet] P6 -- Aavail less than Areq --> P8[Iterate: Increase tp
or Increase dpad or Increase tsh] P8 --> P4 ``` ### 4.4 Diagram — Reinforcement of Multiple Adjacent Openings ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MultipleOpenings["Multiple Adjacent Openings Case UG-43"] O1[Opening 1
diameter d1] --> Z1[Pitch Zone 1
width 2 d1] O2[Opening 2
diameter d2] --> Z2[Pitch Zone 2
width 2 d2] Z1 --> ZQ{Pitch spacing
greater than 2d} Z2 --> ZQ ZQ -- YES --> IND[Treat as Independent Openings] ZQ -- NO --> COMB[Combine into Single Equivalent Opening
dcombined equals sum of diameters plus spacing] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > When two nozzles are placed closer than $2d$ apart (where $d$ is the larger nozzle diameter), the reinforcement zones overlap. ASME UG-43 requires that the **combined area be checked as if the two openings were one larger oval opening**, with the long axis equal to the sum of the diameters plus the spacing. This is a frequent cause of under-design in distillation columns with stacked trays. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "compensation of openings" as applied to pressure vessels. Why is reinforcement required around openings?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Compensation of openings is the design procedure used to restore the membrane strength of a pressure vessel wall that has been reduced by cutting an opening for a nozzle, manhole or branch connection. Reinforcement is required because the material removed at the opening reduces the load-carrying cross-sectional area of the shell, causing stress concentration at the discontinuity. The design rule (ASME UG-36) requires that the **available metal area within the reinforcement zone** must be greater than or equal to the **required reinforcement area** $A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh} \cdot F$. If not satisfied, additional reinforcement in the form of excess thickness or a pad is added. *(3 marks)* --- #### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a "reinforcement pad"? State the limits on pad thickness and pad diameter as per ASME Section VIII.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A reinforcement pad is a flat plate welded to the outside of the shell around an opening to provide additional cross-sectional area for reinforcement of that opening. The limits are: 1. **Thickness limit:** effective pad thickness $t_p \le 1.5 \times t_{sh}^{actual}$. 2. **Effective width each side:** $b = \min(d,\;0.5 d_{pad})$. 3. **Area credit:** the pad contribution cannot exceed $A_{req}$ (UG-40(a)). 4. The pad must be provided with a tell-tale hole (1/4 in NPT) to detect any leak through the pad-to-shell weld. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** A cylindrical pressure vessel has the following data: - Shell inside diameter $D = 2000$ mm - Design pressure $P = 3.0$ MPa - Allowable stress of shell $S_{sh} = 138$ MPa (SA-516 Gr.70) - Allowable stress of nozzle $S_n = 138$ MPa (SA-106 Gr.B) - Joint efficiency $E = 1.0$ - Corrosion allowance $C_A = 2.0$ mm - Nozzle inside diameter $d = 300$ mm - Actual shell thickness provided $t_{sh}^{act} = 22$ mm - Actual nozzle neck thickness $t_{nn}^{act} = 8$ mm - Nozzle projects inward by $h_i = 30$ mm - Fillet weld legs: $\ell_1 = \ell_2 = 10$ mm **(a)** Calculate the required reinforcement area. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the available reinforcement area and determine whether a reinforcement pad is necessary. If yes, design the pad. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Required reinforcement area (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Required shell thickness:** $$ t_{sh}^{req,bare} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S_{sh} E - 0.6 P} = \frac{3.0 \times 1000}{138 - 1.8} = \frac{3000}{136.2} $$ $$ t_{sh}^{req,bare} = 22.03 \text{ mm} $$ Add corrosion allowance: $$ t_{sh}^{req} = 22.03 + 2.0 = 24.03 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Required nozzle neck thickness:** $$ t_{nn}^{req,bare} = \frac{P \cdot r}{S_n E - 0.6 P} = \frac{3.0 \times 150}{136.2} = \frac{450}{136.2} = 3.30 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_{nn}^{req} = 3.30 + 2.0 = 5.30 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Strength reduction factor:** $$ F = \min\!\left(1.0,\;\frac{S_n}{S_{sh}}\right) = \min(1.0,\;1.0) = 1.0 $$ **Step 4 — Required reinforcement area:** $$ A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh}^{req} \cdot F = 300 \times 24.03 \times 1.0 = 7209 \text{ mm}^2 $$ *(7 marks)* --- **(b) Available reinforcement area and pad design (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Excess shell contribution $A_1$:** $$ A_1 = (t_{sh}^{act} - t_{sh}^{req}) \cdot 2d = (22 - 24.03) \times 600 $$ Since $t_{sh}^{act} < t_{sh}^{req}$, the value is **negative** and $A_1 = 0$ (no excess). **Step 2 — Excess nozzle neck contribution $A_2$ (inward projection):** $$ A_2 = 2 \cdot h_i \cdot (t_{nn}^{act} - t_{nn}^{req}) = 2 \times 30 \times (8 - 5.30) = 60 \times 2.70 = 162 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Weld metal contribution $A_4$:** $$ A_4 = (\ell_1)^2 + (\ell_2)^2 = 100 + 100 = 200 \text{ mm}^2 $$ But capped at $0.5 \times A_{req} = 0.5 \times 7209 = 3604.5$ mm² — so $A_4 = 200$ mm² is fully credited. **Step 4 — Total available (no pad):** $$ A_{avail} = 0 + 162 + 200 = 362 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Deficit:** $$ \Delta A = A_{req} - A_{avail} = 7209 - 362 = 6847 \text{ mm}^2 $$ A pad is **mandatory**. **Step 6 — Pad design:** Choose pad overall diameter $d_{pad}$. Try $d_{pad} = 1.6 d = 480$ mm: $$ b = 0.5(d_{pad} - d) = 0.5(480 - 300) = 90 \text{ mm} $$ Pad thickness limit: $1.5 \times t_{sh}^{act} = 1.5 \times 22 = 33$ mm. $$ t_p = \frac{\Delta A}{2 \cdot b} = \frac{6847}{2 \times 90} = \frac{6847}{180} = 38.04 \text{ mm} $$ This exceeds 33 mm limit → increase $d_{pad}$. Try $d_{pad} = 2.0 d = 600$ mm: $$ b = 0.5(600 - 300) = 150 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_p = \frac{6847}{2 \times 150} = \frac{6847}{300} = 22.82 \text{ mm} $$ Within the 33 mm limit. **Final pad: 600 mm diameter, 24 mm thick (next standard size)**. Pad contribution: $$ A_3 = 2 \times 150 \times 24 = 7200 \text{ mm}^2 > \Delta A = 6847 \text{ mm}^2 \;\;\checkmark $$ **Final design is adequate.** Provide a tell-tale hole (1/4 in NPT) on the pad. *(7 marks)* --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel is fitted with two nozzles on the same longitudinal line. The data are: - Shell inside diameter $D = 1800$ mm - Shell thickness (actual) $t_{sh}^{act} = 20$ mm - Nozzle A inside diameter $d_A = 200$ mm - Nozzle B inside diameter $d_B = 250$ mm - Centre-to-centre spacing between nozzles $S = 350$ mm - Design pressure $P = 2.0$ MPa - Allowable stress $S_{sh} = 138$ MPa, $S_n = 138$ MPa - Joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, $C_A = 1.5$ mm **(a)** Determine the required reinforcement area for nozzle B (treated independently). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine whether the nozzles must be treated as a single combined opening under UG-43. If yes, recompute the equivalent reinforcement area. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Independent reinforcement area for nozzle B (7 marks):** Required shell thickness: $$ t_{sh}^{req,bare} = \frac{2.0 \times 900}{138 - 1.2} = \frac{1800}{136.8} = 13.16 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_{sh}^{req} = 13.16 + 1.5 = 14.66 \text{ mm} $$ Strength factor $F = 1.0$. Required reinforcement area: $$ A_{req,B} = d_B \cdot t_{sh}^{req} \cdot F = 250 \times 14.66 = 3665 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Available from excess shell:** $$ A_1 = (20 - 14.66) \times 2 \times 250 = 5.34 \times 500 = 2670 \text{ mm}^2 $$ Net deficit (without pad/nozzle contribution): $\Delta A_B = 3665 - 2670 = 995$ mm² — to be made up by weld + nozzle excess or pad. *(7 marks)* --- **(b) Combined opening check under UG-43 (7 marks):** Larger diameter $d_B = 250$ mm. Pitch spacing threshold: $$ 2 d_B = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ mm} $$ Given spacing $S = 350$ mm $< 500$ mm → **must be treated as a combined opening**. Equivalent combined diameter (longitudinal axis): $$ d_{combined} = d_A + d_B + S = 200 + 250 + 350 = 800 \text{ mm} $$ The reinforcement area is computed over an opening of effective diameter $d_{combined}$ for the longitudinal direction, while in the transverse direction it is the maximum of $d_A$ and $d_B$, i.e., **$d_B = 250$ mm**. However, under UG-43, the reinforcement area is conservatively evaluated using the larger dimension. The required area is approximated as: $$ A_{req,combined} = d_{combined} \cdot t_{sh}^{req} \cdot F = 800 \times 14.66 \times 1.0 = 11728 \text{ mm}^2 $$ This is **3.2× the single nozzle requirement**. The designer must: 1. Increase nozzle-to-nozzle spacing (preferred), OR 2. Provide a large reinforcing pad covering both openings, OR 3. Reduce design pressure. *(7 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that the UG-43 rule uses the **sum of diameters + spacing** as the equivalent combined length. They compute only the sum of diameters ($d_A + d_B = 450$ mm), underestimating the combined opening by 350 mm and producing an unsafe design. Always include the spacing. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Compensation of Openings (Module 2):** - The rule is **equal area**, not equal thickness or equal diameter — the cross-sectional area of metal in the reinforcement zone must equal or exceed the area removed. - The reinforcement zone extends **$d$ (nozzle inside diameter) on every side** of the opening axis, parallel to the shell surface. - The required reinforcement area is **$A_{req} = d \cdot t_{sh}^{req} \cdot F$**, with $F = \min(1.0,\;S_n/S_{sh})$. - Available area is the sum of **five contributions**: $A_1$ (excess shell) + $A_2$ (excess nozzle) + $A_3$ (pad) + $A_4$ (weld) + $A_5$ (inward projection). - Pad effective thickness is **capped at $1.5\,t_{sh}^{actual}$**; effective width each side is $\min(d,\;0.5\,d_{pad})$. - Pad area credit **cannot exceed $A_{req}$** (UG-40(a)). - Weld metal credit is **capped at 0.5 $A_{req}$** for nozzles directly welded to the shell. - A **tell-tale hole** (1/4 in NPT) must be provided on every reinforcement pad. - The required thickness used in $A_{req}$ is the **as-required value** (including corrosion allowance), NOT the as-built thickness. - For branch connections, $f_r$ is multiplied with $F$ in the area formula. - Adjacent openings within $2d$ spacing → **combined opening rule** of UG-43 applies. - Corner radius at opening **$r_1 \ge 0.25\,t_{sh}$** reduces stress concentration — preferred $r_1 \ge 3\,t_{sh}$. - **Integral reinforcement** (forged or weld-overlay built-up nozzles) is preferred over pads for high-pressure and cyclic-service vessels. - Openings $d \le 51$ mm (≈ NPS 2) may be exempted from explicit compensation check, but corner radius rule still applies. - The most common KTU valuation error is **forgetting the corrosion allowance** when computing $t_{sh}^{req}$. - The second most common error is **using $t_{sh}^{actual}$ instead of $t_{sh}^{req}$** in the $A_{req}$ formula — remember: $A_{req}$ uses required thickness, not actual. - The third most common error is **ignoring the $1.5\,t_{sh}$ pad thickness cap**, leading to over-credited pad area and unsafe designs in reality (although calculations may pass). - For spherical shells and formed heads, the same methodology applies with $t_{sh}$ replaced by $t_{hd}$. - Always document the **final $A_{avail}/A_{req}$ ratio** in the vessel data sheet — KTU and ASME both expect this in design documentation.
